To ask the Minister of Health whether the Fair Play Grant Scheme will continue in the 2026-27 financial year.
To ask the Minister of Education to detail any reason departmental Circular 2026/03 does not detail his Department's duties arising from the Integrated Education Act.
answer_text: The purpose of Circular 2026/03 is to advise schools on the processes involved in applying for a Temporary Variation (TV); it does not list every relevant legal duty on my Department. TV policy recognises Integrated education, irrespective of management type, as a distinct school sector. As a result, additional places at Integrated schools are normally approved, unless a suitable place is available for the children concerned at another Integrated school.  In doing so, my Department meets its statutory duties under the Integrated Education Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department has carried out any assessment of the impact on (i) schools; (ii) families; and (iii) children, when his Department's funding for Tiny Life ends on 31st March 2026.
answer_text: The Executive has not yet agreed a budget for 2026-27, however, to provide clarity for affected organisations, I have agreed an Interim Resource Budget for the first three months of the 202627 financial year. As a result TinyLife has received an interim three-month resource funding allocation in respect of the administration and delivery of the TinyStart Programme from 1 April 2026 to 30 June 2026.Any decision regarding future funding will be considered in the context of my Department’s 2026-27 budget allocation, the allocation of earmarked funding for the Executive Early Learning and Childcare Strategy and wider financial pressures.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the specific activities of the Education Authority FLARE project.
answer_text: The FLARE project operated as a communitybased emotional health and wellbeing programme delivered by professionally qualified youth workers within generic JNC youth work roles. The programme provided early support, confidencebuilding, wellbeing activities, positive peer culture and appropriate signposting for children and young people. In line the Education Authority’s (EA) Regional Youth Development Plan, the FLARE Team no longer operates as a discrete programme and work has been mainstreamed across statutory youth services. Youth workers across all localities now deliver universal provision, including safe spaces, openaccess programmes and positive peer culture, together with earlyintervention support, such as onetoone guidance, targeted group work and early identification of need.  The EA continues to provide training and capacitybuilding to fulltime youth workers, parttime staff and volunteers through the Partnership and Practice Development Unit, ensuring consistent delivery of emotional wellbeing support across the service.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any actions planned to deliver faster diagnoses of multiple myeloma at primary care level.
answer_text: The NI Cancer Network (NICaN) Haematology Clinical Reference Group (CRG) is currently updating NICaN Primary Care Referral Guidance for suspected cancers. This revised guideline will provide more specific advice on identifying suspected haematological malignancies, including multiple myeloma, as well as guidance on the appropriate referral pathways.      In addition, NICaN, in partnership with the Belfast Trust and the Macmillan GP facilitators, delivers monthly primary care education and awareness sessions for GPs, nurses, pharmacists and allied health professionals. These sessions aim to improve understanding of red-flag symptoms and support earlier recognition.  Further GP education sessions will be scheduled to coincide with release of updated NICaN Primary Care Guidance for Haematology Cancers (expected mid-late 2026). Patients may also hesitate to consult their GP when experiencing vague, non-specific symptoms, often attributing these to ageing or less serious conditions. This highlights the importance of improving both public awareness and GP awareness to support earlier identification and diagnosis of multiple myeloma in primary care. My Department has also established two Rapid Diagnostic Centres (RDCs) to support cancer diagnosis. GPs across Northern Ireland can now refer patients who do not meet the standard suspected cancer criteria but for whom they have ongoing clinical concern to the ‘Vague Symptoms Pathway’ provided through the RDCs. This service is designed to support earlier investigation and diagnosis for patients whose vague symptoms may indicate a blood cancer.In addition, work has commenced to facilitate region-wide access to Whole-Body MRI (WB-MRI) for patients with suspected myeloma, with dedicated imaging capacity now ring-fenced at the Rapid Diagnostic Centres (RDCs). WB-MRI is recommended as the first-line imaging modality for suspected myeloma because of its high sensitivity in detecting bone marrow infiltration before any bone destruction becomes apparent.Preliminary work is also underway as part of Action 16 of the NI Cancer Strategy 2022-2032 to establish a Specialist Integrated Haematology Malignancies Diagnostic Service (SIHMDS) for Northern Ireland, subject to the availability of funding. A SIHMDS will improve patient outcomes for haematological cancers through a more accurate, timely, and cost-efficient diagnostic process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any work being undertaken to improve public awareness of multiple myeloma.
answer_text: While there are currently no campaigns focused exclusively on multiple myeloma, the Public Health Agency (PHA), working in partnership with the Department of Health, Health and Social Care Trusts and a wide range of stakeholders, delivers programmes aimed at improving public awareness of cancer. This includes initiatives that support earlier diagnosis by raising awareness of key signs and symptoms and encouraging people to seek timely advice from their GP.The Northern Ireland Cancer Network (NICaN) Haematology Clinical Reference Group (CRG) is currently updating the NICaN Primary Care Suspected Cancer Referral Guidance for haematology. This update will include enhanced and more detailed guidance on myeloma to support earlier recognition and referral from primary care.The revised guidance is anticipated to launch in May/June, followed by a Primary Care education session for GPs and other healthcare professionals working in primary care.In parallel, NICaN will work in partnership with the Public Health Agency (PHA) to update the public-facing ‘Be Cancer Aware’ website to include information on myeloma/multiple myeloma, with the aim of improving public awareness and supporting earlier recognition of potential symptoms.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will extend funding for the SEN Inclusion Fund for settings in receipt of funding until the end of the current academic year.
answer_text: As you will be aware, no Executive Budget has yet been agreed for 2026/27.  Current indications are that Education is facing a projected funding gap of c£0.6 billion in 2026/27, increasing to c£0.9 billion in 2028/29.  Funding decisions for the 2026/27 financial year will be taken following confirmation of my Department’s 2026/27 final opening budget.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 37562/22-27, whether the site is on the open market for sale.
answer_text: The Education Authority has advised that its Estate Agent is currently preparing the marketing report.Once this has been approved, the property will proceed to the open market.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the current anticipated cost of establishing a managing authority for Controlled Schools.
answer_text: The proposed creation of a new organisation will create a more efficient and effective managing authority function for the controlled school sector.  Costs will be offset by the transfer of functions and respective budgets from the Education Authority and Controlled Schools Support Council, the latter being likely to be subsumed in its entirety to the new body. The overall confirmed financial impacts are yet to be fully explored as planning remains at an embryonic stage, but I can confirm, that overall final financial costings will be subject to full appraisal through the required business case process and costs will be met from within the current Departmental financial allocation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any work undertaken alongside the Minister of Health to support the long-term sustainability of the allied health workforce, particularly in relation to SEN transformation.
answer_text: The Minister for Health is responsible for the creation of Allied Health Professional (AHP) posts.  However AHPs will play a key role in supporting special educational needs (SEN) transformation. Working under the auspices of the Joint Health and Education Oversight Group, my department, the Department of Health and the Education Authority are in discussions regarding the deployment of AHPs, including Occupational Therapists, to support children with SEN in education settings.Work will be undertaken in due course to increase the number of AHPs and protected posts, subject to additional budget being identified.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether an assessment of the need and feasibility of a pedestrian crossing at Assumption Grammar School, Ballynahinch, will be carried out.
answer_text: Road safety is a high priority for my Department, and we are committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and to address the needs of all road users. We are acutely aware that children and young people are amongst the most vulnerable groups using our roads, particularly around schools.I have asked officials to undertake pedestrian crossing assessment, in line with the Department’s policy, to determine the need and priority of any crossing facility at this location.The existing warning signs and road markings will also be assessed to determine if they are appropriate for the area.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will provide a temporary extension of funding to Tiny Life while he awaits confirmation of his final 2026-2027 budget allocation.
answer_text: The Budgets for 2026-27 or subsequent periods have not yet been formally set by the Executive, therefore, I am not presently in a position to provide any commitments to funding for TinyLife. Any decision regarding future funding will be considered in the context of my Department’s 2026-27 budget allocation, the allocation of earmarked funding for the Executive Early Learning and Childcare Strategy and wider financial pressures.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the amount of funding provided to statutory nursery settings for places for non-target age children, in each of the last five academic years.
answer_text: The table below provides data on the number of penultimate age children that obtained a funded pre-school education place in a statutory pre-school setting in each of the last five academic years.2021/222022/232023/242024/252025/2612531342105913961401Source: NI school censusAll grant aided schools are funded under the Common Funding Scheme.  The Common Funding Formula (CFF) is the Department’s mechanism for allocating budgets to schools funded through Local Management of Schools arrangements. A school’s CFF allocation is determined by both the quantum of funding available to the Aggregated Schools Budget and a combination of the school’s overall enrolment, the differentiated needs of its pupils and the school’s characteristics.  Pupils that attend a statutory nursery school or nursery unit attract the same level of funding depending on the hours of attendance (part time/full-time), irrespective of age.  It is not possible to disaggregate the funding attributable to individual pupils from the total funding allocated to individual schools.  The table below provides details of the CFF Age Weighted Pupil Unit (AWPU) funding for nursery schools and units for each of the last five financial  years, for illustrative purposes.Setting TypeAWPU  Rate2021/22AWPU Rate2022/23AWPU Rate2023/24AWPURate 2024/25AWPU Rate2025/26Nursery School (FT)£3,444£3,422£3,488£4,093£4,493Nursery School (PT)£2,265£2,251£2,294£2,692£2,955Nursery Unit (FT)£3,202£3,182£3,243£3,806£4,177Nursery Unit (PT)£2,123£2,109£2,150£2,523£2,769The cost attributable to penultimate age pupils attending statutory nursery settings has to be considered in the context of the financial sustainability of these schools which will incur the staffing and running costs irrespective of pupil enrolments.The draft Early Learning and Childcare Strategy, which is currently out for public consultation, includes an action to establish a programme specifically for two year olds and an action to remove the current legislation that places a duty on statutory pre-school providers to admit penultimate aged children.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 40318/22-27, to detail an overview of the cross-departmental bid to the Transformation Fund on improving pathways and transitions.
answer_text: The aim of the Transformation Fund project - Transitions and Pathways for Post Schools Special Educational Needs - is to improve the life experiences and opportunities for individuals with Special Educational Needs as they leave school. The project, led by my Department, is in collaboration with Department of Education (DE), Department for Communities (DfC), Department of Health (DoH) and Department for Infrastructure (DfI).     The Transformation Fund bid was initially for £7.5m across three years, and set out three strands:a whole system service design model, enhanced transition, provision and pathways, andlive monitoring, evaluation and broader system recommendations.Strand 2, Enhanced transition, provision and pathways, will be taken forward primarily through collaboration between my Department and the Department of Health, with the other Departments involved where appropriate.In my Department the work includes pilots to develop and test SEN and Supported Apprenticeships, the co-design and testing of provision to meet gaps in provision between existing programmes/services, alongside developing approaches to enhance the transition for young people with SEN from school into the Further Education Colleges and DfE funded provision and programmes.In the Department of Health, the work will include piloting changes to Learning Disability Day Services, and co-ordinating and standardising transitions between Children and Adult Health and Social Care Services.The Public Sector Transformation Board recommended that an element of Strand 1, a whole system service design model, be removed from the proposal, resulting in the Board recommending an award of £6.925m instead of the initially requested amount. The bid has been recommended for funding by the Transformation Board.  My officials have begun preparatory work to ensure activity can begin as soon as funding is confirmed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 40318/22-27, to detail the physical enhancements that have taken place on some further education sites.
answer_text: I have been advised by the Further Education colleges that they have delivered or are delivering a range of physical enhancements across their campuses to support learners with special educational needs. 	At Northern Regional College’s Newtownabbey campus, work is ongoing to upgrade the existing accessible lift, ensuring safe and inclusive access for all learners, and this is scheduled for completion by the end of this month. Southern Regional College has installed new sensory pods at its Newry and Portadown campuses. North West Regional College has also installed new sensory pods at its Limavady and Strand Road campuses, with all offering supportive environments for learners to regulate. 	At South Eastern Regional College’s Lisburn campus, design work to upgrade the main entrance to facilitate students with special educational needs is nearing completion, with delivery planned, subject to funding provision, for the 2026/27 financial year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any exceptions which may be considered for schools unable to meet the general principle on reasonable numbers when seeking to transform to integrated education status.
answer_text: The legal requirement for reasonable numbers is based in a requirement set out in the Education Reform (Northern Ireland) Order 1989 as amended by the Integrated Education Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 which do not provide for exceptions.The policy position on reasonable numbers was outlined in the Written Ministerial Statement issued in September 2025.To ensure the process of Transformation is both well-grounded at the outset andcapable of developing over time, the Department will expect, as a general principle, the proposer to provide evidence that the school is likely to be able to attract at least 10% of its total year 1 or year 8 from the minority religion, or in those schools seeking to Transform to Integrated status, 15% of the combined number of Protestant and Catholic year 1 or year 8 pupils, in the first year of Transformation.Evidence is also required that this is likely to increase over the next 7 years toachieve reasonable numbers from the minority religion and an aspiration for therelative proportions of the two groups to be similar.While a rigid approach should not be taken to ‘reasonable numbers,’ whatconstitutes reasonable numbers should be considered having regard to, the intention of the 1989 Order, the Northern Ireland CIE Statement of Principles, and the underlying rationale of contact theory that there should be a reasonable balance between Protestant and Catholic children. Any potential exemptions to this are not pre-determined and each case will be considered in its own unique circumstances, regardless of the percentage of Protestant and Catholics evidenced by the proposer.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether any of the funding arising from the Chancellor's Spring Statement will be allocated to fund pressures in special educational needs services.
answer_text: The Spring Forecast provided the Executive with Barnett Consequentials £387.2 million across the budget period 2026-27 to 2029/30.Barnett consequentials are not hypothecated and it is for the Executive to determine how to allocate this funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether any assessment has been made of the impact of ceasing departmental funding to the charity Tiny Life.
answer_text: I would refer the member to my previous answer to AQW 41254/22-27 published in the weekly answer booklet for week ending 27 February 2026. The position has not changed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the review of the accessible parking bay road markings outside Boots chemist on Regent Street, Newtownards.
answer_text: The Department is currently undertaking a review of the accessible parking bay road markings outside Boots chemist on Regent Street, Newtownards in line with relevant legislation. Once this review has been completed, a work order will be issued to refresh the road markings as part of the scheduled maintenance programme. Should any further adjustments be necessary, these will be implemented subject to the availability of resources, with the aim of ensuring the space remains compliant with accessibility and legislative requirements.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any engagement he has had with the Department of Education in relation to the draft We Matter Learning Disability Service Model For Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Officials in my department have engaged with a range of stakeholders during the development of the draft ‘We Matter’ Learning Disability Service Model (LDSM) and associated Service Delivery Plan (SDP), including engagement with the Department of Education (DE) relevant to the role of education services.In particular, work relating to transitions from children’s services and education settings into adult Learning Disability services has involved engagement with education stakeholders to help inform policy development. Effective transitions require engagement and co-ordination between health, education and other services to support young people with a Learning Disability as they move from school into adult life.The public consultation on the draft LDSM and SDP closed on 25 November 2025 and my officials are currently analysing the responses received. Engagement with other relevant Departments, including DE, will continue as part of the process to refine and finalise the Model and to support consideration of its future implementation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the costing exercise associated with the draft We Matter Learning Disability Service Model For Northern Ireland.
answer_text: My Department undertook an analysis of Trust reported expenditure across the Learning Disability Programme of Care to understand the existing cost base, as part of work to develop the draft ‘We Matter’ Learning Disability Service Model (LDSM) and associated Service Delivery Plan (SDP). The available data indicated that expenditure on Learning Disability services increased by approximately 36% between 2018/19 and 2022/23.My officials are currently analysing responses to the recent public consultation on the draft LDSM and associated SDP, which closed on 25 November 2025. An initial high level summary report was published on my Department’s website in January 2026, with a full analysis report expected to be published in Spring 2026.As the Model remains in draft form, a detailed costing of its implementation has not yet been undertaken. Financial implications will be considered once the LDSM and SDP have been finalised which will inform decisions on the future configuration of Learning Disability services, including any additional investment required to support delivery. In addition, I will also wish to consider the financial implications of any relevant recommendations that may arise from the report of the Muckamore Abbey Hospital Inquiry.Delivery of the ambitions set out in the Model will be subject to funding made available to my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any (i) support; and (ii) advice, provided to patients diagnosed with Lynch syndrome.
To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the implementation of the draft We Matter Learning Disability Service Model For Northern Ireland.
answer_text: My officials are currently analysing responses to the recent public consultation on the draft ‘We Matter’ Learning Disability Service Model (LDSM) and associated Service Delivery Plan (SDP), which closed on 25 November 2025. An initial high level summary report was published on my Department’s website in January 2026, with a full analysis report expected to be published in Spring 2026.The consultation findings will be used to inform my decisions on finalisation of the Service Model, and I will also wish to consider any relevant recommendations that may arise from the report of the Muckamore Abbey Hospital Inquiry.Once finalised, the Service Model and SDP will inform the future configuration of Learning Disability services, including any additional investment required to support delivery. Delivery of the ambitions set out in the Model will be subject to funding made available to my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to provide an update on the creation of an register for Lynch syndrome.
To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 39977/22-27, to detail when he anticipates the business case for Newtownards Library will be finalised.
answer_text: The Outline Business Case (OBC) for the redevelopment of Newtownards Library has been finalised and approved.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail when the draft statutory guidance on restrictive practice in schools will be put out to public consultation.
answer_text: The Department has launched a public consultation on the draft ‘Restrictive Interventions: Guidance for Educational Settings’. The consultation runs from 12 March to 4 June 2026.Further details of the consultation are available on the Department’s website Consultations | Department of Education (education-ni.gov.uk).

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission for an update on Mandate Three 2025-2027 of the Northern Ireland Youth Assembly.
answer_text: Mandate three of the Youth Assembly is underway following recruitment in September last year, which saw a thirty-five percent increase in applications this time around. A diverse group of ninety Youth Assembly Members were selected, ensuring there are voices from all constituencies, and a cohort which reflects the wider youth population in terms of Section 75. The Youth Assembly’s ‘Big Youth Survey,’ widely promoted in schools and on social media, received almost seventeen hundred responses. The survey results formed the basis for the Youth Assembly’s first plenary in January, when Youth Assembly Members chose their three Committees for this mandate. Following a vote, the committees were confirmed as Education, Health, and Rights & Equality. The Youth Assembly Committees will now meet with experts, conduct primary research, and present findings to Assembly Committees and Ministers later in the mandate.Youth Assembly Members have already given evidence to the Committee for Education on their proposed Committee Bill and met with the All-Party Group on Youth Participation on young people’s voices in government. Youth Assembly Members have been supported with initial training in public speaking, political knowledge, and children’s rights to help them carry out their role. They have also considered and adopted a Code of Conduct and further support is scheduled throughout the mandate.They have already attended several conferences and events including the recent Women’s Caucus conference, the Northern Ireland Human Rights Commission Annual Human Rights Statement launch, and the Diverse Youth Conference. They have also met with officials from the Department ofEducation to give views on the ‘Attendance Matters Strategy’ and the curriculum review. The energy and commitment shown by Youth Assembly Members in these early months signals a strong and ambitious start to what promises to be another highly productive mandate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he intends to extend the funding provided to the Tiny Life charity, beyond March 2026.
answer_text: The Budgets for 2026-27 or subsequent periods have not yet been formally set by the Executive. However, the Finance Minister has published his proposals in respect of a draft budget for 2026-2029/30 and a consultation exercise is currently underway.  Current indications are that the Department will have a resource funding gap of c£0.7bn increasing to over £1 billion in 2028-29.I am therefore not presently in a position to provide any commitments to funding.  Any decision regarding future funding will be considered in the context of my Department’s 2026-27 budget allocation and wider financial pressures.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the roll-out of Solution Focused Hubs as part of Local Impact Team service to schools.
answer_text: I refer the member to AQW 40317/22-27 published in the weekly answer booklet for week ending 20th February 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail when the task and finish group on restrictive practice last met.
answer_text: The Department’s Restraint and Seclusion Task and Finish Group last met on 10 April 2025.A multi-agency workshop, including representatives from the Task and Finish Group and colleagues from both health and education, was convened by the Department on 16 June 2025 to collaboratively address key issues identified in relation to the draft guidance, arising at the intersection of health and education within school settings.Following this workshop, revised draft guidance was formally circulated to the Task and Finish Group on 7 July 2025 for comment, with an initial response date of 8 August, subsequently extended to 13 August 2025. Following the receipt of feedback, officials further revised the document and submitted it to the Departmental Solicitor’s Office (DSO) on 21 October 2025. A response was received from DSO on 8 December 2025.The Department updated the guidance in line with DSO’s advice and returned it for final review on 22 January 2026. The Department is currently awaiting a response from DSO.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the current number of vacancies in the Careers Service.
answer_text: My Department’s Careers Service currently has 35.64 Full Time Equivalent (FTE) vacancies against an approved staffing level of 159.87 FTE posts. The current staffing complement includes 78 professionally qualified Careers Advisers (69.20 FTE) against an approved staffing level of 92.33 FTE. Other vacancies relate to various operational, administrative and policy roles within the Careers Service. Staff are based in 25 different locations throughout the North. The Careers Action Plan 2025-2028 sets out a programme of actions to build capacity. It also highlights the cornerstone role of professionally qualified Careers Advisers. To be appointed as a Careers Adviser within the Department, candidates must hold a recognised Level 6 or above qualification in Careers Guidance. However, recruiting and retaining suitably qualified Careers Advisers has become increasingly challenging, and therefore developing longterm, sustainable recruitment and workforce planning solutions is a key priority for the Careers Service.Following an external recruitment campaign in 2025, six new Careers Advisers have recently taken up post within the Service. In addition, the apprenticeship-style Trainee Careers Adviser recruitment programme, established in April 2024, to support the longer-term supply of qualified Careers Advisers, will see all Trainees complete the Level 6 qualification in Career Guidance and Development and transition to substantive Careers Adviser roles in April 2026. A second, larger cohort of Trainee Advisers commenced the Level 6 programme in January 2026. Taken together, external recruitment and the apprenticeship-style model, means there is now a sustainable supply of professionally qualified Careers Advisers which play a key role in providing the support people need to access opportunities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the review of the Children Looked After Advisory Service.
answer_text: A review of the Education Authority Primary Children Looked After Advisory Service is currently being carried out by the Innovation & Consultancy Services and it is expected that a report will be completed by the end of March 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of patients in the Strangford constituency registered as a health service dental patient, in the last five years.

To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 39020/22-27, whether the Education Authority employ their own in-house legal services for representation at Special Educational Needs and Disability Tribunals.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that it does have an inhouse legal function, EA Solicitors, which represents the Authority in certain matters before the Special Educational Needs and Disability Tribunal (SENDIST).However, appeals relating to an EA decision not to carry out a Statutory Assessment are managed separately by a dedicated SENDIST and Correspondence Team within the EA’s Children and Young People’s Services Directorate. This work is not undertaken by the EA’s inhouse legal team.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 39020/22-27, to detail the number of appeals heard by the Special Educational Needs and Disability Tribunal, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The Special Educational Needs and Disability Tribunal (SENDIST) has provided the following information on the number of appeal hearings held for each of the last five financial years:2021/222022/232023/242024/252025/26(01/04/25- 25/02/26)3834245446

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 39020/22-27, to detail any other expenditure associated with defending appeals heard by the Special Educational Needs and Disability Tribunal.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that there is no other expenditure associated with the Special Educational Needs and Disability Tribunal (SENDIST), as no fees are payable to SENDIST by the EA, and the Tribunal does not award costs to successful appellants.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 38042/22-27, AQW 30309/22-27 and AQW 27512/22-27, to detail the reason why the rollout of a new system for delivering low value maintenance works in schools has moved from a system-wide rollout, to a pilot scheme with no assurance of adoption for all schools.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that the Low Value Maintenance Scheme is initially being piloted due to the complexity of the scheme and concerns about the impact on school leader workload. Work is already well underway within the EA to implement the pilot programme, with the objective of having all necessary arrangements, including compliance processes, in place before the end of the current school academic year. This pilot will help ascertain the risk and value for money for school leaders and to manage any negative impact on workload for school leaders. The introduction of the new Term Service Contracts will enable the EA’s maintenance service to deliver a more performancefocused and proactive approach to school maintenance. This enhanced model is expected to address and help reduce many of the concerns previously raised by school leaders.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the rollout of Solution Focused Hubs, as part of Pathway One provision.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised:Solution Focused Hubs are currently offered to schools as part of the Local IMPACT Team programme of offer at Pathway 1.  Early Years settings can also access a Hub through the Advice and Guidance lines (pre Request for Involvement) or following a setting meeting. From early March 2026, Hubs will also be offered to schools for children and young people with more than one area of special educational need who have accessed Pathways 2 or 3 support, and who would benefit from a multi-specialist approach.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether the new Religious Education (RE) syllabus will provide guidance, to ensure that when schools bring in third parties to deliver RE and collective worship, that they do so in a way that is objective, critical and pluralistic.
answer_text: A syllabus is a document that outlines what will be taught in a specific subject or course. The new Religious Education (RE) Core Syllabus will set out the key learning objectives by Key Stage, as defined in the Review’s Terms of Reference. It will not provide guidance on how schools should engage with external groups, which is an operational matter for schools across all aspects of the Curriculum.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the anticipated timescales for changes to the inspection of Religious Education.
answer_text: I will shortly bring forward a paper to the Executive outlining my proposals in relation to the inspection of Religious Education.  Subject to Executive agreement, a bill would be prepared for introduction before summer recess.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail when minor works budgets for 2026-2027 will be finalised.
answer_text: Minor works budgets for 2026-27 will be finalised upon the agreement of departmental budget allocations by the Northern Ireland Executive. The draft Budget 2026-2029/30 has been published for public consultation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the comprehensive transformation in services for school leavers with special educational needs.
answer_text: Since my statement to the Assembly in June, outlining my proposals to enhance provision and support for school leavers with Special Educational Needs (SEN), we have been making progress across all the work strands. My officials have led the development of a cross-departmental bid for funding via the Transformation Fund. This collaborative bid is specifically focused on improving pathways and transitions for young people as they move on from school into adulthood.  The bid has been recommended for funding by the Transformation Board.  My officials have begun preparatory work to ensure activity can begin as soon as funding is confirmed as it will enable us to progress vital reforms to support every young person’s potential.The proposal will enhance transition support, develop and expand suitable post-school pathways such as SEN and Supported apprenticeships and evaluation to support the wider application of outcomes and learning across mainstream provision.  Since September, my officials have been working with the Education and Training Inspectorate (ETI) on an evaluative review of the SEN related provision across the Further Education (FE) Colleges. The review will conclude later this month, with a report to follow. It will help shape and inform the good practice we want to build on, as part of the proposed statutory Assessment and Support model, as well as identifying the issues to target for improvement. There have been physical enhancements on some the FE sites, while FE staff have also been participating in bespoke accredited and non-accredited training developed by Ulster University. My officials are also progressing much needed cross-departmental work on data sharing related issues and a working group has been established to scope the current data landscape.         Following my Statement, I also wrote to my Executive colleagues, to drive crossgovernment action to tackle significant system gaps for young people – including in relation to the lack of Specialist Provision in schools for 16-to-18 year olds, as well as highlighting with the Department of Health the difficulties created by the current Learning Disability eligibility thresholds.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail his rationale for not progressing fundamental changes to collective worship, given that the Supreme Court judgment in the matter of an application by JR87 and another for Judicial Review (Appellant) stated that both the teaching of Religious Education and the arrangements for Collective Worship was in breach of both appellants’ rights.
answer_text: The Supreme Court judgment, in the matter of ‘JR 87 and another’, explicitly states that “there is no challenge in this case to collective worship in schools in Northern Ireland being focused on the Christian religion or that in Catholic maintained schools the focus of collective worship may be distinctive of the Catholic denomination”. The Supreme Court did not strike down or question the legality of collective worship, and the duty placed on schools remains firmly in place.While the Court found that the arrangements for collective worship in JR 87’s school breached rights, this was because how it was delivered in that school. The finding related to ineffective withdrawal arrangements and the risk of stigmatisation not because Christian collective worship itself was unlawful. My focus, therefore, has been to ensure that every school now implements a simple, immediate, and confidential withdrawal process. I issued new guidance confirming that an unqualified right exists for parents to withdraw their children wholly or partially from RE and/or collective worship.By strengthening the right of withdrawal and ensuring consistent implementation across all schools, I have taken the precise and proportionate action needed to meet the Court’s requirements while maintaining the statutory framework for collective worship in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any steps he will take to ensure that other world religions, beliefs and worldviews will inform the new Religious Education curriculum.
answer_text: The approach to drafting the revised RE Core Syllabus and the wider engagement process is a matter for the drafting group, led by Professor Noel Purdy.  The Religious Education Advisory Committee has no formal role in the review process, though the drafting group may wish to engage with members of the Committee and consider previous work undertaken by the Committee and others.  The Terms of Reference for the work make clear that the revised Core Syllabus should be pluralist and inclusive, specifically ensuring pupils explore a range of religious and non-religious worldviews, and encourage engagement with questions of belief, ethics, identity and meaning, reflecting the diversity of contemporary society.  The Terms of Reference also set out the need for wider engagement and consultation as part of the drafting process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any steps he will take to ensure that the new Religious Education syllabus does not present Christianity, or any other faith or world view, as objective truth, but rather a world view open to debate and critique.
answer_text: The Supreme Court judgment made clear that the legislation itself does not prevent Religious Education (RE) being taught in an objective, critical and pluralistic manner, because schools are permitted, and now encouraged, to add broader material beyond the Core Syllabus.  The Court was, therefore, satisfied that the legislation did not compel a breach and indeed was explicit that ‘The School in teaching religious education was required to include the core syllabus, as specified by the Department, but it could have taught additional matters so that overall, the education could have been conveyed in an objective, critical, and pluralistic manner. Accordingly, it was possible for a school lawfully to teach religious education and to provide collective worship, and the legislation did not require the School to breach A2P1 or article 9 ECHR’The Terms of Reference for the Review explicitly required the revised syllabus to ensure that RE in Northern Ireland is taught as an academic discipline, developing analytical and evaluative skills, and ensuring that pupils explore a range of religious and non-religious worldviews, and encourages engagement with questions of belief, ethics, identity and meaning, reflecting the diversity of contemporary society.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the rational as to why schools are being advised to include additional objective, critical and pluralistic material in the teaching of Religious Education, to ensure that there is no breach of European Convention rights, as opposed to advising that all Religious Education should be taught in a way which is objective, critical and pluralistic in all aspects of delivery.
answer_text: The Supreme Court judgment made clear that the legislation itself does not prevent Religious Education (RE) being taught in an objective, critical and pluralistic manner, because schools are permitted, and now encouraged, to add broader material beyond the Core Syllabus.  The Court was, therefore, satisfied that the legislation did not compel a breach and indeed was explicit that ‘The School in teaching religious education was required to include the core syllabus, as specified by the Department, but it could have taught additional matters so that overall, the education could have been conveyed in an objective, critical, and pluralistic manner. Accordingly, it was possible for a school lawfully to teach religious education and to provide collective worship, and the legislation did not require the School to breach A2P1 or article 9 ECHR’The Terms of Reference for the Review explicitly required the revised syllabus to ensure that RE in Northern Ireland is taught as an academic discipline, developing analytical and evaluative skills, and ensuring that pupils explore a range of religious and non-religious worldviews, and encourages engagement with questions of belief, ethics, identity and meaning, reflecting the diversity of contemporary society.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether the right of withdrawal for children from Religious Education will include partial withdrawal for certain topics or lessons within Religious Education.
answer_text: The right of withdrawal from Religious Education (RE) does include partial withdrawal from specific topics or individual lessons.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any rationale for not removing the legislative requirement for Religious Education to be taught according to the Holy Scriptures, in light of the Supreme Court judgment in the matter of an application by JR87 and another for Judicial Review (Appellant).
answer_text: The Supreme Court judgment made clear that the legislation itself does not prevent Religious Education (RE) being taught in an objective, critical and pluralistic manner, because schools are permitted, and now encouraged, to add broader material beyond the Core Syllabus.  The Court was, therefore, satisfied that the legislation did not compel a breach and indeed was explicit that ‘The School in teaching religious education was required to include the core syllabus, as specified by the Department, but it could have taught additional matters so that overall, the education could have been conveyed in an objective, critical, and pluralistic manner. Accordingly, it was possible for a school lawfully to teach religious education and to provide collective worship, and the legislation did not require the School to breach A2P1 or article 9 ECHR’The Terms of Reference for the Review explicitly required the revised syllabus to ensure that RE in Northern Ireland is taught as an academic discipline, developing analytical and evaluative skills, and ensuring that pupils explore a range of religious and non-religious worldviews, and encourages engagement with questions of belief, ethics, identity and meaning, reflecting the diversity of contemporary society.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any steps he will take to establish who should have a role in the drafting group for the new Religious Education curriculum.
answer_text: I have put in place a comprehensive and transparent process for revising the Religious Education (RE) Core Syllabus. My Department has launched an open expression-of-interest process, inviting applications from experienced practitioners across all schools in Northern Ireland. This open process is designed to attract a range of applicants from across school sectors, who can provide their subject knowledge and classroom experience to the drafting process. Applications will be assessed against the agreed essential criteria. Alongside this, there will be extensive wider engagement. The specific approach is a matter for the Review Chair, Professor Noel Purdy OBE, but engagement  will include an open call for evidence, public surveys, and focus group discussions involving children, young people, parents, and a wide range of other stakeholders. The process will culminate in a full public consultation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any role the (i) Religious Education Advisory Committee; and ii) previous work undertaken by the Religious Education Advisory Committee, will play in the drafting of a new Religious Education curriculum.
answer_text: The approach to drafting the revised RE Core Syllabus and the wider engagement process is a matter for the drafting group, led by Professor Noel Purdy.  The Religious Education Advisory Committee has no formal role in the review process, though the drafting group may wish to engage with members of the Committee and consider previous work undertaken by the Committee and others. The Terms of Reference also set out the need for wider engagement and consultation as part of the drafting process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any steps he will take to ensure that a wide range of (i) expertise; (ii) opinion, and (iii) cultural backgrounds, is brought into the new Religious Education curriculum drafting group.

The process will culminate in a full public consultation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of the extent to which Religious Education can be taught in a manner that is objective, critical and pluralistic, whilst the requirement that it be taught according to the Holy Scriptures remains in legislation.
answer_text: The Supreme Court judgment made clear that the legislation itself does not prevent Religious Education (RE) being taught in an objective, critical and pluralistic manner, because schools are permitted, and now encouraged, to add broader material beyond the Core Syllabus. question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 38799/22-27, whether (i) any of the £200,000 capital funding allocated to Newtownards Library will be spent on that project, in this financial year; and (ii) he will allocate capital budget to this project in 2025-2026.
answer_text: (i)  While capital funding was set aside to start the Newtownards Library project in the current financial year, spend is subject to business case approval. The business case process has not yet been finalised.(ii) The draft Capital DEL Budget 2026-30 has not yet been approved. Capital requirements are currently being considered and will be confirmed following agreement of the budget.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any plans her Department has to address flooding from the Enler River, Comber.
answer_text: My Department has no plans for upgrades to the river drainage infrastructure in Comber. Comber is not identified in either the current or previous Flood Risk Management Plan (FRMP) as an APSFR (Area of Potential Significant Flood Risk) nor has it been identified by our local teams as an area with a history of significant flooding therefore it is not on our current programme. However, during the summer months of 2025, significant maintenance was undertaken on the section of the Comber (Enler) River extending from Enler Gardens to the Killinchy Road Bridge. We will continue to inspect and maintain designated watercourses and their associated infrastructure within the Comber catchment, in line with our maintenance standards. If an inspection determines that there is a blockage significantly impeding the free flow of water within a watercourse or that drainage infrastructure is in an unsatisfactory condition, then works will be programmed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 33788/22-27, to detail (i) the number of the 11 seven-seater vehicles purchased by the Education Authority that are now in use to transport children; and (ii) the number of current vacancies for drivers of these vehicles.
answer_text: The Education Authority has advised that (i) five seven-seater school transport vehicles are currently in operation and (ii) six applicants for the role of driver are in the final stages of the recruitment process and are completing their preemployment checks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline any consideration he has given to implementing lifelong care and support plans for disabled people.

To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of houses purchased by the Housing Executive for use as temporary accommodation, since permission to use reserves for this purpose was granted in 2025.
answer_text: None.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 37562/22-27, to detail any outstanding legal issues that prevent the site from being placed on the open market for sale.
answer_text: The EA is proceeding to place this property on the open market, despite certain burdens remaining on the title.The site comprises two plots. The front plot, which borders the Portaferry Road, previously accommodated the former Castle Gardens School (now demolished), and is subject to an Educational Trust. The trust conditions primarily restrict the use of this portion of the land to a public elementary school, or other education related purposes.Although it has been established that the freehold title is held by the Post Office for the second plot, they have not confirmed their interest despite repeated attempts to engage with them.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 37562/22-27, to detail how long the Education Authority have been working to progress the disposal of the former site of Castle Gardens Primary School.
answer_text: The Education Authority has been actively working on the disposal of this site from November 2021.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 37562/22-27, to detail when it is anticipated that the site will be on the open market for sale.
answer_text: The EA appointed estate agent is currently undertaking the preparatory work required to bring the site to market. It is expected that the property will be advertised for sale on the open market within the next 3–4 weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail how he is working with the Minister for the Economy to maximise the capacity of further education colleges, to help deliver the Entitlement Framework, and Essential Skills, for young people.
answer_text: As you are aware, the Department of Education and the Department for the Economy (DfE) jointly published a 14-19 Education and Training Framework which sets out a shared vision and actions to improve the outcomes for young people. To deliver on this commitment there is ongoing collaboration between my officials and officials in DfE. Additionally, they meet formally three times a year through thematic groups relating to careers, post-16 and curriculum and qualifications. The aim of these groups is to drive delivery of actions relevant to the Framework, promote policy awareness and alignment and collaborate on cross-cutting activities. To ensure that there is a strategic platform to advance shared priorities, collaboration and improve outcomes for young people, I have established a cross-departmental ministerial group with the Minister for the Economy which meets twice a year. The first meeting of the Group took place on 24 November 2025. The next cross-departmental ministerial group meeting is scheduled for June 2026. There is a wide range of general and applied qualifications available to all learners at Key Stage 4 and Post-16.  To support delivery of the Entitlement Framework I have set aside funding of £2.9m in 2025/26. This funding helps to support the cost of collaboration and the operation of Area Learning Communities which bring together schools, Further Education Colleges and Training Providers to discuss curriculum provision in a local area.To further support all learners to achieve and progress in their chosen pathways, schools have partnership agreements with the Careers Service. This ensures that impartial, professional, accurate and up-to-date   advice is offered to learners at Key Stage 4 and post-16 to help them make informed choices with regard to qualifications and pathways.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail how the Education Sustainability Taskforce will support wider transformation of the education system.
answer_text: For some time Education has relied on substantial in-year allocations to meet the significant shortfalls in its opening budgets. Whilst the EA has been able to operate within budget in the last two financial years, in the main, this has only been possible because of significant additional, in-year funding totalling c£296.8m in 2023-24 and c£247.7m in 2024-25.  2025-26 is the first financial year in which in-year budget allocations will fail to meet the sector’s financial pressures.Within this context, the overarching objective of the Taskforce is to support the achievement of financial sustainability within the EA, while at the same time supporting improvements to services. Any savings identified by the Taskforce will form part of my wider reform agenda (i.e. Five-Year Strategy for Financial Sustainability in Education) which will seek to establish a structural reform programme that will oversee the delivery of education reform at scale, including, but not limited to, areas such as an alternative SEN support model for statemented pupils in mainstream schools, home-to-school transport, area planning and enhanced control of schools’ expenditure. I must, however, stress that even after all necessary transformational change has been implemented and after every possible efficiency has been realised, education will still require greater investment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether the Education Sustainability Taskforce will undertake an audit of the cost of division in education.
answer_text: I refer the member to my answer to AQW 38801/22-27 that sets out the scope of the work of the Taskforce for Future Financial Stability of Education.There are no plans to undertake an audit of the cost of division in education at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether the Education Sustainability Taskforce will be exploring any recommendations from the Independent Review of Education regarding area planning.
answer_text: The overarching objective of the Taskforce for Future Financial Stability of Education (the Taskforce) is to achieve financial sustainability for the EA and thereby the education sector as a whole, while supporting improvements to services. It will not directly lead on recommendations from the Independent Review of Education regarding area planning, including the recommendation 20 to right-size the schools estate.The Independent Review of Education highlighted a need to develop a new approach to Area Planning.  I agree that revised arrangements are needed to reconfigure the current network of schools to create thriving, sustainable schools that support higher standards of education and promote inclusion for all children and young people. Depending on local circumstances, the way forward in local communities may include rationalisation, amalgamation, right-sizing of schools or innovative solutions to ensure continuity of provision.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether the Education Sustainability Taskforce will undertake a review of home to school transport.
answer_text: The Taskforce for Future Financial Stability of Education (the Taskforce) aims to achieve financial sustainability for the Education Authority (EA) and thereby the education sector as a whole, while supporting improvements to services, such as home to school transport. I remain committed to ensuring that all pupils receive the transport assistance they need to access education. To protect this essential provision, we must ensure that the transport service is both affordable and sustainable. Transport provision is a statutory requirement rooted in Department of Education policy. The EA is currently reviewing its transport service to identify potential efficiencies, savings and alternative delivery options. The Taskforce will take a holistic overview, strengthening joint working between my Department and the EA to identify where policy or legislative change may be required to deliver improvements and support a more sustainable system.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether a budget has been allocated to the Education Sustainability Taskforce.
answer_text: I can confirm that no budget has been allocated to the Education Sustainability Taskforce.  The taskforce is comprised of Departmental Policy leads, senior EA officials and Department of Finance officials who will undertake their responsibilities on the taskforce as part of their existing duties.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 37656/22-27, whether the capital funding for Newtownards Library will be returned to the Department of Finance if it is not utilised by the end of this financial year.
answer_text: My Department’s Capital Budget is closely managed throughout the year to ensure that any Non-Ringfenced Capital easements which emerge in-year are utilised to meet other capital pressures within the Department. This oversight ensures that the department can maximise the delivery of services to the community and return a balanced Capital Budget.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the scope of the Education Sustainability Taskforce.
answer_text: The Taskforce for Future Financial Stability of Education (the Taskforce) comprises of the Department’s Permanent Secretary, Deputy Secretaries and Finance Director; the Education Authority’s (EA) Chief Executive and Chief Officers; and Department of Finance Education Supply officials.The overarching objective of the Taskforce is to achieve financial sustainability for the EA and thereby the education sector as a whole, while supporting improvements to services.  This will include, though not necessarily be limited, to:assess the comprehensiveness of the EA’s Category 2 savings measures approved by the EA Board and submitted to DE and determine whether any additional savings proposals should be incorporated into this savings category;develop a workplan to advance work on these items – including identifying areas where policy review or legislative change is necessary;review the financial implications of any parallel reform or transformation work taking place in the EA (in particular, the Special Educational Needs Transformation agenda);subject to Ministerial approval, take forward recommendations outlined in the Independent Review of Baseline Schools Funding Report; and  review in detail all EA budget lines and the services currently offered:develop an understanding of why the underlying services are needed and method of delivery;conduct benchmarking exercises to assess EA services with other organisations, sectors or jurisdictions;identify options for alternative delivery models; andevaluate these options and make recommendations.andongoing review of existing savings and efficiency measures.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether the Education Sustainability Taskforce will explore right-sizing of the school estate.
answer_text: The overarching objective of the Taskforce for Future Financial Stability of Education (the Taskforce) is to achieve financial sustainability for the EA and thereby the education sector as a whole, while supporting improvements to services. It will not directly lead on recommendations from the Independent Review of Education regarding area planning, including the recommendation 20 to right-size the schools estate.The Independent Review of Education highlighted a need to develop a new approach to Area Planning.  I agree that revised arrangements are needed to reconfigure the current network of schools to create thriving, sustainable schools that support higher standards of education and promote inclusion for all children and young people. Depending on local circumstances, the way forward in local communities may include rationalisation, amalgamation, right-sizing of schools or innovative solutions to ensure continuity of provision.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she will undertake further assessments to determine whether a puffin crossing can be installed on the Killyleagh Road, Darraghcross.
answer_text: I refer the Member to my answer to AQW 38696 / 22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail who sits on the Education Sustainability Taskforce.
answer_text: The Taskforce for Future Financial Stability of Education (the Taskforce) comprises of the Department’s Permanent Secretary, Deputy Secretaries and Finance Director; the Education Authority’s (EA) Chief Executive and Chief Officers; and Department of Finance Education Supply officials.The overarching objective of the Taskforce is to achieve financial sustainability for the EA and thereby the education sector as a whole, while supporting improvements to services.  This will include, though not necessarily be limited, to:assess the comprehensiveness of the EA’s Category 2 savings measures approved by the EA Board and submitted to DE and determine whether any additional savings proposals should be incorporated into this savings category;develop a workplan to advance work on these items – including identifying areas where policy review or legislative change is necessary;review the financial implications of any parallel reform or transformation work taking place in the EA (in particular, the Special Educational Needs Transformation agenda);subject to Ministerial approval, take forward recommendations outlined in the Independent Review of Baseline Schools Funding Report; and  review in detail all EA budget lines and the services currently offered:develop an understanding of why the underlying services are needed and method of delivery;conduct benchmarking exercises to assess EA services with other organisations, sectors or jurisdictions;identify options for alternative delivery models; andevaluate these options and make recommendations.andongoing review of existing savings and efficiency measures.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of the current speed limit on the Killyleagh Road, Darraghcross.
answer_text: Road safety is a high priority for my Department, and we are committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and to address the needs of all road users.When considering the appropriate speed limit for a road, the Department’s policy is used to ensure speed limits are consistent and motorists are presented with the same speed limit on similar roads. This takes into account factors such as the characteristic of the road, the speed of existing traffic, the degree of frontage development, and road traffic collision data which is monitored using data provided by the PSNI. The speed limit on the Killyleagh Road, Darraghcross is considered to be appropriate.However, research shows that there is a correlation between speed and the frequency and severity of road collisions and as part of my broader strategy to improve road safety, I have recently launched the public consultation on speed limits.The consultation will seek views on the wider use of permanent 20mph speed limits, the lowering of national speed limit on rural single and dual carriageways, and alignment of HGV speed limits with other regions.I would encourage you to contribute to this significant and important piece of work. Details can be found at Speed limit review launched by DfI | Department for Infrastructure.Concerns about driver behaviour, such as speeding, should be directed to the PSNI on their 101 number.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of the current street lighting provision at the bus stops on both sides of the A24, at the junction to the Drumaness Road.

To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 37655/22-27, to detail (i) where this funding allocation has been drawn down from; (ii) the number of placements this funding is intended to provide; and (iii) whether the full allocation of £311,865.42 will be spent in the 2025-26 academic year.
answer_text: The Education Authority has advised that:The funding has been drawn from the core budget.The total number of places was 40.Yes, the budget was fully spent.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of the adequacy of current pedestrian crossing facilities on the Killyleagh Road, Darraghcross.
answer_text: As the last assessment at this location was undertaken in 2019, I have asked officials to undertake a revised assessment. This will be added to the schedule of Pedestrian Crossing Surveys to be completed. The Department have provided crossing points in the form of dropped kerbs with tactile paving on the Killyleagh Road which provides a safe crossing point for pedestrians.Any concerns about driver behaviour should be directed to the PSNI on their 101 number.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she will update her Department's street lighting policy to account for the road safety of bus passengers who need to cross unlit roads on a daily basis, particularly during the winter months.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail when legislation will be in place to ensure that the double yellow lines, in place at the uncontrolled crossing point on Killinchy Road, Comber, are enforceable.
answer_text: Legislation is currently being processed to cover the existing double yellow markings.  A Traffic Regulation Order submission has been completed and introduction is likely to be within 6-9 months.However, concerns about driver behaviour such as parking which causes an obstruction, to other road and footway users, should be directed to the PSNI on their non-emergency 101 number.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the review of suspensions and expulsions.
answer_text: I am currently considering the review of the current suspension and expulsion arrangements, including recommendations for change. Once my consideration is complete, I will determine the appropriate next steps.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 30309/22-27, whether a new system for delivering low value maintenance works in schools will be in place by end of January 2026.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) continues to work on the development of a pilot scheme to facilitate the procurement of low value maintenance works. This approach seeks to balance local flexibility for schools with the need for strong governance, compliance and value for money across the school estate. The development work includes consideration of tax regulations, including the Construction Industry Scheme (CIS). It is now anticipated that this pilot will be operational towards the end of the current school year. The pilot will need to be fully evaluated before any decisions are taken on a wider rollout across the EA school estate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the development of an Initial Teacher Education programme, focused on special educational needs.
To ask the Minister of Education whether he intends to incentivise completion of the questionnaire used by schools for monitoring the community background of staff.
answer_text: I am satisfied that the Fair Employment (School Teachers) Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 has been successfully implemented.To ensure the smooth implementation of the Act a working group was established with management representatives from all of the education sectors, who worked in partnership with the Equality Commission Northern Ireland. The Equality Commission delivered a series of training webinars specifically on the recruitment and selection guidance. Guidance for schools was published by the Equality Commission with the support of Management Side of the Teachers’ Negotiating Committee. The Guidance, ‘School Teachers A Guide on Recruitment & Selection after the commencement of the Fair Employment (School Teachers) Act (Northern Ireland) 2022’ is available online. My Department does not collect or hold data in relation to the Fair Employment (School Teachers) Act (Northern Ireland) 2022. Education Authority (EA) and Council for Catholic Maintained Schools (CCMS) are the employers of teachers and carry out the monitoring for their teachers and submit the returns directly to the Equality Commission Northern Ireland.There is no proposal to incentivise the completion of the questionnaire used by schools for monitoring the community background of staff.The EA, CCMS and the teaching trade unions have encouraged all teachers to use the online computerised system to input and update their personal equality data profile.It is now a compulsory requirement for teaching applicants to complete the monitoring form when applying for a post and this is used to populate the equality data profile for teachers. By utilising this data substantial progress has been made in the capture of teacher records for monitoring purposes.

answer_text: I am satisfied that the Fair Employment (School Teachers) Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 has been successfully implemented.To ensure the smooth implementation of the Act a working group was established with management representatives from all of the education sectors, who worked in partnership with the Equality Commission Northern Ireland. question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 37564/22-27, to detail when the review into the funding discrepancy for nursery units facilitating specialist provisions will be concluded.
answer_text: My officials are exploring the discrepancy and I expect conclusion to be known in the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of the implementation of the Fair Employment (School Teachers) Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.
answer_text: I am satisfied that the Fair Employment (School Teachers) Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 has been successfully implemented.To ensure the smooth implementation of the Act a working group was established with management representatives from all of the education sectors, who worked in partnership with the Equality Commission Northern Ireland. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will implement the Royal College of Emergency Medicine's recommendations in relation to overcrowding and corridor care in Emergency Departments.
answer_text: I recognise the significant challenges faced by Emergency Departments (EDs) and I am acutely aware that the current level of use of non-designated clinical spaces to provide care is too high and must be reduced. While I accept it is reflective of the current pressures on hospitals and the wider health system, the impact on those providing and receiving care in that environment is not acceptable as routine, nor is it sustainable.I thank the Royal College of Emergency Medicine (RCEM) for producing their report. It is an important addition to understanding what needs to be done to make the sustainable improvements to current patient outcomes and experiences of EDs that are required. I am actively engaging with representatives of RCEM on the report findings and will continue to do so.I note that the RCEM report raises familiar issues, around hospital and whole-system flow, and I want to provide reassurance that work is already ongoing that will help address them, subject to the resources available to me. For example, by diverting patients to more appropriate pathways, increased focus on self-care, utilisation of Pharmacy support and GP services. All of this will provide patients with timely alternatives to ED attendance, ensuring they are seen and treated in the most appropriate place for their needs.I am acutely aware that the ever-increasing pressure on our hospital services, including admission to a ward, is being reflected in the overcrowding and corridor care in EDs.  I note this is being exacerbated by the delayed discharges of patients who are medically fit to leave hospital, but are waiting on care packages, including domiciliary care, and residential and nursing home care. My Department continues to work with Trusts and community services to support timely discharge through access to support where patient needs cannot otherwise be safely met at home. Ultimately there is no one system lever that of itself can address the overcrowding and corridor care in EDs. The role of the public is critical and recognises the shared responsibility for ensuring ED services are available for those who need them most.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether the review of the Chronically Sick and Disabled Persons (Northern Ireland) Act 1978 will involve engagement with families and young people directly affected by the current legislation.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail who is conducting the review of the Chronically Sick and Disabled Persons (Northern Ireland) Act 1978.
To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the Royal College of Emergency Medicine's December 2025 report Overcrowding and Corridor Care in Northern Ireland Emergency Departments.
answer_text: My Department recognises the challenges faced by emergency departments (EDs) and is working closely with Health and Social Care Trusts and community services to implement a range of actions aimed at improving patient flow and reducing delays.I thank the Royal College of Emergency Medicine (RCEM) for producing this report, which is a fundamental addition to understanding what needs to be done to make the improvements to patient outcomes and experience that are needed. I am actively engaging with RCEM on the findings and will continue to do so.I note that the RCEM report raises familiar issues, for example, around workforce, hospital and whole-system flow, and I want to provide reassurance that work is already ongoing that will help address them, subject to the resources available to me. For example, to help alleviate pressures in EDs by diverting patients to more appropriate pathways, we have expanded urgent care centres, enhanced Phone First services, and increased access to minor injury units. There is also an increased focus on self-care, utilisation of Pharmacy support and GP services. To further support the use of alternative pathways to ED, £1.3 million has been allocated to NIAS for the development of an Integrated Care Hub (ICH), to facilitate clinically appropriate ‘Hear and Treat’ and ‘See and Treat’ pathways. These services provide patients with timely alternatives to ED attendance, ensuring they are seen and treated in the most appropriate place for their needs.I am acutely aware that the ever-increasing pressure on our hospital services, including admission to a ward, is being reflected in the overcrowding and corridor care in EDs. This is exacerbated by the delayed discharges of patients who are medically fit to leave hospital, but are waiting on care packages, including domiciliary care, and residential and nursing home care. My Department continues to work with Trusts and community services to support timely discharge through access to support where patient needs cannot otherwise be safely met at home. Working in partnership with the RCEM and Trusts, my Department has also strengthened consultant capacity in EDs across Northern Ireland. Eighteen additional emergency medicine consultants have already taken up posts, with a further five due to start in the coming months.
