This additional expertise will improve senior decision-making during evenings and weekends, when consultant cover has traditionally been limited.Ultimately, the measures being taken are designed to improve patient flow throughout hospitals, reduce congestion in EDs and enhance waiting times, therefore supporting patients to receive timely, safe, and effective care both within EDs and across the wider health and social care system.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether legislative proposals will be required to support the service changes outlined in the Learning Disability Service Model Delivery Plan.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail how findings from the review of the Chronically Sick and Disabled Persons (Northern Ireland) Act 1978 will feed into the implementation of the Learning Disability Service Model.

To ask the Minister of Health whether the review of the Chronically Sick and Disabled Persons (Northern Ireland) Act 1978 will include (i) post-19 education; (ii) day opportunities; (iii) health and social care transitions; (iv) supported living pathways; and (v) continuity of therapeutic supports.
To ask the Minister of Health whether he will publish the terms of reference of the review of the Chronically Sick and Disabled Persons (Northern Ireland) Act 1978.
To ask the Minister of Health whether any interim reports will be published during the review of the Chronically Sick and Disabled Persons (Northern Ireland) Act 1978.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail the anticipated timescales of the review of the Chronically Sick and Disabled Persons (Northern Ireland) Act 1978.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail the scope of the planned review of the Chronically Sick and Disabled Persons (Northern Ireland) Act 1978.
answer_text: I am committed to reviewing the Chronically Sick & Disabled Persons (NI) Act 1978 and any related legislation during the first year of implementing the three-year Service Delivery Plan (SDP) for the Learning Disability Service Model (LDSM), to determine whether there is a need for legislative change, which better reflects changing demographics and modern models of support for people with lifelong complex needs. However, while I am fully supportive of the case for exploring legislative change, I have previously indicated the need to be mindful of the practical challenges of progressing new primary legislation within the remainder of the current mandate.My Department recently completed a 14-week public consultation on the Learning Disability Service Model (LDSM) and the Service Delivery Plan (SDP) which closed on 25 November 2025. The LDSM sets out clear ambitions for young people with a learning disability, which includes individuals with complex needs, to receive lifelong, co-ordinated support, tailored to their needs and enabling choice and control. My officials are undertaking an analysis of the responses to the consultation which will inform any required amendments to the LDSM and SDP, including the commitment to carry out a legislative review.In terms of timescale, officials are aiming to publish an initial summary report of the consultation in January 2026, with the full analysis report to follow in March 2026. The findings from this will be used to further refine and finalise the LDSM and SDP by Spring 2026. Once finalised, they will inform decisions on the future reconfiguration of services for people with a learning disability and any additional investment required. Delivery of the ambitions outlined within the Model will be subject to funding made available to my Department. As part of developing the Learning Disability Service Model, my officials undertook a comparative analysis of policy and frameworks across England, Scotland and Wales. This work concluded that, if fully implemented, the LDSM, the SDP and associated transition reforms have the potential to meet and, in some respects, exceed the frameworks published in other jurisdictions particularly where legislation has not been matched by service capacity or effective delivery arrangements.However, until the LDSM and SDP are finalised, it is not possible to provide an exact timeframe for this review or to assess whether legislative reform will be required. I am clear however that any legislative reform must be properly sequenced, evidence-based, and aligned with the system reform already underway. In addition, as the legislation relates to a number of Health and Social Care programmes of care and will also require cross Governmental involvement (spanning Health, Education, Economy, and Communities), a working group will need to be established which will determine the terms of reference, scope and timeframe for the review.It is my clear position that any future legislative refor

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail how the £200,000 allocated to Newtownards Library, announced in his statement to the Assembly on 2 June 2025, has been used.
answer_text: To date whilst there has been no capital spend pending the necessary approvals for the business case, there has been £55k in resource expenditure on professional fees, which includes design costs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he intends to review the current questionnaire used by schools for monitoring the community background of staff.
answer_text: There is no proposal to review the current questionnaire used by schools for monitoring the community background of staff as the questionnaire has been agreed with the Equality Commission and is in use for all categories of staff. It is also based on the standard monitoring questionnaire used by all public sector employers in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 37384/22-27, to detail the complex issues which need to be resolved to allow the development of Newtownards Library to progress.
answer_text: There has been ongoing constructive engagement around the Newtownards Library Business Case between Libraries NI, Ards and North Down Borough Council (ANDBC) and officials in my Department. Following a series of recent meetings to examine the outstanding issues within the Business Case, a set of actions necessary to advance the project has been agreed and is being progressed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 35167/22-27, to detail (i) the amount of funding allocated via the Non-Statutory Specialist Pre-School Provision for Children with Complex Needs and Severe Learning Difficulties, to providers to provide specialist pre-school placements; and (ii) the number of placements this funded, and (iii) the number of these places that have been allocated, in the 2025-2026 academic year.
answer_text: Education Authority (EA) has advised that:(i)	Specialist pre-school places do not exist within the Pre-School Education Programme in the non-statutory sector. However, there are a number of children with SEN that avail of a funded pre-school place within the non-statutory sector. (ii)      The total funding for 2025/26 school year is £311,865.42.	 Kids Together – six placements confirmed.	 Solás – one placement confirmed.Consultations are ongoing and departmental approval is currently being sought for   a further six placements.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of the effectiveness of the current process for monitoring the community background of teachers employed in Controlled and Maintained schools.
answer_text: The Fair Employment (School Teachers) Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 came into effect on 12 May 2024. It revoked the remaining part of the Fair Employment and Treatment Order teachers’ exception. The Education Authority (EA) is responsible for monitoring the teachers employed in Controlled Schools and providing advice to their schools and the Council for Catholic Maintained Schools (CCMS) is responsible for their schools. All other schools that employee 11 or more employees were required to register with the Equality Commission and provide annual monitoring information to the Commission.The EA, CCMS and the teaching trade unions have encouraged all teachers to use of the online computerised system to input and update their personal equality data profile.It is now a compulsory requirement for teaching applicants to complete the monitoring form when applying for a post and this is used to populate the equality data profile for teachers. By utilising this data substantial progress has been made in the capture of teacher records for monitoring purposes. A joint TNC management working group was established to provide support and assistance to all sectors with the requirements of the Fair Employment (School Teachers) Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 and the working group has worked in partnership with the Equality Commission.The Equality Commission published guidance for schools with the support of Management Side of the Teachers’ Negotiating Committee and delivered a series of training webinars specifically on the recruitment and selection guidance. The Guidance, ‘School Teachers A Guide on Recruitment & Selection after the commencement of the Fair Employment (School Teachers) Act (Northern Ireland) 2022’ is available online.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the amount of funding given to the Intercultural Education Service; and (ii) the number of children the Intercultural Education Service has supported, in each of the last seven years.
answer_text: The Intercultural Education Service (IES) is a mainstream Education Authority (EA) service and, as such, the Department does not provide earmarked funding for this service. The quantum of funding allocated to IES from the EA’s core budget is therefore a decision for the EA. I understand that the budget allocated to IES has remained largely constant at an average level of £1.261m per annum over the period from 2018 to 2025. IES has directly supported an average of 824 children per year over the period from 2018 to 2025. This support included an average of 5,204 face-to-face or telephone/video interpreting sessions and 823 Help Desk enquiries per annum. IES also supports pupils through teaching, learning and curriculum support provided to schools and meeting with families, including Asylum Seekers and Refugees who need help to access education.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail why the £3000 additional resource budget for schools providing a Specialist Provision in a Mainstream Setting does not apply to standalone nursery units.
answer_text: All grant aided schools are funded under the Common Funding Scheme.  The Common Funding Formula (CFF) is the Department’s mechanism for allocating budgets to schools funded through Local Management of Schools arrangements. A school’s CFF allocation is determined by both the quantum of funding available to the Aggregated Schools Budget and a combination of the school’s overall enrolment, the differentiated needs of its pupils and the school’s characteristics.  Under the CFS, primary and post-primary schools with approved special provision classes attract a £3,000 lump sum per annum for each specialist provision class.  However, under the current CFS nursery schools and units are not eligible for this factor. The department recognises this discrepancy, and it is currently being reviewed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 36299/22-27, to detail any criteria used to determine whether a document is outdated or no longer required.
answer_text: I refer the Member to my previous answers of AQW 36299/22-27 and AQW 36302/22-27, published in the weekly answer booklet for week ending 5th December 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the disposal of the old Castle Gardens School site in Newtownards.
answer_text: The Education Authority has confirmed it is continuing to work actively to progress the disposal of the former site of Castle Gardens Primary School.The Land and Property Service (LPS) Strategic Asset Management Unit has completed the internal market (D1) process for the former Castle Gardens PS site and confirmed that no interest has been registered by other public sector organisations. LPS has now been asked to engage the appointed estate agent to place the property on the open market as soon as possible.EA solicitors continue to carry out legal due diligence to resolve complex title issues and ensure the property has a marketable title.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether proceeds from the sale of capital assets are (i) held by the Department selling the asset; (ii) held by his Department to redistribute; or (iii) returned to the Treasury.
answer_text: Each financial year departments will have a planned level of both Capital and Resource DEL receipts set out in their agreed budget position. Receipts up to this level can be retained by the department.In line with the current In Year Monitoring Guidance any receipts above the level planned in the relevant budget position, must be surrendered by departments as a reduced requirements for redistribution by the Executive.  To encourage the better utilisation of assets, in my draft Budget proposals 2026-2029/30 I have proposed to the Executive that departments be allowed to retain any capital receipts of up to £20 million per annum from the sale of assets.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, following the 2024 Northern Ireland Life and Times Survey, which found that 55 per cent of respondents said they would prefer an Integrated school, to outline any action he is taking to meet demand for integrated education.
To ask the Minister for Communities when the business case for the redevelopment of Newtownards library will be completed.
answer_text: My Department has received an updated business case for the Newtownards Library project and continues to engage with Libraries NI to address several complex issues therefore no definitive timescale can be provided at present.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the Religious Education Advisory Committee.
answer_text: The Religious Education Advisory Working Group has not met since March 2023.   My Department is considering legal advice following the recent Supreme Court judgment in the case of ‘JR87 and another’.   I will make an announcement on the RE syllabus in coming weeks, including interim guidance.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail how he will ensure that all relevant stakeholders will be able to contribute to the Northern Ireland Curriculum Review Task Force Committee consultation process.
answer_text: Stakeholder engagement has been central to the curriculum reform programme to date, with extensive input during the Strategic Review process. This included school visits and classroom observations, over 70 meetings and focus groups with a wide range of stakeholders, an open call for evidence which received 149 submissions, and a teacher survey completed by 1,432 individuals.An online survey was launched on 13 November 2025 to inform the Curriculum Taskforce Advisory Committee. This survey sought views from school leaders, teachers, and wider education stakeholders on individual subjects and areas of learning, including examples of current effective practice.There will be further opportunities for engagement in the coming months as drafting commences. Furthermore, the draft curriculum framework will be subject to a full public consultation. This will ensure all stakeholders have the opportunity to review the draft curriculum and provide feedback.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the consultation process that will be undertaken by the Northern Ireland Curriculum Review Task Force Committee.
answer_text: Stakeholder engagement has been central to the curriculum reform programme to date, with extensive input during the Strategic Review process. This included school visits and classroom observations, over 70 meetings and focus groups with a wide range of stakeholders, an open call for evidence which received 149 submissions, and a teacher survey completed by 1,432 individuals.An online survey was launched on 13 November 2025 to inform the Curriculum Taskforce Advisory Committee. This survey sought views from school leaders, teachers, and wider education stakeholders on individual subjects and areas of learning, including examples of current effective practice.There will be further opportunities for engagement in the coming months as drafting commences. Furthermore, the draft curriculum framework will be subject to a full public consultation. This will ensure all stakeholders have the opportunity to review the draft curriculum and provide feedback.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any planned implementation of Education for Sustainable Development, including outdoor learning, in (i) the Northern Ireland Curriculum review; and (ii) the new Northern Ireland Curriculum.
answer_text: The Curriculum Taskforce will develop a new statutory curriculum framework for Northern Ireland, drawing on the recommendations of the Strategic Review of the Northern Ireland Curriculum. The Taskforce will consider a wide range of priorities identified during the review.The proposed framework will be subject to full public consultation before any implementation, ensuring that all stakeholders have the opportunity to contribute.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 32470/22-27, AQW 32472/22-27 and AQW 32473/22-27, to detail (i) the reason priority repairs have been delayed; and (ii) any updated delivery timescales.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) is delivering the project to repair the storm-damaged mobile at St Francis’ Primary School. Regrettably, due to the ongoing pressures in relation to Special Educational Needs placements, the EA has confirmed that the programme for the works has been delayed. 		The EA now anticipates that the tender will issues in January 2026, with a contractor appointed in February 2026 and works commencing on site in March 2026. The estimated contract period is 12 weeks. The EA will actively investigate opportunities to expedite these timelines.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 35934/22-27, to detail (i) the amount of the £2.4 million investment announced in October 2024 that was allocated to South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust for early intervention within Severe Learning Disability programme; and (ii) how this funding has been allocated.
answer_text: During October 2024, I had announced additional investment aimed at improving the range of services for children with disability (CWD) across the region. The identified priorities included; increasing short breaks capacity, expanding family therapeutic and behavioural supports, and enhancing residential care.  The additional investment provided to the Children with Disability Service was made following an increased demand for family support, short breaks, residential care and transitions support across services for children with disabilities. The South-Eastern Health and Social Care Trust was awarded approximately £2.4m annually to fund the development of the identified priorities. In relation to the query pertaining to the ‘amount of finance that was allocated to South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust for Early Intervention within the Severe Learning Disability programme’ - the allocated funding was not specifically allocated to the Trust for Early Intervention. Furthermore, the term Early Intervention is often used as an umbrella term, which may be used to describe a number of services across a number of Directorates and Departments. The South Eastern HSC Trust can detail the areas of spend relating to the CWD Ministerial Investment, some of which may be interpreted as Early Intervention Services. There were approximately thirty areas of spend, however the main areas that the CWD Ministerial Investment has been used to enhance in the South Eastern HSC Trust have been summarised by the South Eastern HSC Trust to have been as follows:Residential Short Breaks.Expansion of Community Short Breaks.Expansion of Pre-School Support.Additional Learning Disability Nurses.Additional Autism Workers and Support Staff.Recruitment of 6 Senior Support Workers for Community Teams.Managerial Support.In Year Funding for Redwood Children’s House to November.The South Eastern HSC Trust has stated that they incurred additional spend through the expansion of Community / Voluntary Contracts, Staff Training, Child Sensory Equipment, and Recruitment Campaigns. The South Eastern HSC Trust has reported that up to an additional 70 staffing posts have been or are in the process of being recruited into the Learning Disability Service to enable the expansion of services in line with the priorities identified.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he has any plans to make the flu vaccine available to staff working in schools (i) this year; and (ii) in future years.
answer_text: Eligible cohorts for the autumn and winter 2025-26 influenza vaccination programme in Northern Ireland are based on the advice of the Joint Committee on Vaccination and Immunisation (JCVI).  In making its advice, the JCVI conducts a thorough review of the latest data including vaccine effectiveness data from UK influenza disease surveillance to inform expert recommendations.The national influenza vaccination programme aims to provide direct protection to those who JCVI consider are at higher risk of flu-associated morbidity and mortality, and to reduce transmission to all age groups through the vaccination of children.   Vaccination of school children was recommended by JCVI, but they did not recommend extending vaccination to those working in schools.Whilst JCVI does not recommend influenza vaccination for those working in schools, a person working in a school would be eligible for vaccination if they belong to any of the following groups:Those who are in a clinical risk group, Close contacts of immunocompromised individuals or A carer.The JCVI keeps its advice on seasonal flu vaccination programmes under regular review and publishes this on an annual basis. My Department will consider any updated advice made by JCVI relating to eligible cohorts for future programmes.While staff working in schools are not part of the national influenza vaccination programme, individuals can avail of private flu vaccination via a community pharmacy provider, if they wish to do so.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 36386/22-27, to detail his assessment of whether the current level of settings identified as being prepared to take a specialist provision for September 2026 is adequate to meet the forecasted demand for placements.
answer_text: As outlined in my response to AQW 36386/22-27, projects have been initiated at 44 schools aimed at creating an additional 47 Special Provisions. It is expected that this will create 406 places.This will not be enough to meet the projected demand for 2026. The Education Authority (EA) is continuing to engage with schools in areas of high demand to ensure there is sufficient provision in the right places to meet the needs of children with Special Educational Needs (SEN). This week a series of online sessions are being held with primary and post-primary schools who were unable to attend the main events last month. Over 30 schools accepted the invitations to these events. The EA is in the process of meeting with all special school leaders to identify what capacity will be available in special schools for next year.The EA is reliant on schools engaging with it, particularly in areas of high demand, and developing Specialist Provisions to meet the demand for 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail when the crossing point at Ballynahinch Primary School will be operational.
answer_text: Road safety continues to be a priority for my Department to ensure that all road users can travel safely and with confidence. I am acutely aware that children and young people are amongst the most vulnerable groups using our roads, especially near our schools.You will be aware that works to install the new controlled pedestrian crossing on the Dromore Road in Ballynahinch are nearing completion, with the new traffic signals expected to be operational before the Christmas holidays.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any tender process followed to award commercial contracts for the mobile phone pouches trial.
answer_text: On 31 January 2025, the Education Authority Central Procurement Service launched an open market procurement for delivery of the pilot, including the delivery of equipment and associated support and training.  Following a competitive procurement process, a preferred supplier was selected.  In June 2025, Odyssey Education was awarded the contract.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any engagement he has planned with teaching unions, following the publication of the Independent Review of Teacher Workload.
answer_text: I am committed to addressing the issue of teacher and school leader workload.  I want to ensure that teachers can focus on what matters most – teaching, learning and supporting pupils in the classroom. That is why I established an independent panel to review this issue.The Independent Review of Teacher Workload report was only published on 1 December 2025.  My officials and I will require some time to carefully consider the recommendations, including the recommendation in relation to controlled assessment, before we can provide a detailed response on the way forward. We will be engaging with teaching unions as part of this process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the rationale for requiring Ministerial sign off for Education Authority communications in relation to emergency school closures.
answer_text: Following Storm Amy, I commissioned a review of the emergency school closure processes across Counties Antrim, Londonderry, Fermanagh, and Tyrone.I approved the report and its recommendations and subsequently, issued a written Ministerial Statement to the Northern Ireland Assembly, summarising the key findings and confirming that the recommendations have been accepted and will be implemented.The report identified several issues relating to communications with schools and recommended that future messaging from the Education Authority should be agreed with the Department.I advised the Assembly that an Education Civil Contingencies Protocol was being developed. This protocol will clearly define the respective roles and responsibilities of the Department and the Education Authority and establish a robust communications framework. This communications framework will help ensure consistency and clarity of messaging in relation to emergency school closures.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail how the Independent Review of Teacher Workload final report's recommendation regarding controlled assessment will inform any decisions regarding the future of CCEA qualifications, particularly the future of controlled assessments.
answer_text: I am committed to addressing the issue of teacher and school leader workload.  I want to ensure that teachers can focus on what matters most – teaching, learning and supporting pupils in the classroom. That is why I established an independent panel to review this issue.The Independent Review of Teacher Workload report was only published on 1 December 2025.  My officials and I will require some time to carefully consider the recommendations, including the recommendation in relation to controlled assessment, before we can provide a detailed response on the way forward. We will be engaging with teaching unions as part of this process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the rationale for the proposal to require Ministerial approval for temporary school closures in an emergency situation.
answer_text: The legal authority to close a school rests with the Department under the 1973 Regulations - The Primary Schools (General) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 1973 and The Secondary Schools (Grant Conditions) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 1973 which set out the number of days a school must be operational other than those sanctioned by the Department in exceptional circumstances.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any actions that will be taken following the publication of the Independent Review of Teacher Workload.
answer_text: I am committed to addressing the issue of teacher and school leader workload.  I want to ensure that teachers can focus on what matters most – teaching, learning and supporting pupils in the classroom. That is why I established an independent panel to review this issue.The Independent Review of Teacher Workload report was only published on 1 December 2025.  My officials and I will require some time to carefully consider the recommendations, including the recommendation in relation to controlled assessment, before we can provide a detailed response on the way forward. We will be engaging with teaching unions as part of this process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail how he will establish which persons should have a role in drafting any revised syllabus for Religious Education, as per Article 11(2)(a) of the Education (Northern Ireland) Order 2006.
answer_text: In taking forward any revisions to the Religious Education syllabus, I will ensure that the process reflects the requirements of the Education (Northern Ireland) Order 2006. The order states that the syllabus should be prepared by “a group of persons appearing to the Department to be people who have an interest in the teaching of religious education in grant-aided schools.” I will set out the process for reforming the Religious Education syllabus in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline when her Department will complete the purchase of the planned park and ride site at William Street, Newtownards.
answer_text: On 2nd October 2025 the department’s Solicitors completed the Title report and issued it to Land & Property Services. This will allow the LPS Valuer to complete the formal valuation of the land. Compensation can then be agreed between both parties. However, my officials are aware that further queries have been raised by the Council’s Solicitors, and whilst these have been addressed, they may hold up the final agreement. On receipt of the Valuer’s Report my officials will issue instructions to complete the legal matters and make payment of the agreed compensation, including Statutory Interest and legal fees.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline how his Department intends to utilise the statutory duties and mechanisms available under the Children’s Services Co-operation Act (Northern Ireland) 2015 in the development of the Anti-Poverty Strategy.

To ask the Minister of Education whether he will be bringing any (i) policy; or (ii) legislative, changes arising from the Supreme Court judgment in the matter of an application by JR87 and another for Judicial Review (Appellant), to the Executive.
answer_text: The Supreme Court’s decision in the matter of JR87 and another has significant implications for our schools. I will consider the judgment in full before determining any essential remedies that both respect the law and safeguard the role of religion in education. This is a complex ruling and I have requested further legal advice to understand the full implications of the judgment and the next steps.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of mainstream settings (i) identified; and (ii) confirmed, to take on an additional Specialist Provision in Mainstream Schools, for September 2026.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that:As of the 3 December 2025, 281 schools are on the 2026 Engagement Plan (including 64 that attended engagement events). Development Officers are actively engaging with these schools to discuss the need for provision for 2026 and to secure approval/buy-in from schools to proceed. A summary of the current position in relation to discussions is provided below. 61 schools - have given permission for site visits to take place to explore feasible options to meet the accommodation needs associated with setting up specialist provision classes 50 schools - are engaging positively with officers but have yet to give permission for a site visit to take place 84 schools - have indicated that they do not wish to proceed to establish Specialist Provision classes for September 2026 86 schools - Initial contact has been made or attempted but no detailed discussions have taken place. As a result of positive site visits, projects have been initiated at 44 schools aimed at creating an additional 47 specialist provision classes. It is anticipated this will create approximately 406 places. This includes a number of schools where interim solutions have been identified to create capacity for 2026 placements, but which may still require school approval and acceptance of an interim solution.  Additionally, EA have now offered the 236 schools that who did not/could not attend the engagement events in November, 4 additional dates in December to engage directly on this important issue for our children and young people. We remain committed to ensuring all pupils with SEN can access education in line with their individual needs, in their local communities, with their peers and siblings.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any (i) policy; or (ii) guidance, his Department has on the removal of materials from departmental websites.
answer_text: The DE website is not an archive for documents or information intended solely for historical record.As the website is an important source of information for the public it is important that the content is up-to-date and accurate. To support this, periodic reviews of website content are carried out across the Department and any material that is outdated or no longer required will be removed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) where material which is removed from the Department’s website is archived; and (ii) whether this information is still available to the public.
answer_text: Documents, regardless of whether they have been published on the website, are saved by business areas in the Department’s content management system. The Department’s website should not be used to archive documents or information as a matter of historical record. An archive version of the DE site is held by PRONI and can be accessed by  members of the public at Capture Calendar | PRONI Collections, archived by The Public Record Office of Northern Ireland (PRONI) | Archive-It Wayback Machine.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail whether he has any plans to review either (i) The Education and Libraries (Northern Ireland) Order 1986; or (ii) The Education (Northern Ireland) Order 2006, to assess the extent to which they enable (a) religious education; and (b) collective worship, to be delivered in compliance with the Supreme Court judgment in the matter of an application by JR87 and another for Judicial Review (Appellant).
answer_text: The Supreme Court’s decision in the matter of JR87 and another has significant implications for our schools. I will consider the judgment in full before determining any essential remedies that both respect the law and safeguard the role of religion in education. This is a complex ruling and I have requested further legal advice to understand the full implications of the judgment and the next steps.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail who is responsible for decisions regarding the removal of materials on his Department’s website.
answer_text: Material is removed, published or revised on the Department’s website on request from business areas.  Business areas within the Department must periodically review the content on their respective sections of the website to ensure it remains accurate, current, and accessible, including any materials classified as historical. Each business area is responsible for deciding whether reviewed material should be retained or removed and for submitting the appropriate request.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any (i) policy; or (ii) guidance, his Department has on the removal of access to online archives and research.
answer_text: The DE website is not an archive for documents or information intended solely for historical record. An archive version of the DE site is held by PRONI and can be accessed by  members of the public at Capture Calendar | PRONI Collections, archived by The Public Record Office of Northern Ireland (PRONI) | Archive-It Wayback Machine.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail when Land and Property Services will complete the (i) valuation; and (ii) assessment, of compensation arising from the vesting of the lands at William Street, Newtownards, to facilitate the development of the planned park and ride site.
answer_text: Progress to value and assess compensation for these lands has been impacted by necessary action to establish the pre-vesting title position. Considerations are at an advanced stage with Land & Property Services (LPS) awaiting confirmation of agreement between solicitors acting for the parties in this matter. Every effort is being taken to bring this matter to conclusion and upon LPS being advised of the agreed pre-vesting title, the valuation advice will be completed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to provide an update on the opening of Lindsay House, to deliver short breaks.
To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the provision of CAR-T cell therapy.
answer_text: The Belfast Trust have confirmed that the current infrastructure and inpatient accommodation at Ward 10 North is not suitable to deliver CAR-T Therapy and this deficiency of environment does not meet the British Society for Haematology and Royal College of Physicians established standards, which are endorsed by NICE.The significant redesign and modernisation of the facility as described in the Outline Business Case for the new Regional Haematology Ward is required in order to remedy these issues and ensure that the BHSCT haematology clinical team is best supported to continue to provide safe, effective and appropriate care to patients requiring complex inpatient treatment.  Delivery of CAR-T Therapy will require this build to be completed.In this respect I have approved funding for the build subject to final approvals from Departmental officials.CAR-T services in the new ward will also require service development funding to increase capacity and this will be taken forward by the DoH and the Trust in advance of the completion of the ward as per established commissioning processes and subject to available funding at the time.In line with current timelines for completion of the build and attainment of relevant accreditation, Trust colleagues have advised that a CAR-T Cell Therapy service could be developed in Northern Ireland by 2030-31.I recognise the importance of delivering this complex service as close to home as possible for patients, their families and carers.  I remain committed to ensuring that we continue to make progress in achieving the necessary standards in infrastructure to sustainably and safely establish this specialist therapy locally in Northern Ireland. My Department has overseen expansion at scale with regards access to new and very specialist therapies, as endorsed by NICE and secured investments to support the introduction of over 730 Technology Appraisals and a further 30 Highly Specialised Technology Appraisals since 2011.  The consistency of this approach, I hope, provides assurance and confidence in both our commitment and our ability to deliver the best and most effective therapeutic regimes available for the people of Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether CAR-T cell therapy can be delivered at the City Hospital before the delivery of a bespoke ward for the service.
answer_text: The Belfast Trust have confirmed that the current infrastructure and inpatient accommodation at Ward 10 North is not suitable to deliver CAR-T Therapy and this deficiency of environment does not meet the British Society for Haematology and Royal College of Physicians established standards, which are endorsed by NICE.The significant redesign and modernisation of the facility as described in the Outline Business Case for the new Regional Haematology Ward is required in order to remedy these issues and ensure that the BHSCT haematology clinical team is best supported to continue to provide safe, effective and appropriate care to patients requiring complex inpatient treatment.  Delivery of CAR-T Therapy will require this build to be completed.In this respect I have approved funding for the build subject to final approvals from Departmental officials.CAR-T services in the new ward will also require service development funding to increase capacity and this will be taken forward by the DoH and the Trust in advance of the completion of the ward as per established commissioning processes and subject to available funding at the time.In line with current timelines for completion of the build and attainment of relevant accreditation, Trust colleagues have advised that a CAR-T Cell Therapy service could be developed in Northern Ireland by 2030-31.I recognise the importance of delivering this complex service as close to home as possible for patients, their families and carers.  I remain committed to ensuring that we continue to make progress in achieving the necessary standards in infrastructure to sustainably and safely establish this specialist therapy locally in Northern Ireland. My Department has overseen expansion at scale with regards access to new and very specialist therapies, as endorsed by NICE and secured investments to support the introduction of over 730 Technology Appraisals and a further 30 Highly Specialised Technology Appraisals since 2011.  The consistency of this approach, I hope, provides assurance and confidence in both our commitment and our ability to deliver the best and most effective therapeutic regimes available for the people of Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail when he will update the Traveller Child in Education Action Framework.
answer_text: The Department’s Traveller Child in Education Action Framework was published in 2013, in response to a 2011 report by the Taskforce on Traveller Education, which had been established to assist the Department to develop an action plan on Traveller education.  The existing framework outlines commitments to Traveller children and the steps to be taken to ensure that every traveller child has full access to education and outlines the actions in place in order to ensure information is provided to Traveller families detailing how to avail of all educational provisions and opportunities; help improve and maintain school attendance for Traveller children; ensure Traveller parents are aware of the role they play in their children getting the required level of education they deserve; help schools develop an inclusive and welcoming environment for Traveller children; and to provide education on Traveller culture in schools to eradicate the potential for prejudice and hate crimes. The Department is currently undertaking a review of the Traveller in Education Framework. The review is considering the degree to which the Framework has achieved its stated aims and objectives, with a view to informing the exploration of options for improvement and identifying emerging or new needs that need to be addressed within a revised Action Framework.A Stakeholder Engagement Group was established by the Department to support the review.  The Stakeholder Group was established to support the review of the commitments from the existing Traveller in Education Framework, as well as providing their experience and expertise to help inform the development of the evidence base for the review, in order to ensure that the key barriers and challenges for Traveller pupils are fully captured, and potential actions identified.  It is now planned to undertake school surveys and focus groups/discussions with Traveller pupils and their families to further inform the underpinning evidence base for the review.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) how funds for early intervention disability services have been allocated; and (ii) any plans he has to further develop early intervention resources, in the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: (i) Within the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust (SEHSCT); there is no discrete budget allocation specifically ring fenced for children’s early intervention disability services. Instead, early intervention is delivered within and across departments and directorates and may be delivered through universal services including GP and Health Visiting. Disability services also span both physical disability and intellectual disability provision, wherein a broad range of professionals including Social Workers, Nurses and Allied Health Professionals undertake initial assessments and often signpost to resources such as Family Support Hubs, Community and Voluntary services, for early help and advice. Within the Children’s Emotional Health and Wellbeing services including services for Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD) and Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder, (ADHD) Child Health Assistants provide resources and signposting to parents, depending on the presenting need, at that time.The South Eastern HSC Trust is in the process of receiving £2.4 Million investment announced by the Minister of Health during October 2024, and a proportion of this was allocated to early intervention services within the Severe Learning Disability programme.(ii) In relation to potential future investment; the South Eastern HSC Trust is working with the Department of Health and the other HSC Trusts, through the mechanism of the Strategic Reform Board, with a view toward securing additional investment through the Big Lottery funding and the Transformation Budget announced by the Northern Assembly during March 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of disability social worker posts that are vacant, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: BHSCTThe Belfast Health and Social Care Trust currently have one vacant social work position across Adult Disability Services.There are currently 8 vacant social work posts (6 temporary vacancies and 2 permanent vacancies) across Children’s Disability Services in the Belfast Trust. NHSCT The Northern Health and Social Care Trust have 13.8 whole time equivalent Social Work vacancies across Adult Disability Services.  Two of those vacancies relate to maternity leave.The NHSCT have 7 Social Work vacancies across Children’s Disability Services.The NHSCT have recently completed a restructuring exercise which created a number of new Band 7 social work positions.  The recruitment exercise to fill these positions has been completed and has resulted in a temporary increase to the number of Band 6 vacancies due to internal promotion.  The trust is actively recruiting to these positions.SHSCTThe Southern Health and Social Care Trust currently have 2 vacant Social Work posts across Adult Disability Services.The Children with Disabilities service in SHSCT currently has 2 Social Work posts vacant. SEHSCTThe South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust currently has 1.68 whole time equivalent social work vacancies across their Adult Disability services.Within the Children’s Disability Service there are 6 whole time equivalent Social Work vacancies.WHSCTThe Western Health and Social Care Trust have 4 permanent vacancies and 1 temporary vacancy - these are in Learning Disability Services.There are no vacant social work posts within Physical Disability services.Within the Western Health and Social Care Trust’s Children’s disability service there are 3.5 whole time equivalent social work vacancies.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the Redwood children’s home.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of families who are waiting to be allocated a disability social worker, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of disability social workers currently employed, in the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: There are 70.31 whole time equivalent social workers employed across adult and children’s disability services in the South Eastern Trust.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will publish an Equality and Human Rights Policy Screening for the Special Educational Needs (i) Reform Agenda, and (ii) Reform Agenda Delivery Plan.
answer_text: All Equality and Human Rights Policy screenings related to the Special Educational Needs (SEN) Reform Agenda and associated Delivery Plan will be published on the department’s website in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any action his Department is taking to inform and educate young people about the value and usefulness of alternative qualifications and their equivalence to GCSE qualifications.
answer_text: Through the Entitlement Framework, schools are required to offer access to a minimum of 21 courses at Key Stage 4 and Post-16, of which at least one third must be general and one third applied.  This aims to ensure that schools provide access for pupils to a broadly based and balanced range of subjects and qualifications which meet the interests and progression needs of their pupils, whether that is in to training, employment, further or higher education opportunities.It is the role of all teachers, including careers teachers, to ensure that learners are aware of these subject options and the progression pathways available through particular qualifications. Young people are further supported through independent, impartial information and advice provided by the Careers Service which has partnership agreements with each post primary school.Through the joint careers action plan, my officials are working with colleagues in the Department for the Economy to implement several actions that will enhance the information available to young people and increase opportunities for work related learning and engagement in careers events.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will publish an Equality and Human Rights Policy Screening for TransformED NI.
answer_text: The TransformED strategy represents much needed core education reform across curriculum, assessment, qualifications and the education workforce. Overall, there are 30 separate actions within the TransformED Delivery Plan. My Department will undertake screening exercises as appropriate for each individual policy change within TransformED.A copy of all completed screening forms is published on the Department's website at https://www.education-ni.gov.uk/articles/equality-screenings.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the work undertaken by Northern Ireland Statistics and Research Agency, to better understand parents’ childcare requirements.
answer_text: The first release of the Northern Ireland Statistics and Research Agency (NISRA) Northern Ireland Childcare Survey Results were published as Official Statistics on 8 May 2025. Since that time, NISRA has published supplementary data.  The survey results and supplementary data can be accessed via the following link - Northern Ireland Childcare Survey Results.The NISRA survey, alongside stakeholder engagement and other research, has informed the development of the draft Early Learning and Childcare Strategy which I issued to Executive colleagues on 14 November 2025 seeking agreement to issue for public consultation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the work of the Taskforce on Traveller Education.
answer_text: The Department’s Traveller Child in Education Action Framework was published in 2013, in response to a 2011 report by the Taskforce on Traveller Education, which had been established to assist the Department to develop an action plan on Traveller education.  The existing framework outlines commitments to Traveller children and the steps to be taken to ensure that every traveller child has full access to education and outlines the actions in place in order: ensure information is provided to Traveller families detailing how to avail of all educational provisions and opportunities; help improve and maintain school attendance for Traveller children; ensure Traveller parents are aware of the role they play in their children getting the required level of education they deserve; help schools develop an inclusive and welcoming environment for Traveller children; and to provide education on Traveller culture in schools to eradicate the potential for prejudice and hate crimes. The Department is undertaking a review of the Traveller in Education Framework. The review is considering the degree to which the Framework has achieved its stated aims and objectives, with a view to informing the exploration of options for improvement and identifying emerging or new needs that need to be addressed within a revised Action Framework.A Stakeholder Engagement Group was established by the Department to support the review.  The Group is comprised of a range of interested parties and key stakeholders, including Travellers with lived experience. The role of the Group is to act as an advisory forum to provide advice and expertise on Traveller education to Department in support of the development of any future proposals for change arising from the review.  As such, the role of the Group is: to provide advice on the approach to the wider co-design process; to share best practice / relevant research; to support analysis of data / trends / evidence relevant to the review; to review and advise on proposals emerging from the review; to monitor progress against timescales.   The Stakeholder Group has helped to support the review of the commitments from the existing Traveller in Education Framework, as well as providing their experience and expertise to help inform the development of the evidence base for the review, in order to ensure that the key barriers and challenges for Traveller pupils are fully captured, and potential actions identified.  It is now planned to undertake school surveys and focus groups/discussions with Traveller pupils and their families to further inform the underpinning evidence base for the review.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 29931/22-27, to detail any work being undertaken to ensure (i) suitable school placements are available in September 2026, for pupils with a statement of special educational needs; and (ii) appropriate contingency provisions are in place to provide placements for children of this age cohort, who are statemented after the October 2025 census date.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that:In recent years there has been an increase in the number of children presenting with Special Educational Needs (SEN) and in the complexity of their needs identified. While many children with SEN have their needs met through enhanced support within mainstream classes, the changing pupil profile has raised placement challenges due to the limited capacity within Special Schools and Specialist Provision within mainstream settings for pupils with complex educational needs.Ensuring all children with Special Educational Needs (SEN) receive appropriate placements to meet their individual needs remains a critical priority for the EA.The EA is actively working to increase capacity across the school estate to meet demand for September 2026. To date, 39 schools are establishing specialist provision, which will create an additional 41 classes and approximately 328 places. This includes several schools where interim solutions have been identified to create capacity for 2026 placements, though some of these arrangements still require formal school approval.Additionally, the pressure identified for Special Schools is currently being reviewed through capacity meetings with the 40 individual special school principals.  The EA's SEN Capacity Team continues to engage directly with schools to discuss provision requirements for 2026, with a view to increasing capacity further. In November 2025, over 300 schools were invited to engagement events, with 64 schools attending.  EA have now offered the 236 schools that who did not/could not attend these events, 4 additional dates in December to engage directly on this important issue for our children and young people.The EA will continue to work directly with parents of children who are statemented after the October 2025 census date to identify and secure access to education in line with their individual needs identified. Our vision is to ensure that all children are educated in an inclusive environment within their local community, receiving support tailored to their identified needs to help them reach their full potential.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any early help emotional wellbeing interventions, available to pupils in (i) primary; and (ii) post-primary schools.
answer_text: Pupil emotional health and wellbeing will be supported within schools in a variety of ways, and the joint Department of Education and Department of Health Emotional Health and Wellbeing in Education Framework provides overarching guidelines to schools around having a whole school approach to emotional health and wellbeing. The Education Authority (EA) Being Well Doing Well programme is the foundational programme in helping schools adopt, embed and improve this approach.  Other education and health programmes associated with the Framework, and available to schools include: RISE KS2 programme (primary),EA Youth Service REACH programme (primary and post-primary)Emotional Wellbeing Teams in Schools project (post-primary), andThe HOPE post primary counselling service Schools have flexibility around the type of support provided for pupils and authority to use their individual budgets to fund external providers for programmes aimed at promoting positive emotional health.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, further to the NI Audit Office report, Managing the Schools' Estate, whether he will develop a plan to support the delivery of targets, as set out in Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.
answer_text: My Department’s response to the Northern Ireland Audit Office report on Managing the Schools’ Estate, and the subsequent Public Accounts Committee hearing, will be set out in the Memorandum of Reply (MoR) to the Minister of Finance.Once the MoR has been reviewed by the Minister of Finance, it will be presented to the Assembly and uploaded to the Department of Finance website.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, further to the NI Audit Office report, Managing the Schools' Estate, whether he will develop a plan to prepare for the transfer of assets from Public Private Partnerships and Private Finance Initiative schools in 2027 and 2033, to ensure these are appropriately maintained.
answer_text: My Department’s response to the Northern Ireland Audit Office report on Managing the Schools’ Estate, and the subsequent Public Accounts Committee hearing, will be set out in the Memorandum of Reply (MoR) to the Minister of Finance.Once the MoR has been reviewed by the Minister of Finance, it will be presented to the Assembly and uploaded to the Department of Finance website.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, further to the NI Audit Office report, Managing the Schools' Estate, whether he will develop a programme of planned maintenance, informed by up to date condition data, for the school estate.
answer_text: My Department’s response to the Northern Ireland Audit Office report on Managing the Schools’ Estate, and the subsequent Public Accounts Committee hearing, will be set out in the Memorandum of Reply (MoR) to the Minister of Finance.Once the MoR has been reviewed by the Minister of Finance, it will be presented to the Assembly and uploaded to the Department of Finance website.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, further to the Northern Ireland Audit Office Report entitled Managing the Schools' Estate, to detail whether he will develop a central database detailing the condition of the whole school estate.
answer_text: My Department’s response to the Northern Ireland Audit Office report on Managing the Schools’ Estate, and the subsequent Public Accounts Committee hearing, will be set out in the Memorandum of Reply (MoR) to the Minister of Finance.Once the MoR has been reviewed by the Minister of Finance, it will be presented to the Assembly and uploaded to the Department of Finance website.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on any work undertaken by his Department to develop an accessible, quick-read, child-friendly guide detailing the various types of specialist provisions and classes, as recommended within the Education and Training Inspectorate's report on Specialist Provision in Mainstream Schools.
answer_text: The Education Authority are in final stages of production of an accessible, quick-read child-friendly guide detailing the various types of specialist provisions and classes.  Following the required checks it is hoped that this will be available to families in the near future.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 27511/22-27, to detail whether any further consideration has been given to undertaking a review of the effectiveness of the Education Authority's Specialist Settings Support Team, as recommended in the November 2024 Education and Training Inspectorate report on Specialist Provision in Mainstream Schools.
answer_text: The Education Authority has advised that:The Specialist Provisions’ Service (previously named SSST) is currently subject to a full audit. All information has been submitted to EA Internal Audit and Assurance in Nov 2025 and the Service awaits the final audit report.The audit focused on the effectiveness of the service in key areas, including:1.	Training Programme Development2.	Programme Design3.	Monitoring and Feedback4.	Record Keeping5.	Performance MeasurementPlease note that support from the service has also, from this academic year, been extended to include all Specialist Provisions (including legacy) as recommended in the November 2024 Education and Training Inspectorate report on Specialist Provision in Mainstream Schools.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, further to the Northern Ireland Audit Office report entitled Managing the Schools' Estate, to detail whether he will update his Department's Asset Management Plan annually.
answer_text: My Department’s response to the Northern Ireland Audit Office report on Managing the Schools’ Estate, and the subsequent Public Accounts Committee hearing, will be set out in the Memorandum of Reply (MoR) to the Minister of Finance.Once the MoR has been reviewed by the Minister of Finance, it will be presented to the Assembly and uploaded to the Department of Finance website.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to provide an update on the development of an outcomes dashboard for the Special Educational Needs Disability Reform Agenda.
answer_text: Work to develop an outcomes dashboard has not yet begun.  Dashboard development will begin once the ongoing work to identify sources of data for the indicators in the framework has completed.  This is expected to complete in the new year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail how he will utilise the Partnership Agreement between Government and the Voluntary and Community Sector to support non-statutory pre-school providers.
answer_text: I welcome the Partnership Agreement between Government and the Voluntary and Community Sector. Non-statutory pre-school settings are an equally valued partner in the delivery of the Pre-School Education Programme (PSEP) and account for approximately 50% of all funded settings.  DE and the Education Authority (EA) work collaboratively with the non-statutory pre-school sector at all levels of the PSEP.  Representatives of the sector are members of the EA Pre-School Education Group (EA PEG) and the Pre-School Standardisation Programme Board where they make a valued contribution to the work of the department and the EA, helping shape how pre-school provision is delivered and transformed.Fair funding principles are a core component of the Partnership Agreement and are reflected in the Pre-School Standardisation Programme (PSP). I approved a new funding model, which includes increased funding rates for non-statutory providers participating in the Pre-School Education Programme (PSEP) in June 2025.  The model is based on the recommendations from  the Standardisation of the Pre-school Education Programme: Scoping Study Final Report and is intended in line with the application of full cost recovery, to take account of all relevant costs to facilitate the approved session length for each setting, including paid release time for activities such as planning, administration and training as well as overheads such as rent, utilities and consumables. In addition, an uplift has also been applied to echo the additional pupil-related factors that are included in funding to statutory pre-school settings; while the mechanism of payment on a real-time monthly basis has been retained to account for the particular needs of the non-statutory sector in regard to prompt payment.My officials also engage with community and voluntary representatives through the Stakeholder Engagement Forum as part of the development of the Executive Early Learning and Childcare Strategy to ensure the principles of the Partnership Agreement are evident throughout the strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the work of his Department’s Inclusion Development Team.
answer_text: The Inclusion Development Team (IDT) is responsible for the development of Inclusion policy, delivery of the Inclusion aspects of the SEN Reform Delivery Plan and leading on collaborative working with the European Agency for Special Needs and Inclusive Education (EASNIE).  Since the team was established in June 2025, its primary focus has been on progressing the two Inclusion projects funded by the Public Sector Transformation Board, in collaboration with key delivery partners: the Inclusion Capacity Building Programme and Inclusive Play Programme.  Both projects are expected to commence in early 2026.IDT has also been working closely with EASNIE who is supporting my department’s policy work on Inclusion in Education for Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the Common Funding Formula review.
answer_text: The independent panel which is carrying out a Review of Baseline School Funding Provided Through the Common Funding Formula has concluded its fieldwork and is due to submit its report to me.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the review of the effectiveness of the Pre-School Education Inclusion Support Fund.
answer_text: An evaluation of Pre-School Education Inclusion Support Fund (PSEISF) for the 2024/25 academic year was expected by the end of October 2025; however, due to competing priorities within the directorate responsible for it, this has been delayed slightly.  A review of the administration of the Fund has been completed, a survey to all settings that received funding will be issued shortly and it is expected that the evaluation will be completed by the end of December.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the review of the Children Looked After Advisory Service.
answer_text: The scope of the Primary Children Looked After Advisory Service review is being finalised, with the completion expected early in the new year.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline any support being provided to children who have been displaced from their homes as a result of racial attacks and intimidation.
To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of children allocated a specialist pre-school placement, who are attending on reduced hours.
answer_text: The Education Authority has advised that:A total of 17 pupils pre-school pupils are attending specialist provision placement on reduced hours. From Tuesday 18 November this will reduce to 15 pupils.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of children requiring a specialist pre-school placement, who are still awaiting their place for the 2025-2026 academic year.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that:There are currently five children across Northern Ireland who are waiting to be placed on the 2025-26 year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the comprehensive analysis of the therapeutic needs of children with special educational needs in special schools.
To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 31938/22-27, to detail why information on multiple sclerosis patients who have received an annual review is not tracked.
answer_text: As outlined in my previous response on this area, services for people with multiple sclerosis (MS) are provided as part of wider Neurology services in each Trust area. The information on new and review appointments provided for people with a diagnosis of MS and indeed for other subspecialist areas within this specialty has historically been reported in the monitoring information provided for the wider neurology service and has not therefore been identified separately.  To date, there has been no requirement to report on the proportion of patients within Neurology who receive an annual review.As you will be aware, the HSC is in the process of implementing a new digital care record across the region with the Encompass system now available in each Trust area. The process is underway to ensure system optimisation. There are no confirmed timelines for this element of the implementation to be completed. It is intended that the new digital health record system will allow an improved level of reporting which has the potential to provide data that will inform service needs for those across a range of chronic long-term conditions including for those people with a diagnosis of MS.This is a welcome development and important in the context of the current review waiting times for neurology, which as previously acknowledged are lengthy and growing for adults across Northern Ireland. This situation has arisen due to several factors, including funding that falls short of objectively assessed need and previous missed opportunities to reform health and social care delivery. I am committed to addressing this, subject to available funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail whether schools are permitted to change their school uniform policy, without publishing it in their prospectus.
answer_text: The Education (School Information and Prospectuses) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2003 require the Board of Governors to publish certain information in the annual school prospectus.   The requirement in relation to school uniforms for primary or post-primary schools is to publish “The policy or rules of the school, if any, in respect of the way in which pupils are to be dressed including, in particular, any recommendations or rules relating to the wearing of school uniform.”Whilst it is therefore to be expected that the prospectus will set out the school uniform policy in full, it may be the case that it summarises the uniform rules and refers parents to the school’s website (for example) where the full policy is almost always published – whether this be as well as, or instead of, fully within the prospectus itself.   The important point is that the Board of Governors satisfy themselves they are compliant with all statutory requirements and that parents have access to information about the school uniform policy of a school before they express their preferences for the school they wish their child to attend. My Department’s letter of 14 October 2025 explicitly advised schools to ensure that nothing was published in their school prospectus for 2026/27 in relation to school uniform that did not align with the School Uniforms (Guidelines and Allowances) Bill – information about its requirements was also provided.  I trust that schools will have acted upon this appropriately.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on any work being carried out to best meet the needs of pupils with persistent, and challenging behaviour, in Special Schools.
answer_text: I understand that the Education Authority (EA) advocates for and encourages special schools to support pupils who display behaviours that challenge within an inclusive, trauma-informed and nurture principle-based framework of response.The EA has advised that there is a tiered approach in place to effectively support staff respond to the range of profiles of needs within special schools. This includes the appointment of a bespoke behaviour advocate role within each special school alongside the ongoing development of a Behaviour for Learning framework in providing early intervention and preventative strategies.Further strategies and programmes include: The iThrive for Learning framework: partnership working across services within Health and Education. A behaviour intervention officer role which is being trialled in a small number of special schools.Participation in the Better Together programme which is an established whole school response programme in Special Schools.Team Teach Ltd is an accredited training provider currently used by the EA to deliver training in restrictive physical intervention to staff within Special Schools.A Special Schools’ Protocol was developed and commenced in October 2025 to support schools in managing very distressed behaviours of individual pupils. All schools have a statutory responsibility within their scheme of management (The Education (Northern Ireland) Order 1998:  Articles 3-6) for developing their own policies to promote good behaviour and discipline.In addition, under Article 6 of the 1998 Order the EA is required to prepare a statement outlining the arrangements it has made, or proposes to make, for the education of children with behaviour difficulties. This includes arrangements for providing advice and support to schools.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of (i) specialist pre-school places funded by the Non-Statutory Specialist Pre-School Provision for Children with Complex Needs and Severe Learning Difficulties; and (ii) places that have been allocated, in this academic year.
answer_text: The Education Authority has advised that:There are currently 6 children placed in these provisions.  Article 12 of the Education (Northern Ireland) Order 1996 deals specifically with special educational provision in institutions in Northern Ireland other than grant-aided schools.  This applies when a child has a Statement of Special Educational Needs and the EA considers provision outside a grant-aided school.  EA cannot place a child in a non-grant-aided school without the consent of the Department of Education.  DE will only grant permission if satisfied that: •	The placement is necessary to meet the child’s needs.•	No suitable provision exists within grant-aided schools.•	The arrangement complies with statutory duties and funding rules.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any work currently being undertaken in relation to the review of the special educational needs classroom assistant model.
answer_text: The Department in collaboration with the Education Authority continue to progress preparatory work in this area.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the Priorities for Youth Policy Review.
answer_text: The review of Priorities for Youth is ongoing.  I will consider any revision to the existing policy in light of the review findings which have included extensive engagement with young people as well as the Education Authority, which has statutory responsibility for youth services, and community and voluntary youth providers.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the Review of Suspensions and Expulsions.
answer_text: I will be considering the draft report on the review of suspension and expulsion arrangements shortly. Once I have considered the draft report, I will determine the next steps to be taken.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail when her Department will complete the purchase of the planned park and ride site at William Street, Newtownards.
answer_text: Since the land was vested, the Department, with assistance from the Departmental Solicitor’s Office (DSO), has spent a considerable amount of time addressing complex legal title issues in conjunction with Ards and North Down Borough Council.I understand that these issues have largely been resolved with the matter having recently been referred to Land and Property Services (LPS) with a view to determining formal valuation and compensation. On receipt of the Valuer’s Report the Department will issue further instructions to DSO to address any remaining legal matters and to complete the purchase of the land.As my Department is awaiting the Valuer’s Report, I am unable to provide a timeframe for completion of the purchase.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 33386/22-27, to detail the number of new Specialist Provisions in Mainstream Schools required to provide appropriate special educational needs placements in the 2026-2027 academic year.
answer_text: There is an estimated pressure for an additional 468 pupil places for September 2026 across Specialist Provision Classes.  This equates to approximately 51 additional Specialist Provision (Learning) and Specialist Provision (Social Communication) classes.  In addition, there is an estimated pressure for an additional 607 pupil places for September 2026 across special schools. Approximately 25-30 Specialist Provision (Special) classes will be required in mainstream schools to support special schools in meeting this need. This number is subject to change as capacity meetings with special school principals are ongoing and due to be completed before the Christmas break.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail how feedback received from parents, as part of the survey undertaken by Parenting Focus, will inform outcomes of the RAISE Programme.
answer_text: As a key stakeholder group and recognising the importance of parents in the RAISE Programme, my officials engaged Parenting Focus to capture the views of parents across RAISE localities. Locality Co-Ordinators in each of the 18 RAISE localities received local reports from Parenting Focus on feedback received from parents in their area through focus groups and a survey.
