The input gathered from parents through this process was shared with other key stakeholders and used by the Locality Reference Groups, in each of the RAISE localities, to help inform the identification and prioritisation of needs and the development of Strategic Area Plans.Each RAISE Strategic Area Plan includes a section reflecting the views of parents from that locality.  A composite report capturing the views of parents across all localities has been published on the DE website.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 32916/22-27, to detail the criteria used for exceptional circumstances appeals, where transport assistance is requested.
answer_text: The Department of Education’s Home to School Transport Policy states that it is for the Education Authority (EA) to consider any case which is thought to be outside the provisions of the policy, and that such cases should be, by their very nature exceptional. The EA’s policy in relation to appealing for transport assistance on the grounds of exceptional circumstances is published on its website. The EA does not provide a definitive list of criteria which qualify as exceptional, rather it considers each application on its individual merits, and it is the responsibility of the parent to submit all facts, circumstances and evidence to the EA to demonstrate the exceptional nature of the case. The EA’s policy offers guidance and examples of evidence required and it may request further information from parents where necessary.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the progress of the Ballynahinch Bypass scheme.
answer_text: On 12th March 2025 I announced the progression of the A24 Ballynahinch Bypass scheme with my officials moving forward on the development of the business case and procurement of the scheme. My officials are undertaking a number of actions to progress the scheme including meeting with landowners, updating surveys and reviewing relevant documents due to the passage of time.  This work will contribute to the preparation of the procurement documentation.As with all projects within the road infrastructure portfolio, and in light of the A5 judgement and my subsequent appeal, I am taking this opportunity to consider its potential impact before determining the next steps for this scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 33791/22-27, to detail whether the guidelines arising from the School Uniforms (Guidelines and Allowances) Bill will be published and provided to schools, before 26 November 2025.
answer_text: The School Uniforms Guidelines issued to schools today, 12 November 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 32916/22-27, to detail the number of unsuccessful appeals for transport assistance due to enforced relocation to temporary accommodation outside the school catchment area, in each of the last 3 academic years.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) previously provided information on cases that its Transport Appeals Panel considered exceptional in nature and the reasons recorded for that decision included enforced relocation to temporary accommodation. The EA is unable to identify pupils who have relocated to, or who are living in temporary accommodation. The EA has provided information below on cases it has identified where the reasons cited by parents include relocation to temporary accommodation, however its Transport Appeals Panel did not deem the circumstances to be exceptional. Academic YearAppeals panel did not uphold the case – unsuccessful2022/2352023/2452024/25102025/26 year to date10

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail whether any independent body inspects the Education Authority's Education Welfare Service team responsible for parental prosecutions.
answer_text: The EA Internal Audit Team has conducted audits relating to two areas of the Education Welfare Service practice, waiting lists and school expulsions, as per the 2024/2025 Internal Audit Plan.A further audit has been requested on court processes, and this will be included in the Internal Audit Plan for 2025/2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 34104/22-27, to detail how members are appointed to Education Authority Education Welfare Service Court Panels.
answer_text: The responsibility for Court Panels in Education Welfare (EA) is delegated to each Regional Assistant Head of Service by the Head of Services.Each Panel is chaired by the Assistant Head of Service (EWS) for the relevant EA Locality. The remaining members comprise the Team Leaders for that locality.The Panel convenes monthly in each of the five areas to consider cases where, despite sustained intervention and completion of Education Strategy and Education Planning Meetings, the required improvements in school attendance have not been achieved.At each meeting, cases are presented by the relevant Team Leader. The Panel will then decide one of the following outcomes:Defer the case for further monitoring or intervention,Request additional updates or information, orInitiate Court Action, either through:Family Proceedings (Education Supervision Order – ESO), orMagistrates’ Court Proceedings (Parental Prosecution).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 33484/22-27, to detail when the vacancies in the Local Impact Teams will be filled.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that: The 29.4 Full Time Equivalent (FTE) Teacher grade specialist posts referred to in AQW 33484/22-27 are currently in recruitment. Interviews for the 21.4 School Age Teacher grade specialist FTEs will be completed by week commencing 15 December 2025 at which point individual circumstances will determine when posts can be taken up. The 8 FTE Early Years Teacher grade specialist posts are currently at the advertisement stage and this is due to close on 20 November 2025. Following which shortlisted candidates will be invited to the interview stage of the recruitment process in line with EA Protocol.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 32807/22-27, to detail whether this policy statement has undergone a Child Rights Impact Assessment.
answer_text: A Child Rights Impact Assessment was not completed in respect of the policy statement.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 32584/22-27, to detail how he will ensure that the views of Special Educational Needs Coordinators are reflected in the development and delivery of the new Education Psychology Service consultation model.
answer_text: Structured engagement mechanisms, including working groups and feedback loops, have been established to ensure Special Educational Needs Coordinators (SENCos) insights inform the development and delivery of the model.  The model will be subject to ongoing evaluation throughout the five-year SEND Transformation rollout, ensuring continuous improvement and that SENCo views remain central to its implementation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline any engagement she has had with the Minister of Education in relation to his proposals for 16-18 legislation.
To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 32807/22-27, to detail the legal standing of this policy statement.
answer_text: The policy statement of 8 September was issued pursuant to the Department’s statutory duty under Article 3(b) of the Education Reform (Northern Ireland) Order 1989.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail when he will publish the Executive Early Learning and Childcare Strategy.
answer_text: I will bring forward a draft Early Learning and Childcare Strategy for Executive consideration and public consultation in the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 33788/22-27, to detail (i) when the 11 seven-seater vehicles were purchased by the Education Authority; and (ii) when all 11 vehicles will be in use to transport children.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) informs me that the first seven-seater vehicle was purchased and delivered in March 2024, at the end of the 2023/24 financial year. Ten further vehicles were purchased in at the end of the 2024/25 financial year to expand the pilot project. Three of these vehicles were delivered to the EA in January 2025 and the remaining seven in March 2025. The first vehicle delivered to EA in March 2024 became operational in April 2024. The EA advises that the recruitment of drivers for the 10 vehicles purchased in 2025 has been extremely challenging. One of these vehicles became operational in September 2025 and two further vehicles are coming into operation in November 2025. Recruitment remains ongoing for drivers for the remaining 6 vehicles.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 33047/22-27, for an update on the establishment of an Enhanced Advocacy Service, and a one-stop website for parents.
answer_text: The SEN Reform Governance Framework will be published on the department’s website in the coming weeks. (i) The following funded initiatives are planned to commence in the first term of the 2025/26 academic year:  Transforming the Support Model; A programme for 2-3 year-olds with emerging SEN; In-school support model for children in pre-school and foundation stage; Introduction of SEN Assistant Training in Pre-School & Foundation stage; Special Schools as Resource Centres of Expertise.(ii) Detailed indicative budget allocations for specific actions are not intended for publication as they may be subject to change.It is intended that the appointment of a Special Educational Needs Champion will be progressed in 2026.The Governance Structures include the establishment of an expert advisory forum. In addition to this, there are a number of other advisory panels across the Department of Education (DE) and consideration is being given to introducing SEN expertise to these to ensure alignment with the SEN Reform Agenda.     The action to establish an Enhanced Advocacy Service is under consideration.  Early scoping has commenced on the development of a ‘one-stop’ website for parents; this action will be developed further in 2026.

(i) The following funded initiatives are planned to commence in the first term of the 2025/26 academic year:  Transforming the Support Model; A programme for 2-3 year-olds with emerging SEN; In-school support model for children in pre-school and foundation stage; Introduction of SEN Assistant Training in Pre-School & Foundation stage; Special Schools as Resource Centres of Expertise.(ii) Detailed indicative budget allocations for specific actions are not intended for publication as they may be subject to change.It is intended that the appointment of a Special Educational Needs Champion will be progressed in 2026.The Governance Structures include the establishment of an expert advisory forum. question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 33791/22-27, to detail whether direction can be issued to schools, based on powers arising from the School Uniforms (Guidelines and Allowances) Bill, if their school uniform policy for the 2026-2027 academic year was published prior to the bill receiving Royal Assent.
answer_text: The School Uniforms (Guidelines and Allowances) Bill comes into operation on the day after it gains Royal Assent.  This will include the power for any directions required, or permitted, to be given under section 8 notwithstanding when a school uniform policy is published.  When the Bill gains Royal Assent the guidelines will be formally issued to schools and then they must be adhered to under section 7.  This will be in advance of the 2026/27 school year which schools have been advised of and the guidelines are being shared with them shortly - in advance of Royal Assent - to assist them.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 32807/22-27, to detail who was consulted in the creation of this policy statement.
answer_text: No formal consultation process was undertaken in the development of this policy.

answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that:The 42.2 full time equivalent vacancies equate to approximately £2.2 million per annum which is already covered within the EA’s existing budget.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 31023/22-27, to detail the amount of funding required to fill the 24 full-time equivalent Teacher posts that have been proposed across the Local Impact Teams network.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that: The 24 full time equivalent vacancies equates to approximately £1.7m per annum which is already covered within the EA’s existing budget.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 31023/22-27, to detail when all vacancies will be filled.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that: All recruitment is being progressed as quickly as possible in line with HR policy and Trade Union Side (TUS) engagement requirements. A substantial proportion of the recruitment exercises have already been completed but individual circumstances dictate when posts can be taken up. It should also be noted that vacancies continue to arise and have to be filled throughout each year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of pupils, living in households with homelessness status, who have (i) appealed a decision of the Education Authority to refuse to fund school transport, required as a result of being moved into temporary accommodation outside of their school catchment area; (ii) had their appeal rejected, in each of the last three years.
answer_text: The Education Authority has advised me that it does not record the household status of all pupils applying for home to school transport assistance. Eligibility for transport is based on two main criteria, distance and suitable school. If a family moves house, including into temporary accommodation, they need to make a new application for transport assistance. They may be eligible for transport assistance if their current address is beyond statutory walking distance from their school and they provide evidence that they are unable to secure a place in any school in the same category within that distance. If their application for transport has been refused, they are able to appeal that decision under the EA's exceptional circumstances policy and can reference accommodation issues such as enforced relocation and concern for their child’s welfare in the evidence provided to the EA. The EA’s exceptional circumstances panel will consider the evidence provided in each case on its individual merits and determine if transport assistance should be provided.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any Allied Health Professionals involved on an ongoing basis, in the implementation of the SEN Reform Agenda and Delivery Plan 2025-2030.
answer_text: My officials have engaged with Allied Health Professional (AHP) representatives throughout the development of the Special Educational Needs (SEN) Reform Agenda and associated Delivery Plan. AHPs are represented on the Operational Implementation Oversight Group (OIOG) which is an integral part of the SEN Reform governance structures and will be invited onto to the Expert Advisory Forum and Lived Experience Forum.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 33047/22-27, to detail (i) the role of; (ii) the staffing roles within; and (iii) the vacancies outstanding in, the SEN Reform Delivery Unit.
answer_text: The Special Educational Needs (SEN) Reform and Inclusion Development Directorate (SRIDD) has been established as the Delivery Unit for SEN Reform.  It is led by a Director and Assistant Director.  The Unit is structured into three distinct teams. The SEN Reform Delivery Team (SRDT) is currently made up of a Grade 7 officer, three Deputy Principals, three Staff Officers and one Executive Officer 2.  Its primary focus is on the design, implementation and operationalisation of a range of actions outlined within the SEN Reform Agenda and Delivery Plan, subject to funding. The SEN Reform Support Team (SRST) is currently made up of two Deputy Principals, and one Executive Officer 2.  This team provides technical support for the delivery of SEN Reform.  Its responsibilities include governance and accountability, project management, financial monitoring, outcomes evaluation, data collection and analysis and the development and delivery of a comprehensive communication and engagement plan.The Inclusion Development Team (IDT) currently consists of one Grade 7 officer and one Deputy Principal.  It is tasked with advancing the Inclusion Framework being developed in conjunction with the European Agency for Special Needs and Inclusive Education (EASNIE). The Directorate is supported by 0.5 NISRA statistician.Currently there are seven vacancies within SRIDD:one Executive Officer 1 and one Administrative Officer within SRDT; one Grade 7 and two Staff Officers within SRST;one Staff Officer and one Executive Officer 2 within IDT.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 33047/22-27, to detail (i) the amount of funding secured to progress early actions; and (ii) a breakdown of how the funding secured has been allocated to specific actions, in the SEN Reform Agenda and Delivery Plan.
answer_text: Additional investment of £31.5m has been secured to progress the early actions within the Special Educational Needs (SEN) Reform Delivery Plan.  This includes £27.5m from the Public Sector Transformation Board (PTSB) and £4m from the Early Learning & Childcare (ELC) budget.Detailed costings for specific actions funded from the PTSB are not planned for publication as they are indicative and subject to change as projects progress, with some dependent on final proposals from delivery partners.  Nonetheless, full transparency and accountability regarding expenditure will be maintained.The £4m secured to date from the ELC budget has been allocated to the Pre-school Education Inclusion Support Fund (PEISF) which delivers on the following action within the SEN Reform Delivery Plan: Resource pre-school education settings to support children undergoing statutory assessment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the rational behind the October 2025 report Review of Population Health Needs of Children with Complex Medical Needs Attending Special Education Needs Schools in Northern Ireland, focusing primarily on nursing provision and not considering more fully the role of other members of the multidisciplinary team, including occupational therapists, physiotherapists, and speech and language therapists.
answer_text: In October 2024, you brought a delegation of four Principals of SEN schools to meet with me and the Education Minister to discuss their concerns around the potential withdrawal of on-site nursing staff from their schools. The Principals’ view was that children with significant healthcare needs can most safely access the curriculum in a school environment where there is on-site Community Children’s Nursing (CCN) in the school.To address those specific concerns, the Chief Nursing Officer commissioned the Public Health Agency (PHA) to scope the population health needs of pupils attending special schools in Northern Ireland. The objective was to better understand the nursing care needs of this population in order to make recommendations on future service provision.Following the publication of the PHA’s report last month, officials in my Department have commenced work alongside colleagues from the Department of Education and other key stakeholders, to consider and progress its recommendations, and ensure that all children in SEN schools have access to necessary healthcare.The PHA is also leading a separate comprehensive analysis to identify what the assessed therapeutic needs are for children and young people who attend Special School to inform the development of an effective model of therapy provision and associated workforce development and planning. This will include a comprehensive scoping exercise to capture the assessed needs of children and young people in the Special School sector in respect of Occupational Therapy, Orthoptics, Physiotherapy and Speech and Language Therapy. I expect to receive the completed review, with recommendations, by March 2026.Taken together, these reports and resulting recommendations will seek to ensure the best outcomes for all children in SEN schools, enabling them to access the curriculum in a school setting while receiving the right care at the right time by the right person and in the right place.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister how they will ensure a cross-departmental and strategic approach to the Programme for Government 2024-2027 commitment to improve pathways to appropriate opportunities and improve protections for young people with special educational needs when they leave school.
answer_text: The provision of Better Support for Children and Young People with Special Educational Needs is a Programme for Government priority for the Executive. The Department for Education, Department for the Economy, Department of Health and the Department for Communities are working together to ensure pathways are available and young people with special educational needs are supported in the last years of school to prepare for the transition to adult life.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any support provided to parents in order to improve a child’s school attendance, following prosecution for non-attendance.
answer_text: An Education Welfare Officer continues to work with pupils and their families after Court Action in a constructive manner to support the achievement of an acceptable level of school attendance. If progress is not made and maintained, then consideration is given to the need for further legal action being taken by the Education Authority. Cases are reviewed by the Education Welfare Service (EWS) during professional supervision and at EWS Complex Case Panels.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 31022/22-27, to detail (i) the type of case he has referenced; (ii) which body, or statutory agency, has made this case; (iii) to whom the case has been made; and (iiii) any financial implications that have been considered as part of this case.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that:An assessment of staffing pressures across the Local IMPACT Team (LIT) network and associated recruitment requirements was submitted to senior management within the EA and the department. This included an estimated additional funding requirement of approximately £3m per annum My department submitted a bid for the SEN Reform Programme as part of the recent Budget 2026 -2029/30 process, this included additional funding for LITs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any engagement that takes place with pupils, following a prosecution for school non-attendance.
answer_text: An Education Welfare Officer continues to work with pupils and their families after Court Action in a constructive manner to support the achievement of an acceptable level of school attendance. If progress is not made and maintained, then consideration is given to the need for further legal action being taken by the Education Authority. Cases are reviewed by the Education Welfare Service (EWS) during professional supervision and at EWS Complex Case Panels.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail who is responsible for the oversight of any decision to prosecute for school non-attendance.
answer_text: Local Education Welfare Service Court Panels consider cases to determine if legal proceedings are necessary to ensure that the child is educated. Oversight of each case is provided by a senior member of the Education Welfare Service.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 32809/22-27, to detail the 13 early priority actions he is referring to.
answer_text: The 13 early priority actions being progressed with the £27.5m secured from the Public Sector Transformation Fund (PSTF) are:Transforming the support model.Establish and deliver a pilot early childhood SEN intervention programme which includes:A parental engagement programme;A programme for 2-3-year-olds with emerging special educational needs (SEN);Creation of early intervention/ assessment hubs;In-school support model for children in pre-school and foundation stage;Introduction of SEN specific training for assistants in pre-school and foundation stage.Establish and deliver a pilot speech, language and communication intervention toolkit and programme.Transform inclusive play environments.Early Intervention Enhanced Nurture Programme.Establish Special Schools as Resource Hubs/Centres of Expertise.Design and trial a Teacher & Classroom Assistant Professional Learning Programme to support Inclusive Education.Design and trial an Inclusive Capacity Building Programme.‘Transforming the statementing process’ is also being progressed as an early action and was included as one of the 13 projects under the PSTF but is now being funded under the Education Authority (EA) Connect programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 31810/22-27, to detail how he will ensure the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust helpline will be fully staffed and accessible to patients.
answer_text: As outlined in my previous response, services for people with multiple sclerosis (MS) are provided as part of wider Neurology services in each Trust area. There has been detailed engagement with the clinical teams in each Trust over the past number of years with a view to improving the care and treatment available for people with this condition. As part of this engagement, a Service Specification was developed, consistent with NICE Clinical Guidelines for the service, which sets out the requirements for delivering a multi-disciplinary service consistently across the region. The priorities that were identified from the Service Specification were for all Trusts to have dedicated clinics for people with MS, for each Trust to have dedicated MS nursing staff and for the capacity to be available in each Trust to allow infusion delivered disease modifying treatments (DMTs) to be available locally.Investments were made over a number of years and each of these priority objectives have been achieved. My officials work closely with HSC Trusts each year to consider the requirements associated with the increasing number of people with MS who are on DMTs. Over £20m is invested across the region in supporting the provision of approved drug treatments to eligible patients. As part of the arrangement in place for people with MS to access services locally, each of the five Trusts have advice lines for patients under their care to make contact. It is important to note that these advice lines are not an emergency service. People with MS are advised to liaise with their GP, out of hours service or Emergency Department when the advice line is closed and always when the issue is non-MS related.As services for people with MS have been established locally, support has been provided to allow Trusts to allow patients to make contact between hospital appointments if they have any queries regarding their MS care. The arrangements for receiving and responding to calls will be Trust specific but generally involve administrative staff accepting the initial call or managing messages left for the clinical team and then arranging for the contact to be made with the patient. The discussions that take place with Trusts each year to consider the requirements associated with increasing numbers of patients with MS will take account of any further support that would be required to ensure timely advice and support is available. Expansion of the arrangements for providing advice and communicating with patients has not been highlighted as an area of pressure to date and any investment in this or other areas of pressure related to service provision will be subject to resources being available in the context of the current financial pressures facing the HSC.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail why RISE NI is not supporting children placed in specialist provisions in mainstream schools.
answer_text: The Regional Integrated Support for Education (RISE) teams received funding across the 5 Trusts in 2007 as part of the Children & Young People’s Package 2006-2008, following a report from Peter Hain, to tackle inequalities and address barriers to learning. The RISE teams are a multi-professional health service that support all mainstream pre-school and primary educational settings in Northern Ireland up to and including children enrolled in Key Stage 1 (P4). These teams deliver a transdisciplinary model of support, involving Speech and Language Therapists, Occupational Therapists, Physiotherapists, Behavioural Therapists, Clinical Psychologists and Therapy Assistants. This service works directly in the mainstream school environment to support the holistic needs of children by promoting a positive and integrated service around the needs of the child, family and school. The RISE service aims to achieve the best outcomes for children, parents, teachers and schools through the delivery of a tiered model which includes Universal, Targeted and Specialist support. This support has been shown to be effective in improving outcomes for children, young people, families and educational settings. The teams work closely with schools, the children and their families in a preventative manner, to assist in addressing needs which are likely to impact on the child’s learning and access to the educational curriculum. The RISE model has two key aims:To reduce educational underachievement by optimising children’saccess to learning within the educational environment.To foster health, wellbeing and social inclusion and improve the life chances of children.Given the preventative focus adopted by RISE from the outset, it was agreed that this service would operate within the mainstream classes - both in the nursery and primary school sectors. The range of Specialist Provisions within mainstream schools (SPIMS) had and continue to have access to the diverse range of RISE training and strategies developed at a universal level. Children attending SPIMS who have a Statement of Special Educational Needs (SEN), and who require specialist support from health and social care practitioners are predominantly supported by core community services. This approach to SPIMS was deemed most appropriate at the time RISE was first established to ensure the wide range of health and social care needs were met that existed in the child’s home and school environment, and as such required very close working with the child’s parents/carers. In addition to the initial funding provided to establish RISE in 2007, non-recurrent funding was provided by DE to the 5 Trusts under their Emotional Health and Wellbeing in Education Framework to extend RISE into mainstream classes Key Stage 2 (KS2) - i.e. from Primary 5 - 7. This expansion aligned with the overarching principles of the pre-existing RISE programme, however, had a specific focus on supporting children with established social and emotional b

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of children with special educational needs, whose parents or carers have been prosecuted for school non-attendance.
answer_text: I have been advised that the Education Authority’s (EA) Education Welfare Service (EWS) does not hold this data or breakdown cases based on special educational needs (SEN).  A case is taken against a Parent/Carer based on the level of co-operation.  Under Article 45 of the Education and Libraries (Northern Ireland) Order 1986, it is the duty of parents who have a child of compulsory school age to ensure their child receives efficient, full-time education suitable to their age, aptitude, ability and to any special educational needs they may have.Paragraph 3 (1) of Schedule 13 to the 1986 Order states that it is the duty of a parent of a registered pupil of a school to secure their regular attendance at school. This applies to all children of compulsory school age who are on the roll of a school. Parents are legally responsible for making this happen.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of meetings that have taken place between him, and the Minister of Health since the restoration of devolution.
answer_text: Since the restoration of devolution, I have held four meetings with the Minister of Health. However, this figure does not fully reflect the breadth of our engagement. In addition to these meetings, we have interacted regularly through Executive meetings and at joint events, such as our recent visit to Forthill Integrated College.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of cases in which, following a prosecution for school non-attendance, school attendance has improved.
answer_text: Collection of this data commenced in September 2025 as part of the Education Welfare Service Transformation Programme and will be included in the service data set for Term One of this academic year. Information is expected to be available in February 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail how he is addressing the shortage of one to one support workers for adults with special educational needs.
answer_text: I have publicly acknowledged that our workforce represents the Health Service's greatest asset. Whilst I am committed to finding solutions to the challenges faced within the social care workforce and am mindful of how staff shortages impact people with Learning Disabilities I would observe that the ability of my Department to fully address workforce challenges within health and social care is dependent on the financial resource made available. My Department is addressing the social care workforce shortage through a combination of a newly launched 10-year strategy, a system-wide reset plan, and ongoing negotiations to resolve pay disputes.The Social Care Workforce Strategy 2025-2035, sets out seven priorities to develop a skilled, motivated, and valued social care workforce including support workers equipped with the necessary skills to meet the needs of people with Learning Disabilities.  The strategy focuses on career pathways, enhancing professional identity, recruitment and retention and learning and development.In addition, the Health and Social Care Reset Plan, contains measures to counter the unprecedented financial pressures which we currently face and to reform the Health and Social Care system. Whilst the Reset Plan adopts a focus upon the entire system, the Plan also has direct bearing upon workforce challenges including a focus on reduction of staff absence, limiting reliance on agency staff and stabilising the permanent workforce.In October 2025, the Executive allocated £100 million towards this year's healthcare pay deal. I have publicly stated that this only covers half the shortfall, and my Department is still working to find the remaining funds. Pay parity is a critical consideration in enabling my Department to deliver services, particularly to those people whose needs are most complex and who rely upon the availability of a skilled and reliable workforce.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any planning taking place to ensure that there will be adequate provision for adults with special educational needs, given the increased number of children with special educational needs in our schools.
answer_text: The requirement for one to one support would not exclude a person with Learning Disabilities from accessing Adult Centre provision. The ratio of staff provided within an Adult Centre environment is assessed by the appropriate Health and Social Care Trust and staff are provided accordingly.  If there is a required need for one to one nursing or medical support at all times, a holistic assessment of need would be completed by the Health and Social Care Trust and appropriate supports put in place to enable attendance at an Adult Centre or a bespoke arrangement would be put in place to provide meaningful day activity. Self- Directed Support is also available to provide flexibility and choice in how social care services are delivered to meet assessed need. Self-Directed Support could be utilised to introduce flexible provision around any required medical or nursing intervention. The Learning Disability Service Model is a proposed three-year plan to transform Learning Disability Services in Northern Ireland and is currently out for public consultation. The Model aims to better plan and commission services to meet the needs of the Learning Disability population, with consideration of the increased number of children with complex needs transitioning from special education. The model outlines 6 key ambitions, including Meaningful Lives and Citizenship, which includes plans to develop and expand Day Services. The aim is to offer people, including those with complex needschoice and flexibility in respect of meaningful day activity, The ability to deliver on the plans outlined within the Model will be dependent on the financial resource made available to my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail how the new Learning Disability Service Model will work alongside the SEN Reform Agenda and Delivery Plan
answer_text: The SEN Reform Delivery Plan commits to a Transitional Support Programme at various stages of a learner’s journey. This includes cross-departmental working to develop appropriate pathways into adulthood for school leavers with SEN. Health and Social Care will have a more active role for children with SEN that also have a learning disability. It is recognised that the wider SEN population will likely transition into a range of non-HSC services, such as further education and supported employment. My Department is currently consulting on the draft Learning Disability Service Model (LDSM). The Model proposes a new approach to supporting adults with a learning disability through Health and Social Care services in Northern Ireland. It sets out the key outcomes we want to achieve, the principles that will guide delivery, and the actions needed to improve services across the system.The 5-year SEN Delivery Plan aligns to the LDSM on areas including:Person-centred approachTransitions from school into adulthood.Improved inter-agency working, collaboration and sharing best practice to support those with disabilities.Expansion of community-based Day Opportunities. The LDSM has several key actions to improving transitions over the next 3 years. One of the key ambitions within the service model is for people supported with learning disabilities, who need specialised health and social care, to receive a lifelong, coordinated transitions program tailored to their needs.A final draft of the Transitions Protocol has been completed including an Easy Read version and following a pilot, a new database for tracking young people transitioning to adult services has been rolled out across all regions.  There are also plans within the model to introduce a Transition Link Worker within each Health and Social Care Trust, to support quality and robustness in this area of work.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail what support his Department can provide to an adult with special educational needs who is unable to attend day centre provision due to requiring one to one support at all times.
answer_text: The requirement for one to one support would not exclude a person with Learning Disabilities from accessing Adult Centre provision. The ratio of staff provided within an Adult Centre environment is assessed by the appropriate Health and Social Care Trust and staff are provided accordingly.  If there is a required need for one to one nursing or medical support at all times, a holistic assessment of need would be completed by the Health and Social Care Trust and appropriate supports put in place to enable attendance at an Adult Centre or a bespoke arrangement would be put in place to provide meaningful day activity. Self- Directed Support is also available to provide flexibility and choice in how social care services are delivered to meet assessed need. Self-Directed Support could be utilised to introduce flexible provision around any required medical or nursing intervention. The Learning Disability Service Model is a proposed three-year plan to transform Learning Disability Services in Northern Ireland and is currently out for public consultation. The Model aims to better plan and commission services to meet the needs of the Learning Disability population, with consideration of the increased number of children with complex needs transitioning from special education. The model outlines 6 key ambitions, including Meaningful Lives and Citizenship, which includes plans to develop and expand Day Services. The aim is to offer people, including those with complex needschoice and flexibility in respect of meaningful day activity, The ability to deliver on the plans outlined within the Model will be dependent on the financial resource made available to my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail what level of (i) investment; and (iI) co-operation has been committed in the current school year to the SEN Reform Agenda, particularly in relation to (a) the development of multi-agency support for schools; and (b) the provision of Allied Health Professional input for pupils with speech, language and communication needs in mainstream schools.
answer_text: (i) In 2022, my predecessor approved an additional £2.5m recurrent funding to address pressures on Allied Health Professionals (AHP). This enabled greater collaboration between Health and Education to ensure that AHP provision was reflected in the current Reform Agenda and Delivery Plan. Given the level of financial pressures currently facing my Department it is challenging to further increase funding in this current school year. I am unable to confirm allocations for the next financial year until the outcome of the 2026/27 budget is known.(ii) My Department is committed to the SEN Reform Agenda and is engaged with DE through a number of structures as set out in the Governance Framework including the Central Strategic Committee, Operational Implementation Group, Advisory Forum and Data and Monitoring Hub. This collaboration includes input from senior Allied Health Professionals from my Department and the Public Health Agency.At a service level, the Regional Integrated Support for Education (RISE) NI service is a multi-professional early intervention team that works in all education funded mainstream primary schools and nurseries up until Primary 4 funded through the Children and Young People’s Package. The RISE teams include speech and language therapists, alongside occupational therapists, physiotherapists, behaviour therapists, clinical psychologists and therapy assistants who work in a transdisciplinary manner delivering a tiered model of support to proactively address barriers to learning.A range of supports are provided by RISE staff who work directly in the mainstream school setting supporting children and young people with speech language and communication; physical, sensory and perceptual; and social emotional and behavioural needs to assist them to develop, achieve and learn. Work continues across Health with the Education Authority to develop collaborative working between RISE and the newly established EA Local IMPACT teams who are currently implementing and refining their model of support for schools, which are located around Trust boundaries.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the date by which schools must publish their 2026-27 academic year prospectus, including details of their school uniform policy; and (ii) when the guidelines, arising from the School Uniforms (Guidelines and Allowances) Bill, will be published and made available to schools, to ensure they are reflected in their updated school uniform policies.
answer_text: The Education (School Information and Prospectuses) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2003 require a grant-aided primary or post-primary school to publish their prospectus not later than six weeks before the date parents express their preference for a school.  This includes information on recommendations and rules relating to the wearing of school uniform.Parents express their preference for a school via the online admissions portal managed by the Education Authority (EA).  The EA’s website advises that the primary school portal opens 7 January 2026, closing 21 January 2026.  Therefore, the primary school prospectuses need to be published by 26 November 2025, stretching to 10 December 2025 based on the closing date.  The post-primary school portal opens 27 January 2026, closing 19 February 2026.  Therefore, the post-primary school prospectuses need to be published by 16 December 2025, stretching to 8 January 2026 based on the closing date. As I stated at Final Stage of the School Uniforms (Guidelines and Allowances) Bill on 13 October 2025, the guidelines will be made available to schools in the coming weeks.  They will have statutory effect on the day after the Bill gains Royal Assent therefore be in operation for the 2026/27 school year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any assistance offered to families who receive Self-Directed Support to ensure they can find appropriate support.
answer_text: Children’s Community Services:Self-directed support is offered to families following an assessment of need and presentation at the Trust Allocation and Resource Panel. Within this assessment, when support is being identified by a social worker, advice and guidance is given to parents in relation to types of support that may be beneficial or appropriate for their family. This can include, for example, local service provision including after schools, day short breaks, clubs and/or activities for the child/young person. If the Allocation and Resource Panel approve an assessment of need for use of a personal assistant, the family will need to identify a person within their network of support – this can be family, friends or within their professional network. Social Workers do provide advice in this regard; however, the ethos of Self-Directed Support is about giving people control of the support they receive and empowering families to have more autonomy within their lives. Adult, Community, Older People’s Services & Allied Health Professionals; Mental Health, Intellectual Disability & Psychological Services:Self-Directed Support is available to people who have been assessed as needing social care support.  It offers choice, control and flexibility to individuals and families so that they can: Tailor a package of support that suits their lifestyle.Have an informed choice about how their support is provided.Control their personal budget.As part of the process of Care and Support Planning, a person’s social worker or key worker will work with them to identify the best way for them to receive their care.  This can include Trust arranged services, Direct Payments or a combination of options.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the number of seven seater vehicles purchased by the Education Authority for home to school transport; (ii) the cost of these vehicles; and (iii) the number of seven seater vehicles currently in operation.
answer_text: The Education Authority has advised that it has purchased 11 seven-seater vehicles to support the delivery of home to school transport at a cost of £402,967.Two of these vehicles are currently in operation with another two due to start delivering transport in the coming weeks.  The EA informs me that the delay in the full deployment of these vehicles is due to challenges faced in recruiting drivers.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any work he is doing to expand day centre provision for adults with special educational needs.
answer_text: The Learning Disability Service Model is currently out for public consultation. Within this model my Department has proposed the extension of operating hours of Adult Centres for adults with Learning Disabilities to optimise existing capital resource.Due to the urgent need to increase Adult Centre capacity, a pilot of extended operating hours is currently underway in 1 Trust. There is a plan to upscale this transformative work across all 5 Trusts, aligned to the Learning Disability Service Model. However, this will be dependent on the financial resource made available to my Department and workforce availability, due to the need for investment in staff to facilitate this work.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether changes to the Teaching Appointments Scheme for Controlled Schools requires legislation before being enacted.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) is carrying out a review of the Teaching Appointment Scheme for Controlled Schools and consultation with controlled schools, governors and wider stakeholders is on-going.  My Department has been advised that EA expect the work on a revised scheme to be completed early in 2026.As the EA consultation process is ongoing and no decisions have been made it is not clear if there will be any proposed amendments to the scheme which will require legislative changes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on work undertaken by his Department on post-19 provision for adults with special educational needs.
answer_text: It is important to recognise that special educational needs relates to services provided for children and young people. A cohort of children with SEN with be known to HSC children’s disability teams, who will likely transition into an adult HSC service. The Department of Health is currently consulting on the draft Learning Disability Service Model. The Model proposes a new approach to supporting adults with a learning disability through Health and Social Care services in Northern Ireland. It sets out the key outcomes we want to achieve, the principles that will guide delivery, and the actions needed to improve services across the system.The draft model sets out a regionally consistent framework to improve outcomes across several HSC areas, including: (i) Transitions; (ii) Health & Wellbeing; (iii) Day Services and Meaningful Activity; (iv) Support for Families and Carers; (v) Home and Independent Living; and (vi) Mental Health and Behaviours of Concern.A key ambition within the draft Learning Disability Service Model is to expand and improve access to short breaks and greater support for families and carers. My Department remains committed to working with HSC Trusts and other agencies to offer short break provision to adults with a learning disability and to offer support to their families/carers.My Department is also leading on the reform of Adult Social Care through the Social Care Collaborative Forum (SCCF) on how to support best use of current provision and expansion of placement provision within care home settings from a residential and nursing care perspective - this will assist with the provision of short breaks to support individuals and their carers. The SCCF Supported Living workstream is also developing an overall vision for supported living in Northern Ireland which aligns to the LDSM ambition for adults with a Learning Disability to be supported to live with the highest level of independence possible.Recognising that improved access and support for adults is required, a review of Adult Autism Services was also commissioned by the Department in 2023. In response to the findings, the post of Adult Autism Commissioning Lead was established.  A project lead was subsequently appointed and in February 2025 took up post in my Department’s Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG).  The project lead’s focus is to better understand the population, pathways, presenting need and to further develop supports and services across the region.On 2 July 25, SPPG convened a regional workshop bringing Trust colleagues together with autistic adults to review current adult autism diagnostic and support pathways and identify steps that can be taken collectively within a financially constrained environment to manage growing waiting lists and improve access to support and services.An initial action plan was co-developed during the workshop and a regional Strategy and Planning group has been established to take this work forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) any work (a) being undertaken; or (b) planned to be undertaken, this year within the Design and Trial an Inclusive Capacity Building Programme, Special Educational Needs Transformation proposal; and (ii) the project deliverables.
answer_text: The Inclusive Capacity Building Programme is at the advanced design stage following staff recruitment.  This trial is scheduled to commence in February 2026.The Inclusion Capacity Building Programme is a whole-system initiative that aims to ensure every child and young person in Northern Ireland experiences equitable, high-quality education.  It will comprise of resources, professional development in inclusive teaching methodologies and design of inclusive school environments.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of whether the Executive Office should lead on coordinating reform to post-19 special educational needs provision, given the cross-cutting nature of this work.
answer_text: Special Educational Needs (SEN) transitions is a significant cross-cutting issue across the Executive. Education leads on SEN transformation which includes commitments on transitions, and Economy has undertaken significant work on post-19 SEN support. Health and Social Care plays a significant role in supporting children with SEN that have a learning disability, within services for children with disabilities and adult learning disability. Effective transitions and next steps for people with SEN requires a whole government approach. It is critical that we support children with SEN to have the same choices and opportunities as everyone else to further their education, seek employment and live in their home. It is recognised that effective transitions management is ultimately dependent on the availability of appropriate resources, and I am committed to close working with other Departments to improve the provision and options available to all young people.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail how data collection is used to inform provision and services required for adults with special educational needs.
answer_text: My Department receives annual Statutory Function Reports from each Health and Social Care Trust. Within each report there are specific data requirements for adults with Learning Disabilities to demonstrate how statutory functions are being met. My Department uses this information to hold HSC Trusts to account for the discharge of their statutory functions and to plan and commission services accordingly.  In addition, implementation of the Learning Disability Service Model will involve the introduction of a new Regional governance framework. Regional data associated with key performance indicators for people with Learning Disabilities will be routinely collated, monitored and analysed for the purpose of planning and performance by my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any action he is taking to ensure data relating to adults with special educational needs is collected consistently across all Health and Social Care Trusts.
answer_text: My Department has drafted a Transitions Protocol aligned to the Learning Disability Service Model, which is currently out for public consultation. Within the protocol all HSC Trusts are required to complete a standardised database with regards to people transitioning out of special education, into Adult Learning Disability Services. Collection of this information commenced in April 2025 and will enable planning up to 5 years in advance of a person’s transition. In addition, implementation of the Learning Disability Service Model will involve the introduction of a new Regional governance framework. Regional data associated with key performance indicators for people with Learning Disabilities will be routinely collated, monitored and analysed for the purpose of planning and performance by my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on work within his Department to establish a regional policy and specification for the future supply of wigs.
answer_text: I recognise that hair loss resulting from a medical condition or treatment can be both physically and emotionally distressing for patients, and having access to a good quality wig is an important part of their recovery and wellbeing.The provision of wigs is managed by each Health and Social Care Trust in line with their responsibilities for ensuring that patients have access to appropriate medical devices in line with their clinical needs, and that contracts with suppliers deliver safety, quality and value for money. This involves developing appropriate specifications and procuring the necessary products and services required to fulfil these responsibilities. As such, my Department does not set policy or specifications for individual medical devices, rather it is the case that these Trust processes are guided by relevant procurement and effective use of resources policies. However, I am aware that Trusts have encountered challenges in developing a specification for wigs which has made it difficult to progress a regional tender and has led to alternative approaches being considered in recent years. These challenges include variation in patient needs, differences in supplier offerings, and the absence of a recognised framework or standard for quality and value of wigs.  Despite this, I understand that Trusts are currently exploring options for a regional solution that would allow them to approach the market collectively. This is aimed at securing provision that is high quality, equitable, and value for public money. My Department will continue to encourage and support this work as required.In the meantime, each Trust maintains its own arrangements for wig provision through contractual arrangements with existing wig providers to ensure patients continue to receive appropriate support.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether the further withdrawal of nursing provision from special schools will be halted, as further work continues on a comprehensive multi-agency study, following the publication of the Review of Population Health Needs of Children with Complex Medical Needs Attending Special Schools in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: All Special Education Needs (SEN) schools across Northern Ireland have appropriate nursing provision available to manage the identified healthcare needs of the pupils attending them.  While the service delivery model can differ across the region, and has been amended in at least one school recently, no nursing provision has been withdrawn and there are no plans to do so.My Department is set to commence work, alongside colleagues from Education and other key stakeholders, to progress the recommendations contained in the Review of Population Health Needs of Children with Complex Medical Needs Attending Special Schools in Northern Ireland.  This will seek to ensure the best outcomes for all these children, to enable them to access the curriculum in a school setting while receiving the right care at the right time by the right person and in the right place.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) any work (a) being undertaken; or (b) planned to be undertaken, this year under the heading Trial and implementation of a Speech, Language & Communication Programme including the introduction of Toolkits, within the SEN Reform Agenda and Delivery Plan 2025-2030; and (ii) what will be delivered by this project.
answer_text: The ‘Trial and Implementation of a Speech, Language & Communication Programme including the introduction of Toolkits’ is an early priority action in the Special Educational Needs (SEN) Reform Delivery Plan and one of 13 projects funded by the Public Sector Transformation Fund (PSTF). 	This initiative will deliver a range of digitised resources and screeners for schools to support children’s speech, language and communication development and help them to identify any needs in this area.  		Officials are engaging with the Education Authority (EA) who have developed proposals for this project, which is expected to commence in term 2 of the 2025/26 academic year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) any work (a) being undertaken; or (b) planned to be undertaken, this year under the heading Trial and Implementation of Special Schools as Resource Hubs/Centres of Expertise to support inclusion, within the SEN Reform Agenda and Delivery Plan 2025-2030; and (ii) what will be delivered by this project.
answer_text: The ‘Trial and Implementation of Special Schools as Resource Hubs/Centres of Expertise’ is an early priority action in the special educational needs (SEN) Reform Delivery Plan and one of 13 projects funded by the Public Sector Transformation Fund (PSTF). 	This initiative will test the effectiveness of establishing Special Schools as ‘Resource Hubs’ providing support to local mainstream schools in supporting children with SEN via a ‘hub and spoke’ model, sharing best practice, in-class modelling and advice.Engagement with Special Schools is ongoing and the selection of both special schools and mainstream schools to participate in this project will take place in November 2025.  Following school selection, planning and recruitment of staff will take place in preparation to ‘go live’ in early 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) any work (a) being undertaken; or (b) planned to be undertaken, this year under the heading Trial and implementation of an Inclusive Play Programme approach, within the SEN Reform Agenda and Delivery Plan 2025-2030; and (ii) what will be delivered by this project.
answer_text: The Inclusive Play Programme is at the final design stage.  Officials are engaging with Playboard, our delivery partner, to refine the programme which is expected to commence in January 2026.The Inclusive Play Programme will provide a whole school intervention, that will embed understanding of inclusive play alongside play-based approaches across the school environment in terms of pedagogy, pupil relations, spaces, policy and connections into families.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) any work (a) being undertaken; or (b) planned to be undertaken, this year under the heading Design and implementation of a Professional Learning Programme for teaching staff, within the SEN Reform Agenda and Delivery Plan 2025-2030; and (ii) what will be delivered by this project.
answer_text: The Department of Education and Education Authority are currently progressing work in this area. This project will facilitate the design and implementation of a Teacher Professional Learning (TPL) SEN Framework promoting effective professional development for teaching staff.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) any work (a) being undertaken; or (b) planned to be undertaken, this year under the heading Resetting the statutory assessment and annual review process and support model, within the SEN Reform Agenda and Delivery Plan 2025-2030; and (ii) what will be delivered by this project.
answer_text: The Department of Education and Education Authority are currently progressing work in this area. This project will deliver the actions within the SEN Reform Delivery Plan as outlined under the theme of Statutory Assessment and Annual Review Process and support model.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the current roles within each of the Education Authority's local IMPACT teams, including a breakdown of (i) each specific job role; (ii) the number of each role within a team; and (iii) current vacancies for these roles.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that:Each school age Local Impact Team (LIT) needs to be able to deploy Teacher grade specialist staff across each of the following specialisms: Cognition & Learning (C&L), Language & Communication (L&C), Autistic Spectrum Disorder (ASD), Post-Primary Social Behavioural and Emotional Wellbeing (PPSBEW) and Primary Social Behavioural and Emotional Wellbeing (PPSBEW). Each Early Years Local IMPACT Teams needs to be able to deploy at least four Full Time Equivalent (FTE) Early Years specialist Teachers.The formation of the 28 LITs had to respect the existing contractual terms and conditions of the staff coming from eight different services placing some limitations on the geographic area which staff could work across. As a result, the number of each job role within a LIT currently varies.  The below table details the distribution of existing Teacher grade staff by specialism across all 28 LITs as of 30 September 2025. question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the number of established specialist provisions in mainstream schools; and (ii) the number of these provisions that have all places filled.
answer_text: Specialist Provision classes are needs based and capacity measurements will be more complex than in a mainstream setting. The Education Authority (EA) works closely with schools to ensure that children with special educational needs (SEN) are placed in the most appropriate provision to meet their individual needs.As of 10th October 2025, there are 273 schools with operational specialist provisions across the education estate. 140 schools have specialist provision types where pupil numbers are aligned with or exceed the typical capacity range — reflecting a high level of demand and appropriate placement decisions. Of these, 75 schools have provision types that are currently operating at or above their usual capacity range.163 schools have provision types where pupil numbers are currently below the typical capacity range, which may reflect a change in pupil profile or a strategic decision to maintain smaller class sizes to support wellbeing and effective learning. Any remaining available capacity is used by schools for reverse integration to support pupils within mainstream and to ensure in year capacity is available for those who require a change of placement.SEN provision is not a “one size fits all” model. As pupil needs evolve, demand for certain types of provision may decrease in some areas while increasing in others. EA continues to respond to these changes to ensure equitable access to education across all council areas.Demand for SEN placement is not evenly spread across Northern Ireland. The areas where demand is highest include Greater Belfast, Armagh Banbridge & Craigavon and Newry, Mourne and Down Councils. Expanding specialist provision in these areas is an absolute priority as classes are frequently operating at or above capacity.EA remain committed to significantly increasing specialist SEN provision in mainstream schools. We want all young people with SEN to receive the best possible education in their own communities - in schools as close as possible to where they live.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) any work (a) being undertaken; or (b) planned to be undertaken, this year under the heading Transforming the support model for children with a statement of Special Educational Needs, within the SEN Reform Agenda and Delivery Plan 2025-2030; and (ii) what will be delivered by this project.
answer_text: The Department of Education and Education Authority are currently progressing work in this area. This project will transform the support model for children with a statement of Special Educational Needs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) any work (a) being undertaken; or (b) planned to be undertaken, this year under the heading Establish and deliver pilot early childhood intervention programmes in the SEN Reform Agenda and Delivery Plan 2025-2030; and (ii) what will be delivered by this project.
answer_text: The range of actions detailed under ‘Establish and deliver pilot early childhood intervention programmes’ in the Special Educational Needs (SEN) Reform Agenda and Delivery Plan 2025-2030 are at different stages of operationalisation.		A number of these actions will be funded by the Public Sector Transformation Board (PSTB) funding of £27.5m over the next 4 years.  Delivery of the following will commence in 2025/26: Engagement programme for parents of children with SEN age 0-4; a targeted programme for 2-3 year olds with emerging needs that will include support for parents and families; an Early Intervention and Assessment Centre approach for children aged 3-6 years; trial of in-school support to children in pre-school and foundation stage; bespoke training and development for SEN classroom assistants; and an Inclusive Play Programme.One of the actions ‘Address inequalities between statutory and non-statutory pre-school providers’ access to EPS and SEN support services’ requires significant additional investment; a bid for funding has been made.The final action under this theme ‘Resource pre-school education settings to support children undergoing statutory assessment’ was implemented in November 2024 with the establishment of the Pre-school Education Inclusion Support Fund (PSEISF).  This has delivered over £1m of funding support to pre-school education settings to date and will continue in 2025/26.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i)  to provide an update on his Department's work to establish a regional policy and specification for the future supply of wigs being undertaken alongside the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust; and (ii) to detail any engagement undertaken with the Procurement and Logistics Service as part of this review.

To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the number of schools that have been approached regarding the creation of a Specialist Provision in Mainstream Schools (SPiMs) for 2026-2027; and (ii) the number of schools that have agreed to taking on a new SPiMs for 2026-2027; and (iii) the number of these SPiMs currently being developed.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that:Circa 400 schools.53 schools have agreed and projects are progressing to establish provision for 26 or as soon as possible thereafter. In addition, there are over 120 site assessments progressing to explore feasibility on school sites to identify further opportunities to create capacity for 2026 placements. These have been identified through engagement with schools. All 53 schools identified in (ii) above are progressing as projects with anticipated delivery date for 2026. This delivery date is subject to BC approval, statutory approvals and contract award.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to provide an update on the publication of Statutory Guidance on the Reduction and Management of Restrictive Practices in Educational Settings.
answer_text: Draft guidance was shared with the Task and Finish Group and the Departmental Solicitor’s Office (DSO) on 7 July 2025. A range of feedback was received.  This has been used to further refine the draft guidance and officials are currently engaging with DSO with a view to finalising it for my consideration.Once I have considered the finalised draft guidance, I will determine the next steps to be taken thereafter, including a timescale for publication.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail how the Education Welfare Service informs eligible families that they can be supported with transport for displaced pupils.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised me that its Education Welfare Service advises parents about their children’s eligibility for transport when they are referred to the service, which is assessed in line with the Department of Education’s (DE) Transport policy.The DE transport policy outlines the criteria which the EA must use to determine eligibility criteria for transport assistance; however, the policy acknowledges that the application of the criteria in the general transport policy may not always be appropriate. This enables the EA to consider cases where transport may need to be provided in exceptional circumstances, which can include where a family is forced to relocate to a temporary address at short notice for reasons beyond their control.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW /22-27, to provide an update on the case referenced.
answer_text: There is no update at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether the Online Teacher Continued Professional Development Academy will be made available on an equitable basis to all teachers, regardless of their individual school budget position.
answer_text: The Department appointed InnerDrive through a Direct Award Contract to deliver the online Continued Professional Development (CPD) Academy for post-primary schools. All required public sector procedures were followed and the contract was awarded on behalf of my Department by Construction and Procurement Delivery (CPD) within the Department of Finance. The online CPD Academy was made available on an equitable basis, with every post primary school invited to participate through an expression of interest process, irrespective of their individual budget position. Schools that accepted this offer have full access to the programme at no cost, ensuring that budgetary constraints did not restrict participation.  This is the only commercial contract awarded to date by the Department for the delivery of CPD programmes associated with TransformED NI.A competitive tendering process will shortly be commenced for an online science of learning development programme for all primary schools. The £31m TransformED Teacher Professional Learning (TPL) Fund gives schools flexibility by allowing them to choose professional development activities that best match their specific needs and priorities. Schools can use the funding for a wide range of options including accredited courses, coaching, mentoring and collaborative learning. This autonomy supports tailored, high-impact learning aligned with each school’s context and improvement goals.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline how he is ensuring that all Health and Social Care Trusts are providing the best possible service to patients requiring wigs at this time.

To ask the Minister of Education to outline any specific support being made available by (i) the Education Welfare Service; and (ii) schools, to children identified as experiencing hidden homelessness.
answer_text: My Department, in partnership with the Education Authority (EA) and the Council for the Curriculum, Examinations and Assessment (CCEA), is committed to enhancing support for children and young people experiencing all forms of homelessness.  This includes the development and promotion of targeted educational resources, teacher training, youth engagement initiatives, and direct support through the Education Welfare Service (EWS). Schools and the EWS collaborate to identify and support pupils experiencing hidden homelessness. Schools are encouraged to liaise with EA and EWS when a pupil’s housing situation changes, to ensure timely support.The EWS works directly with affected families to support school attendance, continuity of education, and access to entitlements such as free school meals and uniform grants. The EWS assists with exam arrangements, including identifying suitable test centres and organising transport for displaced pupils. Where homelessness affects a child’s ability to sit entrance assessments (e.g. SEAG), the EWS supports applications for Special Circumstances or Special Provision.Supports available to schools include resources developed by CCEA on homelessness, financial capability, and wellbeing.  These resources are shared through newsletters, social media campaigns, and teacher support events. CCEA is currently evaluating these resources through a teacher questionnaire to inform future improvements.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the tender process followed to award any commercial contracts for the Online Teacher Continued Professional Development Academy, arising from TransformEd NI.
answer_text: The Department appointed InnerDrive through a Direct Award Contract to deliver the online Continued Professional Development (CPD) Academy for post-primary schools. All required public sector procedures were followed and the contract was awarded on behalf of my Department by Construction and Procurement Delivery (CPD) within the Department of Finance. The online CPD Academy was made available on an equitable basis, with every post primary school invited to participate through an expression of interest process, irrespective of their individual budget position. Schools that accepted this offer have full access to the programme at no cost, ensuring that budgetary constraints did not restrict participation.  This is the only commercial contract awarded to date by the Department for the delivery of CPD programmes associated with TransformED NI.A competitive tendering process will shortly be commenced for an online science of learning development programme for all primary schools. The £31m TransformED Teacher Professional Learning (TPL) Fund gives schools flexibility by allowing them to choose professional development activities that best match their specific needs and priorities. Schools can use the funding for a wide range of options including accredited courses, coaching, mentoring and collaborative learning. This autonomy supports tailored, high-impact learning aligned with each school’s context and improvement goals.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the companies awarded commercial contracts by his Department to deliver the continuous professional development programmes for teachers, associated with TransformEd NI.
answer_text: The Department appointed InnerDrive through a Direct Award Contract to deliver the online Continued Professional Development (CPD) Academy for post-primary schools. All required public sector procedures were followed and the contract was awarded on behalf of my Department by Construction and Procurement Delivery (CPD) within the Department of Finance. The online CPD Academy was made available on an equitable basis, with every post primary school invited to participate through an expression of interest process, irrespective of their individual budget position. Schools that accepted this offer have full access to the programme at no cost, ensuring that budgetary constraints did not restrict participation.  This is the only commercial contract awarded to date by the Department for the delivery of CPD programmes associated with TransformED NI.A competitive tendering process will shortly be commenced for an online science of learning development programme for all primary schools.
