The £31m TransformED Teacher Professional Learning (TPL) Fund gives schools flexibility by allowing them to choose professional development activities that best match their specific needs and priorities. Schools can use the funding for a wide range of options including accredited courses, coaching, mentoring and collaborative learning. This autonomy supports tailored, high-impact learning aligned with each school’s context and improvement goals.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any specific actions undertaken under the heading Investing in Classroom Assistants in the SEN Reform Agenda and Delivery Plan 2025-2030.
answer_text: Officials are currently working alongside a range of stakeholders to identify the necessary actions required to ensure effective investment in the classroom assistant workforce. This will involve improvements to the current employment model, development of training packages, and opportunities for career progression.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any work that has been undertaken to plan for post-16 specialist provision in mainstream schools, as committed to in the SEN Reform Agenda and Delivery Plan 2025-2030.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that:Planning for specialist provision for pupils with statements of Special Educational Needs (SEN) who attend mainstream schools requires consideration of the full pathway of learning from the early years through to the young person leaves school.EA is monitoring emerging policy around statutory provision in full time education for all young people to the age of 18 and is actively starting to implement actions of the SEN Reform Delivery Plan.Moving forward EA officers will work with schools to explore future options and develop place-based solutions for inclusive 16-18 provision in different parts of Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the outcome of any discussions with the Regional Procurement Board on the provision of wigs for patients.

To ask the Minister of Education to detail any formal data sharing arrangements between the Department of Health and the Department of Education, to inform planning for pre-school provision and placements, which have been established as set out in the SEN Reform Agenda and Delivery Plan 2025-2030.
answer_text: Engagement with the Education Authority is underway to establish joint requirements of the Department of Health to progress various workstreams including the sharing of early years health data to allow for better planning for SEN provision across the education estate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any work he is undertaking to address the lack of suitable accredited qualifications available, for young people moving from specialist provisions in mainstream schools into post-16 education in mainstream settings, as highlighted in the Education and Training Inspectorate's report on specialist provisions in mainstream schools (part 1), published in November 2024.
answer_text: The Education and Training Inspectorate (ETI) report’s findings, of November 2024, highlighted that ‘In all post-primary schools, a range of general and applied qualifications were available to all pupils at KS 4’ and noted that ‘the evidence shows that the individual pupils in specialist provisions achieve well in these accreditations.’ The report also found that in a significant minority of the post-primary schools, the curriculum offer at KS 4 was not sufficiently broad and balanced for the pupils; staff reported that they sometimes found it difficult to offer a range of suitable courses to the pupils in the specialist provisions due to a range of factors. These are cited as a lack of specialist teachers, funding and a similarity of skills developed across some of the courses offered.I am committed to ensuring that all young people with special educational needs are supported in their progression pathways.   Furthermore, my officials have initiated the first stage of a review of qualifications approved for teaching by schools in Northern Ireland to ensure that they remain relevant, fit for purpose, and meet the needs of all learners.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail when Clarawood School, Belfast, will be granted full access to the Willow Building, located on their site, for classroom provision.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised it is currently anticipated that Willow will leave the existing building at the Clarawood school site, allowing Clarawood to move into the ‘Willow Building’, following the successful installation of temporary rental accommodation at another local EA site. This is currently estimated with a target completion of November 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any work that is underway to support the publication and implementation of an Inclusive child-centred Integrated Workforce Planning Framework, as outlined in the SEN Reform Agenda and Delivery Plan 2025-2030.
answer_text: The publication and implementation of an Inclusive child-centred Integrated Workforce Planning Framework is planned for implementation in years three to five of the Special Educational Needs (SEN) Reform Delivery Plan.  Work on this action has not yet commenced. My department is committed to advancing this work at a later stage, subject to additional funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail which enabling actions from the SEN Reform Agenda and Delivery Plan 2025-2030 have been completed.
answer_text: The Special Educational Needs (SEN) Reform Agenda and Delivery Plan 2025-2030 enabling actions that have been completed are: a SEN Policy Statement has been published;the SEN Reform Outcomes Framework has been published, including the development of a range of population-level indicators;a SEN Reform Governance Framework has been agreed and inaugural meetings have been held for the Central Strategic Committee (CSC) and the Operational Implementation Oversight Group (OIOG).  Other groups within the structures, including a Lived Experience Forum, are in the process of being established;funding has been secured to commence implementation of early actions;the SEN Reform Delivery Unit has been established and largely staffed; the Business Case has been approved and implementation of funded initiatives is due to commence in Term 1 of the 2025/26 academic year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail when his Department will (i) produce a framework of support models for children with special educational needs; and (ii) communicate this to schools and stakeholders, as detailed in the SEN Reform Agenda and Delivery Plan 2025-2030.
answer_text: My department, in collaboration with the Education Authority, is progressing this work at pace.Communication with schools is due to commence shortly, followed by updates to all stakeholders across the lifetime of the project.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the implementation of (i) year 1; and (ii) year 2, actions included in the SEN Reform Agenda and Delivery Plan 2025-2030.
answer_text: There has been good progress made in the early and priority year one actions as follows:Digitisation of the statutory assessment and review process is underway with roll-out expected to commence in early 2026;The Education Authority’s (EA) Operational Plan 2 (OP2) was published in January 2025; Pre-school settings are receiving additional resource to support children undergoing statutory assessment from the Pre-school Education Inclusion Support Fund (PSEISF) established in November 2024, which will continue in 2025/26;The EA’s Graduated Response Framework was published in March 2025;The EA’s Local IMPACT Teams (LITs) are live from September 2025;The Education & Training Inspectorate (ETI) is expected to release Phase 2 of its review of specialist provisions by December 2025;The following projects will commence in 2025/26: Transforming the support model; pilot early intervention programme; Speech, Language & Communication programme; Inclusive Play programme; Special Schools as Resource Hubs; Inclusive education capacity-building programme; professional development in special educational needs (SEN) for teachers and classroom assistants.All other year one and future year actions will be implemented as and when additional funding is secured.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 30778/22-27, for her assessment of the progress made by the new councils, created in 2015, towards creating Local Development Plans.
answer_text: Councils in the North have made good progress developing their new Local Development Plans (LDPs). Nine out of the eleven councils here have published development plan documents, six of which, have adopted Plan Strategies and are working towards their Local Policies Plan publication. Council timetables indicate that the remaining two councils are due to publish draft Plan Strategy documents within the next year.The progress to date includes the significant work carried out by councils and stakeholders to progress preparation of LDP documents including the gathering of evidence, the consideration of complex environmental issues, or indeed the extensive engagement between my Department and councils to front load the system.I am committed to a Planning Improvement Programme and as part of this, my Department is working in collaboration with councils to identify measures to improve the effectiveness and efficiency of the LDP process. While this is ongoing, my officials continue to work closely with councils to support them with their LDP preparation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of pupils living in families with homelessness status who have been supported by the Education Welfare Service, in each of the last three years to (i) access suitable test centres for exams; and (ii) apply for special circumstances or special provision.
answer_text: Neither my Department nor The Education Authority holds a record of pupils living in families with homelessness status who have been supported by the Education Welfare Service (EWS). Not all children and Young people experiencing homelessness divulge their living arrangements.Any relevant information would likely be held by individual schools. If a pupil’s homelessness is identified, it may lead to a UNOCINI (Understanding the Needs of Children in Northern Ireland) referral or support through a Family Support Hub. Schools are responsible for helping pupils access test centres and for submitting applications for special circumstances or special provision.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of pupils living in families who have homelessness status, in each of the last three academic years that have been supported through the exceptional circumstances process to access transport to attend school as a result of being moved to temporary accommodation outside of the school catchment area.
answer_text: Neither my Department nor the Education Authority (EA) holds records of pupils living in families with homelessness status that have been supported through the exceptional circumstances process for transport assistance.  Not all children and Young people experiencing homelessness disclose their living arrangements. However, data is available for successful exceptional circumstances appeals where transport assistance was granted due to enforced relocation to temporary accommodation outside the school catchment area:	Summary of Successful Appeals:	Academic YearSuccessful CasesReasonNotes 2022/23 1 Intimidation- 2023/24 5 4 x Intimidation 1 x Property was Condemned by                Local council                                      	 4 out of the 5   	 successful cases were   	 siblings                                                   2024/25 1 Intimidation- 2025/26  year to date 0--

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he has provided a response to recommendations contained within The National Deaf Children's Society report Next Steps: Developing specialist education support for deaf children in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: My department has received a copy of the National Deaf Children's Society (NDCS) report Next Steps: Developing specialist education support for deaf children in Northern Ireland. I welcome the report and its contribution to our shared goal of improving outcomes for deaf children and young people and their families.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 30778/22-27, to detail why any of the new councils, created in 2015, have not completed Local Development Plans.
answer_text: All councils in the North are advancing their Local Development Plan programme. The pace of this programme is set by councils.To date nine out of the eleven councils have published development plan documents, six of which, have adopted Plan Strategies and are working towards their Local Policies Plan publication. Council timetables indicate that the remaining two councils are due to publish the first of their development plan documents within the next year.There are a number of reasons why councils have not completed both stages of their LDPs. Robust evidence is required to assist the formulation of sound policies, and this evidence gathering process takes time. In addition, at the transfer of planning functions, some councils also benefited from recently adopted Departmental Development Plans reducing the urgency to produce new plans. While LDPs are being prepared planning applications continue to be determined having regard to councils Plan Strategies where relevant and to any other material considerations including the existing departmental development plans and the provisions of the RDS, SPPS and supplementary planning guidance.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 31697/22-27, to detail whether he will consider notifying schools as soon as possible, after receiving his 2026-27 budget, the amount of funding that will be provided for summer schemes in special schools.
answer_text: I fully recognise the importance of providing clarity to schools in relation to this matter, and it is my intention to do this at the earliest practicable opportunity, following confirmation by the Northern Ireland Executive of the 2026-27 Education budget.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the level of (a) investment; and (b) co-operation, that has been committed in the current school year to the Special Educational Needs Reform Agenda, particularly in relation to the development of multi-agency support for schools; and (ii) the provision of input from allied health professionals for pupils with speech, language and communication needs in mainstream schools.
answer_text: Just under £4m investment has been profiled this year from the £27.5m secured from the Interim Public Sector Transformation Fund for the 13 early priority actions as detailed in the Special Educational Needs (SEN) Reform Agenda’s associated five-year Delivery Plan.  My department is working closely with a wide range of stakeholders across the entire education system and continued co-operation is vital as those early priority actions progress to operationalisation.  Input from a range of agencies, including health professionals, is vital in developing some elements of these actions, especially in the establishment and delivery of the early childhood intervention programmes.  There has been no specific input from Allied Health Professionals (AHPs) for pupils with speech, language and communication needs in mainstream schools outside of those actions noted above relating to the SEN Reform Agenda.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 31108/22-27, how he will ensure that when an area is identified as having a shortfall in special education needs places, and requires additional Specialist Provisions in a mainstream setting to address this shortfall, that schools in the area in question which are approached to facilitate a provision, will do so.
answer_text: EA’s focus is on collaborative partnership with schools rather than compulsory mechanisms, to deliver a truly inclusive, nurturing education system for our children and young people with Special Educational Needs.EA continues to work with NITC Trade Unions with engagements events planned for school leaders in high-demand areas to explore solutions for expanding specialist provisions for 2026 and beyond.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 31595/22-27, to detail where his Department's policy on supporting transgender pupils can be accessed.
answer_text: The policy statement can be found through the following link - Policy Statement on the use of certain facilities, participation in competitive sport and use of pronouns .In terms of supporting children and young people, I refer the member to my answer to AQW 31597/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 30778/22-27, to detail whether he has set a target date for the completion of Local Development Plans, by the new councils established in 2015.
answer_text: No target has been set by my department for the completion of Local Development Plans by councils. Councils set their own timetables for the preparation of their plans, taking into consideration their own resourcing, evidence gathering etc, and these are forwarded to my Department for agreement.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any consideration given to amalgamating the various referral portals used by special educational needs coordinators.
answer_text: The EA has advised:The Request for Involvement portal amalgamated previous referral routes across Special Educational Needs and Disability (SEND) Pupil Support Services as a single point of entry.The Request for Statutory Assessment Portal is currently hosted on an external platform but will move to EA Connect in the second term of this academic year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline how he will ensure that children with special educational needs who are placed in specialist provision in mainstream schools are able to access the same support services, and allied health professionals as those in special schools.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that:The Specialist Provisions service provides a graduated response to Specialist Provisions in mainstream schools. This includes link support, universal support, targeted support and specialist support. Universal programmes have been intentionally designed to build the capacity of settings to meet the needs of children and young people and parents/carers with increasing confidence and skill. This is done through training, modelling, and capacity building. Targeted support programmes are aimed at setting staff, parents/carers or children and young people in specific targeted groups or at specific times in a pupil's developmental journey. This could include, for example, transition programmes, parent/carer and setting cluster groups, school-based training, teacher advice and guidance or group interventions. However, specialist support is also available to settings. Specialist, intensive support will focus on the needs of an individual child or young person. It will have clearly agreed outcomes and is time-bound. A programme of intensive support will focus on the identified and assessed needs of an individual pupil. This type of support may be offered for those pupils with persistent needs who have not responded positively to other strategies. Intensive support can be accessed through the specialist provision’s allocated link teacher or by emailing SPSInfo@eani.org.uk The Specialist Provisions Service provide extensive professional learning and development to aid schools in supporting pupils displaying behaviours of concern. Through the SDS online calendar staff in specialist provisions can  access training on  ‘Supporting pupils with Social Behavioural Emotional and Wellbeing needs in Specialist Provisions' as well as training and follow-up support of the ‘Inclusion and Wellbeing toolkit’ for Post Primary Settings. Further to this we provide accredited TEAM Teach training to all staff working in Specialist Provisions Special. This details de-escalation strategies and how to avoid injury. This is renewed on an annual basis. For pupils in other provisions the Education Authority’s (EA) support services have developed an enhanced professional learning whole school framework, called “Connecting & Caring Across the Whole School Community” This programme was delivered as a pilot for staff working in specialist provisions during 2023/ 2024 academic year A reviewed and a revised version was delivered again in February 2025. Whilst this framework is to support the embedding of a trauma informed and nurturing whole school community ethos and culture for responding to behaviours that require intervention, there is recognition that some of the more complex pupil profiles require a training package that includes an aspect of risk reduction involving physical intervention in the form of prompts, guides and restrictive physical interventions (RPI).To address this need, the Specialist Provisions Service is in the proc

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the criteria that must be met in order for a child to access literacy support services.
answer_text: The Education Authority has advised that:The new Request for Involvement model recognises that literacy difficulties are multi-factorial and therefore fixed literacy criteria are not appropriate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to provide a breakdown of the number of children attending each specific type of specialist provision in mainstream schools.
answer_text: Please see below the breakdown of the number of children attending each specific type of specialist provision in mainstream schools;Specialist Provision (Special) – SPS- 564Specialist Provision (Behavioural) – SPB- 31Specialist Provision (Hearing) – SPH- 14Specialist Provision (Learning) – SPL- 2,074Specialist Provision (Physical) – SPP- 16Specialist Provision (Social Communication) -SPSC- 2,124Specialist Provision (Speech and Language) – SPSL- 196TOTAL- 5,019

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for an update on the review of the population health needs of children attending special educational needs schools; and (ii) when he will publish this report.
answer_text: I have received the final report of the commissioned review of the population health needs of children attending Special Educational Needs (SEN) schools in Northern Ireland.  The report identifies a number of areas where immediate improvements are required. I have therefore asked my Chief Nursing Officer to work closely with policy leads in the Department and other key stakeholders to progress the report’s recommendations. A copy of the Report has been placed in the Assembly Library and will also be made available on my Department’s website.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on the development of her Department’s bail support scheme.
answer_text: AQO 2520 22-27 - ANSWER My officials are working with justice, health and housing partners to develop a pilot bail support scheme to offer the Judiciary a robust and viable alternative to remand.  A framework has been shared with voluntary sector partners to seek views and determine the level of market interest in delivering the scheme.   There has been significant engagement with statutory and voluntary sector partners, including those with a lived experience to garner support, discuss potential challenges and shape the scheme accordingly.   The pilot will initially support women to remain in the community while awaiting the outcome of their case, providing support to live independently, whilst helping address any offending behaviours.  The importance of suitable housing is a key supporting factor with regards to bail compliance.  The scheme seeks to include accommodation for a number of women that otherwise would not be granted bail.  Subject to the identification of a delivery partner, it is anticipated that the scheme will be implemented in early 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the rationale for not including nurture provision as part of the new Local IMPACT Teams model.
answer_text: ‘Nuture’ is only available to a specific group of settings, has a specific intervention model and is delivered by a relatively small specialist team. In order to maintain fidelity to that delivery model it is not included in the Local IMPACT Teams (LITs). If Nurture is mainstreamed in future, then LITs would potentially be the structure to use.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail whether pre-school providers can access educational psychology services.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that pre-school children in both statutory and non-statutory settings who present with significant and complex needs may be referred to Educational Psychology Services via agreed pathways involving Health Trusts, Paediatric Services and Allied Health Professionals.Statutory nurseries continue to have access to Educational Psychology support. Each setting has a named Educational Psychologist and can request consultation through the Request for Involvement process.The EA is currently developing a referral pathway for children attending non-statutory nursery settings. This work is being phased in as part of the five-year strategic programme and is not yet fully operational. In the interim, non-statutory nurseries can request support through Local Impact Teams Solution-Focused Hubs, co-ordinated by EA.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he plans to consult with special educational needs coordinators for their assessment of the new educational psychology consultative support model.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised:The Education Psychology Service consultation model will be subject to ongoing evaluation. This continuous improvement approach will ensure that feedback from schools, Special Educational Needs Coordinators, families, and professionals informs future refinements embedding a model that is responsive, effective, and sustainable.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the rationale for not including education otherwise than at school, as part of the new Local Impact Teams model.
answer_text: The Education Authority has a statutory obligation to provide education otherwise than at School (EOTAS) in centres for full-time and part-time provision, which extends beyond the support that can be provided by a Local Impact Team (LIT). For those attending mainstream school and part-time EOTAS provision, they can access Social, Behavioural, Emotional and Well-being support through the LIT outreach service.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the Northern Ireland Wildfires Strategic Framework.
answer_text: The publication of the Northern Ireland Wildfires Strategic Framework was agreed by the Executive on the 04 September. The Strategic Framework was launched and published on the 08 October. Publication of the strategic framework will enable the development of an associated Wildfire Action Plan during 2026.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the reason why educational psychologists will no longer recommend that a child with special educational needs is referred for a statutory assessment, following an initial assessment.
answer_text: The role of Educational Psychologists (EPs) within the statutory assessment process has been amended following a review as part of the Special Educational Needs and Disability (SEND) Transformation Programme. The Education Authority (EA) has advised the decision to initiate statutory assessment lies with the Statutory Assessment and Review Service (SARS), not with individual professionals.Under the SEND framework, EPs are one of several professionals who may contribute evidence to the statutory assessment process.Education Psychologists advice and consultation outputs can be used by schools, parents, and other professionals to support an application for Statutory Assessment. SARS is responsible for reviewing all submitted evidence independently and determining whether the legal test for initiating a statutory assessment has been met.For pre-school children referred through Health and Social Care Trust (HSCT) pathways, especially those with significant and complex needs, Educational Psychologists (EPs) remain empowered to progress statutory assessments directly to ensure that children who require the highest level of support continue to benefit from the expertise and advocacy of EPs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline the rationale for moving to an educational psychology consultative support model.
answer_text: The Education Authority has advised through adopting a consultation-based approach, the Educational Psychology Service (EPS) can support more schools proactively, reducing unnecessary statutory assessments while empowering school staff to implement psychological interventions earlier and more effectively. Extending the consultation function of the EPS aligns with the new draft Special Education Needs Framework and the strategic goals of the Education Authority and Department of Education, including promoting early intervention, capacity building, and sustainable service delivery.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 31699/22-27, to detail any specific actions his Department has taken to progress the repair of the storm-damaged classroom at St Francis’ Primary School, Drumaroad, since 25th January 2025.
answer_text: The business case for the works to repair the storm damaged modular unit at St Francis’ Primary School has been approved and the project is moving to construction tender. The tender is due to issue in November 2025. Regrettably, progress was somewhat delayed due to the unprecedented level of Special Educational Needs placements to be delivered over the summer period. However, relevant approvals are now in place and works are currently programmed to commence on site in early 2026. Officials from my Department and the Education Authority are continuing to keep the school updated.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 31699/22-27, whether he plans to expedite the priority repairs at St Francis' Primary School, Drumaroad, which are currently scheduled to commence one year after the storm damage occurred.
answer_text: The business case for the works to repair the storm damaged modular unit at St Francis’ Primary School has been approved and the project is moving to construction tender. The tender is due to issue in November 2025. Regrettably, progress was somewhat delayed due to the unprecedented level of Special Educational Needs placements to be delivered over the summer period. However, relevant approvals are now in place and works are currently programmed to commence on site in early 2026. Officials from my Department and the Education Authority are continuing to keep the school updated.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 31699/22-27, to provide his assessment of the suggested timescale of approximately one year since the damage occurred, for the priority repair of the storm damaged classroom at  St Francis’ Primary School, Drumaroad.
answer_text: The business case for the works to repair the storm damaged modular unit at St Francis’ Primary School has been approved and the project is moving to construction tender. The tender is due to issue in November 2025. Regrettably, progress was somewhat delayed due to the unprecedented level of Special Educational Needs placements to be delivered over the summer period. However, relevant approvals are now in place and works are currently programmed to commence on site in early 2026. Officials from my Department and the Education Authority are continuing to keep the school updated.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any engagement he has had with special educational needs coordinators on the effectiveness of the Request for Involvement portal.
answer_text: While I have not engaged with Special Educational Needs Coordinators (SENCos), the Education Authority (EA) has engaged as follows: Multiple face to face familiarisation, feedback and system testing sessions completed in advance of Portal go live;Live online drop in clinics for all registered SENCo users during the Portal go live period to allow queries to be resolved and feedback provided; andBeyond go live, the same MS Teams site was retained to allow continuing direct contact between SENCos and the EA Portal team, both in terms of having queries resolved and to provide feedback, which was then reflected in subsequent system updates.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) an update on the Request for Involvement Portal used by special educational needs coordinators; and (ii) whether this portal is being used on a temporary basis.
answer_text: The Request for Involvement Portal closed on 16th May and then reopened on 15th September.  Yes, the RFI portal has always been designed to be an interim solution until the enduring EdIS case management system has been developed and implemented.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the criteria used when considering whether a child should move to Stage 3 of the SEN Code of Practice.
answer_text: The Education Authority has advised that in accordance with the SEN Code of Practice (CoP) a child/young person will only move to stage 3 following a statutory assessment of their educational needs (CoP part III). If the advice received as part of the statutory assessment indicates that special educational provision required to meet the needs of the child/young person is over and above that normally available to a school, then a proposed statement will be drafted and due process followed to issuing a final statement.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the legal status of the revised code of practice on the identification and assessment of special educational needs; and (ii) how it is applied in decision-making by the Education Authority and schools.
answer_text: The draft code of practice has no legal status.The Education Authority has advised that, schools must continue to have regard to the 1998 SEN Code of Practice and its 2005 supplement in all respects with the exception of recording children with SEN under the new three stages, as outlined in DE Circular 2021/06.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail all stakeholders involved in the moderation panel which decides whether a child should move to the next stage of the special educational needs code of practice.
answer_text: The moderation panels consist of experienced Special Educational Need (SEN) professionals across the SEN category areas. The moderation panels do not make decisions on the stage of the code of practice the child is on; they determine the level of support required.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail when new regulations relating to special educational needs support will be brought before the Assembly.
answer_text: It is anticipated that the new SEN regulations will be brought to the Education Committee before Christmas, followed by their introduction to the Assembly early in the new year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the location of his private office.
answer_text: Department of Education, Rathgael House, Balloo Road, Bangor, BT19 7PR.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of grievance cases that are currently live within his Department.
To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of staff within his Department who have taken a period of stress-related absence, for each of the last six years.
To ask the Minister of Education to detail the levels of staff absence due to sickness within his Department.
answer_text: The number of working days lost per staff year for the Department of Education over the last five full financial years is presented in the table below – 2019/209.92020/216.42021/228.52022/2310.22023/2411.6DoF have indicated 2024/25 figures will be published on 28 October 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he intends to take forward the Independent Review of Education recommendation regarding the establishment of an Independent Planning Commission.
answer_text: My Department is currently fully committed to delivering a sustainable, inclusive, and high-quality education system that meets the diverse needs of all learners, including those with special educational needs. Managing authorities and sectoral bodies are actively progressing two strategies Strategic Area Plans (2022-27) for the ‘Planning for Sustainable Provision’ and ‘Planning for Special Education Provision’ to ensure effective planning and provision across Northern Ireland. This is delivered by an Area Planning Framework, which supports strategic and collaborative approaches to school estate planning. The recommendations from the Independent Review of Education are under careful consideration as part of our broader commitment to improving educational outcomes. While no decision has been made on the specific proposal for an Independent Planning Commission, my Department is focused on ensuring that our planning processes are robust, inclusive, and responsive to the needs of all pupils. We will continue to engage with stakeholders to refine our approach and ensure that our education system is sustainable, inclusive and equitable.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any changes to grief and bereavement education which will be considered as part of the work of the new Curriculum Taskforce Advisory Committee.
answer_text: Schools in Northern Ireland currently have a duty to address grief and bereavement through the statutory curriculum. In primary schools, pupils explore feelings and emotions, including grief, under Personal Development and Mutual Understanding. In post-primary education, Learning for Life and Work (LLW) includes strategies for coping with grief and loss. The Council for the Curriculum, Examinations and Assessment provides a range of teaching resources that support the delivery of grief, loss, and bereavement education across key stages. These resources are designed to ensure that schools are well-equipped to support pupils during such experiences.The Curriculum Taskforce will consider how best to develop the core components of a reformed PDMU and LLW, including Citizenship, Employability and Personal Development, to ensure clear progression throughout each key stage.  A key priority is ensuring the curriculum is coherent and manageable. It is essential to avoid curriculum overload and strike an appropriate balance between breadth and depth.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he intends to introduce guidance, similar to that recently introduced in England, regarding a minimum learning requirement on grief and bereavement for primary and post-primary schools.
answer_text: Schools in Northern Ireland currently have a duty to address grief and bereavement through the statutory curriculum. In primary schools, pupils explore feelings and emotions, including grief, under Personal Development and Mutual Understanding. In post-primary education, Learning for Life and Work (LLW) includes strategies for coping with grief and loss. The Council for the Curriculum, Examinations and Assessment provides a range of teaching resources that support the delivery of grief, loss, and bereavement education across key stages. These resources are designed to ensure that schools are well-equipped to support pupils during such experiences.The Curriculum Taskforce will consider how best to develop the core components of a reformed PDMU and LLW, including Citizenship, Employability and Personal Development, to ensure clear progression throughout each key stage.  A key priority is ensuring the curriculum is coherent and manageable. It is essential to avoid curriculum overload and strike an appropriate balance between breadth and depth.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail his assessment of new statutory guidance for schools in England, which places a minimum learning requirement on grief and bereavement for primary and post-primary schools.
answer_text: Schools in Northern Ireland currently have a duty to address grief and bereavement through the statutory curriculum. In primary schools, pupils explore feelings and emotions, including grief, under Personal Development and Mutual Understanding. In post-primary education, Learning for Life and Work (LLW) includes strategies for coping with grief and loss. The Council for the Curriculum, Examinations and Assessment provides a range of teaching resources that support the delivery of grief, loss, and bereavement education across key stages. These resources are designed to ensure that schools are well-equipped to support pupils during such experiences.The Curriculum Taskforce will consider how best to develop the core components of a reformed PDMU and LLW, including Citizenship, Employability and Personal Development, to ensure clear progression throughout each key stage.  A key priority is ensuring the curriculum is coherent and manageable. It is essential to avoid curriculum overload and strike an appropriate balance between breadth and depth.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 30087/22-27, to detail when he will complete the development of long term options for nurture provision.
answer_text: I fully acknowledge the significant value and impact of Nurture Provision, particularly in delivering timely interventions for pupils facing social, emotional, and behavioural challenges that may otherwise hinder their learning and academic progress.		The short and long-term benefits for these children are well-documented, most notably in terms of enhanced life outcomes. Moreover, there are wider societal and economic advantages, including the potential to reduce future demand on education, health, and justice services, which would otherwise incur substantially greater costs.		Against this background officials are currently finalising a series of options for the Department’s future, long-term approach to supporting Nurture provision, with a view to establishing a sustainable model that will ensure effectively targeting and supporting those children who are most in need of this type of intervention. These proposals will be brought forward shortly for my consideration within the context of the Department’s challenging budgetary situation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 30422/22-27 and AQW 30423/22-27, to outline the capacity his Department has to facilitate a jointly managed community school, should a successful proposal be taken forward.
answer_text: My Department stands ready to facilitate and progress such a proposal should one emerge, to include consideration of the appropriate legislative, governance and operational foundations. No action is being taken at this time to specifically gauge interest in jointly managed community schools. However, my Department regularly engages with schools and planning authorities across the sector as part of Area Planning considerations. No proposals have been indicated at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 30422/22-27 and AQW 30423/22-27, to detail whether he will progress work to create a joint scheme of management for schools.
answer_text: Article 9A-9D of The Education and Libraries (Northern Ireland) Order 1986 advises that every school must have a scheme of management approved by the Department of Education. This scheme provides for the membership of the Board of Governors as well as its functions and procedures.Should a jointly managed community school be approved for establishment the Department will work with any such school to approve a Scheme of Management.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 30422/22-27 and AQW 30423/22-27, to detail any actions he is taking to understand the interest in creating jointly managed community schools.
answer_text: My Department stands ready to facilitate and progress such a proposal should one emerge, to include consideration of the appropriate legislative, governance and operational foundations. No action is being taken at this time to specifically gauge interest in jointly managed community schools. However, my Department regularly engages with schools and planning authorities across the sector as part of Area Planning considerations. No proposals have been indicated at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any international best practices that informed his proposals regarding the future of CCEA GCSEs, AS Levels and A Levels.
answer_text: My proposals for the consultation on the Future of CCEA GCSEs, AS levels and A levels, build on insights from the TransformED Strategy, the Strategic Review of the Curriculum, the Independent Review of Education, the ‘A Fair Start’ report, and the 14–19 Strategy, all of which drew from research and evaluation of best practices.  My Department has also engaged with the OECD in a research project on establishing comparative best practices regarding assessment and certification. This has established that the current system in Northern Ireland, whereby many students sit high-stakes examinations across four consecutive years, is highly unusual by comparison to other countries. Many successful economies require students to take significantly fewer examinations and assessments.The consultation is an opportunity for students, parents, educators, employers and wider stakeholders to share their views on my proposals to ensure that CCEA’s qualifications remain relevant and fit for purpose.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 30422/22-27 and AQW 30423/22-27, and considering significant budgetary pressures, to outline his assessment of the potential annual savings of approximately £100 million that could be generated from rightsizing the school estate.
answer_text: In the Independent Review of Education (IRE) report, the Panel stated that their proposed new approach to Area Planning had ‘the potential to create new jointly managed community schools by merging schools from different sectors. Maximum annual savings of approximately £100m could be generated for reinvestment in education’.The Panel completed a series of purely desktop exercises to arrive at this figure which employed a range of assumptions and exclusions but did qualify this by advising that ‘We must be clear that the modelling process was a desktop exercise that did not take local issues into account and would not therefore represent the ‘real world’ plan that would ultimately be implemented. The figures produced are illustrative only.’The Panel added that ‘A real-world implementation of a “right-sizing” exercise would be delivered differently and the resulting figures would be different, but this modelling gives a feel for the level of efficiency savings that may be possible’.  The Panel further commented that ‘this reformed network of schools will bring benefits but will require significant capital and resource investment.’As I have previously stated, the Panel estimated capital costs at £1bn over a 10-year period.  That level of funding is not available at present, but I will continue to make the case to the Executive for additional funding to deliver on my priorities for Education.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any action he is taking to ensure all newly-diagnosed multiple sclerosis patients receive a baseline MRI scan.
answer_text: Services for people with multiple sclerosis (MS) are provided as part of wider Neurology services in each Trust area. There has been detailed engagement with the clinical teams in each Trust over the past number of years with a view to improving the care and treatment available for people with this condition. As part of this engagement, a Service Specification was developed, consistent with NICE Clinical Guidelines for the service, which sets out the requirements for delivering a multi-disciplinary service consistently across the region. Work has taken place with clinical teams in each Trust to develop guidelines for referral of people with suspected MS. These guidelines are available on the electronic referral system utilised by General Practice to refer into the hospital-based neurology teams. The information included as part of a referral allows the clinical teams undertaking the triage to consider patient need and allocate people to the relevant clinic. Referrals for patients with suspected MS will be from General Practice to a consultant neurologist or via consultant to consultant referrals. Before making a referral to a consultant neurologist for a patient with suspected MS, alternative diagnoses should be excluded by performing relevant blood tests.A consultant neurologist will make a diagnosis of MS on the basis of established criteria, such at the revised 2010 McDonald criteria. These criteria include the requirement for an MRI scan to inform the diagnosis, so a baseline scan will normally be available as part of the process to confirm a patient diagnosis.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline (i) how frequently multiple sclerosis patients should be undergoing blood tests; and (ii) what steps he is taking to ensure regular and reliable blood testing is provided for these patients.
answer_text: HSC Trusts follow the NICE clinical guideline for the management of Multiple Sclerosis. With regard to the requirement for blood tests, there is no set position on how frequently patients should be undergoing blood tests. This can be dependent on a number of factors including whether a patient might have other co-morbidities or whether a patient is on a disease modifying treatment. For each disease modifying treatment there is guidance on how often blood tests need to be undertaken these can vary from monthly to six-monthly. The frequency can also be amended, if for example, results are not within acceptable levels. In recent years, there have been some changes to the provision of blood tests for patients under the care of hospital clinicians with some testing previously undertaken in primary care now being provided in hospital settings. Resources have been made available to establish or extend phlebotomy hub capacity in Trust settings.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, further to the NI Audit Office report Managing the Schools' Estate, whether he will develop a school estate management strategy.
answer_text: Officials from my Department are progressing the development of an Estate Strategy as recommended in the recent Northern Ireland Audit Office report on Managing the Schools' Estate.  Estate Strategies in other jurisdictions and sectors have been examined to ensure that the Department's emerging Estate Strategy aligns with those approaches and adopts best practice wherever possible.I am advised that good progress has been made to date and a work in progress draft of the Strategy will be presented to senior management within the Department for consideration, discussion and further development as necessary in the coming weeks. Following this engagement, and subsequently my own consideration and endorsement of the emerging strategy, I expect the Department's Estate Strategy to be in place in the coming months.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the proportion of multiple sclerosis patients currently accessing psychology services.
answer_text: Services for people with multiple sclerosis (MS) are provided as part of wider Neurology services in each Trust area. Historically, information such and new and review outpatient activity associated with patients with a diagnosis of MS has been included in the wider reporting of neurology services. Within secondary care people with MS under the care of a Neurologist can be referred to a range of other disciplines, including psychology and are managed as internal referrals within a Trust setting. This level of information is not subject to reporting as part of performance management arrangements. I fully recognise the importance of psychological support to patients diagnosed with a long term condition.  This recognition is reflected in my Department’s 2021-31 Mental Health Strategy which directs the service to develop care on a whole person and whole system basis for patients with chronic conditions.  The suggested models to deliver this involve greater integration of psychology within MDTs and additional training for staff from other disciplines in the recognition and referral of patients.  The aim is to ensure that those with physical ill health that leads to mental ill health receive the care and treatment they need.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail what proportion of multiple sclerosis patients received an annual review, in each of the last five years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Services for people with multiple sclerosis (MS) are provided as part of wider Neurology services in each Trust area. Historically, information on new and review outpatient activity associated with patients with a diagnosis of MS has been included in the wider reporting of neurology services. Information on MS patients who have received an annual review is not therefore available.Ii is however acknowledged that the current waiting times for neurology for both new and review appointments are lengthy and growing across Northern Ireland. This situation has arisen due to several factors, including funding that falls short of objectively assessed need and previous missed opportunities to reform health and social care delivery. I am committed to addressing this, subject to available funding.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, pursuant to AQW 28824/22-27, to outline the role the Executive Office has in coordinating cross-departmental engagement to develop cross-cutting legislation on issues of Executive priority.
answer_text: Co-ordination is primarily the responsibility of the sponsoring department with lead responsibility for the policy issue. The Executive Office has responsibility for oversight of the Legislative Programme overall and would only adopt such a role in respect of legislation on specific issues where no obvious lead department could be identified.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, further to the findings of the April 2025 report Drawing the Future, to detail any actions he is taking to increase awareness of apprenticeships among children and young people.
answer_text: The “Drawing the Future: Exploring the Career Aspirations of Children in Northern Ireland” report was launched on 29 January 2025.The study examined the career aspirations of children aged 7-11 years old in Northern Ireland.  It considered how their aspirations are shaped by gender, location, and socio-economic deprivation; compared children’s aspirations with current and projected market demands and the future needs of the economy; and investigated who and what is influencing their choices.The programme provides an opportunity for primary school pupils to engage directly with inspirational role models, providing them with a more accurate picture of the world of work and details of routes into employment. Events held in May and June involved 1,116 pupils from 31 schools and featured employees, employers and entrepreneurs from various fields such as publishing, law, horticulture, broadcasting, marine engineering, web design, charity leadership, translation, and air traffic control.While the programme does not focus specifically on apprenticeships the conversation may develop incidentally with some of the volunteers presenting to the pupils where they have completed apprenticeships and share this experience during the sessions.As set out in the joint Careers Action Plan, published in June 2025, a number of other actions are also being progressed to ensure that students are provided with high quality, relevant information. This includes increased sharing of careers resources and bulletins and roadshow events in Area Learning Community areas to share labour market focussed insights.  Information about apprenticeships has been included in both the bulletins and the careers roadshow.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any plans he has to bring the inspection of Religious Education under the remit of the Education & Training Inspectorate.
answer_text: I refer the member to my previous response to AQW 24356/22-27 on 31 March 2025. The position remains the same.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps he is taking to ensure multiple sclerosis patients receive access to an annual MRI scan to aid with monitoring their condition.
answer_text: There is no official guidance which specifies the frequency that people with Multiple Sclerosis (MS) should have an MRI scan. For people with a diagnosis of MS the requirement for an MRI will be dependent on their clinical condition and response to treatment. It is not always clinically indicated for a person who is stable on their treatment and reports no new symptoms or symptom changes to require an MRI on an annual basis.The requirement for a scheduled MRI can be associated with monitoring for people on specific disease modifying treatments (DMTs). For a small number of DMTs a new MRI scan might also be offered to determine eligibility for treatment.More widely, MRIs will be conducted at the discretion of a Consultant Neurologist, considering how they think a new scan may influence or improve the care they can offer the person in their MS treatment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail how he will ensure the MS helpline is fully staffed and accessible to patients requiring advice and support.
answer_text: Services for people with multiple sclerosis (MS) are provided as part of wider Neurology services in each Trust area. There has been detailed engagement with the clinical teams in each Trust over the past number of years with a view to improving the care and treatment available for people with this condition. As part of this engagement, a Service Specification was developed, consistent with NICE Clinical Guidelines for the service, which sets out the requirements for delivering a multi-disciplinary service consistently across the region. The priorities that were identified from the Service Specification were for all Trusts to have dedicated clinics for people with MS, for each Trust to have dedicated MS nursing staff and for the capacity to be available in each Trust to allow infusion delivered disease modifying treatments (DMTs) to be available locally.Investments were made over a number of years and each of these priority objectives have been achieved. Colleagues from DoH work closely with HSC Trusts each year to consider the requirements associated with the increasing number of people with MS who are on DMTs. Over £20m is invested across the region in supporting the provision of approved drug treatments to eligible patients. As part of the arrangement in place for people with MS to access services locally, each of the five Trusts have advice lines for patients under their care to make contact. It is important to note that these advice lines are not an emergency service. People with MS are advised to liaise with their GP, out of hours service or Emergency Department when the advice line is closed and always when the issue is non-MS related.As services for people with MS have been established locally, support has been provided to allow Trusts to allow patients to make contact between hospital appointments if they have any queries regarding their MS care. The arrangements for receiving and responding to calls will be Trust specific but generally involve administrative staff accepting the initial call or managing messages left for the clinical team and then arranging for the contact to be made with the patient. The discussions that take place with Trusts each year to consider the requirements associated with increasing numbers of patients with MS will take account of any further support that would be required to ensure timely advice and support is available. Any investment in this or other areas of pressure related to service provision will be subject to resources being available in the context of the current financial pressures facing the HSC.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 30308/22-27, whether there are any actions arising from the Review on the Use of Temporary and Substitute Teachers requiring additional resources that he will deliver in this financial year.
answer_text: In responding to AQW 26998/22-27 and AQW 30308/22-27 I explained that a detailed assessment of the recommendations from the Review on the Use of Temporary and Substitute Teachers had been jointly carried out with the trade unions. Recommendations from all of the eight completed workforce reviews which (i) have been actioned; and (ii) require additional resources have been deposited in the Assembly Library. In reaching agreement on pay in April 2025 the Northern Ireland Teaching Council (NITC) and Management Side of the Teachers’ Negotiating Committee (TNC) negotiated a Heads of Agreement which form the agreed basis on which teachers’ and school leaders’ workload issues will be addressed going forward. It was also agreed that an independent review panel would be established to undertake a review of teacher workload issues. The independent review panel was established on 1 May 2025. In addition to examining all aspects of teacher workload the independent review panel will be monitoring progress with the implementation of a series of agreed workload commitments that were part of the April 2025 pay settlement. The focus of TNC is to address and implement the workload commitments detailed in the Heads of Agreement.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 31242/22-27 and AQW 31243/22-27, whether he will consider recurrent funding for summer schemes in special schools to provide certainty to schools and parents.
answer_text: As previously outlined in my response to the previous two questions to which this question is pursuant, I, along with my Executive colleagues, anticipate a significant resource overcommitment next year and beyond. Given where the Executive is in the future years’ budget cycle (i.e. Education does not have an opening 2026-27 budget), I cannot currently commit to any recurrent funding at this time. Instead, once an opening 2026-27 Education Budget has been agreed by the Executive, I will consider funding for summer schemes in special schools alongside the many other and significant funding pressures my Department will face next year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail why the modular classroom at St Francis' Primary School, Drumaroad, damaged by Storm Éowyn in January 2025 is still awaiting repair.
answer_text: The project to repair the modular classroom at St Francis’ Primary School is a high priority and will be progressed as quickly as possible by the Education Authority (EA). Regrettably, delivery of the project has been delayed due to the unprecedented level of Special Educational Needs placements to be delivered over the summer period. The EA has advised that it is currently preparing the construction tender package. It is anticipated that this will issue in November 2025, with work starting on site early in 2026, for an anticipated contract period of 12 weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any actions he has taken on recommendations contained with the Independent Review of Education on (i) religious education; and (ii) collective worship in schools.
answer_text: My department is awaiting the outcome of the ongoing judicial review on the nature of Religious Education and collective worship in state-funded schools to inform a review of the core syllabus for Religious Education.  Currently, it will not form part of the Taskforce’s work.  My department will then initiate a review, which will involve a wide range of key stakeholders including the four main churches in Northern Ireland. This will also provide an opportunity to address the recommendation made in the Independent Review regarding the review of the RE syllabus. The Independent Review made no recommendations relating to collective worship in schools.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 30779/22-27, whether he will publish the findings of the review into Education Authority's capital and minor works maintenance service.
answer_text: The Education Authority has advised that it will publish the findings of the external ‘critical friend’ review into its capital and minor works maintenance service in October 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail what actions his Department has taken to identify approaches, which would help to encourage a progressive and welcoming environment for young LGB&T people, as recommended in the departmentally commissioned research Post-primary school experiences of 16-21 year old people who are lesbian, gay, bisexual and or transgender.
answer_text: Supporting all children and young people is key priority for the department.As a starting point, schools should refer to the ‘Safeguarding and Child Protection in Schools’ in terms of their general duty to safeguard and promote the welfare of their pupils and to determine the measures to be taken at the school with a view to protecting pupils from abuse (whether at school or elsewhere). More specifically the Addressing Bullying in Schools Act (Northern Ireland) 2016, also requires a Board of Governors of the school to determine the measures pursued at the school to prevent, respond to and record all incidents of alleged and/or confirmed bullying type behaviour involving registered pupils.Schools will also have in place other key policies that will form part of their overall offer of pastoral care, with support to promote a culture of whole school wellbeing available through the projects currently funded under the Joint Health and Education Emotional Wellbeing Framework in Education. Specific support is also available from several Education Authority services in line with departmental policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail who provided the legal advice preceding his decision to remove the 2019 transgender guidance from the Education Authority.
answer_text: Legal advice was commissioned through the Departmental Solicitor’s Office and provided to the department by Mr John Larkin, KC.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursant to AQW 30922/22-27, to detail the content of his Department's policy on how best to support transgender pupils in school.
answer_text: As I advised in my previous answer, support and advice remain available to schools on this matter from Education Authority support services in accordance with my department’s policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on her Department's scoping exercise, undertaken to better understand the needs of the childcare sector.
answer_text: The Childcare Providers Targeted Business Support Research Report published on my department’s website on 24 September and can be accessed at Targeted Business Support for Childcare Providers Market Research .

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the rationale for removing the Post-primary school experiences of 16-21 year old people who are lesbian, gay, bisexual and or transgender, guidance from his Department's website.
answer_text: To ensure information published on the DE website remains clear, accurate and relevant, pages may be reviewed and updated or removed at any time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any plans he has to implement Plans of Action for Specialist Provision in Mainstream Schools, similar to Operational Plan 2: 2024-26 Special Schools – Plans of Action.
answer_text: Specialist Provision in Mainstream Schools offers a strategic approach to supporting the special school estate in Northern Ireland by building capacity within mainstream settings. The success of this initiative not only depends on the availability of capital funding, but also the willingness of individual schools to establish specialist classes, which currently limits the ability to create or implement a Plans of Action for Specialist Provision in Mainstream Schools.The Education Authority (EA) has identified key areas of pressure for the coming years, which will enable it to proactively engage with those schools, helping to ensure that emerging demand is effectively met.  However as outlined in my executive paper, given the many competing needs for capital investment across the schools’ estate, I will require a significantly increased and sustained ring-fenced capital budget allocation to fully implement the published action plans. I would greatly welcome your support in securing all Executive parties support for this approach.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any plans he has to review the 2007 Core Syllabus for Religious Education, further to the acknowledgement in the Strategic Review of the Northern Ireland Curriculum that there is agreement on the need to review the teaching of Religious Education and to include the teaching of religions other than Christianity in the primary syllabus.
answer_text: My department is awaiting the outcome of the ongoing judicial review on the nature of Religious Education and collective worship in state-funded schools to inform a review of the core syllabus for Religious Education.  Currently, it will not form part of the Taskforce’s work.  My department will then initiate a review, which will involve a wide range of key stakeholders including the four main churches in Northern Ireland. This will also provide an opportunity to address the recommendation made in the Independent Review regarding the review of the RE syllabus. The Independent Review made no recommendations relating to collective worship in schools.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to provide an overview of work undertaken by the cross-departmental Children and Young People’s Strategy Monitoring and Reporting Board.
answer_text: The Children and Young People’s Strategy (CYPS) Monitoring and Reporting Board (the Board) undertake a range of activities providing collaborative leadership and cross-departmental strategic direction on the co-ordination of policy matters affecting children and young people and the implementation of the CYPS and Delivery Plan.  Additionally, the Board have an oversight role in respect of child safeguarding and protection issues requiring a cross departmental approach.Current work of the Board includes developing proposals for a refreshed CYPS Delivery Plan 2025 – 2028 and consideration of the findings of the second report on the operation of the Children’s Services Cooperation Act (Northern Ireland) 2015.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he plans to bring the teaching of Religious Education into the work of the Curriculum Taskforce Advisory Committee.
answer_text: My department is awaiting the outcome of the ongoing judicial review on the nature of Religious Education and collective worship in state-funded schools to inform a review of the core syllabus for Religious Education.  Currently, it will not form part of the Taskforce’s work.  My department will then initiate a review, which will involve a wide range of key stakeholders including the four main churches in Northern Ireland. This will also provide an opportunity to address the recommendation made in the Independent Review regarding the review of the RE syllabus. The Independent Review made no recommendations relating to collective worship in schools.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail whether the Education Authority maintain a record of the number of pupils who have been withdrawn from (i) Religious Education; and (ii) collective worship.
answer_text: The Education Authority does not maintain a record of the number of pupils who have been withdrawn from (i) Religious Education; and (ii) collective worship.Under legislation (The Education and Libraries (Northern Ireland) Order, 1986), there is provision for parents to request that a pupil should be wholly or partly excused from attendance at religious education or collective worship or both. Records in relation to such requests are held by individual schools and there is no requirement to return information related to such requests to the Education Authority.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to outline when he will make provision through regulations, for procedures to be followed when pooling resources, as part of The Children's Services Co-Operation Act (Northern Ireland) 2015.
answer_text: ANSWERMy department published guidance on pooled budgeting in April 2025, intended to provide direction and advice for all departments in overcoming many of the barriers faced when implementing pooled budgets. NILGA also published similar guidance for local councils in March 2025.My officials have recently engaged with stakeholders identified in the Children’s Services Co-operation Act, completed a review of the barriers to the Act, and are currently developing an action plan that seeks to reduce and remove these barriers to allow organisations to pool resources and share outcomes. Work is well advanced, and it is due to be published in the coming months.It is therefore my intention to monitor the impact of these developments on the ability of departments to work collaboratively, and if necessary, consider the need for regulations at that stage.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 30087/22-27, to detail (i) the nine special educational needs transformation projects to be delivered using the funding received from the Interim Public Services Transformation Board; (ii) the cost breakdown of each of these individual projects; and (iii) the intended outcomes of each of these individual projects.
answer_text: I refer the Member to the answer to AQW 24357/22-27, published in the weekly answer booklet for week ending 21st March 2025.The costings for each project are indicative and subject to change as the projects progress, with some subject to final proposals from delivery partners.  The working detail of costings are not planned for publication; however, there will be full transparency and accountability on expenditure.As outlined in the Special Educational Needs (SEN) Reform Outcomes Framework, monitoring will utilise ‘performance measures’ to measure the quantity and quality of activity and effect of each action within the SEN and Disability (SEN/D) Reform Delivery Plan.  The population indicators to which each project will contribute are outlined in full in the SEN Reform Outcomes Framework, published in June 2025.Performance measures are being developed for all actions as they progress and annual report cards will be produced.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail who is responsible for the oversight of the Programme for Government commitment to improve pathways to appropriate opportunities and improve protections for young people with special educational needs when they leave school.
answer_text: The Department for the Economy is responsible for overseeing the Programme for Government commitment to improve pathways to appropriate opportunities and improve protections for young people with Special Educational Needs when they leave school.In June 2025, Minister Archibald set out her proposed programme of reform, subject to appropriate investment, to improve and expand the Department for the Economy funded pathways and support on those pathways for learners with special educational needs.Officials from the Department for Education, Department for the Economy, Department of Health and the Department for Communities continue to engage and collaborate to ensure that appropriate pathways are available and to ensure that young people with SEN are supported in the last years of school to prepare for the transition to adult life.The commitment aims to ensure clear pathways for further education, training, or employment for young people with SEN.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline how they will ensure that all Ministers with a responsibility for improving post-19 provision for young people with special educational needs are held accountable to deliver on this commitment.
answer_text: The Executive has considered and agreed recommendations arising from a review of post-school provision for young people with special educational needs and has asked for quarterly reports to be provided on progress by each department.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to provide their assessment of the most effective way to address issues that require cross-departmental collaboration, such as post-19 special educational needs provision.
answer_text: The management of cross-cutting issues within the Executive will depend on the nature and scope of the issue. In the majority of cases, the department with lead policy responsibility will assume responsibility for co-ordinating the contribution of all relevant departments and producing recommendations for adoption by the Executive. Inter-departmental steering groups may also be established to monitor and report on progress and delivery.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any plans they have to coordinate a cross-departmental approach to post-19 special educational needs provision.
answer_text: The Executive has considered and agreed recommendations arising from a review of post-school provision for young people with special educational needs and has asked for quarterly reports to be provided on progress by each department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will be providing funding for the provision of summer schemes in special schools for summer 2026.
answer_text: I fully recognise the importance of providing clarity to schools in relation to this matter at the earliest opportunity.  However, as you are aware, department budgets for 2026-27 and beyond have not yet been agreed by the Northern Ireland Executive.   The Department of Finance recently commissioned an exercise to assess budget requirements for the periods 2026/27 to 2028/29. My Department has submitted its return and is awaiting the outcome of this exercise.Similar to recent financial years, I anticipate a significant resource overcommitment next year and beyond.
