Consequently, any additional funding required to provide summer schemes for our children with special educational needs will need to be considered alongside other significant funding pressures my Department will face. I remain committed to advocating for investment in Education.  Given the scale of pressures faced by the Northern Ireland Block Grant, the Executive needs to continue to make the case to HM Treasury for additional funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the membership of the cross-departmental Children and Young People’s Strategy Monitoring and Reporting Board.
answer_text: The Children and Young People’s Strategy Monitoring and Reporting Board comprises of senior civil servants from departments as follows:Two members from my Department, which includes the Chair of the Board; and One member from the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, the Department of Finance, the Department of Health, the Department for the Economy, The Executive Office, the Department for Communities and the Department of Justice.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any plans they have to coordinate cross-departmental engagement to deliver cross-cutting legislation on post-19 special educational needs provision.
answer_text: The Executive considered and agreed recommendations arising from a review on post-school provision for young people with special educational needs and has asked for quarterly reports to be provided on progress by each stakeholder department. This will include updates on the legislative provision to give effect to the proposed programme of reforms on the issue announced recently by the Minister for the Economy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail how he will ensure that funding allocated to special schools in order to provide summer schemes will be secured and delivered in a timely fashion ahead of summer 2026, to allow schools to appropriately plan and recruit staff for the schemes.
answer_text: I fully recognise the importance of providing clarity to schools in relation to this matter at the earliest opportunity.  However, as you are aware, department budgets for 2026-27 and beyond have not yet been agreed by the Northern Ireland Executive.   The Department of Finance recently commissioned an exercise to assess budget requirements for the periods 2026/27 to 2028/29. My Department has submitted its return and is awaiting the outcome of this exercise.Similar to recent financial years, I anticipate a significant resource overcommitment next year and beyond. Consequently, any additional funding required to provide summer schemes for our children with special educational needs will need to be considered alongside other significant funding pressures my Department will face. I remain committed to advocating for investment in Education.  Given the scale of pressures faced by the Northern Ireland Block Grant, the Executive needs to continue to make the case to HM Treasury for additional funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the implementation of recommendations contained within the Review of the Northern Ireland Curriculum.
answer_text: My department responded to the Strategic Review of the Northern Ireland Curriculum on 9 July 2025, with the vast majority of recommendations either fully agreed or agreed in principle. In most cases, the recommendations will be implemented through the work of the Curriculum Taskforce, which will lead the design of a new curriculum framework, develop an implementation plan, and advise on appropriate evaluation mechanisms.Two recommendations (relating to primary languages and Learning for Life and Work) will be considered in further detail by the Taskforce. A further two recommendations (concerning qualifications and accountability, and a Citizenship, Employability and Personal Development programme) fall outside the scope of the curriculum programme and will be considered as part of the broader work on qualifications.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 30089/22-27, when he expects to publish the findings of the review of the population health needs of children attending special educational needs schools.

To ask the Minister of Education to detail how he will ensure (i) that all schools in areas where the need for additional specialist provision has been identified will commit to taking on specialist provision in their settings; and (ii) that these schools are properly resourced and supported to establish and deliver such provision.
answer_text: The EA has advised that:Planning for September 2026 is already well underway as part of our year-round approach. Operational Plan 3 development commenced in September 2025, with over 60 projects identified for 2026-27 academic year. EA are conducting early engagement with schools in identified areas, progressing business cases for major capital works, and undertaking consultation processes with school communities.We acknowledge that schools are partners in this process, need adequate time and assurance about ongoing support. This is why we are implementing earlier engagement processes, providing comprehensive support packages including guaranteed staffing cost coverage and equipment provision, and offering extensive professional development opportunities. The £3,000 setup allowance and annual LMS allocations from £18,000 to £46,000 provide financial security. We are also establishing mentorship programmes pairing new Specialist Provisions with experienced provision/special schools, and ensuring Specialist Provision Service support and training is available from day one.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update of recommendations that have been progressed following the Landscape Review of the Education Authority.
answer_text: The Landscape Review of the Education Authority (EA) continues to be a key priority for my Department and officials, along with EA colleagues, continue to make good progress. The specific work to address the 13 recommendations were collated into seven project workstreams. Of these, significant work has been undertaken on workstreams one (Clarity on Role and Function of EA), three (EA Performance, Data and Governance), four (End-to-End Reviews), five (EA People), six (EA Culture, Communication and Engagement) and seven (EA Partnership Agreement), with most individual actions completed or nearing completion. A small number of actions within these projects remain in progress, which are now being taken forward as business-as-usual. As such, the Action Plan has been formally stood down. The Department continues to monitor progress in completing all 13 recommendations via the appropriate governance and accountability mechanisms.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the Independent Review of Teacher Workload.
answer_text: Since the Independent Review of Teacher Workload Panel was appointed in May 2025, they have undertaken a structured programme of work with the aim of submitting a final report in November 2025. This has included a review of existing reports and workstreams taken forward by the Teachers’ Negotiating Committee and direct engagement with a range of stakeholders.  The Panel has also conducted benchmarking exercises with officials in neighbouring jurisdictions to assess comparative approaches to teacher workload management.In order to gain a comprehensive understanding of the key workload issues and, more importantly, the steps that could be taken to address them, the Panel wrote to Boards of Governors on 25 June to seek written submissions on the workload position in their schools.  In addition, they issued an engagement questionnaire to all teachers and school leaders in Northern Ireland on 18 August. Over 7,000 responses to the questionnaire have been received to date.  I look forward to receiving the final report from the Independent Review of Teacher Workload Panel in November 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the roles that make up each individual Local Impact Team.
answer_text: I refer the Member to the answer to question AQW 30988/22-27, published in the weekly answer booklet for week ending 26th September 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of Boards of Governors that have not completed safeguarding training (i) for the 2024-25 academic year; and (ii) as of the start of the 2025-26 academic year.
answer_text: The Education Authority lead on Governor training and has advised: The following figures represent the number of schools where no members of the Board of Governors have yet completed Safeguarding/Child Protection training.It is important to note that this training is delivered as part of a four-year cycle, rather than on an annual basis. As such, it is not possible to disaggregate the data by individual academic years. In some cases, training may currently be underway but not yet completed.Additionally, certain training is delivered at school level by the Principal and recorded in Board of Governors’ meeting minutes; however, this information is not routinely submitted to the Education Authority for central recording.It should also be highlighted that some Boards of Governors have only recently been reconstituted and are in the early stages of initiating their training programme for the current four-year term.School Management TypeNoOut ofControlled15540Grant Maintained Integrated138Irish Medium627Maintained31431Grand Total531036

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of vacancies remaining in Local Impact Teams, as of the start of the academic year 2025-26.
answer_text: By the end of the last academic year, 42.2 FTE frontline vacancies had arisen across the EA’s pre-existing SEND support services as part of ‘business as usual’. In addition to that a further 24 FTE Teacher posts had been proposed across the Local Impact Teams (LiTs) network. EA-wide restrictions meant that recruitment for these posts was delayed over the summer but is now underway.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the amount of funding allocated to the creation of Local Impact Teams.
answer_text: The Local IMPACT Team (LiT) model was developed on a budget neutral basis. The process of establishing the LiT network was funded as part of the wider DE/EA SEND Transformation Programme and the LiT element of that funding has been approximately £3.75m in total spread across four financial years.A case has been made to increase the overall staffing footprint of the LiT network, particularly in the early years phase (in line with previous Fair Start recommendations).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail why Local Impact Teams were not fully staffed and operational for the start of the new academic year.
answer_text: The SEND Local IMPACT Team (LiT) model was developed on a budget neutral basis, so no expansion of the underlying staffing cohort was planned for September 2025. EA-wide recruitment constraints also meant that the backfilling of vacancies was delayed at the end of last term, but this work is now underway. Above and beyond ‘business as usual’ recruitment to backfill, an additional cohort of approximately 30 FTE specialist teachers will also be required in order for the LiT Network to be at an operational capacity that is sustainable over the long-term.All frontline staff from the pre-cursor services transitioned into their respective LiTs on 20th August. Moderation Panels began at that point to allocate support to children carried over from the end of last term with direct engagement with the relevant Learning Support Coordinators/ SEN co-ordinators (SENCos) immediately following on to allow frontline support to begin from the start of term. The SENCo portal was then opened for the submission of new ‘Requests for Involvement’ on the 15th September alongside the revised SEND Central contact model which includes access to a ‘one stop shop’ online query form and specialist advice and guidance helpline for SENCos.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the design and implementation of the Inclusion Capacity Building Programme project as part of the SEN Reform Agenda and Delivery Plan 2025-2030.
answer_text: I refer the Member to the answer to question AQW 30087/22-27. Published in the weekly answer booklet week ending 12 September 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the implementation of the Teacher and Classroom Assistant Professional Learning Programme project, as part of the SEN Reform Agenda and Delivery Plan 2025-2030.
answer_text: I refer the Member to the answer to question AQW 30087/22-27, published in the weekly answer booklet for week ending 12th September 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any support that will be provided to schools on how best to support transgender pupils, following his announcement that the 2019 transgender guidance has been withdrawn.
answer_text: I wrote to all schools (8 September) to advise that the ‘Guidance for Schools, EOTAS Centres and Youth Service on Supporting Transgender Young People’, issued by the Education Authority, had been withdrawn with immediate effect. Schools were advised to contact my department with any queries in relation to the policy statement.  In addition, support and advice remain available to schools on this matter from Education Authority support services in accordance with Department of Education policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any plans he has to review the Common Funding Scheme.
answer_text: On 3 July I announced the appointment of an independent panel to undertake the Review of Baseline School Funding Provided Through the Common Funding Formula.In accordance with the Terms of Reference, the panel formally notified the Education Committee of the review in late July, by correspondence to the Clerk of the Committee.The Panel has been asked to conclude its work by Halloween 2025 and provide a report to me shortly thereafter.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any role his Department has in the (i) creation; and (ii) amendment of statements of special education needs for children in non-statutory pre-school settings.
answer_text: The statutory responsibility for securing provision for pupils with special educational needs rests with schools and the Education Authority (EA). The department has no role in the identification and assessment process nor any role in the determination of appropriate provision for young people with SEN.Placements for children with a statement of SEN in non-grant-aided schools, including non-statutory pre-school provision, requires Department of Education approval (pursuant to Art 12 of the Education (Northern Ireland) Order 1996).Therefore, when the EA has determined that a placement in an institution other than a grant-aided school is appropriate and the pupil concerned has a statement of special educational needs, the EA must seek the department’s consent before naming the placement in the pupil’s statement.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the establishment and delivery of the Special Schools as Resource Hubs - Centres of Expertise project, as part of the Special Educational Needs Reform Agenda and Delivery Plan 2025-2030.
answer_text: I refer the Member to the answer to question AQW 30087/22-27. Published in the weekly answer booklet week ending 12 September 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the financial value of the Online Teacher Continued Professional Development Academy contract that was awarded to Innerdrive; and (ii) the procurement process that was followed in the awarding of this contract.
answer_text: The total value of the contract is £396,605 plus VAT.Due to the bespoke nature of the product, and to ensure it was available for the start of the 2025-26 academic year, InnerDrive was appointed under a Direct Award Contract to deliver the online Continued Professional Development Academy for post-primary schools.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any review of the Education Authority's minor works procurement process that has taken place on the past five years.
answer_text: In April 2025, the Education Authority (EA) Chief Executive engaged the services of an external reviewer to act as a ‘critical friend’ and undertake an independent review of the EA’s capital minor works and maintenance service processes. The outcomes of this review were shared with the EA Board at its meeting on 26 June and the report was shared with the Department on 7 August. The report notes that the EA is an accredited Centre of Procurement Expertise (CoPE), so did not consider procurement procedures. The EA’s Minor Works Service primarily uses a competitively tendered dynamic shortlisting system (DSS) to procure pre-qualified design teams and contractors.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 29230/22-27, to provide all the stakeholders involved in the review of the population health needs of children attending special educational needs schools.
answer_text: The review has taken account of engagement with health and education professionals, including school principals, and parents. The report will include details of all those stakeholders engaged with in its development. I expect the PHA report to be finalised and provided to me shortly.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 29230/22-27, whether he will publish the review of the population health needs of children attending special educational needs schools.

Once I receive the report, I will consider the recommendations it contains as a priority and at that point a decision will be made on whether it would be appropriate to disseminate the report or associated recommendations more widely.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the impact that the Partnership Agreement between Government and the Voluntary and Community Sector will have on his Department.
answer_text: The ‘Partnership Agreement between Government and the Voluntary and Community Sector’ provides an important framework for collaboration between departments and the sector. It emphasises the value of the sector’s contribution to policy and service delivery while outlining principles to ensure that engagement with and funding for the sector are fair, proportionate and focussed on outcomes. My department will continue to work constructively with voluntary and community partners in the best interests of all children, young people and their families.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail what support is in place to assist Boards of Governors of schools who become community schools.
answer_text: In practice, many schools across Northern Ireland provide access to facilities or services for the wider community and can do so without formal designation as a ‘community school’ (provision for which exists under Article 139 of the Education Reform (Northern Ireland) Order 1989). The Department recognises the benefits of ensuring schools have strong links to the communities that they serve. All schools are encouraged to engage with the wider community, and in line with Article 140 of the 1989 Order, schools are required to have regard to the desirability of their premises being made available for use by members of the community when not otherwise required for education purposes. Arrangements are made and agreed locally with decisions ultimately the responsibility of Boards of Governors.  According to the last set of data available from the DE School Omnibus Survey (2018), 80% of schools reported that they had some form of community use. Of those schools which confirmed that they allowed community use of their school premises, approximately 86% stated that the main reason was “community engagement”.  Detailed guidance has been available since 2014 to assist school leaders with a range of practical considerations associated with the community use of schools which is under review currently. Further related guidance is also available via the Governors’ Guide as well as advice and support from the relevant managing authority.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 29230/22-27, what role special school principals have in the review of the population health needs of children attending special educational needs schools.
answer_text: Following initial engagement with a number of concerned Special School principals, the Public Health Agency was commissioned by the Chief Nursing Officer to review the population health needs of children attending Special Educational Needs (SEN) schools in Northern Ireland and the current nursing contribution in meeting their health needs. The approach to this review has included consultation with key stakeholders. Interviews and focus groups have been completed with health and education professionals, as well as parents.  Special School principals were included in this process, as they have a key role to play in the delivery of services to this cohort of students.I expect the PHA report to be finalised and provided to me shortly.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education when he will update the Guidance Toolkit for schools in relation to community use of school premises.
answer_text: Community Use of School Premises – A Guidance Toolkit (2014) is currently under review by the Education Authority’s (EA) Community and Schools Team to update its operational details. The review will conclude by the end of October 2025, after which my officials will work with the EA to finalise and publish the updated Toolkit.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any work that has been undertaken regarding provision for a joint scheme of management for schools.
answer_text: Clear guidance, developed in consultation with the Catholic Trustees and Transferors and circulated to all schools, is currently available on the mechanism through which jointly managed schools may be established (DE Circular 2015/15).The establishment of a new jointly managed school is required through the Development Proposal process and only where there is a clear desire to do so on the part of the prospective constituent schools and the communities they serve. Any proposed school – jointly managed or otherwise - must be viable in the longer term when assessed against the criteria set out in DE’s Sustainable Schools Policy.My Department would welcome proposals for jointly managed schools, however no such proposal has been brought forward to date. No joint scheme of management has been developed in respect of such schools.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any work that has been undertaken to move towards the establishment of jointly managed community schools.
answer_text: Clear guidance, developed in consultation with the Catholic Trustees and Transferors and circulated to all schools, is currently available on the mechanism through which jointly managed schools may be established (DE Circular 2015/15).The establishment of a new jointly managed school is required through the Development Proposal process and only where there is a clear desire to do so on the part of the prospective constituent schools and the communities they serve. Any proposed school – jointly managed or otherwise - must be viable in the longer term when assessed against the criteria set out in DE’s Sustainable Schools Policy.My Department would welcome proposals for jointly managed schools, however no such proposal has been brought forward to date. No joint scheme of management has been developed in respect of such schools.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 29230/22-27, whether the review of the population health needs of children attending special educational needs schools will address nursing provision in special schools.
answer_text: An assessment of nursing provision in Special Schools forms a key element of the review.I expect the PHA report to be finalised and provided to me shortly, and that it will include recommendations to support decisions around how to best meet the population health needs of this cohort of children, ensuring child-specific timely care.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any engagement his Department has had with pre-school providers regarding the effectiveness of the Pre-School Education Inclusion Support Fund.
answer_text: The Pre-School Education Inclusion Support Fund (PSEISF) was launched in November 2024. An evaluation of the programme for the 2024/25 academic year will be completed by the end of October 2025, which will include a survey to all participating settings to assess impact for both children and staff.A total of 213 settings have received payment from the Fund to date.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 27512/22-27, to detail his anticipated timescales for the implementation of updated arrangements for the delivery of low value tenant maintenance works.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that it is currently working through logistical issues to ensure the effective delivery of low value maintenance works does not place an additional burden on school leaders.A date for implementation is currently being considered for January 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of settings that have received a payment from the Pre-School Education Inclusion Support Fund.
answer_text: The Pre-School Education Inclusion Support Fund (PSEISF) was launched in November 2024. An evaluation of the programme for the 2024/25 academic year will be completed by the end of October 2025, which will include a survey to all participating settings to assess impact for both children and staff.A total of 213 settings have received payment from the Fund to date.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline her proposals for criminalising sexually explicit deepfake images.

To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 26998/22-27, to detail which of the recommendations arising from the Review on the Use of Temporary and Substitute Teachers which require additional resources will be taken forward for action in this academic year.
answer_text: In responding to AQW 26998/22-27 I explained that a detailed assessment of the recommendations from the Review on the Use of Temporary and Substitute Teachers had been jointly carried out with the trade unions.Recommendations from all of the eight completed workforce reviews which (i) have been actioned; and (ii) require additional resources have been deposited in the Assembly Library. In reaching agreement on pay in April 2025 the Northern Ireland Teaching Council (NITC) and Management Side of the Teachers’ Negotiating Committee (TNC) negotiated a Heads of Agreement which form the agreed basis on which teachers’ and school leaders’ workload issues will be addressed going forward. It was also agreed that an independent review panel would be established to undertake a review of teacher workload issues. The independent review panel was established on 1 May 2025.In addition to examining all aspects of teacher workload the independent review panel will be monitoring progress with the implementation of a series of agreed workload commitments that were part of the April 2025 pay settlement.The focus of TNC is to address and implement the workload commitments detailed in the Heads of Agreement.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to provide an update on work being undertaken to develop a new strategic approach to special education needs placements.
answer_text: The Education Authority’s long-term vision is for every school to provide access to specialist provision classes, ensuring that all children with SEN can be supported within their local community alongside their peers.The provision of additional specialist provisions is a year-round priority with approximately 60 projects identified for progression for the 2026 academic year, demonstrating our sustained commitment to expanding current capacity across the education estate. For 2027 and beyond, engagement is being made with schools for future provision.For special schools, the Education Authority is taking forward a Plan of Action, to be published in Autumn 2025 for each special school setting out the projected need and outlining short, medium and long term actions to meet that need.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 29230/22-27, for an update on the review of the population needs of children attending Special Educational Needs schools.
answer_text: This issue remains a key priority for my Department.  Over the summer period, work has continued to complete the Public Health Agency’s review of the population health needs of children attending Special Educational Needs (SEN) schools in Northern Ireland and the current nursing contribution in meeting their health needs. I expect the report to be finalised and provided to me shortly. In addition to this work, as set out in my response to AQW 29514/22-27, the PHA, is leading a comprehensive analysis of the therapeutic needs of children with Special Educational Needs in Special Schools.  I expect the first stage of this process to complete by the end of the year. Both these exercises will provide evidence-based assessments of the complex healthcare and therapeutic needs of children and young people in SEN schools, to inform a wider, child centred multidisciplinary understanding and approach, which in turn will help support decisions around how to best meet the population health needs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail his assessment of the success of the Pre-School Education Inclusion Support Fund.
answer_text: The Pre-School Education Inclusion Support Fund (PSEISF) was launched in November 2024; 213 pre-school education settings have received support equating to an investment of £1,103,250 to date.An evaluation of the Fund for the 2024/25 academic year will be completed by the end of October 2025.  All funded settings will be surveyed to determine how they used the funding and the effectiveness and impact of it for children and staff.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the timeline following the closure of the consultation on the final report of the Regional Review of Neurology services.
answer_text: The public consultation on the final report of my Department’s Regional Review of Neurology Services ran from 7 May 2025 until 6 August 2025.Now that the consultation has closed my officials are currently reviewing the responses to the consultation which will inform a final implementation plan. I expect this work to be completed in the coming months. Subsequent to this, it is intended that a Neurology Delivery Team will be established to lead on implementation. As the Review was tasked with identifying the optimum configuration for services for the next 10-15 years, the implementation period will extend over many years with some recommendations able to be implemented quicker than others. In this context, the Review report has identified short, medium and long-term actions along with associated timescales.It is clear that full implementation of the Review’s recommendations will require considerable additional investment at a time when my Department is facing significant in year budgetary pressures. It may be the case, therefore, that the pace of implementation will have to be adjusted in line with the funding available. I will however carefully consider funding to implement the Neurology Review in the context of future budget settlements and competing priorities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 24357/22-27, for an update on the nine Special Educational Needs Transformation projects to be delivered using the funding received from the Interim Public Services Transformation Board.
answer_text: Eight of the nine Special Educational Needs (SEN) projects funded by the Public Sector Transformation Fund (PSTF) will commence in the 2025/26 academic year.    The timing for implementation of the Enhanced Nurture project is to be determined, pending the development of long-term options for current Nurture provision, aimed at establishing a sustainable model for both delivery and funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the functions of the new Controlled Schools Unit within the Education Authority.
answer_text: Staff in EA's Controlled School Unit will spend time in the Autumn progressing structured engagement with the controlled sector to shape the development of the Unit's structure and work programme.  This work will precede the Unit becoming fully operational, which should be the case in early 2026.  The work will be initially led by a Programme Director. The main initial function of the CSU will be primarily structured engagement and relationship building. School governance and school improvement linked services will ultimately be part of the functions. Further functions may be added following sector engagement.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any consideration he has given to the Independent Review of Education recommendation to create a one year course for university graduates leading to the award of a Post-Graduate Certificate in Education in special educational needs.
answer_text: As part of the SEN Reform Agenda, my Department is committed to ensuring that all teachers are equipped with the necessary skills and knowledge to effectively support children with special educational needs. My officials are working with Initial Teacher Education (ITE) providers to consider the development of an ITE programme focused on special educational needs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the recent report published by the Neurological Alliance that reveals (i) 64 per cent of people living with a neurological condition in Northern Ireland do not feel supported by the health system; (ii) 60 per cent are unable to get follow-up appointments when they need them; and (ii) more than half, 55 per cent, do not know who to contact when they need support for their neurological condition.
answer_text: I recognise that current pressures within Neurology mean that services are not as responsive as they should be. Many of the issues raised in responses from people in Northern Ireland to the Neurological Alliance’s ‘My Neuro Survey 2024’ are in line with the findings of my Department’s Regional Review of Neurology Services.   A consultation on the final report of the Regional Review is currently underway. The report of the Review identifies four priority areas to drive improvements in Neurology, underpinned by recommendations which, if implemented, will have a direct impact on the issues raised in the Neuro Survey. The report recommends that patients should have a designated point of contact. There are also recommendations for significant workforce growth to bring capacity in line with best practice guidance which will make services more responsive. The additional capacity will also support new ways of working, driving efficiency across Neurology. I must be clear however that implementation of the Review’s recommendations will require significant additional investment with the report identifying a need for over £65m over the first five years of implementation. It is therefore likely that the pace of implementation, will be constrained by the current budgetary pressures faced by my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the number of special schools required to meet demand in Northern Ireland; (ii) the budget required to deliver this; and (iii) when construction will start on any new special schools.
answer_text: As outlined in my April 2024 statement on transforming capital investment to support children with special educational needs, projections indicate a need for up to eight entirely new special schools across Northern Ireland over the next decade.  Estimated construction costs for a new special school are in the region of £45-50 million. Of the five new build special school projects which have already been initiated, estimated construction start dates are detailed in the table below.  I should highlight that at this very early stage these are indicative dates only; they are contingent upon issues such statutory approvals and potential site acquisitions which are outside the direct control of my Department.  SchoolEstimated Construction Start DateBelfast 01February 2028Belfast 02March 2030Ardnashee 02July 2029Knockevin January 2030SperrinviewJune 2030 (pending identification of site)

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 29370/22-27, to detail any work being undertaken to (i) ensure suitable school placements are available in September 2026 for the 1,736 pupils with a statement of special educational needs currently identified in P6; and (ii) ensure appropriate contingency provisions are in place to provide placements for children of this age cohort who are statemented after the October 2024 census date.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that:A critical priority for the Education Authority (EA) is ensuring that all children are placed in an appropriate setting that meets their needs.  In recent years this has been a particular challenge as regards children with Special Educational Needs (SEN).  While the needs of many children with SEN can be met through enhanced support in a mainstream setting, the requirements of children with more acute needs can only be met through either a placement in a Special School, or in Specialist Provision in a mainstream school (essentially a dedicated class with small numbers of children).  The EA have worked to increase capacity across the school estate and as of 30th June 2025, have created 125 new Specialist Provisions classrooms and 29 Special School classrooms for the new school year (2025/26).  Whilst securing placement for this academic year is the key focus of our work at this juncture, early engagement has taken place with post primary schools across the region with number of projects already in planning to expand current accommodation in both specialist provision classes and special schools to support our children and young people with SEN transferring to these settings. EA will continue to liaise with Post Primary Schools in the incoming academic year with a view to increasing capacity for September 2026.It is the vision of the EA to ensure that all children are educated in an inclusive environment in their local community, receiving support in line with their needs identified, to help them to reach their full potential.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) when was he first made aware by the Education Authority that there would not be enough appropriate placements for children with special educational needs for the start of the 2025/26 academic year; and (ii) the action he took following this.
answer_text: I was made aware of the scale of the problem in May 2025 and immediately moved to write personally to the schools identified by the EA as being in areas of priority need who could potentially deliver solutions.I have consistently emphasised that priority must be given to our children with Special Educational Needs (SEN) in the context of an urgent need for reform.SEN placement minor works are delivered under the minor works programme and includes works to deliver additional accommodation in special schools and specialist provision in mainstream schools for SEN placements, but the programme also includes pupil specific works on DDA works and other related minor works.The 2024/25 spend on minor works was £46.76m, while in 2025/26 the spend is currently profiled to be £63.49m.Following my announcement to the Assembly in April 2024 on SEN capital commitments and the positive response to the department’s survey, there was considerable momentum with the EA creating 178 new classes - 112 specialist provisions and 66 new special school classes, providing over 1,450 new special education places for the 2024/25 academic year.  The first Special Education Strategic Area Plan (SESAP) for the period 2022-2027, developed by the EA in partnership with the sectoral bodies, was published in June 2022 and covers special schools and specialist provision in mainstream primary and post-primary schools.  Operational Plans were developed that translated the strategic plan’s vision into practical workings, with the EA publishing its Operational Plan 2 (OP2) 2024-26 on 16 January 2025 which prioritises special education provision, including a review of all special schools.  The department and the EA continue to work closely to ensure there is adequate forward planning based on projected need for specialist education provision which is intended to break the cycle of emergency planning.Furthermore, my department was successful in its bid to secure £27.5m of the Public Sector Transformation Fund through which my SEN Reform Agenda and associated five-year Delivery Plan will seek to ensure a more effective and sustainable model of special education provision.It is crucial that we address the planning, process and communication around placements to make it a more positive experience for children and their families and ultimately to ensure they receive the most appropriate placement to meet their needs.While responsibility for the planning for, and provision of, education places for children with statements of SEN rests with the EA, I am committed to working with it to break the cycle of year on year reacting to pressures and move to more strategic planning.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether the posts of staff who are redeployed to the new Controlled Schools Unit in the Education Authority will be backfilled.
answer_text: The new Controlled Schools Unit (CSU) will be established by September 2025. Further intensive engagement with the controlled school’s sector will then take place to gather insights to help shape the unit’s final structure and work programme. This process should then see the CSU fully operational in early 2026. At this juncture, the overall staffing structure and number of staff that will be operational with the CSU has still to be confirmed. It is anticipated that the CSU will be resourced by internal restructuring and via recruitment where required. In relation to the legislative process required for the provision of a managing authority for controlled schools, it is the intention of myself and Department officials to follow the necessary legislative steps required to bring primary legislation to the Northern Ireland Assembly to ensure that this critical work is carried out correctly for the Controlled School sector. I recently circulated a paper to Executive colleagues outlining my intention to legislate on this matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any measures she is taking to ensure the Ballynahinch Bypass scheme can progress in compliance with provisions of the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.
answer_text: On 12th March 2025 I announced the next steps in the progression of the Ballynahinch bypass scheme.  The A5 judgement was extremely detailed and was the first legal test for the Climate Change Act and the Climate Action Plan – something every department will now have to deal with the outworkings of.  We are working through the judgement and it is too soon to conclude on its impact to wider infrastructure projects.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether staff for the new Controlled Schools Unit in the Education Authority will be redeployed internally or externally recruited.
answer_text: The new Controlled Schools Unit (CSU) will be established by September 2025. Further intensive engagement with the controlled school’s sector will then take place to gather insights to help shape the unit’s final structure and work programme. This process should then see the CSU fully operational in early 2026. At this juncture, the overall staffing structure and number of staff that will be operational with the CSU has still to be confirmed. It is anticipated that the CSU will be resourced by internal restructuring and via recruitment where required. In relation to the legislative process required for the provision of a managing authority for controlled schools, it is the intention of myself and Department officials to follow the necessary legislative steps required to bring primary legislation to the Northern Ireland Assembly to ensure that this critical work is carried out correctly for the Controlled School sector. I recently circulated a paper to Executive colleagues outlining my intention to legislate on this matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the intended timeline for (i) the opening of a new Controlled Schools Unit; and (ii) the legislative process required for the provision of a managing authority for controlled schools
answer_text: The new Controlled Schools Unit (CSU) will be established by September 2025. Further intensive engagement with the controlled school’s sector will then take place to gather insights to help shape the unit’s final structure and work programme. This process should then see the CSU fully operational in early 2026. At this juncture, the overall staffing structure and number of staff that will be operational with the CSU has still to be confirmed. It is anticipated that the CSU will be resourced by internal restructuring and via recruitment where required. In relation to the legislative process required for the provision of a managing authority for controlled schools, it is the intention of myself and Department officials to follow the necessary legislative steps required to bring primary legislation to the Northern Ireland Assembly to ensure that this critical work is carried out correctly for the Controlled School sector. I recently circulated a paper to Executive colleagues outlining my intention to legislate on this matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of full-time equivalent positions that are required to staff the new Controlled Schools Unit.
answer_text: I am pleased to report that work towards creating a Controlled Schools Unit (CSU) within the Education Authority (EA) is on target to establish this by September 2025. 	My officials and the Controlled Schools Support Council (CSSC) have been working in tandem with the EA on this. The EA have confirmed that the Controlled Schools Unit will be established by September 2025 and will be fully operational in early 2026, following further intensive engagement with the controlled school’s sector to gather insights and further shape the unit’s final structure and work programme. Therefore, at this juncture, the full-time equivalent number of staff has still to be confirmed. The CSU will be resourced by internal restructuring and via recruitment where required  A total of £1.2 million was ringfenced as part of the 2025/26 allocation to EA, to develop and create the CSU.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 29164/22-27, to outline when the comprehensive analysis of the therapeutic needs for children with special educational needs in special schools will be completed.
answer_text: The Public Health Agency is leading the work on ‘Towards Understanding Therapeutic Needs’ outlined in AQW 29164/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail an update on the development of an outcomes dashboard for the Special Educational Needs Disability/Reform Agenda.
answer_text: As indicated in the published Special Educational Needs (SEN) Reform Outcomes Framework, an Outcomes dashboard will be developed to report on performance measures throughout the lifetime of the five-year SEN Reform Delivery PlanTechnical expertise is currently being sourced to take forward the development of the Outcomes dashboard.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail how monitoring and analysis of the Special Educational Needs/Disability Reform Agenda Outcomes Framework 2025-2030 will take place with (i) children and young people; (ii) parents and carers; and (iii) staff and schools.
answer_text: The Special Educational Needs (SEN) Reform Agenda Outcomes Framework was developed following extensive engagement with a wide range of stakeholders involved in the End to End (E2E) Review of SEN/Disability (SEN/D).  Engagement included Government departments and Arms Length Bodies (ALBs), Community and Voluntary sector representatives, academics, schools, practitioners, children and young people and parents/carers.Performance measures have been developed using an Outcomes Based Accountability™ (OBA) approach and includes population indicators for the following four identified groups:  children and young people, parents and carers, staff and schools and services.  Together, these population indicators will measure the impact of delivery of the SEN Reform and Delivery Plan actions at a population level. Collection of data will be via surveys and analysis of existing data sources.  For those data required through surveys, baseline data will be gathered at the beginning of the 2025 academic year and followed up with annual surveys to monitor performance.  Data will also be gathered from existing Northern Ireland Statistics and Research Agency (NISRA) data sources across Health and Education and administrative data sources held by the Education Authority (EA). Monitoring and analysis will take place throughout 2025-2030 with annual reporting.  Contribution to outcomes will be monitored via ‘direction of travel on the population indicators’ and whether things are progressing positively, negatively or remaining static.  The SEN Reform Outcomes Framework details all of the above and also covers how individual actions will be monitored.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when the comprehensive analysis of the therapeutic needs for children with special educational needs in special schools will be completed.
answer_text: The Public Health Agency is undertaking a comprehensive analysis to identify what the assessed therapeutic needs are for children and young people (CYP) who attend Special School to inform the development of an effective model of therapy provision and associated workforce development and planning. The first stage of this analysis is the completion of a comprehensive scope of the assessed needs of CYP in the Special School sector in respect of Occupational Therapy, Orthoptics, Physiotherapy and Speech and Language Therapy. It is expected that the findings of this stage will be complete by the end of 2025.These findings will be crucial to inform the next stage of the exercise, which will involve careful analysis of the capacity, workforce, model and skill mix required to meet the identified demand and to inform the development of an integrated service pathway proposal that meets the needs of the CYP. It is anticipated this second phase will be completed by the end of 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the sickness absence rate for health care professionals working in neurology services in each of the last 12 months, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
To ask the Minister of Health what mental health and emotional support services are available to multiple sclerosis patients at the Ulster Hospital.
To ask the Minister of Health whether there are any vacancies for multiple sclerosis nurses in the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust service for people with multiple sclerosis (MS) is supported by three specialist MS nursing staff.All three posts are filled and there are no vacancies. However, the nursing cover for the MS service has been affected by periods of unplanned absence. There are currently two members of staff available to support the service.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he intends to encourage prioritising training students in (i) neurology; (ii) neurological physiotherapy; and (iii) neuropsychology to (a) incentivise it as a speciality; and (b) reduce workforce shortfalls.
To ask the Minister of Health what actions he is taking to ensure that the multiple sclerosis service in the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust is adequately staffed at all levels.
answer_text: Services for people with multiple sclerosis (MS) are available as part of wider Neurology service provision in each Trust area. The teams of staff dedicated to MS services in each Trust reflect the cohorts of people being managed and can be relatively small as part of the wider neurology teams who provide care across a range of conditions. The services can therefore, at times, be vulnerable if there are any unplanned absences in the team. However, this needs to be weighed against making service provision more locally accessible for patients who can often have mobility issues.Officials from my Department work closely with Trusts, including South Eastern Trust, to assess the needs of people with MS and over the years investment has been made to allow:Appointment of additional medical staff with a specific interest in MS servicesThe provision of dedicated multi-disciplinary clinics for people with MS;Appointment of dedicated MS nursing and physiotherapy staff;Local provision of infusion delivered disease modifying treatments (DMTs);Local prescribing and dispensing of other disease modifying medications for patients.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether there is any legal provision permitting the use of reasonable force in youth settings.
answer_text: There is no legal framework for the use of reasonable force in youth settings.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the amount of funding that has been allocated to delivering teachers’ focus groups on key issues and challenges, on 25 and 26 June 2025.
answer_text: Planning work for the focus groups commenced in late May 2025. The purpose of the groups is to gather authentic insights into the perspectives of both teachers and parents on a wide range of education topics, which will support my Department in developing effective approaches, policies and programmes. The use of such groups is common across government and the employment of trained moderators, adept at creating neutral, open environments that encourage honest and diverse feedback, is designed to minimise bias and ensures that the data collected is reliable, objective and rigorous.  A full report on the outcomes of the groups will be completed by the end of July 2025. The short window for teacher recruitment was to facilitate the groups taking place before the summer holidays.  This had no significant impact on recruitment, with over 800 teachers expressing interest in participation. Government conducts ongoing wide-ranging engagement with multiple stakeholders on a significant range of issues.  Stakeholder consultation and engagement is at the heart of good government.  There is no anticipated impact of four teacher focus groups assembled to discuss a wide variety of education issues on engagement with the Independent Teacher Workload Panel.  Indeed, the Panel’s engagement is scheduled for August. There is no requirement for consultation with Teaching Unions prior to a request for voluntary engagement and my officials regularly engage with practitioners across the education system. Total costs of the project are £23,470 and an approved business case is in place.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the work that has been undertaken to date to understand the needs of children in special schools.
answer_text: Following engagement with stakeholders late last year, my Chief Nursing Officer commissioned the Public Health Agency (PHA) to work alongside colleagues within the Education sector to undertake a review of the population health needs of children attending Special Educational Needs (SEN) schools in Northern Ireland and the current nursing contribution in meeting their health needs.A draft version of the PHA’s findings has been submitted to my Department and is currently being assessed by the Chief Nursing Officer. While some further refinements, data collection and analysis are likely to be required I expect the report to be finalised and provided to me later in the summer. In addition to this work, as part of its normal function the PHA, in partnership with Education sector colleagues, is leading a comprehensive analysis of the therapeutic needs of children with Special Educational Needs in Special Schools as set out in my response to AQW 29514/22-27.This work is supported within each Trust by identified Project Leads, with four of these posts having been recruited to, and the remaining Trust actively engaged in recruitment procedures.The PHA is actively collaborating with colleagues in the Education Authority (EA) and HSC Trusts to agree and develop clear and timely arrangements to facilitate the secure exchange of relevant, up to date child specific educational based data (in line with GDPR requirements) which is key in supporting this exercise.Joint working with key stakeholders across EA and HSCTs has facilitated the development and testing of a robust data capture tool that will collate HSCT data sets alongside that provided by EA.  This tool has completed a testing phase and is ready to be implemented by HSCT SEN Project Managers.Both these exercises will provide evidence-based assessments of the complex healthcare and therapeutic needs of children and young people in SEN schools, to inform a wider, child centred multidisciplinary understanding, which in turn will help support decisions around how to best meet the population health needs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the work that has been undertaken to date to understand the needs of children in special schools.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that the Public Health Agency and the EA continue to work together on ‘Towards Understanding Therapeutic Needs’. A number of meetings have taken place and discussion is currently focussed on appropriate and relevant data sharing with advice from information governance colleagues. EA is supporting this work particularly in relation to accessing information in a comprehensive and timely manner.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 28307/22-27, to detail any consideration he will give to making these conditions medical categories for inclusion on a school’s medical register to aid data collection.
answer_text: During the End to End Review of Special Educational Needs (SEN) in 2023/24, my officials along with representatives from schools, Department of Health, Health and Social Care Trusts, Education Authority, and Royal College of Speech and Language Therapists completed a review of the current list of SEN and Medical categories. Findings from stakeholder engagement alongside a cross jurisdictional analysis led to a decision that no immediate changes were required to the overarching categories on the medical register. Nonetheless, this will be kept under ongoing review to ensure that categories remain evidence based and continue to reflect the changing needs of children and young people in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail why there is no specific measure to monitor the (i) timeliness; and (ii) appropriateness, of placements for learners with special educational needs and disability in the Special Educational Needs Reform Agenda Outcomes Framework 2025-30.
answer_text: The timeliness and appropriateness of placements for learners with special educational needs and disability will be monitored under the SEN Reform Outcomes Framework population indicator ‘Effectiveness of SEN/D services: % of children and young people who transition successfully between education stages'.The following population indicators will also provide data on the timeliness and appropriateness of school placements from a parental perspective:  % of parents and carers satisfied with the quality and timeliness of SEN/D support their child receives; and % of parents and carers who report that their child’s needs are being met in a timely manner.As indicated in the SEN Reform Outcomes Framework, a performance level report card will be developed for each action within the SEN Reform Delivery Plan as they are progressed and will include reporting against agreed performance measures.  This will include the actions under ‘Timely Confirmation of School Placements’.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the steps being taken to ensure that basic staff support and well-being measures are in place within the (i) radiology; and (ii) oncology departments.

To ask the Minister of Health whether he intends to encourage prioritising training students in (i) oncology; and (ii) radiology to (a) incentivise it as a speciality; and (b) reduce the workforce shortfalls.
answer_text: My officials work closely with colleagues in the Northern Ireland Medical and Dental Training Agency, who manage the medical specialty training programmes on my Department’s behalf, to ensure that all posts on these programmes are filled on a timely basis each year. This includes considering how to address any potential shortfalls in recruitment, for example running a local recruitment process for certain specialties instead of the normal UK-wide recruitment process. The Agency is currently developing plans to address difficulties in attracting applicants to the oncology specialty.My recent announcement on 14 May of a record £160m investment in workforce commissioning across all professions included 24 new medical specialty training posts which comprised 13 in Internal Medicine, 5 in Core Psychiatry, and 6 in other specialties. I can advise that 1 of these 6 other training posts will be in the Radiology training programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the steps being taken to reduce staff shortages within (i) radiology; and (ii) oncology.
answer_text: Trusts are implementing a number of strategies to address workforce challenges and reduce staff shortages within radiology and oncology.Trusts are actively engaged in targeted local, regional and international recruitment campaigns to ensure vacancies are filled as quickly as possible.   Strong links with workforce planning teams and regional recruitment efforts ensure that vacancies are prioritised and advertised in a timely and strategic manner.  Trusts currently utilise the support of locum doctors and the independent sector to cover vacancies and ensure services are maintained.To maximise existing workforce capacity, Trusts are expanding advance practice roles.  These roles can help alleviate pressure on consultants as well as offering a structured pathway for career progression.  In respect of Radiology, a business case for the Multi-professional Regional Imaging Academy is being developed by the Belfast HSC Trust.  However, the development remains subject to business case approval and the identification of additional recurrent funding to take it forward. Staff retention is also a key focus of Trusts.  Initiatives to support staff wellbeing, flexible working and career development are in place to help retain experienced staff.  Regular staff engagement through team meetings, feedback mechanisms and recognition programmes help foster a positive and supportive working environment.As part of a long term strategy, Trusts are actively involved in education and training initiatives.  This includes promoting professions through participation in careers fairs, offering work experience placements, and delivering talks at local schools and colleges.  Additionally, Trusts are working with academic partners to provide high quality clinical placements and expand training posts, particularly in radiology.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline any long-term workforce growth planning within (i) radiology; and (ii) oncology to meet the increasing demand.
answer_text: (i) Imaging services underpin the vast majority of hospital pathways and clinical specialties and my Department has invested significantly to grow the future imaging workforce in Northern Ireland. Commissioned radiology medical specialty training places have expanded from 37 (2015/16) to 55 places (2025/26) – a 49% increase.As the local radiology training scheme is now at almost maximum capacity, my Department recognises that an innovative approach is required to meet future demand. Establishment of a multi-professional regional Imaging Academy is a strategic commitment for the Department and the longer-term solution to addressing current challenges in HSC imaging services.  The Academy aims to provide a continuous supply of locally trained consultant radiologists (growing capacity to 100 training places) and advanced radiographer practitioners to address the increasing demand for imaging investigations and reduce reliance on costly outsourcing.This will deliver an overall increase in imaging capacity within HSC, benefitting all clinical specialties and helping reduce excess waiting lists for diagnosis and treatment, as well as meeting the demands from unscheduled care.A business case for the Academy is being developed by the Belfast HSC Trust.  However, the development and any acceleration of it remains subject to business case approval and the identification of additional recurrent funding.(ii) In terms of oncology, my Department is similarly aware of the need to expand the capacity of our existing workforce to meet demand and to train the next generation of oncology professionals.  My Department has no current plans to increase further the local clinical oncology medical specialty training programme.  However, since 2019/20, a key priority of my Department is to address ongoing resilience issues regarding oncology and haematology services stabilisation and the delivery of Systemic Anti-Cancer Therapy (SACT).  By 2024/25, my Department invested a total of £10.8m across all five HSC Trusts for Oncology and Haematology Stabilisation Plans.  This has funded 167.76 whole time equivalent (WTE) additional staff across a range of health professions, including 9.75 WTE oncology consultant posts.  These will improve consultant capacity and skills mix in oncology and haemato-oncology services and will help to streamline pathways to reduce bottlenecks and expedite treatment initiation.  HSC Trusts continue working to fill posts with ongoing recruitment strategies.  In addition, £2.5m recurrent funding has been identified in the 2025/26 Elective Care Framework Implementation and Funding Plan to help complete Oncology and Haematology Stabilisation Plans.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 28567/22-27, to detail which projects will be funded from the £70m allocated to the SEN Capital Investment Programme in 2025-26.
answer_text: The current profiled spend across the SEN Capital Investment Programme for 2025/26 is as detailed below, you will note that there is already a £4m pressure on this £70m:£60m on over 400 minor works schemes in special schools and to provide additional specialist provision classes and pupil specific works in mainstream schools;£10m across the existing 10 school enhancement projects in special schools;£0.7m on the five projects for new build special schools; £3.4m in furniture and equipment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any consideration he has given to funding Action for Children's, The Blues Programme, as part of the Emotional Health and Wellbeing Framework.
answer_text: Programmes funded by the Children and Young People’s Emotional Health and Wellbeing in Education Framework are led by the Education Authority and the Department of Health / Public Health Agency.  My department does not promote, nor endorse external services or programmes, with the Framework written in an intentionally non-prescriptive way in respect of the programmes that can be provided for pupils and providing guidance on selecting programmes and services. Schools have flexibility around the type of support provided for pupils and authority to use their individual budgets to fund external programmes based on their own assessment of the needs of their pupils.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 28567/22-27, to detail which actions will be funded in 2025-26 by the of £7.5m budget ring-fenced for the delivery of the Special Educational Needs Reform Agenda and Delivery Plan.
answer_text: The £7.5m ring-fenced Transformation funding budget for 2025-26 will fund the nine Special Educational Needs (SEN) projects outlined in my previous response to AQW 24357/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 28567/22-27, to detail the specific actions which will be funded in 2025-26 by the budget of £3m ring-fenced from the Early Learning and Childcare Strategy funding.
answer_text: The £3m ring-fenced budget from the Early Learning and Childcare (ELC) Strategy will fund the action within the Special Educational Needs (SEN) Reform Delivery Plan ‘Resource pre-school education settings to support children undergoing statutory assessment’ through the pilot Pre-School Education Inclusion Support Fund (PSEISF).

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail how he is promoting opportunities for young people to collaborate on community projects, as a way to tackle sectarian bullying behaviour.
answer_text: The Department for Communities (DfC) is responsible for taking forward interventions that contribute to The Executive Programme on Paramilitarism and Organised Crime (EPPOC).  DfC’ s two main areas of work on EPOCC are known as ‘Fresh Start in the Community Projects’ and ‘The Developing Women in the Community Programme’. These projects provide primary intervention in communities where paramilitarism is prevalent, helping to build capacity, skills and aspirations, active citizenship, encouraging positive decision making and help minimising the impact of negative influences, whilst indirectly having a positive impact on communities in terms of encouraging and creating a more tolerant and respectful environment for all.Additionally, although there are no projects specifically funded to tackle sectarian bullying behaviour within the Executives People and Place Strategy: A strategy for neighbourhood renewal (2003), as delivered by DfC through the Neighbourhood Renewal Programme. The programme does however support a wide range of community empowerment initiatives in the most deprived areas across Northern Ireland, engaging young people in positive community-based activities, encouraging collaboration across community lines, and promoting inclusion and mutual respect, contributing to more cohesive and resilient communities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail how schools are supported to create inclusive policies which create safe environments for young people to express their identities, without fear of discrimination.
answer_text: Boards of Governors of grant-aided schools have statutory duties to safeguard the welfare of all their pupils and schools should strive to provide a safe and secure learning environment.My department has commenced work on the development of an Inclusive System Development Programme and the research, design and trial of inclusion methodologies to promote equitable inclusive learning experiences.  Whilst this is one of the actions in the Special Educational Needs (SEN) Reform Delivery Plan, the programme relates to all children at risk of exclusion from education but will focus on SEN in the initial phases.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how he will ensure that the children’s workforce have a trauma-informed approach to sectarian bullying behaviour.
answer_text: The Addressing Bullying in Schools Act (Northern Ireland) 2016 came into effect in September 2021, and it requires schools to record incidents or alleged incidents of bullying type behaviour, their motivation and their outcome.  Each school’s Board of Governors (BoG) has responsibility to oversee the implementation of the Act in their school.   The Act places a statutory duty on a BoG collectively to take responsibility for the development, implementation, monitoring and periodic review of the school’s Addressing Bullying policies and procedures. The Education Authority (EA) has several services whose methodology is underpinned by the need to promote and model trauma informed approaches to meeting the needs of pupils, staff and school communities.  Trauma informed services include the Intercultural Education Service; Primary Children Looked After Advisory Service; Being Well Doing Well; Nurturing Approaches in Schools Service; the Restorative Practice team; Primary and Post Primary Behaviour Support; and the Addressing Bullying in Schools Implementation Team (ABSIT). ABSIT was established in September 2022 to provide schools’ workforce with the skills, knowledge and support required for the implementation of the Act.Furthermore, the EA, in partnership with the Safeguarding Board for Northern Ireland, has made available Trauma Informed Practice Level 1 Training which is an online resource that staff can access.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline any consideration he has given to incorporating education on sectarianism in History and Citizenship lessons.
answer_text: The current Northern Ireland Curriculum provides opportunities for young people to explore diversity, identity, and community in meaningful, age-appropriate ways through the statutory Areas of Learning of Personal Development and Mutual Understanding (PDMU) and Learning for Life and Work (LLW).The Strategic Review of the Northern Ireland Curriculum was published on 3 June 2025 and provides a compelling case for comprehensive curriculum reform.  The report makes 21 recommendations and sets out the need for a new curriculum framework that is purpose-led; knowledge-rich; continuous and coherent; specific and focussed; and flexible and inclusive.  The Review recommends the rebranding and designing of LLW, as well as the need for a new Citizenship, Employability and Personal Development Programme.    I have announced my intention to establish a Curriculum Taskforce to lead the design and development of a new curriculum framework that embodies the principles outlined in the Review and meets the evolving needs of learners across Northern Ireland.  The Taskforce will be operational in Autumn 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline if there is an obligation for schools to create anti-bullying policies that specifically address sectarian bullying behaviour.
answer_text: The Addressing Bullying in Schools Act (Northern Ireland) 2016 (the Act), requires schools to record incidents of bullying type behaviour, their motivation, methods used and their outcome.  It requires all schools to have an Anti-Bullying policy and preventative measures which are kept under regular review. The legislation provides guidance on exemplar motivations to inform the school assessment and recording process. This list reflects Section 75 groups including those who may be targeted due to their religious belief or political opinion.Capturing potential motivation for reported bullying type behaviour is important to enable identification of school specific issues, patterns and trends, such as sectarian bullying type behaviour. In this data informed context, staff are better placed to address need appropriately with tailored pastoral, safeguarding and curricular interventions.The Addressing Bullying in Schools Implementation Team (ABSIT) within the Education Authority provides support for schools, educational settings, young people and parents/carers to help them understand the requirements of the Act. ABSIT focuses on supporting the development of inclusive school based systemic approach to empower staff in effective prevention and response to bullying type behaviour. The team provides bespoke support with complex incidents of bullying type behaviour to aid inclusion, including sectarian bullying type behaviour. ABSIT is a member of the Safeguarding Board Northern Ireland (SBNI) and Anti Bullying Alliance which are umbrella organisations comprising broad representation from third sector bodies, many of whom address the needs of Section 75 communities. ABSIT provides advice and support to schools on concerns of bullying type behaviour and, following a schools collaboration programme, has co-developed three tiers of updated training and resources aimed at effectively preventing and responding to reported concerns in line with legislation and guidance.Tier 1 Training: Online session for all staff working in schools.Tier 2: Leadership training and support for school-identified staff.Tier 3: School based training-Targeted advice for key staff working with complex casesABSIT has provided support and tailored advice for 327 cases from April 2024 to March 2025. ABSIT have confirmed that in two cases the motivation for the bullying type behaviour was religion/sectarian (0.6%).ABSIT has co-developed resources for use with children and young people. This has included support for children, young people, and their families in line with the Addressing Bullying in Schools Act (Northern Ireland) 2016; advice for parents, carers and pupils regarding how bullying type behaviour is identified and supported in schools; and assistance with recognising signs that a pupil could be experiencing bullying type behaviour and signposting to further support.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline how he will ensure that young people who experience sectarian bullying feel confident in reporting this.
answer_text: The Addressing Bullying in Schools Act (Northern Ireland) 2016 (the Act), requires schools to record incidents of bullying type behaviour, their motivation, methods used and their outcome.
