answer_text: The power to set admissions criteria lies solely with the Boards of Governors (BoGs) of individual schools and does not lie with my department.  My department does, however, in accordance with Article 16B of the Education (Northern Ireland) Order 1997, issue guidance on the transfer process, including on the type of admissions criteria that are recommended and that are not recommended.  Article 16B (3) requires BoGs to “have regard to” this guidance, however, as adherence to my department’s recommended admissions criteria is not compulsory, it would not be appropriate to direct a school to change its admissions criteria simply because they depart from those that are recommended.It should be noted that the latest version of the DE Circular 2022/15 is Circular 2024/18.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the Review on the Use of Temporary and Substitute Teachers.
answer_text: The Review on the Use of Temporary and Substitute Teachers was one of the nine workforce reviews identified through the 2020 pay agreement.Eight of the reviews, including the Review on the Use of Temporary and Substitute Teachers were completed by joint Northern Ireland Teachers’ Council (NITC) and Management Side working groups and reports from the reviews were submitted to the Teachers’ Negotiating Committee (TNC) for consideration. A number of the recommendations in the report from the Review on the Use of Temporary and Substitute Teachers have already been actioned and it was recognised by TNC that others would require additional resources which are not available at this time.The review also resulted in improvements in NISTR, the system used to source temporary and substitute teachers which was updated in 2022.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he has any plans to review DE Circular 2022/15.
answer_text: The latest version of DE Circular 2022/15 is DE Circular 2024/18 ‘The Procedure for Transfer from Primary to Post-Primary Education’.My department’s Open Enrolment circulars are updated and reissued on an annual basis.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether statutory pre-school settings receive funding for non-target age children in their intake.
answer_text: I refer the member to my answer to AQW 23664/22-27 which sets out in detail funding provided to statutory pre-school settings for non-target age children. The answer is contained in the weekly answer booklet week ending 21 March 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of statutory pre-school settings in 2024-25 that had non-target age children in their intake.
answer_text: In the current 2024-25 academic year 183 statutory pre-school settings have admitted penultimate age children with 1,396 obtaining a funded pre-school education place.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the level of per pupil funding for pre-school settings allocated to (i) statutory settings; and (ii) non-statutory settings in the 2024-25 academic year.
answer_text: The Common Funding Formula is the Department’s mechanism for allocating budgets to Statutory pre-school settings funded through Local Management of Schools arrangements.  The current pupil Age Weighted Pupil Unit (AWPU) for statutory pre-school settings is set out below.  Statutory settings are funded based on the enrolment data on the school census immediately preceding the financial year to which funding relates (i.e. 2024/25 CFF is based upon the October 2023 census).Non-statutory providers participating in the Pre-School Education Programme receive a real-time per-pupil funding allocation (i.e. 2024/25 funding is based on 2024/25 attendance), as set out below. There is no full-time non-statutory provision for the 2024-25 academic year, so no funding rate is available.Per-Pupil Funding – 2024-25 Financial Year Part-time (£)Full-time (£)Statutory nursery school2,6924,093Statutory nursery unit2,5233,806Non-statutory2,550N/A

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what percentage of pre-school settings in 2024-25 were from the non-statutory sector.
answer_text: In the 2024-25 academic year there are a total of 671 funded pre-school education settings participating in the Pre-School Education Programme.  322 of these, or 48%, are non-statutory settings.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of the Independent Review of Education's recommendation that the current arrangements relating to funding, staff ratios and qualifications in public sector pre-school settings should be applied to private sector provision as soon as possible.
answer_text: In 2022 my Department commissioned a scoping study to analyse the current state of the pre-school education sector and cost a range of options for standardisation of funded pre-school education provision, including in relation to funding, staff ratios and qualifications. The report was circulated to Executive colleagues and is available on the Department’s website at Final Scoping Study Report | Department of Education (education-ni.gov.uk)The recommendations contained in that report will be reflected in the Executive Early Learning and Childcare Strategy I plan to bring to the Executive in autumn of this year.  However, implementation will be reliant on Executive funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether the Education Information Solutions system will be operational for the start of the 2025-26 academic year.
answer_text: The EdIS Programme comprises a total of 33 discrete projects, many of which are at varying phases of development.18 projects are complete, including Broadband and Wireless connectivity in all schools including statutory Nursery Schools for the first time. The Northern Ireland Supply Teacher Register, the admissions and appeals process, Special Educational Needs annual review, free school meals and uniform grants have all being digitised under this programme.8 projects are currently in progress with a further 7 projects not yet started. From those projects in progress the Schools Management System (SMS) is currently in pre-pilot phase with implementation expected to begin in May 2025 and full implementation to be completed in August 2026. This will see a replacement to the current Schools Information Management Solution.Continuity of services to schools during implementation will be provided by the incumbent managed services provider, Capita, under its existing contract i.e. Education Network Northern Ireland, which has been extended until 31 March 2027.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the policy or legislation used to determine the Pupil Allocation Number for non-statutory pre-school settings.
answer_text: I would refer the member to my previous response to AQW 24601/22-27, published in the weekly answer booklet for week ending 28 March 2025.The Education (Northern Ireland) Order 1998 (as amended) provides for a range of duties and powers for the Education Authority in relation to its role in securing pre-school education provision and providing an admissions process to access that provision.  These duties and powers must be read in conjunction with other relevant duties and legislation, for example in relation to the use of public funds and specific requirements in relation to, for example, Irish medium and Integrated education. I acknowledge that there may be ways in which the EA’s PAN allocation process could be improved. To that end, I have requested the Chief Executive of the EA to instruct his officials to carry out a full review of their PAN allocation processes and explore potential options for improvement.  Proposed changes coming out of the review will be considered for inclusion within the draft Early Learning and Childcare Strategy due to be published this autumn.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on the Child and Adolescent Mental Health Services pilot in the Youth Justice Agency.

To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 24360/22-27, to detail the number of residential short break beds are assessed as being required by South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust Children's Services.
answer_text: The South Eastern Trust has been working with the Strategic Reform Board and the associated work streams, established by my Department to deliver upon the recommendations arising from the Independent review of Social work and Social care (DoH:2022).  The South Eastern Trust has scoped the number of short break residential beds needed and require an additional two 4 placement homes to match the historic short fall of short break services and to address the waiting list for Residential Short Breaks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 24784/22-27, whether a code will be created to identify facial palsy patients to support the development of a facial palsy pathway.
answer_text: Facial palsy is almost always a presentation of an underlying condition with causes ranging from vascular, viral , trauma or as yet undiagnosed origin.  The pathway for each of these conditions will vary consistent with the cause and severity.  In this regard a code for facial palsy as the primary definition on a patient may not prove helpful in navigating the patient to the next appropriate service.A new facial palsy referral/assessment pathway has been developed for adults and children.  This has been a complex process with multiple services involved in the pathway development, and multiple access points into the service across all Trusts in Northern Ireland.  These standardised pathways will ensure appropriate treatment of patients in local services as far as possible and will also outline the appropriate referral pathway for those requiring more enhanced specialist intervention. Communication will be issued to each Trust in the coming weeks to inform them of the agreed pathway and an information webinar will be delivered to GPs and Emergency Departments to aide with timely and appropriate referral.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education when he will publish the review which has been completed by the Taskforce created to identify solutions to better support the Controlled Schools sector.
answer_text: The full report from the Controlled Schools Taskforce, which was presented to me on 20 December 2024, is available on the Department’s website: https://www.education-ni.gov.uk/publications/taskforce-report-development-proposed-model-support-controlled-schools

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of the use of new technology to repair potholes, similar to those currently being utilised across various local authorities in England.
answer_text: My Department works closely with the construction industry to monitor new technology and innovations in respect of pothole repairs.The Department keeps informed about new approaches to road maintenance through its engagement with other road authorities, Professional Engineering Institutions, universities and the wider construction and infrastructure industry. We are very open to adopting new approaches and innovative materials that offer an improvement to our current approach to road defect repair. Through my Department’s current work on developing a new Road Maintenance Strategy we are actively exploring how emerging technology can be used to focus our investments where they are needed most and better maintain the road network.My Department will continue to liaise with contractors and suppliers to identify any opportunities in the future that could improve the repair of road defects.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 23755/22-27, to detail the actions relating to transitions planning for young people with a statement of special educational need due to leave statutory education that are contained in the Special Educational Need Reform Agenda Delivery Plan 2025-30.
answer_text: Under the pillar ‘Right Time’ in the SEN Reform Agenda and Delivery Plan there is the overarching action for a ‘Transitional Support Programme at various stages of learner’s journey’, with a sub-action to ‘Work with Health, Economy and Communities to develop appropriate pathways into adulthood for school leavers with SEN’.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education when he will publish (i) a Special Educational Needs Policy Statement; and (ii) an Inclusive Education Policy.
answer_text: The publication and application of a Special Educational Needs (SEN) Policy Statement to clearly set out the legislative position and the roles and responsibilities of the Department and the Education Authority (EA) is an enabling action within the SEN Reform Delivery Plan.  The Policy Statement is currently being reviewed with planned publication in the coming weeks.Supporting inclusion for children and young people with SEN is an underpinning principle throughout the SEN Reform Agenda and Delivery Plan and the development of a longer-term plan to support inclusion is a specific action within the Delivery Plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 23755/22-27, to detail the actions being taken forward based on the work of the Transitions Workstream of the End to End Review of Special Educational Needs.
answer_text: The Transitions Working Group have informed a range of specific actions relating to the transitions in the SEN Reform Agenda and Delivery Plan 2025-2030. The actions include a Transitional Support Programme, at various stages of the learner’s journey, as well as continuing to work with Health, Economy, and Communities to develop appropriate pathways into adulthood for school leavers with SEN.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 23860/22-27, whether a designated absence code will be created to record the numbers of pupils on a reduced timetable.
answer_text: Officials are working closely with EA colleagues to ensure that it will be possible to record absences at a more detailed level than at present through the use of a designated absence code from the commencement of the 2025/26 academic year, subject to the implementation of Edis.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how many of the 600 houses approved to be purchased by the Housing Executive for temporary accommodation will be in the Strangford constituency.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.The planned acquisition of 600 properties will be focused on reducing NIHE’s current exposure to high-cost temporary accommodation (particularly hotels and B&Bs).While the final locations have yet to be determined the principal considerations will include:Geographical areas where exposure to high-cost temporary accommodation is most prevalent.The nature of the temporary accommodation client groups (single people, couples, families).The availability of suitable properties to purchase that align with the above criteria.Value for money considerations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when the Public Health Agency plans to next run the FAST campaign to highlight the symptoms of stroke, given the successful evaluation of the 2023 campaign.
answer_text: I recognise the success of the Public Health Agency’s FAST campaign in helping people to recognise the signs of having a stroke and promoting the need to call 999 immediately if someone is showing signs of a stroke. While I would like to be in a position to commit to funding the FAST campaign again, the budgetary pressures imposed on my Department has required a pause on most health-related advertising spend.  Consequently, there are no current plans to rerun the FAST campaign.The Public Health Agency will, however, continue to promote FAST awareness messaging through non-paid for channels including social media platforms and media activity. This includes plans to undertake more comprehensive stroke messaging later this year to coincide with Stroke Awareness Month in May 2025 and World Stroke Day on 29 October 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 24357/22-27, (i) when the details and scope of the nine special educational needs transformation projects will be published; and (ii) when the outcomes frameworks for the nine projects will be published.
answer_text: Work is ongoing to complete a phased, costed operational plan which will sit behind the Special Educational Needs (SEN) Reform Delivery Plan and includes the nine projects funded from the Public Service Transformation Fund.The Outcomes Framework for the SEN Reform Agenda, which includes the nine Transformation Fund projects, is under development and is expected to be completed and published during Spring 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail how he will ensure the recently announced Key Stage Assessments do not disproportionately impact teacher workload in the selected participating sample schools.
answer_text: The statutory Key Stage assessments have been specifically designed to minimise bureaucracy and workload for schools. Outcomes will only be published at system level and will not be used to measure individual school performance nor published at pupil or school level. This means they will be very low stakes at pupil and school level meaning there is no need for selected sample schools to engage in test preparation. The Council for the Curriculum, Examination and Assessments will be responsible for designing and marking the statutory Key Stage assessments. Substitute cover will be provided to assist the invigilation of the assessments. This approach will further lessen workload for schools.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for her assessment of the living roof bus shelters in Belfast; and (ii) whether she will make funding available for living roof bus shelters to other local council areas.
answer_text: As part of a programme of phased improvements Translink is planning upgrades along a number of Metro routes including the installation of Living Roofs on some bus shelters to promote biodiversity and create a more sustainable environment. Work on this project commenced in July 2024 with the installation of the first Living Roof bus shelter on Belfast’s Shankill Road. 		My officials are currently considering a business case from Translink seeking to replace/refurbish existing bus shelters over a ten-year period. This will also include installing several new bus shelters across the public transport network, some of which may be adapted to include green roofs. This work will be progressed subject to the availability of funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the delivery of the Comber bypass.
answer_text: Major Road schemes are an important part of the work that is delivered by my Department as we seek to reduce journey times, increase reliability and improve road safety.Comber Bypass is not a current scheme listed on the Major Works Programme. Any decision on an additional phase of the Comber Bypass will be informed through the development of the Eastern Transport Plan.Details of the Eastern Transport Plan can be found via the following link;https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/articles/eastern-transport-plan-etp-2035

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail who will be responsible for marking the recently announced Key Stage Assessments.
answer_text: The statutory Key Stage assessments have been specifically designed to minimise bureaucracy and workload for schools. Outcomes will only be published at system level and will not be used to measure individual school performance nor published at pupil or school level. This means they will be very low stakes at pupil and school level meaning there is no need for selected sample schools to engage in test preparation. The Council for the Curriculum, Examination and Assessments will be responsible for designing and marking the statutory Key Stage assessments. Substitute cover will be provided to assist the invigilation of the assessments. This approach will further lessen workload for schools.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he plans to protect the budget for the Pathway Fund in the 2025-26 budget.
answer_text: The increased level of eligible demand for the Pathway Fund exceeds what can be funded from my Department’s 2025-26 opening resource allocation.  I therefore intend to seek Executive approval to enhance the Pathway Fund budget from the Early Learning and Childcare Strategy funding being held centrally.  This reflects the approach taken in 2024-25.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will commit to meeting Gael Linn to discuss the positive impact of the Scoil Spreagtha scheme for children in all school sectors in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: To date, my Department has not been involved in the Scoil Spreagtha project nor has it provided any funding.  As a result, I have no plans presently to meet with Gael Linn to discuss this scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of patients who have received treatment relating to facial palsy from the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust Plastics Service in the last year are currently awaiting a follow up appointment, broken down by length of time they have been awaiting this appointment.
answer_text: The South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust have advised that they are not able to provide this information.  Treatments relating to facial palsy are not coded as 'Facial Palsy' and may be used to treat other conditions, therefore it is not possible to extract this specific information accurately.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of patients that have received treatment relating to facial palsy from the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust Plastics Service in the last year.
answer_text: The South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust is not able to provide this information.  Treatments relating to facial palsy are not coded as 'facial palsy' and may be used to treat other conditions, therefore it is not possible to extract this specific information accurately.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 24260/22-27, when the work by the Education Authority to clear the Title to the property will be completed.
answer_text: The lengthy timeline associated with removing the covenant on the title deeds relating to provision of education are mainly outside the Education Authority’s (EA) control, as identification and engagement with the relevant title owners have proven difficult up to this point.For that reason, EA solicitors have advised they are not in a position confirm the timescale, or provide a target completion date, as to when the work to clear the Title to the property will be completed.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission for an update on allowances to improve disability access to constituency offices.
To ask the Minister of Health whether he plans to close the Greenhill facility, which currently provides respite short breaks in the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: There are no plans to close Greenhill. The Trust currently funds Greenhill for two beds 5 nights per week. The additional investment I have announced will enable the Trust to expand this provision to 2 beds for 7 nights per week. This expansion will be contingent upon the successful recruitment of an appropriate workforce. A dedicated recruitment campaign will commence during April 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) when the Redwood facility will be open to provide overnight respite stays in the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust; and (ii) the number of respite beds it will provide.
answer_text: The Trust plans to have Redwood Children’s Home operational by September 2025 and will be registered as a Longer-term home delivering two placements, one of which is intended to be shared between two young people. Redwood will not deliver short breaks or respite. The opening of Redwood will enable the opening of Lindsay House to deliver Short Breaks by September 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of staff required to safely deliver a full overnight respite stay in the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Health and Social Care Trusts (HSCTs) are responsible for the planning and delivery of respite/short break services, aligned to assessed needs. Within both Children’s and Adult Services respite/ short breaks are provided within the person’s own home or alternatively respite/ short breaks can be provided within statutory and independent sector facilities.  Within short break facilities staff are required to safely deliver short break care. The number of staff required to safely deliver overnight care within the South Eastern Trust will vary depending on the type of facility and assessed need of the children or adults who are availing of a short break. Detail regarding a defined number of staff is therefore not possible. Staff required will flex up or down depending on need and service requirements. Staffing ratios are in keeping with minimum standards for the relevant service or facility, as established by the Regulation Quality and Improvement Authority. Required staffing is outlined in standards such as the Minimum Standards for Children’s Homes, Care Standards for Nursing Homes and Residential Care Home Minimum Standards.The number and ratio of staff to residents is calculated using a method that is determined by the Regulation and Quality Improvement Authority. Students and volunteers working in the home are not considered in the overall staffing calculation.There are arrangements within short break facilities to provide cover at all times by appropriately qualified, trained and experienced practitioners. Where necessary, appropriate administrative and ancillary staff are employed to ensure that standards relating to food and meals, transport, laundry, cleaning and maintenance of the premises and administration are fully met. There is a qualified, trained and experienced person in charge of short break facilities at all times.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the amount of funding provided to the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust to deliver an in-reach service for facial palsy patients; and (ii) how this funding has been spent.
answer_text: On average only 3-5 patients are assessed as suitable for Facial Palsy surgery per annum in Northern Ireland.  These referrals are managed through the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust as part of a Network arrangement with a specialist provider in Great Britain. This activity represents a very small component of the Facial Palsy care pathway as the majority of patients are managed locally across all Trusts and supported by colleagues from Primary Care. In-reach services are provided as part of the total commissioning arrangement for Plastic surgery provided annually to the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the plans in place to deliver the required number of overnight respite beds required in the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: I can provide the following information on the current status and plans regarding overnight respite beds/ short breaks in the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust (SEHSCT). This outlines the current operational status and the future plans for enhancing the overnight respite care facilities within the SEHSCT.Children’s ServicesThe SEHSCT currently has one Residential Short Breaks Children’s Home, Green Hill, located in Newcastle County Down. With new Ministerial investment, it is planned that this will deliver two beds per night seven nights per week. In order to deliver the required number of overnight short breaks, the Trust also avails of Residential Short Breaks for children with complex medical needs provided by the Belfast Trust in Forest Lodge, Belfast.Older People Services In the SEHSCT Older Peoples Services there are 10 block booked short break beds available in both independent sector care homes and in Trust statutory residential facilities, to deliver the required number of short breaks:2 in North Down & Ards (1 bed Mount Alexander Care Home – 1 bed Croaghpatrick Care Home)4 in Lisburn (2 beds in Drumlough House Care Home and 2 beds in Laurelhill Care Home)4 in Down (4 beds in Ardview).The SEHSCT plans to utilise any available care home beds that meet assessed need, should there be additional demand for an emergency short break or a planned respite short break.Adult Learning DisabilityThe SEHSCT has 1 statutory short breaks facility for adults with learning disabilities.  The SEHSCT also has block contracts with private providers for short breaks for adults with learning disabilities across the 3 sectors of the Trust.  In order to meet future additional demand, short breaks can be spot purchased.  Short breaks with private providers are also allocated as appropriate, in accordance with assessed need.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any specific special educational needs transformation projects that will be delivered using the funding received from the Interim Public Services Transformation Board.
answer_text: The Finance Minister confirmed ring-fenced funding of £27.5m for Special Educational Needs (SEN) Transformation to my department on 4 March 2025 as recommended by the Interim Public Services Transformation Board.The SEN Transformation proposals to be funded from the Transformation funding are focused on two strands:Strand 1 - ‘Right Support at the Right Time for Children & Young People & Parents/Families’ – focuses on early intervention and includes seven projects to be developed and trialled which will test outcomes for children with emerging SEN, as follows:Transforming the Statutory Assessment Process;Transformation of Support Model;Establish and deliver a pilot Early Childhood SEN Intervention Programme;Establish and deliver a pilot Speech, Language & Communications Intervention Toolkit & Programme;Transforming Inclusive Play Environments;Adopt and trial the Early Intervention Nurture Programme into an Enhanced ‘Nurture Plus’ Programme;Special Schools as Resource Centres of Expertise (pilot).Strand 2 – ‘Right People’ – focuses on building a skilled and confident workforce and includes two pilots:Design & Trial a Teacher & Classroom Assistant Professional Learning Programme;Design & Trial an Inclusive Capacity Building Programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of respite short break beds available in the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust, broken down by location.
answer_text: Children’s ServicesThe South Eastern HSC Trust (SET) currently has one Residential short Breaks Children’s Home, Green Hill, located in Newcastle County Down. This Home, with the new Ministerial investment will be budgeted to deliver two beds per night seven nights per week. However, this will be contingent in a successful workforce recruitment campaign. The Trust also avails of Residential Short Breaks for children with complex medical needs and this is provided by the Belfast Trust, in Forest Lodge, Belfast.Older People Services In SET Older Peoples Services there are 10 block booked short break beds available in both independent sector care homes and in Trust statutory residential facilities: 2 in North Down & Ards (1 bed Mount Alexander Care Home – 1 bed Croaghpatrick Care Home)4 in Lisburn (2 beds in Drumlough House Care Home and 2 beds in Laurelhill Care Home)4 in Down (4 beds in Ardview)In addition to these blocks booked respite beds, service users can be admitted to any care home bed should there be a need for an emergency short break or a planned respite short breakAdult Learning DisabilityThe SET has 1 statutory short breaks facility for adults with learning disabilities.  A panel is held monthly when the registered manager and community social work managers allocate nights for service users based on assessed need.SET also have block contracts with private providers for short breaks for adults with learning disabilities across the 3 sectors of the Trust.  Additional beds can also be spot purchased if required and are available.  Short breaks with private providers are also allocated as appropriate in accordance with assessed need.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any changes introduced by the Education Authority to ensure all non-teaching staff hold a valid Access NI check.
answer_text: I would refer the Member to the answer to question AQW 24056/22-27. The answer is contained in the Written Answers Booklet for the week ending 28th March 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the costings for the actions set out in the Special Educational Needs Reform Agenda Delivery Plan 2025-2030.
answer_text: As set out in the Special Educational Needs (SEN) Reform Agenda, the high level estimated cost of full implementation of the actions within the Delivery Plan is £570m over 5 years.  Costings are being finessed through the development of detailed implementation plans and in collaboration with key delivery partners.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will remove the exemption from inspection that currently applies to Religious Education.
answer_text: I have no plans to prioritise legislation regarding this matter during the current Assembly mandate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline any actions being taken to provide any support for arable farming.
answer_text: My Department’s Sustainable Agriculture Programme (SAP) will promote more efficient and sustainable farming practices to all sectors, including the arable sector, through a mix of incentivisation, innovation, and enhanced farmer knowledge and training with a firm focus on data to help with decision making. As I outlined recently at the Ulster Arable Society and UFU’s Arable Conference, I recognise that the arable sector has an important role to play in contributing to reductions in emissions from agriculture to achieve our net zero targets and in delivering improvements in water quality working in partnership with livestock producers.  Examples of the schemes within SAP that will provide support for arable farming in Northern Ireland include: the Farming with Nature Package, the Farm Sustainability Payment, Farming for Sustainability -Knowledge Transfer, the Sustainable Farming Investment Scheme, and the Protein Crops Scheme.These SAP Schemes will, as far as possible, seek to recognise and reward the public goods provided by arable farmers and other farm businesses through the delivery of improved environmental outcomes.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the disposal of the former Castle Gardens Primary School site in Newtownards.
answer_text: Following legal advice, the Education Authority is taking steps to clear the Title to the property.This has proven a lengthy process but, once resolved, the site will be disposed of in accordance with LPS guidelines.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether Religious Education is included in the current Review of the Northern Ireland Curriculum.
answer_text: The Strategic Review of the Northern Ireland Curriculum, currently being carried out by Lucy Crehan will consider all areas of learning across the curriculum.  Given the nature of the Review, it is for Ms Crehan to determine the level of detail regarding any recommendations on specific subject content.   Ms Crehan will provide her report to my Department in May, at which point I will consider the recommendations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the work of the Religious Education Advisory Working Group.
answer_text: The Religious Education Advisory Working Group has not met since March 2023. My Department is awaiting the ruling of the Supreme Court in respect of Judicial Review 87 before considering the future direction of this group.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any financial savings that have been delivered as a result of the creation of the Education Authority since 2017.
answer_text: From 2017 to 2024, the Education Authority (EA) has reported delivered savings of circa £136.8 million.  It is not possible to disaggregate savings directly attributable to the creation of the EA from those delivered to operate within the budget envelope over this period.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education when he will make a decision on the development proposal for an Irish-medium nursery school at St Colmcille's Primary School, Carrickmore.
answer_text: Development Proposal (DP) 734 which proposes that an Irish-medium statutory nursery unit will be established at St Colmcille’s Primary School, Carrickmore to provide 26 part-time nursery places, with effect from 1 September 2024, or as soon as possible thereafter, was published by the Education Authority on 12 September 2024. The two-month statutory objection period in relation to this DP ended on 12 November 2024 and I am informed that my officials are in the final stages of preparing a report for my consideration. The assessment of any DP is a complex process and requires careful consideration of all evidence to ensure decisions are taken in the best interests of all children and to ensure they have access to high quality education that is viable and sustainable in the long term.I am aware of the need for clarity and certainty for the school and its community, therefore I will endeavour to provide that clarity and certainty as soon as practically possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any data or information held by his Department or the Education Authority in relation to the use of reduced timetables for pupils with additional needs.
answer_text: Under Article 17 of the Education Reform (Northern Ireland) Order 1989 Order, schools may reduce the time pupils are in attendance in school for various reasons such as emotional, behavioural or welfare reasons or phased return after a long illness. Neither the Department nor the Education Authority holds this data as there is no designated absence code specifically for this scenario.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer (i) AQW 21627/22-27; (ii) AQW 21714/22-27; (iii) AQW 21715/22-27; and (iv) AQW 21716/22-27.
To ask the Minister of Education whether mental capacity assessments are considered as part of the transition process for young people with a statement of special educational needs leaving school.
answer_text: I refer the member to my answer to AQW 23321/22-27 with regard to the termination point for the North Belfast route.The proposed Park and Ride at O’Neill Road, Newtownabbey will be the final stop on the North Belfast route and will be served by a dedicated Metro service following Phase 1 of the Belfast Rapid Transit implementation.  This service will benefit from the bus priority measures being provided as part of the £35m funding from Belfast Region City Deal.  On full implementation of the BRT 2 project, Glider vehicles will replace the Metro service on the route and will also terminate at the Park and Ride site

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any assessments conducted to determine the functional abilities of a young person with a statement of special educational needs before they leave school at 19 years of age.
answer_text: As part of transition planning at the Annual Review of the young person’s statement of special educational needs, the relevant Health and Social Care Trust may consider a needs assessment for young people in preparation for moving from children to adult services.This needs assessment may help determine areas such as functional abilities, emotional wellbeing, social/communication skills, awareness/decision making, behaviour, personal care, mobility, travel/independence and determine the young person’s needs or any risks. Such assessment may be required to assist the Health and Social Care Trust in identifying an appropriate supported or governed Health and Social Care post school provision (e.g. day centre or day opportunities). The assessment may also be used to assist in transition arrangements to facilitate any move over to the post school option.  Where a school requests the involvement from Education Psychology as part of the annual review process, Education Psychology will undertake a review assessment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any processes in place to support young people with a statement of special educational needs as they transition out of school after 19 years of age.
answer_text: As part of the annual review of the young person’s statement of special educational needs, school leaders will create a transitions plan and update this every year from Year 10 in preparation for the young person leaving school. Stakeholders and services will along with the young person and parent/carer have the opportunity to contribute to this process.Relevant stakeholders may include Health services, Careers Service, Educational Psychology and EA services as appropriate. Depending on the young person’s individual circumstances opportunities such as sampling, visits, and open days are identified to support or ascertain appropriate pathways.Special Schools very often have transitions classes/programmes already in place, aimed towards preparing the young person in their final year for the move to their post school pathway/adult life.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any cross-departmental work that takes place during the transition process for young people with a statement of special educational needs leaving school at 19 years of age.
answer_text: Schools, the Education Authority (EA), Health and Social Care Trusts (HSCT) and career advisors (within the Department for Economy (DfE)) are all involved in planning for a child’s transition from education.The exchange of information, advice and action in this multidisciplinary environment/forum is aimed to help facilitate the young person’s overall transition as part of the Annual Review of their statement from Year 10 onwards.As part of the End to End Review of Special Educational Needs (SEN) a Transitions Workstream was jointly led by the Department of Education (DE) and Department of Health (DoH) with representatives from the Department for Communities (DfC), Public Health Agency (PHA) and DfE. The focus of this workstream was to review current transition planning, identifying challenges and strengths, and opportunities for development of a more effective approach from education into adulthood. The workstream also focussed on enhanced inter-agency working between DE, DoH, DfC and DfE with a focus on enhancing collaboration and providing clarification of roles and responsibilities. In addition to this, a project focused on post-school SEN skills and training provision and legislation was announced by the Minister for the Economy in June 2024. Transition at various stages of a learner’s journey is also contained with the SEN Reform Agenda and work will continue in this space on a cross-departmental basis.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail how IQ tests are used in determining the level of support required for a young person with a statement of special educational need transitioning out of school.
answer_text: IQ or cognitive testing is just one element of a comprehensive Educational Psychology assessment and should never be viewed in isolation.  While cognitive assessments can provide useful information about a young person’s intellectual functioning, including their strengths and difficulties in areas such as verbal reasoning, processing speed, and working memory, they do not capture the full picture of their learning needs or daily functioning.Educational Psychologists take a holistic approach, considering a wide range of factors beyond IQ scores when carrying out an assessment. These include adaptive functioning, for example, independent living skills, social communication, and problem-solving, academic attainment, social-emotional well-being, and environmental influences such as family, school support, and access to resources.  By integrating these factors through a process of psychological formulation, Educational Psychologists develop a more meaningful understanding of the young person’s needs, ensuring that recommendations are both practical and person centred.This is particularly relevant when determining the level of support required for a young person with a Statement of Special Educational Needs as they transition out of school. Decisions about post-school placements, vocational training, or employment support must consider how the young person functions in real-life situations, rather than relying solely on standardised test scores. For instance, two individuals with similar IQ scores may require very different levels of support depending on their adaptive skills, mental health, and coping strategies.By using a comprehensive assessment approach, Educational Psychologists can provide recommendations that better reflect the young person’s strengths, challenges, and future goals, ensuring they receive the appropriate support to thrive in adulthood.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the role that (i) the young person themselves; (ii) parents and carers; and (iii) school leaders have in the transition process for young people with a statement of special educational needs leaving school at 19 years of age.
answer_text: As the young person is a central figure in the transitions process this is the opportunity for their voice to be heard, and for their perspective to be incorporated into the overall planning. The degree or timing of any young person’s participation in Annual Reviews, transition discussions or transitions plans may be dependent on such aspects of their ability and aptitude. The young person may, where appropriate, participate in accessing visits, sampling or events related to post school programmes.As stakeholders and advocates for the young person, parents/carers are encouraged to participate and assist in the overall transitions process. Their input and support work towards enabling any post school pathway planning. The opportunity to present their views and in-depth knowledge of the young person is key to the planning process.School leaders have knowledge of the young person’s educational experience and development which assists the transitions process and post school pathway development. School leaders can also involve other related external and supportive services to aid the planning process. School leaders are responsible for overseeing the development of Transition Plans within the Annual Review of the Statement process. They also assist as or where appropriate in the programme of any sampling or access visits to potential post school pathways.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any training available to teachers in mainstream schools on managing challenging behaviour.
answer_text: A range of whole school capacity building training is available to mainstream schools from both the Education Authority (EA) Primary and Post Primary Behaviour Support and Provisions services.  This service encourages schools to establish early intervention, preventative, trauma informed and nurturing approaches to support behaviours that challenge and promote a culture of positive emotional health and wellbeing (EHWB). The core focus of this training is:understanding that behaviour is a form of communicationto move away from controlling or managing behaviour to nurturing and support EHWBthe importance of relationships creating a network around a school and the childchild centred: voice of the child is captured in support plans developeda trauma Informed/ trauma sensitive approach is encouragedunderstanding of neurological developmentTraining is available on the following areas:A Positive Approach to Risk Reduction: regional approach to Risk Assessment and development of a Risk Reduction Action PlanDe-escalation and Diffusion trainingUnderstanding and Managing Children that Present with Social Behaviour Emotional and Wellbeing (SBEW) needsWhole School Positive Behaviour Frameworks that address effective staff structure, professional development, space, ethos and resources with emphasis on preventative and solution focused approaches and nurture principlesDevelopment of a Positive Behaviour Policy relating to behaviourRecord keeping and legislationNurture trainingBespoke staff training e.g. classroom assistantSupporting a Pupil with a Statement for SBEW NeedsTrauma Informed Practice training for schoolsBetter Together Programme: the development of regional model of support, specifically focused on support for Special Schools. EA has developed a bespoke training programme for accredited safe handling training for staff working within Specialist Provisions in Mainstream Schools, “Connecting and Caring Across the Whole School Community” which provides a whole school framework to prevent, relate, support and respond to behaviours that require intervention. This programme was delivered as a pilot to over 60 Specialist Provisions in Mainstream Schools Term 3 of 2023/ 2024, with the revised programme delivered to a number of specialist provisions in February 2025. This training will be available to all settings from September 2025. Other available support to schools includes: Regional Primary Behaviour Support and Provisions Telephone Helpline Provision of guidance materials and resources including signposting to specialist programmesSchool visits by Education Authority (EA) staff to provide support with implementing:Best practice in relation to risk reduction including escalation and diffusion and use of reasonable force and safe handling Legislation and guidance in relation to bullying in schools Violence at Work and Procedures – An EA draft Policy in development which includes guidance for school staffTargeted support for pupils with a statement of sp

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 21076/22-27.

To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of young people with special educational needs (i) that left a special school at 19 years of age in the 2023-24 academic year; and (ii) that are projected to leave a special school at 19 years of age in the 2024-25 academic year.
answer_text: School Census information below shows the number of pupils enrolled in special schools who were recorded as being 18. Age is recorded at 1 July, so the pupils below will turn 19 before the end of the academic year. All young people in special schools have special educational needs.Census YearNumber of pupils aged 182023/24284 2024/25250

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of the need to provide additional Special Education Needs Coordinator cover for schools that offer Specialist Provision in Mainstream Schools and Specialist Learning Support Classes.
answer_text: I recently published the Special Educational Needs (SEN) Reform Agenda and five-year Delivery Plan which sets out the actions that are needed to ensure that children and young people with SEN can benefit from greater inclusion and receive the right support from the right people at the right time and in the right place. Included in the delivery plan is an action to design and implement a Leadership Programme for SEN Learning Support Coordinators/ SEN co-ordinators (SENCo’s), along with an early action to review the quality and appropriateness of specialist provision in mainstream schools.As set out in the Code of Practice (1998) paragraph 2.13 …The time and attention which SENCo’s will need to devote to their responsibilities will depend on the circumstances of particular schools.  Boards of Governors and Principals should therefore give careful thought to the SENCo teaching commitments and responsibilities to ensure that structures within the school allow the SENCo functions to be carried out most effectively.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to 22414/22-27, whether there is a requirement for a teacher to be informed when a classroom assistant working in their class does not have an Access NI check in place.
answer_text: Circular 2024/10 ‘Pre-Employment / Access NI Check Requirements and Safer Recruitment Practices for Staff and Volunteers Working in or Providing a Service for Schools/Education Settings’ advises that the school principal is responsible for safeguarding in their school, for completing a risk assessment in respect of unvetted staff and for ensuring that non-vetted staff are supervised by someone who is in regulated activity.  It is the principal’s responsibility to assess the safeguarding risk and to decide how this should be managed within the context of the school.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what additional non-contact time is provided to teachers to complete Personal Learning Plans (PLP) and PLP reviews for children with special educational needs.
answer_text: The new draft SEN Regulations place a duty on Board of Governors of grant-aided schools “to ensure that the designated learning support co-ordinator receives reasonable time to conduct their role effectively”; this includes completion and review of the Personal Learning Plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of (i) Local Impact Teams have been established by the Education Authority; and (ii) schools that each Local Impact Team covers.
answer_text: The SEND Local IMPACT Teams (LIT) model is being implemented in phases. EA’s existing siloed SEND services staff will transition into a network of local multi-specialism teams across Term 3 to be fully operational in September 2025. Extensive regular engagement with stakeholders has highlighted widespread support for the Local IMPACT Team model and its phased implementation providing settings with more time to prepare for the changes the new model will bring.  The EA’s SEND Transformation Team are in the final stages of defining LIT catchment areas and staff allocation to maximise accessibility and capacity. Engagement with directly affected staff and Tade Unions are about to be initiated, and once that engagement has been completed, the details of the LIT network structure and distribution will be shared publicly.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what time a Special Educational Needs Coordinator is entitled to receive, outside of teaching time, to deliver their role.
answer_text: At present there is no specification on the amount of time outside of teaching time, to deliver the Special Educational Needs Co-ordinator (SENCo) role.  The allocation of time is a decision taken at school level and varies from school to school.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether all teachers taking on the role of a Special Educational Needs Coordinator in Northern Ireland are entitled to a management point.
answer_text: The allocation of teaching allowance (previously known as management points) is a decision for each school within its own management structure. The Board of Governors of an individual school is responsible for determining a management structure appropriate to the needs and circumstances of the school.In determining the management structure for posts of responsibility the Governors will also determine the level of teaching allowance (if appropriate).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether the Education Authority has the legal power to direct a Board of Governors to ensure that an Access NI check is completed for a classroom assistant.
answer_text: With regard to the Access NI process, the Education Authority (EA) has advisory powers but cannot mandate a Board of Governors to undertake a specific action. Schools are responsible for ensuring that the Access NI check is completed for any member of staff employed via the temporary engagement process. The EA provides schools with guidance and the necessary documents to complete this check.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 22242/22-27, to detail the number of classroom assistants currently working in schools without an AccessNI check, broken down by those who have been working without a check for (i) more than one month; (ii) more than six months; and (iii) more than twelve months.
answer_text: When a school engages a temporary staff member, they are required to follow the guidance set out in DE Circular 2024/10, related to pre-employment and Access NI requirements and safer resourcing practices. This includes ensuring that non-vetted staff are supervised by someone who is vetted to work in regulated activity. Where a staff member has not submitted the paperwork needed by Access NI, the Education Authority (EA) engages with the school Principal to remind them of the requirement to have this completed. Despite protracted communication between the EA and the employee/school, the documentation required to undertake the check can remain outstanding for a prolonged period.  The table below details the number of classroom assistants without an Access NI check, broken down by the length of time the check has been outstanding.Time PeriodNo. Classroom Assistants14 weeks to 6 months4626 months to 1 year70Greater than 1 year195Total7271 Figures as at 19 February 2025I have asked the EA Chief Executive to urgently review the Access NI process within schools and the EA. As a result, I have been assured that the EA is taking robust action to ensure that the issue of outstanding checks is addressed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail when a project board to support the delivery of Mid Down Integrated College will be established.
answer_text: Where a development proposal is approved by the Department, it is the statutory responsibility of the proposer to implement it.  In doing so The Northern Ireland Council for Integrated Education (NICIE) will provide support to the proposer in establishing the steering group in the process of Mid-Down Integrated College establishing itself as a Grant-Maintained Integrated (GMI) school.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any action he is taking to reduce occupational therapist assessment waiting times for (i) adults; and (ii) children in the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The Public Health Agency (PHA) continues to work with the Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) and Trusts to ensure Allied Health Profession (AHP) services including Occupational Therapy deliver a range of support that reflects the needs of the population of Northern Ireland, and is aligned to evidence based practice. Strategies that are currently being used by South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust to help reduce waiting times are:waiting list validationmonitoring capacity and demand of complex casesproactive recruitmentutilising bank and agency staff to cover long term vacancies or absences as appropriatereviewing new ways of working and assuring appropriate skill mix to maximise efficiency.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the current waiting times for (i) child; and (ii) adult occupational therapist assessments in the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: (i) The current waiting times for a routine Occupational Therapy Appointment in the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust for a child are (valid at 21.02.2025):0-3 weeks3-6 weeks6-9 weeks9-13 weeks>13 weeks Children< 5171342321(ii) The current waiting time for a routine Occupational Therapy Appointment in the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust for an adult are (valid at 21.02.2025):0-3 weeks3-6 weeks6-9 weeks9-13 weeks>13 weeks Adults4142751302371400

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 21519/22-27, to detail the longest period of time any of the 909 classroom assistants working in Northern Ireland have been without an Access NI check.
answer_text: I would refer the Member to the answer to question AQW 22242/22-27. The answer is contained in the Written Answers Booklet for the week ending 21 February 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will undertake a review of the current processes for allocating funding to schools for unavoidable minor works.
answer_text: Due to budget constraints, Unavoidable Minor Works applications are currently prioritised on the basis of health and safety, statutory compliance and the need to address urgent and emergency repairs, which could close a school or present a significant health and safety risk.My Department’s internal audit carried out a review of the Minor Works processes for non-controlled schools in May 2023. One of the objectives of that review was “To ensure that schemes within the minor works programme are prioritised to ensure effective use of the available capital budget”. The audit opinion was that, overall, there is a satisfactory system of governance, risk management and control. In October 2017 the Department increased the Education Authority’s (EA) capital delegation from £500k to £1m. This effectively meant that from that date, all EA capital projects below that threshold are carried out by the EA without reference to the Department.The EA is responsible for determining its own minor works priorities and for planning, managing and carrying out these works.Delivery of projects is continually assessed against available budget.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of people diagnosed with a learning disability that are currently waiting to be allocated a social worker, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 21075/22-27, when the £13 million investment announced in October 2024 to deliver additional short break provision for young people will be delivered.
answer_text: Funding of £1.377m has been allocated in year between the HSC Trusts which was allocated in January 2025. The Department is currently working alongside the Trusts to finalise business cases for 25/26 whereupon the £13.1m will be allocated.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on progress on the roll-out of the Farming with Nature Package.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 21078/22-27, to detail the specific work he has undertaken in collaboration with the Minister of Education to benchmark how many allied health professionals of each discipline are required to support learners with special educational needs and disabilities in all school types.
answer_text: The Public Health Agency, in partnership with Education sector colleagues, is leading a comprehensive analysis of the therapeutic needs of children with Special Educational Needs in Special Schools. Three SEN project leads have been recruited to date to specifically consider the data to inform the needs of children with SEN. This work is planned to be completed on a phased basis by March 2026. The work is complex and will require considerable interagency working between health and education and the analysis of multiple datasets. This comprehensive analysis will establish the level of need based on the current service model. This will inform a new integrated joint health and education workforce plan for AHPs supporting children with SEN.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 21078/22-27, whether he plans to deliver a joint workforce strategy with the Minister of Education to deliver the appropriate number of allied health professionals required to support special educational needs learners.
answer_text: I am working closely with the Minister of Education in relation to AHP pre-registration training places and protected AHP posts, as outlined in the SEN Reform Agenda – Delivery Plan 2025-2030, with a view to growing the AHP workforce supply to support children and young people with special educational needs. In the SEN reform agenda delivery plan, an increase in AHP pre-registration training places and the establishment of protected posts for education is identified as a key enabler of improvement. This will only be deliverable if sufficient funding is made available. A review of therapeutic needs of children and young people with SEN led by the Public Health Agency has commenced and will be completed on a phased basis. This will inform an AHP workforce plan that will support children and young people with SEN, enabling us to proactively plan for their therapeutic support, with a focus on early intervention to stop problems before they escalate. My officials have been fully engaged with education at strategic and operational levels to inform the SEN review and to collaboratively develop policy and commissioning responses to the challenges we face.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 21625/22-27, and in light of licensing changes to the Duke of Edinburgh Award Scheme, to detail the number of young people already engaged on a Duke of Edinburgh or President's Award Scheme in a setting other than a school previously covered by the Education Authority's license who are currently unable to continue their programme.
answer_text: All young people currently engaged with the programme will be able to continue.  DofE have ensured that arrangements are in place to ensure that every young person currently enrolled can continue and complete their Duke of Edinburgh and President’s Award.Twenty centres, other than schools, were required to find alternative licensing arrangements rather than continue to operate under the EA's DofE Operating Authority LicenceTwelve centres have completed their move to alternative licensing arrangements: 4 centres have taken out a Direct Licence with the DofE, 5 centres have moved to alternative Operating Authorities and 3 centres are currently completing the licensing process to take out a Direct Licence with the DofE.  7 centres will be offered an alternative Operating Authority as soon as that new Operating Authority has established their registration process (one centre has recently indicated that they are likely to cease offering the Award, however the offer of the new Operating Authority will still be made for their consideration).  One centre ceased delivering the Award some time ago and has no plans to restart.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 21624/22-27, to detail how many centres other than schools previously covered by the Education Authority's license with the Duke of Edinburgh Award Scheme are now required to become directly licensed centres to deliver the Duke of Edinburgh Award or President's Award Scheme, broken down by (i) centres that have acquired this status; and (ii) centres that have yet to acquire this status.
answer_text: All young people currently engaged with the programme will be able to continue.  DofE have ensured that arrangements are in place to ensure that every young person currently enrolled can continue and complete their Duke of Edinburgh and President’s Award.Twenty centres, other than schools, were required to find alternative licensing arrangements rather than continue to operate under the EA's DofE Operating Authority LicenceTwelve centres have completed their move to alternative licensing arrangements: 4 centres have taken out a Direct Licence with the DofE, 5 centres have moved to alternative Operating Authorities and 3 centres are currently completing the licensing process to take out a Direct Licence with the DofE.  7 centres will be offered an alternative Operating Authority as soon as that new Operating Authority has established their registration process (one centre has recently indicated that they are likely to cease offering the Award, however the offer of the new Operating Authority will still be made for their consideration).  One centre ceased delivering the Award some time ago and has no plans to restart.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 21519/22-27, to detail the processes in place to ensure (i) that child safeguarding risk assessments are carried out when a classroom assistant is engaged by a school without a completed Access NI check; and (ii) these staff are supervised at all times.
answer_text: School leaders are responsible for safeguarding within their school and are required to comply with DE Circular 2024/10 – Pre-Employment/Access NI Check Requirements and Safer Recruitment Practices for Staff and Volunteers Working in or Providing a Service for Schools/Educational Settings.The Circular states that where non-vetted staff are used to provide emergency cover, school leaders/managers must carry out a risk assessment and ensure that non-vetted staff are supervised by someone who is vetted to work in regulated activity. They must also take all necessary steps to ensure the safeguarding and protection of children.Where an Access NI check is outstanding as a result of the school/staff member not submitting the required documentation, the Education Authority (EA) engages with the school principal to remind them of the need to have this completed.I recently asked the EA Chief Executive to urgently review the Access NI process within schools and the EA. As a result, I have been assured that the EA is taking robust action to ensure that the issue of outstanding checks is addressed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 21519/22-27, to detail the longest period of time that a classroom assistant currently working in a school has spent without a valid Access NI check in place.
answer_text: When a school engages a temporary staff member, they are required to follow the guidance set out in DE Circular 2024/10, related to pre-employment and Access NI requirements and safer resourcing practices. Where a staff member has not submitted the required paperwork needed by Access NI, the Education Authority engages with the school Principal to remind them of the requirement to have this completed. Despite protracted communication between the EA and the employee/school, on occasions the documentation to undertake the check can remain outstanding for a prolonged period.  As of 11 February 2025, the EA advise that the longest period that a classroom assistant has an outstanding Access NI check for is two years and five months. I am informed by the EA that in this particular case, the EA has contacted the school/employee nine times seeking the necessary information.I have written to the EA Chief Executive asking him to urgently review the Access NI process and he has advised that the EA will be taking robust action to address outstanding checks.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when surveys of the Pound Street overpass, Comber, will commence to assess the structural works required to make it safe.
answer_text: My Department is aware of the condition of Pound Bridge. While the spalled concrete and exposed steelwork is unsightly, the Department’s structural engineers have examined the bridge and are content with its integrity and that it is safe for public use. My Department is currently planning to take forward a detailed survey in the next two months that will inform a substantive repair of the bridge.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the scoping work required to identify the needs of the childcare sector to support the development of a targeted business support scheme.
answer_text: The research to determine the financial health and needs of childcare providers is progressing at pace. Consultations have taken place with a cross-section of stakeholder organisations and representative organisations within the Childcare and wider sectors. More detailed consultations with individual childcare providers and parents are taking place at present. The research remains on schedule to complete by March 2025. The resultant evidence base will allow my Department to move quickly to make recommendations to the Minister of Education on the nature of assistance required by Childcare providers.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when works to make the Pound Street overpass, Comber, structurally safe will commence.
answer_text: My Department is aware of the condition of Pound Bridge. While the spalled concrete and exposed steelwork is unsightly, the Department’s structural engineers have examined the bridge and are content with its integrity and that it is safe for public use. My Department is currently planning to take forward a detailed survey in the next two months that will inform a substantive repair of the bridge. A timeframe for taking forward the full repair works is not known at this stage.In the meantime, my Department will continue to monitor the bridge to ensure it remains safe for public use.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the establishment of a facial palsy pathway.
answer_text: A Facial Palsy Regional Planning Group was established in September 2023.  The group developed an action plan which included the establishment of a local and regional pathway to assist with assessment and treatment. A pathway has now been developed for local services for adults.  This has been a complex process with multiple services involved in the pathway development, and multiple access points into the service across all Trusts in Northern Ireland.  This standardised pathway will ensure appropriate treatment of patients in local services as far as possible and will also outline the appropriate referral pathway for those requiring more enhanced specialist intervention.Communication will be issued to each Trust in the coming weeks to inform them of the agreed pathway and an information session will be delivered to GPs to aide with timely and appropriate referral.    I understand a paediatric pathway for assessment and treatment is also being developed for local services.  This will be shared with members of the Facial Palsy Regional Planning Group for feedback in the coming weeks. In addition, to assist patients which are experiencing facial palsy symptoms a new NI Direct webpage has recently been established.  This will signpost patients to helpful information whist waiting for assessment. A video has been produced by a service user advocate, which has been used on social media platforms to promote the new NI Direct webpage.It is hoped that further recurrent investment can be secured to put in place an enhanced regional service for those requiring highly specialised services – however this is not possible at the present time given wider service pressures and priorities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of staff currently awaiting an Occupational Health Service medical assessment.
answer_text: There are different types of medical assessment carried out by the NICS’ Occupational Health Service. Data included in the table below is correct at 7 February 2025Pre-employment32 members of staff are awaiting assessment.  14 of these have been allocated an appointment within the next 2 weeks.Fitness for work122 members of staff are awaiting assessment.  57 of these have been allocated an appointment within the next 2 weeks.  2 members of staff cancelled a previous appointment that had been scheduled and are awaiting a new appointment to be allocated. Injury Awards & Pension referrals53 members of staff are awaiting assessment. An appointment is not required for this type of referral.Health Surveillance582 members of staff are awaiting assessment.  121 of these have been allocated an appointment within the next 2 weeks.  42 members of staff cancelled a previous appointment that had been scheduled and are awaiting a new appointment to be allocated.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of staff that have been awaiting an Occupational Health Service medical assessment for more than six weeks.
answer_text: There are several different forms of medical assessment conducted by OHS. Data included in the table below is correct as at 7 February 2025.Pre-employment26 members of staff are awaiting assessment for more than 6 weeks.  12 of these have been allocated an appointment within the next 2 weeks.Fitness for work3 members of staff are awaiting assessment for more than 6 weeks.  2 of these cancelled a previous appointment that had been scheduled and are awaiting a new appointment to be allocated. Injury Awards and Pension referrals29 members of staff are awaiting assessment for more than 6 weeks. An appointment is not required for this type of referral.Health Surveillance522 members of staff are awaiting assessment for more than 6 weeks. Fitness for work referrals are requested by NICS Departments when they require medical advice in relation to staff who are absent due sickness, or those currently still attending work, or to obtain medical opinion to inform ill-health retirement decisions.2 Health Surveillance is a series of repeated health checks which are used to identify ill health caused by work, or to mitigate occupational risk.  With the introduction of OH Technicians, this number will significantly decrease in the coming months. These referrals often require input from multi-disciplinary occupational health clinical staff, they can be complex, and they often take more time than other referral types.  Delays in carrying out these referrals do not prevent staff from attending work.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 10374/22-27, to detail the number of vacancies currently identified within the Occupational Health Service following the recruitment exercise in May 2024 for (i) medical officers; (ii) nursing staff; and (iii) Occupational Health Technicians.
answer_text: There are currently no Medical Officer vacancies. Over 2024, following a successful recruitment exercise, Occupational Health Service (OHS) recruited 3.4 full time equivalent (FTE) Medical Officers.  Offers of employment have been extended to a further two Medical Officers who are expected to commence employment in the next 12 months.  There are currently five Nursing vacancies. The recruitment competition for Nurses in May 2024 failed to yield any successful candidates.  Work on a new recruitment competition to launch in Spring 2025 is underway to recruit three Occupational Health Nurses and two Trainee Occupational Health Nurses. There is currently one Occupational Health Technician vacancy, which OHS plans to fill in Autumn 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 10374/22-27, for an update on the procurement of a new IT system to better manage the Occupational Health Service.
answer_text: Significant work has been ongoing within my Department’s Occupational Health Service (OHS) to procure a new cloud-based IT case management system.  This is a key step that will support digital transformation and service delivery improvements in OHS.OHS has carried out pre-market engagement and a product has been identified.
