Engagement with a supplier is underway and it is anticipated that implementation of the new system will occur around September 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the provision of a park and ride facility for Comber.
answer_text: My Department has plans to develop a park and ride site at Comber.  The preferred site is located along the A22 Belfast Road corridor immediately adjacent to the Comber Greenway with the potential to provide parking provision for 410 vehicles in its first phase of development with scope for further extension.Feasibility and appraisal works are ongoing to enable the submission of a planning application in the near future.  Provision of this park and ride site will be dependent on the availability of funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department provides any funding to local councils for services or activities for children with additional needs outside of school term time.
answer_text: My Department does not provide funding to local councils for services or activities for children with additional needs outside of school term time.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the outcome of the Housing Executive's most recent pay submission.
answer_text: I refer the Member to the answer to AQW 21575/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department provides any funding to local councils to ensure services are accessible to meet the needs of children with additional needs.
answer_text: My department does not provide funding to local councils to ensure services are accessible to meet the needs of children with additional needs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the funding stream used to award £710,000 to Lisneal College, without application, for a new football pitch.
answer_text: The scheme to replace the 3G pitch surface at Lisneal College will be funded from the Education Authority’s Other Capital/Minor Works budget for 2024/25 and 2025/26 financial years, as the project will span both financial years.When an issue impacting on safety is uncovered in a school, irrespective of whether the school made an application, the Department and Education Authority will take action to deal with the problem following the appropriate assessments.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 20696/22-27, whether work is being undertaken to develop a model for Allied Health Professionals to be located in the Education Authority Local Impact Teams to meet the needs of children and young people with special educational needs.
answer_text: The Public Health Agency (PHA)  and the Education Authority (EA) have hosted two regional workshops on 1 December 2024 and 4 February 2025 to support integrated planning and enhanced working between the five HSC Trust Regional Integrated Support for Education (RISE) Teams (which includes AHP staff) and the EA Special Educational Needs and Disabilities(SEND) Local IMPACT (Integrated Multi-Specialist Professionals Actively Collaborating Together) Teams (LITs) which are planned to be established and operational in schools in Sept 2025. Work is underway to map existing service structures, services and service models across these teams which operate in the mainstream school sector to identify areas for integration/commonality. This will include a review of supports delivered at a universal, targeted and specialist/intensive level to consider opportunities to embed innovative and collaborative interventions. In addition, joint training and knowledge-sharing sessions are being planned between health and education professionals across RISE and LITs and the development of robust complementary interface pathways to support seamless models of support.The PHA are keen to continue to work closely with EA to further enhance collaborative working in line with the Children’s Services Co-operation Act 2015. The PHA are keen to develop more multi-disciplinary and multi-agency working across AHP services supporting children in Special Schools with EA as the LITs are further developed. The PHA are also keen to work closely with the Department of Education (DE) and EA to implement objectives set out in DE End to End Review, to consider the type and best model of support which is required to meet the holistic needs of children and young people that makes best use of health and education resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the capacity within (i) orthoptics; and (ii) ocular motility consultants to meet the demand for (a) paediatric squint surgery; and (b) adult squint surgery.
answer_text: The Belfast Trust Ophthalmology currently has two adult motility surgeons with two theatre lists per week. The paediatric service currently has 4 motility surgeons that have access to five theatre lists per week during which they would undertake motility surgery alongside other paediatric surgical procedures. My Department recognises the historical and ongoing workforce challenges that have resulted in these unacceptably-long waiting times. In order to help address this orthoptics are currently operating a pilot initiative in which they are providing support to the adult and paediatric theatre lists alongside running additional clinics see patients currently on the waiting list and prepare them for surgery. The model is currently being audited with a Business Case for mainstreaming currently being progressed, subject to available funding.The pilot has allowed for approximately 14 lists to proceed from October 2024 to date with approximately 20 patients operated on and a further 20 removed by orthoptic assessment (without ophthalmology visits) as surgery no longer required. The paediatric waiting list was approx. Four years at the beginning of the pilot but this has since reduced to 18 months. The DoH of Health has also provided funding for an additional theatre session for paediatric squint surgery in South Eastern Trust.  This additional session commenced in February 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail how he will address waiting times for (i) paediatric squint surgery; and (ii) adult squint surgery.
answer_text: The Belfast Trust is currently focusing efforts on the reduction of the adult and paediatric motility waiting lists. The Trust is current running a pilot utilising Orthoptics colleagues in theatre which has helped reduce the amount of cancellations and improve the overall pathway for patients. The pilot has allowed for approximately 14 lists to proceed from October 2024 to date with approximately 20 patients operated on and a further 20 removed by orthoptic assessment (without ophthalmology visits) as surgery no longer required. The paediatric waiting list was approx. Four years at the beginning of the pilot but this has since reduced to 18 months. My Department has also provided funding for an additional theatre session for paediatric squint surgery in South Eastern Trust.  This additional session commenced in February 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail how waiting times for (i) paediatric squint surgery; and (ii) adult squint surgery compares to other elective surgery wait times.
answer_text: There are two provider Trusts for ophthalmology, Belfast and Western Trusts. Both Trusts undertake adult squint surgery. Only Belfast Trust undertake paediatric squint surgery.Paediatric Squint surgery referrals are held by Belfast Trust on a regional New Out-Patient Waiting List for pre-assessment and children are only added to the waiting list for squint surgery following a pre-assessment appointment. This ensures that those children allocated to a list for surgery have been assessed as appropriate.There are currently 159 paediatric squint cases on the regional waiting list. Of these cases, 41 children have been pre-assessed and surgery is imminent.The remaining 118 children are awaiting their pre-assessment appointment.For adult surgery, there are 238 patients waiting for adult squint surgery, 549 patients waiting for cataract surgery and 1,143 patients waiting for other surgeries in ophthalmology. It should be noted that due to the switchover to the new encompass system, in both Trusts these figures are provisional. Although not sight-threatening, the presence of squint in both adults and children can be very distressing, and indeed can have a negative impact on both learning and employment opportunities. It is not acceptable that anyone should have to wait for the opportunity for surgical correction of squint for longer than is clinically necessary.Recognising this, my recent approval of a total £50m spend for elective backlog clearance will include funding to reduce paediatric squint waits. This funding is part of my Department’s £215m Waiting List Initiative package and officials are currently examining proposals for both paediatric squint backlog clearance, and also building capacity to treat more adult squints.With respect to these sustained long waits, my Department recognises the historical and ongoing workforce challenges that have resulted in these unacceptably-long waiting times. In order to help address this, my Department’s Elective Care Framework made a commitment to examining an extended role for orthoptists (AHPs) as assistant in theatre, which would support Consultant Ophthalmologists and recognise the unique skills of hospital orthoptists.This model has been successfully trialled in Belfast Trust for adult and paediatric cases. It includes a role for orthoptic-led pre-assessment and postoperative review clinics which frees consultant capacity and makes inroads into the long waiting times outlined above. The model is currently being audited with a Business Case for mainstreaming currently being progressed, subject to available funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of schools that remain closed due to (i) storm damage; (ii) loss of power; and (iii) loss of water supply.
answer_text: All schools which had to temporarily close due to the effects of the storm, have now reopened.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, in light of Duke of Edinburgh Award Scheme licensing changes, to detail the number of schools that are required to become Directly Licensed Centres, broken down by (i) schools that have acquired this status; and (ii) schools that have yet to acquire this status.
answer_text: A total of 29 schools were impacted due to the change made by the Duke of Edinburgh Award Scheme (DofE) in their licence arrangement with the Education Authority.Of the schools that are required to become Directly Licensed Centres:16 schools have acquired this status and13 schools have yet to acquire this status.All young people currently engaged with the programme will be able to continue.  DofE have ensured that arrangements are in place to ensure that every young person currently enrolled can continue and complete their Duke of Edinburgh and President’s Award.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline how many classroom assistants are currently employed on a temporary contract.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) is the employing authority for the majority of classroom assistants working in schools in Northern Ireland. The EA has advised that 13,992 classroom assistants are currently employed using a temporary contract.The high number of classroom assistants employed in this manor is undoubtedly a consequence of the current one-to-one classroom assistant support model.  This is because schools are unsure over the length of contract to offer a classroom assistant if there is uncertainty as to how long the pupil they are supporting will remain at the school. It is clear that we need to critically evaluate the current SEN support model with a view to developing a more responsive and flexible approach that provides the best possible support for our children.   We need a model that also provides more stability for the workforce and ensures they have access to quality training and a clear pathway for career progression.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline the impact on community and voluntary groups, delivering services under the UK Shared Prosperity Fund, when no funding will be provided after 31 March 2025.
answer_text: As this Department does not have a role in the design and delivery of the UK Shared Prosperity Fund, it is not possible to detail the impact of any potential shortfall.I am aware of, and very much empathise, with the significant concerns of the Voluntary and Community Sector which is supported under SPF and previously ESF.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 20409/22-27, to detail any engagement his Department has had with Home Office to prepare for the introduction of the Terrorism (Protection of Premises) Act.
answer_text: Tom Tugendhat MP, Security Minister wrote to the Head of the NICS and Permanent Secretaries in December 2022 to provide an update on developments for the Terrorism (Protection of Premises) Act, a UK wide piece of legislation also known as ‘Martyn’s Law’ which is expected later this year.In preparation for the Act the Education Authority will be progressing appropriate work with schools.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, in light of Duke of Edinburgh Award Scheme licensing changes, to detail the number of young people already engaged on a Duke of Edinburgh or President's Award Scheme in a school that are currently unable to continue their programme.
answer_text: A total of 29 schools were impacted due to the change made by the Duke of Edinburgh Award Scheme (DofE) in their licence arrangement with the Education Authority.Of the schools that are required to become Directly Licensed Centres:16 schools have acquired this status and13 schools have yet to acquire this status.All young people currently engaged with the programme will be able to continue.  DofE have ensured that arrangements are in place to ensure that every young person currently enrolled can continue and complete their Duke of Edinburgh and President’s Award.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the current waiting time for (i) paediatric squint surgery; and (ii) adult squint surgery.
answer_text: There are only two provider Trusts for ophthalmology, Belfast and Western, and only Belfast Health and Social Care Trust undertake paediatric squint surgery off a regional list.The current regional waiting list time, reported by the BHSCT, for paediatric squint surgery is 1 year and 9 months.The current waiting list time for adult squint surgery is:BHSCT – 7 yearsWHSCT –  6 yearsAlthough not sight-threatening, the presence of squint in both adults and children can be very distressing, and indeed can have a negative impact on both learning and employment opportunities. It is unacceptable that anyone should have to wait for the opportunity for surgical correction of squint for longer than is clinically necessary.My Department recognises the historical and ongoing workforce challenges that have resulted in these unacceptably-long waiting times. In order to help address this, my Department’s Elective Care Framework made a commitment to examining an extended role for orthoptists (AHPs) as assistant in theatre, which would support Consultant Ophthalmologists and recognise the unique skills of hospital orthoptists.This model has been successfully trialled in Belfast Trust for adult and paediatric cases. It includes a role for orthoptic-led pre-assessment and postoperative review clinics which frees consultant capacity and makes inroads into the long waiting times outlined above.In terms of impact, the pilot has allowed for approximately 14 adult lists to proceed from October 2024 to date with approximately 20 patients operated on and a further 20 removed by orthoptic assessment (without ophthalmology visits) as no longer suitable for the adult lists. The overall number waiting is reducing but has not necessarily impacted as yet on the length as the wait. The principles outlined within Integrated Elective Access Protocols are being adhered to.The paediatric the waiting list was 4 years pre-the pilot but this is now approximately 18 months.  And has permitted the release of extra capacity within paediatric clinics.  The model is currently being audited with a Business Case for mainstreaming currently being progressed, subject to available funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any guidance provided to schools by either the Department or the Education Authority following Storm Éowyn in relation to how they can support pupils experiencing power cuts or loss of water supply at home.
answer_text: EA's strategic priority following Storm Eowyn has been supporting schools for re-opening to ensure continuity of learning and a safe environment for children in their schools. This in turn provides a warm and safe education setting for children and young people. Direction was provided on Saturday 25th and Sunday 26th January to all schools on a number of issues including EA's emergency response, maintenance, continuation of catering services and remote learning. Direction was also provided to school principals via a live Teams call on Sunday 26th January; if a child presents to school without lunch they are to be fed and where additional costs are incurred EA would reimburse them.  EA have contacted all schools with guidance and continue to support schools where issues persist. EA maintained a range of helplines over Saturday 25th and Sunday 26th January and into the following week providing guidance on a number of issues including Maintenance, SEN, Safeguarding.  EA's School Improvement Professionals provide a direct conduit between EA and their cohort schools providing ongoing information and guidance. EA and schools have existing wrap around services already in place to help and support children during difficult situations. These include Pastoral Care, Safeguarding and Remote Learning Policies. These are delivered at a local level via highly trained professionals in Principals and teachers who experience similar occurrences on a business-as-usual basis and are managed at a school level. These will continue to be managed at that level.Schools are best placed to consider any necessary support to their pupils experiencing challenges due to a loss of power at home which may include additional flexibility, for example, around homework or uniform policies.  Local councils continue to offer facilities for the community in respect of showers, recreational areas and access to electric supplies to charge devices.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of classroom assistants currently working without an Access NI check.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) is the employing authority for the majority of classroom assistants working in schools in Northern Ireland. The EA has advised that as of 29 January 2025, 909 classroom assistants are still awaiting the outcome of Access Northern Ireland checks.While all classroom assistants are required to complete an Access Northern Ireland check, in the case of a short-term temporary hire or emergency cover appointment it is not always possible to finalise the Access Northern Ireland check prior to engagement.  In such situations, the school principal is required to complete a risk assessment and take all necessary steps to ensure the safeguarding of children and young people. This includes arranging for staff awaiting the outcome of an Access Northern Ireland check to be supervised at all times by someone who is vetted to work in regulated activity.  In addition, the EA has processes in place to follow-up and confirm that checks that were not able to be completed prior to engagement have subsequently been completed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, due to Duke Of Edinburgh Award Scheme licensing changes, to detail the number of schools now required to become Directly Licensed Centres to deliver the Duke of Edinburgh Award or President's Award Scheme.
answer_text: A total of 29 schools were impacted due to the change made by the Duke of Edinburgh Award Scheme (DofE) in their licence arrangement with the Education Authority.Of the schools that are required to become Directly Licensed Centres:16 schools have acquired this status and13 schools have yet to acquire this status.All young people currently engaged with the programme will be able to continue.  DofE have ensured that arrangements are in place to ensure that every young person currently enrolled can continue and complete their Duke of Edinburgh and President’s Award.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 20412/22-27, whether Bangor Academy and Sixth Form College would be reconsidered for transformation to integrated status if they responded to the Department's Call for Transformation as a means of meeting demand for integrated education.
answer_text: All schools that respond to a future call for transformation initiated by NICIE on behalf of the Department would be considered for transformation to Integrated status. Before any such transformation proposal can be approved a Development Proposal is required under Article 14 of the Education and Libraries (Northern Ireland) Order 1986. All such proposals would have to demonstrate how the school met the definition of Integrated Education within Section 1 of the Integrated Education Act 2022

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 20412/22-27, to detail (i) the six localities in Ards and North Down where there is demand for post-primary integrated education in excess of supply; and (ii) whether Bangor Academy and Sixth Form College is situated in any of these localities.
answer_text: The Section 10 report published in April 2024 sets out the six localities identified as having evidence of demand for post-primary integrated education in excess of supply. These were six localities, not as suggested by the question in Ards and North Down, but six localities across Northern Ireland. They are Belfast, Lisburn and Castlereagh, Ards and North Down, Antrim and Newtownabbey, Mid and East Antrim, and Newry and Mourne.The member will be aware of what constitutes integrated education as per Section 1(1) of the Integrated Education Act 2022.For the absence of any doubt I can confirm that Bangor Academy and Sixth Form College is located in Ards and North Down.Further information can be found at the following link;https://www.education-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/education/IEA%202022%20Section%2010%20Report.PDF

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 19810/22-27, to detail the rationale, based on a child-centred approach to special educational needs provision, for not including non-statutory preschools in the Learning Support Coordinators and Personal Learning Plans of the Special Educational Needs and Disability Act (Northern Ireland) 2016.
answer_text: The Special Educational Needs and Disability (SEND) Act (Northern Ireland) 2016 applies only to grant-aided schools; however, non-statutory pre-schools are encouraged to have regard to the statutory code of practice which provides a clear and consistent approach to identifying and providing support for children and young people with SEN.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any work carried out in collaboration with the Minister of Health (i) to benchmark how many allied health professionals (AHP) of each discipline are required to support the current needs of special educational needs learners in the education system; and (ii) to develop a joint workforce strategy to provide the appropriate number of AHPs required.
answer_text: The Department of Health (DoH) has advised that work is underway to further understand the Allied Health Professional (AHP) therapeutic needs of the children and young people currently enrolled in special schools.  This information will inform the commissioning of services for current and future need.   As we progress the governance and accountability structures around the Special Educational Needs (SEN) Reform Agenda, officials will consider the work of the existing Joint Health and Education Oversight Group (JHEOG), which includes membership from across both departments, the Education Authority (EA) and the Public Health Agency (PHA), to ensure that the work plan is aligned to the relevant delivery actions and that this work is prioritised. I am committed to working in partnership with the Minister of Health to ensure that we can deliver for children and young people with SEN and complex medical and healthcare needs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 20503/22-27, (i) for his assessment of the current capacity at Clarawood School, Belfast, to meet demand for specialist Social, Behavioural, Emotional and Wellbeing (SBEW) Needs; and (ii) whether he plans to introduce new or additional SBEW specialist settings in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: There is pressure for places at Clarawood School for pupils with Social, Behavioural, Emotional and Wellbeing (SBEW) needs.  The Education Authority (EA) is currently carrying out assessments to identify both short and long-term solutions to allow the school to meet this demand; this includes options to extend provision on the current Clarawood site as well as other off-site options.As indicated in the EA’s Operational Plan 2 (OP2), a ‘Plan of Action’ is being developed for special school provision in each Local Government District (LGD) to meet projected need with recommendations to be published by September 2025.  The EA provides Behaviour Support and Provisions to schools and children and young people with identified SBEW needs through its regional service.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 20410/22-27, (i) whether his programme of special educational needs (SEN) transformation and pending delivery plan will review the provisions in the Special Educational Needs and Disability Act (Northern Ireland) 2016, the supporting regulations and the code of practice; and (ii) for his assessment of whether the 2016 provisions, regulations and code of practice require updating to reflect his current SEN transformation agenda.
answer_text: The provisions laid out in the Special Educational Needs and Disability (SEND) Act (Northern Ireland) 2016, the supporting new draft Regulations and the Code of Practice are closely aligned with the changes which are fundamental to the delivery of my SEN Reform Agenda.  These include strengthening the support available to children with SEN, encompassing the views of the child in all decisions which affect them, improving co-operation with Health and reducing bureaucracy for schools and the Education Authority (EA).  As the SEN Reform Agenda progresses, all relevant legislation will be considered to ensure that it continues to support the best outcomes for children and young people with SEN.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail whether the bespoke regional programme for two to three year olds will (i) incorporate the Belfast Wide Early Years (BWEY) special educational needs programme; and (ii) include budget to ensure the continuation of BWEY programme into the 2025-26 financial year.
answer_text: As per my previous answer to AQW 20500/22-27, subject to funding, the regional programme will be co-designed with Health officials, including the identification of appropriate eligibility criteria for the programme.   Timing of roll-out of any regional programme will be subject to a decision on my department’s bid to the NICS Transformation Fund, which is awaited.  My department’s 2025-26 budget strategy process is currently ongoing.  Continuation of the Belfast Wide Early Years (BWEY) pilot will be subject to budget and to robust evaluation of the pilot to date.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of how adverse childhood experiences and trauma impact the criminal justice system.
answer_text: ORAL QUESTION No 4 - AQO 1469/22-27		ANSWERAs Justice Minister, I am acutely aware of the daily and cumulative impact of trauma, because the justice system is often the place of last resort for people and communities with trauma. Justice agencies like Youth Justice and Probation are leading the way in showing how to adopt trauma informed approaches in specialist services – that includes rethinking how we make every point of contact with service users an opportunity for support rather than potential re-traumatisation. This is essential if we want to stop the revolving door of hurt and futility that passes through generations affected by trauma. It also matters because trauma, and particularly complex trauma, is costly. Adverse Childhood Experiences alone cost Northern Ireland an estimated £1.3 billion annually. This is most acutely felt across the justice, education, and health and social care sectors. The Executive Programme for Paramilitarism & Organised Crime has led the way in championing a trauma informed Northern Ireland; and for trauma to feature in the Programme for Government. This is not just about providing specialist services. We all need some understanding of trauma and how it affects our decision-making and perspectives as well as our communities and public sector workforces.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 18330/22-27, when appropriate and consistent access to short break services for people with complex needs will be provided in the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust, arising from the £13 million investment announced in October 2024.
answer_text: Following the Minister’s announcement of the additional £2million expenditure in 2024/25, the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust has submitted a Business Case to the DoH’s Strategic Planning and Performance Group for consideration.  A proposal to fund the staffing model for the Residential Short Breaks Home in Newcastle has been approved. Recruitment will commence within this financial year and consistently delivering this service will be contingent upon successful recruitment of the staff team. Further developments are being explored to enable the resumption of residential short breaks in Lindsay House, however this may take in the region of six months to come to fruition. The South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust also delivers community based Short Breaks through the voluntary sector, as well as through the Trust’s own established team. The Trust’s investment proposal aligned to the Trust’s share of the £13m allocation will significantly enhance the community based Short Break provision for children with disability within the South Eastern HSC Trust.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of people in the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust that (i) are currently assessed as requiring residential short breaks; and (ii) access regular sort break services.
answer_text: (i) There are currently 85 young people assessed as requiring Residential Short Breaks in the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust.   (ii) There are 13 young people receiving regular Residential Short Breaks in the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any work carried out in collaboration with the Minister of Education (i) to benchmark how many allied health professionals (AHP) of each discipline are required to support the current needs of special educational needs learners in the education system; and (ii) to develop a joint workforce strategy to provide the appropriate number of AHPs required.
answer_text: Partnership working between Health and Education is essential to the delivery of a population health approach to support children and young people with SEN and a more seamless provision of multi-disciplinary, multi-service and multi-sectoral support.  The close working relationship between my Department, the Department of Education (DE), the Education Authority (EA) and the Public Health Agency (PHA) is increasingly crucial as both sectors respond to the increasing demands within the Special Schools, helping to inform planning and service response.  In addition to this work is underway to further understand the AHP therapeutic needs of the children and young people currently enrolled in Special Schools. This information will inform the commissioning of services for current and future need.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any action he is taking to address supply shortages of epilepsy medication.
answer_text: Medicines are manufactured as part of a highly regulated, complex and global industry; therefore, medicine supply disruptions can occur for a variety of reasons and are not uncommon. Although, my Department is aware that there have been some supply disruptions affecting various strengths and preparations of epilepsy medications in recent times, most of these disruptions appear to have now been resolved. The Department of Health and Social Care (DHSC) leads on the maintenance of medicine supply chains to the UK and works closely with the devolved administrations, including my Department and other operators within the supply chain to put in place tried and tested tools and processes should any disruptions arise. These existing tools and processes include communication to healthcare professionals such as National Patient Safety Alerts, Medicines Supply Notifications, NHS Specialist Pharmacy Services (SPS) resources including SPS medicines supply tool, and other mitigations such as Serious Shortage Protocols, additions to the export and hoarding ban list, additions to the NIMAR list, and the importation of medicines from other markets. When recent supply disruptions were identified, some of these tools and processes were implemented to ensure patients continued to have access to appropriate anti-epileptic treatmentsI also recently wrote to Wes Streeting MP, Secretary of State for Health and Social Care, and Karin Smyth MP, Minister of Health (Secondary Care), highlighting the impacts ongoing medicine shortages are having in NI and on the need for collaboration to improve the resilience of medicine supply chains across the UK. My Department remains committed to working with DHSC and the other devolved administrations to exploring options for improving the long term resilience of medicines supply to the UK.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 18330/22-27, for an update on the specific interventions to support families of children with complex needs that will be delivered from the £13 million investment announced in October 2024.
answer_text: On 23rd October 2024, I was pleased to be able to approve additional regional funding for the Children with Disability service across all HSC Trusts of up to £13m a year in multi-year funding, including up to £2m for the rest of the current financial year.It is intended that this additional funding will assist with increasing the current short breaks capacity, and expanding family, therapeutic and behavioural supports in the community across all HSC Trusts.In terms of the distribution of funding this has been agreed that it would be split per a capitation model based on population size. At this time, the DoH’s Strategic Planning and Performance Group has reviewed Trusts Prioritisation Proposals related to the £13 million investment. The proposed action plans that have been submitted will assist in alleviating some of the current pressures experienced within the Children with Disability Service. Each Trust is currently in the process of working with the DoH to complete the Business Cases for the final prioritisation proposals, in order to allow the allocation of funding. My Department and Health & Social Care Trusts continue to work collaboratively and at pace in terms of progressing matters. In relation to the short-term action plans – there are various initiatives across multiple trusts to improve family and community support services for Children with Disability ranging from enhancing short break provision to expanding targeted care programs. Collectively, the Trust plans aim to meet the short-term priorities, such as increasing short break provision and delivering tailored family support services. In addition, the Trusts do have plans to increase contracts with a range of voluntary and community services to provide additional family support, or breaks from caring through youth club type provision, after school clubs and enhanced opportunities for socialisation and personal development. Until the procurement process is completed and matters finalised – it would not be appropriate to provide all detail regarding the proposed organisations that the Trusts intend to use to provide support.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how the £0.042 million allocated for Private Schools VAT in the January Monitoring Round will be spent.
answer_text: Due to the introduction of VAT on private school fees by the UK Government, schools in Northern Ireland that meet the legal definition  of a private school must charge VAT on fees that fall within the scope of  this policy. Regrettably, this definition captures a number of grant aided schools funded under the Common Funding Scheme (CFS). As outlined in the Government’s technical note, education and boarding provided by state schools across the UK are not affected by this policy change, meaning they will continue to be exempt from VAT. This reflects the fact that state schools and academies continue to be “eligible bodies”.I highlighted to HM Treasury that state boarding pupils (i.e. pupils funded under the CFS) at Royal School Armagh and Royal School Dungannon have inadvertently been captured by the Government’s amended legislation.  HM Treasury has acknowledged the difference in treatment between state boarding pupils in Northern Ireland compared to other UK Jurisdictions and agreed to provide a funding solution to address this.The £0.042 million ringfenced funding will reimburse the VAT liability of the boarding fees associated with these pupils for the period 1 January to 31 March 2025, to provide an equivalent treatment to state boarding pupils across the UK that remain exempt from VAT.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education why there were no bids for Financial Transactions Capital funding in the January Monitoring Round.
answer_text: Financial Transaction Capital (FTC) funding can only be deployed by the public sector as a loan to, or equity investment in a private sector entity.  The private sector entity will then use the funding to invest in related infrastructure.  As schools funded via the Education Authority are classified to the central government sector, they are not eligible to receive FTC.  In short, FTC does not represent a viable alternative to the funding of my Department’s Schools Capital programmes as compared to conventional Capital DEL funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education why £1.8 million in non-ringfenced resource for the Early Learning & Childcare Strategy has been returned.
answer_text: £2m was allocated within the 2024-25 Early Learning and Childcare Funding package for a Targeted Business Support Scheme for childcare providers.  The Department for the Economy (DfE) is leading on a scoping exercise to develop a clearer picture of the business support requirements of the childcare sector to help inform an appropriate government response.  To date, DfE has drawn down just over £150,000 of the £2m available.  The scoping work is not due to be completed until the end of March 2025 and therefore the balance was returned to the Department of Finance as part of the January monitoring exercise, given that this funding is ringfenced by the Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education why £3.6 million capital funding for Fresh Start projects has been returned.
answer_text: All remaining Fresh Start funding available to my Department relates to projects that are currently in construction.  Unfortunately, as is the case in many construction projects, some minor delays have been encountered in several of the Fresh Start projects.As a result, the anticipated Fresh Start expenditure required for the 2024-25 year has reduced by £3.4m to £58.1m.  I expect any underspend will be carried forward and made available for Fresh Start projects in the 2025-26 financial year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education when the formulation of information sharing with the Department of Health and the Department for the Economy in relation to supporting children's educational journeys will be complete.
answer_text: On 13 January 2025, I announced my programme for reforming the provision of support for children with Special Educational Needs (SEN) and intend to bring the SEN Reform Agenda and Delivery Plan to the Education Committee in the coming weeks.The SEN Reform Agenda will introduce agreed data-sharing arrangements between my department, and the departments for Health, Economy and Communities as part of a number of actions within the five year Delivery Plan.In the interim, officials from my department will continue to work with colleagues across the departments to identify improvements and synergies to create better ways of working and sharing of information.The capturing of data to monitor improved outcomes for children and young people with SEN will be critical and I will be engaging with Executive colleagues to formalise a more comprehensive, coordinated approach to sharing information once this work has progressed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education when he will publish the Special Educational Needs Reform Agenda and Delivery Plan.
answer_text: The Special Educational Needs (SEN) Reform Agenda and associated Delivery Plan will be published in the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the proposed Park and Ride facility in Newtownards.
answer_text: Park and Ride is a key enabler to improving connections, increasing public transport use, promoting sustainability, and encouraging modal shift and active travel.In line with climate change objectives, park & ride facilities provide citizens with improved accessibility to public transport network and therefore encourage its usage as an alternative to commuting by car, helping to drive down congestion and emissions, change travel patterns and protect our environment.My Department has identified a list of priority Park and Ride sites, including a suitable site in Newtownards.  The preferred site at Newtownards is located at the former Ards Leisure Centre site with the potential for 450 vehicles.  This site will also alleviate pressures on Dundonald Park & Ride which continues to operate over capacity.The planning application for the Newtownards Park and Ride site was submitted in November 2023. Responses have been received from relevant statutory bodies and we are currently liaising, as appropriate, to any requests for clarifications.Change will not be possible without decarbonising private cars.  But we also need to provide attractive sustainable transport alternatives.  Such as public transport, walking, wheeling, and cycling.  Without these, our people, our cities and our towns will choke.  People deserve better; a cleaner greener urban environment in which they can thrive.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any engagement he has had with the Minister of Education in relation to placing allied health professionals in multi disciplinary teams to support schools with special educational needs learners.
answer_text: I regularly engage with the Minister for Education and his Departmental officials in relation to relevant matters regarding children with special educational needs (SEN).  My officials work collaboratively across numerous structures as part of the End-to-End Review of Special Educational Need (SEN). AHPs take a multidisciplinary and multiagency approach to address the therapeutic needs of the children and families with special educational needs. Partnership working between Health and Education is essential to the delivery of a population health approach to support children and young people with SEN and a more seamless provision of multi-disciplinary, multi-service and multi-sectoral support.  The close working relationship between my Department, the Department of Education (DE), the Education Authority (EA) and the Public Health Agency (PHA) is increasingly crucial as both sectors respond to the increasing demands within the Special Schools, helping to inform planning and service response.  PHA is currently working in partnership with EA on a comprehensive needs assessment around the health needs of children attending SEN schools. The review aims to identify the specific health challenges faced by this population group, evaluating the complexity of their needs to ascertain the support systems necessary to ensure timely, child-specific care within the school environment. In addition to this work is underway to further understand the AHP therapeutic needs of the children and young people currently enrolled in Special Schools. This information will inform the commissioning of services for current and future need.  My Department has also been working closely with the PHA and EA to proactively identify further opportunities to develop co-operative working, that will assist in delivering a more holistic approach to address the diverse needs of children and young people. This has facilitated cross agency learning, enhanced engagement and interagency working, which is critical in developing a wrap-around approach to supporting this cohort of children and young people at the earliest stage.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any action he is taking to address workforce shortages of classroom assistants.
answer_text: In November 2024, the Pay & Grading Agreement was implemented for support staff across the education system. This was a significant project aimed at addressing recruitment and retention challenges across a range of support roles, including Classroom Assistants, by reviewing the pay on offer for these roles. In addition to the Pay & Grading increase, the NJC annual pay award was implemented in December 2024, and this delivered a £1,920 uplift to all Classroom Assistants pay points. It is envisaged that the improved pay on offer for Classroom Assistants will help attract and retain staff in these roles. Additionally, the Education Authority (EA) is currently seeking to progress several strategically important projects targeted at improving recruitment and retention of Classroom Assistants. They have established a working group to explore and develop solutions to several wider challenges related to the Classroom Assistant recruitment and employment model. They have also engaged with school principals to better understand the challenges faced when appointing Classroom Assistants; and are planning a range of actions in response to this feedback. The EA has also submitted a bid for Public Sector Transformation funding to allow the development of an improved model and system for supplying temporary support staff, similar to the NISTR system for teachers.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any engagement he has had with the Minister of Health in relation to establishing multi-disciplinary teams to support schools to meet the needs of special educational needs learners.
answer_text: The move to Local Impact Team (LITs) is phase one of the Education Authority’s (EA) proposed Transformation Programme.  Further work to develop the model is ongoing to ensure alignment with health services.My department, along with the EA, has established governance and oversight structures that will provide strategic direction, guidance and subject matter expertise.  The Department of Health (DoH) are included within these structures.  Officials have been working closely together and, as the model evolves, will seek ways to further strengthen collaboration, information sharing and joined up delivery of Special Educational Needs/Disability (SEND) supports.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education when the role of special educational needs classroom assistants in preschool settings will begin.
answer_text: As part of my commitment to early intervention, my intention is to commence a pilot to introduce special educational needs (SEN) Classroom Assistants in a sample of preschool education settings in the 2025/26 academic year, subject to success in my department’s bid to the NICS Transformation Fund.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) when he plans to roll-out the regional programme for 2-3 year olds; and (ii) the eligibility criteria to access the programme.
answer_text: As part of my commitment to early intervention, subject to receipt of funding, I will introduce a bespoke regional programme for children age 2 to 3 years olds who may have special educational needs (SEN) or emerging needs.  This programme will be co-designed with Health officials, including the identification of appropriate eligibility criteria for the programme.  Early discussions have taken place through the newly established Health/Education Early Intervention Task and Finish Group which has been set up to develop detailed solutions particularly in the area of Early Years.  Timing of roll-out will be subject to a decision on my department’s bid to the NICS Transformation Fund, which is awaited.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 19621/22-27, whether consultations with schools will commence before February for pupils with special educational needs who have already nominated their preferred school for transition to post-primary education.
answer_text: Following receipt of Annual Review documentation for Primary 7 children, the Statutory Assessment and Review (SAR) teams have been drafting the amendments to these children’s statements.   Draft amended statements will be issued to parents of children in their Primary 7 year by 31 January 2025.  This allows parents to consider the content of their child’s proposed amended statement and formally express their preference of placement for Year 8.  Parents have 15 days to consider the proposed amended statement. Whilst parents may have indicated a school preference at their child’s Annual Review, which is helpful for planning types of places required, parents will also have been attending open nights at prospective post-primary schools and considering their options.  Generally, these take place in January.Consultations to post-primary schools will follow receipt of parental returns.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he plans to increase the number of specialist placements to deal with children with a Statement for Social, Behavioural, Emotional and Wellbeing Needs.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) through its special educational needs (SEN) Capacity Team is building on progress made last year when it created over 1,450 additional special education provision places in both special and mainstream schools.The Capacity Team has commenced work in identifying and creating anticipated additional places required in preparation for September 2025.  The EA has also recently published its Operational Plan 2 (2024-26) (OP2) which has a ‘SEN first’ focus and details at Local Government District (LGD) level what provision will be required during its lifespan.The type of placement for children with statements of SEN, irrespective of what is detailed as the overarching need in their statement, is based on recommendations of the EA’s Education Psychology Service (EPS).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he intends to introduce legislation related to special educational needs provision, including the purpose of such legislation and expected timescales.
answer_text: As part of the Special Educational Needs (SEN) Reform Agenda, the department will consider the need for a more streamlined single legislative Act on SEN provision, while also ensuring alignment with the direction of travel towards a more inclusive education system in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, following his announcement on 2 December 2024, to detail (i) what the specific funding allocation for homelessness prevention will fund in the 2025-26 financial year; (ii) whether the funding allocation includes the restoration of the Homelessness Prevention Fund previously funded by the Housing Executive; and (ii) any consultation his Department proposes to carry out with representatives of the sector on funding for the homelessness sector in 2025-26.
answer_text: My Department does not directly fund homelessness, this is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. 		The Finance Minister has issued an Executive Paper for consideration outlining Departments’ proposed Draft Budget Allocations for 2025-26. This budget is currently subject to consultation. 		My Department has just commenced work on internal budget allocations. Therefore, at this stage I am unable to advise the Housing Executive of its Homelessness budget allocation for 2025-26.  		My Department will continue to engage closely with the Housing Executive on their 2025-26 Budget requirements.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the steps he has taken to assess the level of demand for Integrated Education in the Ards and North Down area; and (ii) how he plans to meet that demand.
answer_text: According to statute, “Integrated education” means the education together, in an integrated school, of (a) those of different cultures and religious beliefs and of none, including reasonable numbers of both Protestant and Roman Catholic children or young persons; (b) those who are experiencing socio-economic deprivation and those who are not; and (c) those of different abilities.My Department set out how it will aim to meet demand in the Section 10 report published in April 2024. Our analysis of existing admissions choices data suggests that while there is broadly sufficient provision of Integrated Education at the primary school level to meet parental preference, the same cannot be said for post-primary provision. In some areas, there is a higher demand for integrated education provision than that which is currently available. It was identified that there is demand for post-primary integrated education in excess of supply in six localities including North Down and Ards. On conclusion of work to update guidance for schools considering transformation to Integrated status, my Department will commission the Northern Ireland Council for Integrated Education (NICIE) to engage with these oversubscribed integrated schools and the Education Authority (EA) in the six localities identified. The affected schools will be encouraged to review their enrolment numbers and school capacity and prepare and bring forward DPs to increase their enrolment numbers (if such changes are significant in nature). DE acknowledges that while this approach may partially address oversubscription issues, it is unlikely to resolve them in full. DE will therefore commission NICIE, working in partnership with EA and CSSC to issue a ‘Call for Transformation’ suggesting schools in the identified localities consider Transformation to Integrated status to meet demand. To further monitor demand across Northern Ireland, Departmental officials have included societal level questions on demand and the factors parents consider when makings school admission decisions in the annual Northern Ireland Life and Times survey.  Results will be available in late Spring with a report in the Summer.Officials are also working closely with colleagues in the Education Authority to develop and conduct a survey of parents who have recently made school preference decisions through the admissions portal. This survey will be carried out in April 2025 and will ask parents and guardians, in line with the Integrated Education Act, whether they have a preference for Integrated Education and how various factors have influenced their school preferences. Both surveys will allow my Department to consider existing and future demand and provide data on which existing Area Planning structures can take action to aim to meet that demand in the context the overall sustainability of the school estate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of the training that is required for teachers and support staff in schools to (i) carry out security risk assessments; and (ii) respond to a critical security incident in school.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) carry out training within schools and have provided the following response:Carry out Security Risk AssessmentsWhilst the EA offer no specific training for this type of risk assessment, schools are advised to complete the Security Risk Assessment to be found in the Department of Education publication Security and Personal Safety in Schools. This assessment is intuitive, to be completed after reading the guidance, with no training required for its completion. For more complex risk assessments involving specific situations, the EA provides generic Risk Assessments for Violence at Work, a Marauding Assailant and Site Security which can be found under Generic School Risk Assessments and Generic School Site Premises Risk Assessments respectively. Respond to a critical security incident in a schoolIn preparation for the impending Terrorism (Protection of Premises) Act, a UK wide piece of legislation also known as 'Martyn's Law' which is expected later this year, the EA will be uploading a security Lockdown Procedure for schools to its health and safety Manual for Principals and Governors. To support and enable planning for such emergency situations, a template for a school emergency plan will be released to all schools online in the Summer of 2025. Any requirement for security training required by the new legislation for 'standard tier' premises, of which most schools are, will be also addressed by the EA at that time.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy when he will bring forward a plan to deliver better post-19 provision for young people with special educational needs and disabilities.
answer_text: Question 11 – AQO 1387/22-27The commitment to improve post-19 provision for young people with Special Educational Needs is shared across the Executive.  My officials are currently taking forward a process of review and engagement, examining evidence on both provision and legislation.  This includes meeting with schools, parents, young people, colleges, other providers and representative organisations. In addition, they are engaging with counterparts in other departments and jurisdictions and have commissioned some comparative research from Stranmillis University College.  All of this work is continuing at pace, however, it is important that this is done with due diligence and care.  As I indicated in a reply to your written question last month, I expect to receive advice for my consideration in the Spring.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 19294/22-27, whether funding to schools for outdoor play will be earmarked for that purpose.
answer_text: All of the £4m will be allocated directly to the schools and pre-school settings for the purposes of purchasing outdoor learning equipment.  The Department is in discussion with the Education Authority regarding the appropriate procurement frameworks from which schools will be able to select items most suitable to their requirements.   Full guidance will be provided to schools in the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 19294/22-27, whether special schools will be eligible for outdoor play funding.
answer_text: I have stated that every grant-aided pre-school, nursery and primary school across Northern Ireland will receive funding for outdoor play equipment.  The level of funding received will be dependent on pupil numbers and details of the scheme will be provided to schools and settings over coming weeks.As our special schools provide education to children and young people of pre-school and primary age, they will also avail of an allocation within this project.In addition, under the overall Curriculum-led Capital Programme, all special schools will receive an allocation of desktop computers to replace those which have reached their end of life to ensure that students have access to secure, modern technology.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the process that was followed that led to the appointment of the new chairperson of the board of the Education Authority.
answer_text: The selection process was conducted in accordance with the Code of Practice issued by the Commissioner for Public Appointments for Northern Ireland (CPA Northern Ireland).The competition launched on 12 September 2024, inviting those interested in applying to complete an application form. Applicants were expected to demonstrate evidence (in their application and at interview) that they had the necessary experience, skills and competencies, to meet six essential criteria, which were as follows:Customer Focus / ServiceStrategic LeadershipFinance and Transformational Change ManagementGovernanceCo-operation and CollaborationCommunicationWritten applications were then considered by an assessment panel, with only those candidates who provided evidence of sufficient quality to meet the eligibility criteria invited to interview.Following the interviews the assessment panel provided an unranked list of four candidates who were considered suitable for appointment. In doing so they provided a candidate summary based on the information provided by each applicant at interview.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 19294/22-27, how schools receiving capital payments for new IT equipment will be identified.
answer_text: Every post-primary school has a number of end-of-life desktops. The funding will be used to replace these devices in every post-primary school to ensure that students have access to secure, modern technology.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 19109/22-27, to detail (i) the original planned completion date for the school management system; and (ii) the new anticipated completion date further to the end of the contract with Fujitsu.
answer_text: The 31 December 2024 was the original planned completion date for the school management system. The new anticipated completion date, following the termination of the Fujitsu contract, is 31 December 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education who is responsible for the line management of school principals.
answer_text: Principals are the lead professionals in their schools and they are responsible for leading and directing the work of all staff employed in the school.Principals report to their respective Board of Governors. The Education Authority (EA) is the employing authority for all controlled schools and the Council for Catholic Maintained School (CCMS) is the employing authority for all Catholic maintained schools.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) when the recommendations arising from the review of his Department's current provision for young people with special educational needs (SEN) will be published; (ii) when a time-bound action plan to improve provision will be published; and (iii) when options for legislation to better protect the rights of young people with SEN will be developed.
answer_text: I expect to receive advice from officials in the Spring and that will inform the next steps.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any additional resource that will be provided to non-statutory preschool settings to employ learning support coordinators and prepare Personal Learning Support Plans as required by the draft SEN Code of Practice.
answer_text: The Special Educational Needs Co-ordinator (SENCo) funding provided to schools in anticipation of statutory effect [Learning Support Co-ordinators (LSCs) and Personal Learning Plans (PLPs)] of the SEN/Disability (SEND) Act 2016, is for grant-aided schools only.  While non-statutory pre-schools should have regard to the draft SEN Code of Practice, there is no statutory obligation for these settings to employ LSCs or to prepare PLPs. The Education Authority (EA) has advised that:The EA’s SEND Implementation and Development team has provided non-statutory pre-school settings with all the resources provided to statutory nurseries to assist with SEN processes.  Non-statutory pre-school settings have also been included in the readiness for the Graduated Response training to take place from January 2025.  This training will focus on what makes a good PLP, provision mapping and setting specific expected outcomes.  In addition, the Pre-school Education Inclusion Support Fund (PSEISF) was launched in November 2024 and provides resource to all Department of Education (DE) funded pre-school education settings to help them to support children undergoing statutory assessment of their educational needs.  The funding may be used flexibly, including to pay for non-contact time to support the development of PLPs or Individual Education Plans (IEPs).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance when decisions will be made on funding to be awarded by the Interim Public Services Transformation Board.
answer_text: Significant progress has been made in this regard, but there are still a number of steps required before transformation funding can be awarded. This includes agreeing the interim Public Sector Transformation Board’s (the interim Board) terms of reference. I wrote to the Secretary of State on 12 November seeking a meeting to agree the terms of reference for the interim Board. This meeting, disappointingly, has not yet taken place. However, I expect to meet in January on this issue. The Executive will also have to agree how funding is allocated, following advice from the interim Board and subsequent recommendations from me as Finance Minister. I received the interim Board’s advice on the transformation proposals received from departments on 21 November, and then met with the Chair of the interim Board, Jayne Brady, on 10 December to discuss that advice in added depth. I intend to seek Executive agreement in January on the transformations proposals received. A Digital Landscape Review is currently ongoing and is estimated to be finalised by April 2025. Following this further funding decisions will be made.It is important to get ringfenced transformation funding to Departments as soon as possible. I am working to achieve this.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education when he will publish the revised Integrated Education Strategy and Action Plan.
answer_text: I am considering the comments made by the Northern Ireland Council for Integrated Education, the Integrated Education Fund and the Controlled Secondary School Council on the draft revised Integrated Education Strategy and Action Plan and expect to publish this for public consultation in January 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education when work will commence on the multi-purpose hall at St Francis' Primary School, Castlewellan.
answer_text: Departmental officials are currently completing a Business Case for the provision of a multi-purpose hall at St Francis’ Primary School. Progression of this scheme requires the vesting of additional land, and this process has been instigated by my department. Delivery of the scheme also depends on statutory approvals, including planning permission, as well as budget availability at tender stage.Given the early stage of this project, I am currently unable to provide a definite commencement date for the work. However, officials will keep the school updated on progress.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) when the impact of the pilot Pre-School Education Inclusion Support Fund will be reviewed; and (ii) when a decision will be taken on whether or not to extend the provision of this fund.
answer_text: The Pre-school Education Inclusion Support Fund (PSEISF) launched on 26 November 2024.  An evaluation of the impact of the pilot to date will be carried out in Term 3 of this year.This Fund is one of the initiatives funded from the Early Learning & Childcare (ELC) package of measures in 2024/25.  A decision on the extension of the Fund will be taken when my department receives confirmation of the funding position for the ELC Strategy for 2025/26.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 15280/22-27.
To ask the Minister of Education when the practice of treating children with special educational needs and disabilities as supernumerary will end.
answer_text: Children with a statement of special educational needs are considered as supernumerary for admissions purposes, meaning a child who holds a statement can access the school most suitable for their needs regardless of whether places remain available through the open enrolment procedure.  While this remains the existing practice, I do wish to consider the issue carefully to ensure the best possible approach to admissions for all children.My officials are in the process of bringing proposals to me for my consideration.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the communication that has been sent to school leaders in relation to the operation of the new local impact teams.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that:Our school and pre-school community is a key Special Educational Needs and Disability (SEND) Transformation stakeholder and engagement and communications with this group has been crucial in the project’s efforts to improve Stage 2 SEND supports for our children and young people. The SEND Transformation Team has engaged with school leaders throughout the life of the project from 2021 onwards, including providing written and video updates, invites to engagement sessions and requests to provide feedback on the Local IMPACT Team model.  In addition to this, the SEND Transformation governance structure includes the SEND Transformation Practitioners Forum which is comprised of principals, teachers, SENCos and classroom assistants.  It has been consulted throughout the development of the Local IMPACT Teams model and is continually updated via its scheduled meetings.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) when he will establish an area planning taskforce; (ii) the make up of this taskforce; and (iii) whether the taskforce will be independent of the Department.
answer_text: In the addendum paper deposited in the Assembly Library and accompanying my Statement to the Assembly of 21 October 2024, I set out my Department’s response to the recommendations made by the Panel for the Independent Review of Education.  I referenced in that paper that the Department accepts, in principle, Recommendation 20 (the reconfiguration of the network of schools) and that an Area Planning Taskforce will be created. In the context of the wider reform agenda that I set out in my statement, it will be important to determine what a Taskforce might meaningfully deliver to meet Area Planning work programmes and challenges prior to determining its membership.  I would expect my officials to proceed on this basis, working initially through established area planning structures and fora and to bring a report to me in due course.At this point I have not determined governance arrangements for the Taskforce or if it will act independent of, or within the Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education when he will answer AQW 19294/22-27.
answer_text: AQW 19294/22-27 was answered on 11 December 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education when the Education Authority aims to complete consultations with schools nominated by children currently with a statement of special educational need for the 2025-26 academic year.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) will be commencing consultations with schools from mid-February 2025 to secure placements for children at Primary 7 transfer age moving to Year 8 with statements for September 2025.  This follows receipt of the annual review of the statements submitted by schools, drafting of the amendments to the statements and receipt of parental views and school preferences for placement. Consultations to secure Primary 1 placements for children currently in Nursery with statements will commence by the end of March 2025 following receipt of the Annual Reviews from schools. All other children with statements requiring a change in placement as recommended by the annual review of their statements will receive amendments to those statements and following parental views and preferences will be consulted on with proposed schools from end of March 2025 also. Once consulted, schools have 15 days to respond having had regard to the statutory criteria in relation to whether the child’s identified needs can be met in their school setting. Children undergoing statutory assessments, which may or may not result in a statement of special educational needs (SEN) being made, must apply through the regular EA Admissions Service for placement in September.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education why children approved for deferred entry to statutory education in primary one are not permitted to complete a second year in a non-statutory preschool education setting.
answer_text: Children approved for deferred entry to statutory education in primary one are permitted to complete a second year in a non-statutory preschool education setting. However, the law requires that children can only be prioritised as target age once within the pre-school admissions process.  Therefore, any child who has attended or currently attends a funded pre-school education place as a final pre-school year child and applies for a second year of funded pre-school education would only be considered at Stage Two of the admissions process after target age applications have been considered and no guarantee can be given that they would obtain a place.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether all new school builds will be delivered to passive house standard.
answer_text: New school buildings are designed and built to exceed Building Regulations and are required to achieve a Building Research Establishment Environmental Assessment Method (BREEAM) rating of Excellent and an energy performance rating of at least 0.6.There are currently no plans to deliver new school builds to passive house standards.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, once the preschool standardisation process has completed, whether approved enrolment number will be allocated to both statutory and non-statutory preschool education setting.
answer_text: Approved enrolment numbers for statutory pre-school education are determined by the Department of Education under the Education (Northern Ireland) Order 1998.  This legislation does not apply to non-statutory pre-school settings.  Health and Social Care (HSC) Trusts are responsible for registering and inspecting non-statutory pre-school settings services against the requirements laid down in the Children (Northern Ireland) Order 1995.Non-statutory settings participating in the Pre-School Education Programme receive a pupil allocation number from the Education Authority, which is set annually taking into account a range of factors including the registration number of the setting and the level of demand for pre-school education provision in the area.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to provide his response to the independent review of education recommendation that current arrangements relating to funding, staff ratios and qualifications in public sector preschool education provision should be applied to private sector provision as soon as possible.
answer_text: In 2022 my Department commissioned a scoping study to analyse the current state of the pre-school education sector and cost a range of options for standardisation of funded pre-school education provision, including options relating to these issues. The report was circulated to Executive colleagues and is available on the Department’s website at Final Scoping Study Report | Department of Education (education-ni.gov.uk).Implementation of the options contained within the report is dependent on the necessary resources being made available to my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will establish an early year partnership board chaired by the Minister of Education and Minister of Health, as recommended by the Independent Review of Education.
answer_text: I would refer the member to the answer to AQW 17453/22-27.  The answer is contained in the Written Answers Booklet for week ending 1 November 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 17942/22-27, how he will assess the effectiveness of the investment in Specialist Provisions in Mainstream Schools when the money provided to establish them is not earmarked to be used specifically in the Specialist Provision.
answer_text: Schools are best placed to decide how the funding provided to them in establishing a specialist provision class is spent to meet the needs of their pupils.In terms of effectiveness of Specialist Provision in Mainstream Schools (SPiMS), as detailed in AQW 18619/22-27, the Education and Training Inspectorate (ETI) has prioritised the inspection of SPiMS.The ETI recently published Part 1 of its evaluation of the quality of education in SPiMS classes, which is available at: https://www.etini.gov.uk/sites/etini.gov.uk/files/publications/specialist-provisions-in-mainstream-schools-part-1.pdf. Part 2 of the evaluation of the quality of education in SPiMS classes commenced in September 2024 and the report is due to publish in Term 1 of the academic year 2025/26.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the criteria that will be used to assess a school's eligibility for funding for (i) outdoor covered sports spaces for primary schools; and (ii) synthetic pitches and sports halls for post-primary schools.
answer_text: The application process for both the pilot project to provide outdoor covered sports spaces for 20 primary schools and the programme for synthetic pitches and sports halls in the post-primary sector, will be launched by my department in the coming weeks. The criteria for eligibility for funding for both projects will be included within the application process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the total investment being made in the 2024-25 year in the Curriculum-Led Capital Investment Programme, broken down by (i) primary school physical education pilot programme for covered outdoor sports spaces; (ii) post-primary synthetic pitch and sports hall provision; (iii) computer equipment; (iv) outdoor play; and (v) any other curriculum area.
