The number of telephone calls and e-mails received by the EA’s FSM helpdesk during this application period has also decreased since last year and response times to both have improved.The measures implemented by the EA include FSM applications now being made through the EA Connect online portal. EA Connect offers a more user-friendly and integrated platform for parents. For those applying for FSM due to a special diet designation, an online form is now able to be used replacing the paper form used in previous years.  Special diet applications are also no longer required to include supporting medical evidence, thus simplifying the process for parents and enabling a faster processing time.  The EA has also intensified its promotional efforts to increase awareness of FSM and how parents may apply. This includes a number of tailored communications to families who were entitled to FSM in the previous school year but had yet to apply. The EA has also issued letters to schools and other key stakeholders that included a social media toolkit to encourage families to apply. The EA has also continued to increase awareness of FSM through a press release and social media as well as communications to other organisations such as public libraries and Sure Start settings. Improved materials on the EA website have also provided more easily accessed, clearer information and support materials for parents such as a sample Universal Credit statement and proof of benefits letter. Arrangements are also in place with the Department for Communities (DfC) whereby if a FSM application is submitted to the EA for the second time with incorrect Universal Credit documentation, DfC is asked to confirm entitlement rather than it being returned to the applicant. There has been a 63% reduction in the number of such cases since last year indicating that families are having fewer issues with the submission of the relevant documentation.My officials also continue to engage with EA and DfC colleagues as well as counterparts across the UK in exploring how the FSM application process can be further streamlined to ensure families are able to access FSM as easily and early as possible. This includes exploring options that could reduce the instances where benefits-based evidence needs to be provided by applicants and which could potentially enable the EA to implement a form of auto-enro

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on repeal to Article 4 (1) (c) of the Education (Northern Ireland) Order 1998.
answer_text: I have considered analysis from the responses to the consultation on the reduction and management of restrictive practices in educational settings and asked officials to work with stakeholders to revise and refine the draft guidance, reflecting on the issues raised.  The issue regarding repeal of Article 4 (1) (c) of the Education (NI) Order 1998, will be considered in the context of the revised guidance.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 14264/22-27, whether he is engaging with the Minister of Health to assess the scope for jointly funding counselling services in primary schools.
answer_text: There is no specific engagement currently ongoing in relation to a jointly funded primary school counselling service between the Departments of Education and Health. You will be aware of the challenging financial context across departments which means that budgets and investments are severely constrained.  As with all services that are not statutory, investment is limited to budget availability.    However, both departments continue to collaborate to provide wider emotional health and wellbeing support in primary schools through the joint DE / DoH Children and Young People’s Emotional Health and Wellbeing in Education Framework. The main emphasis of the Framework is to support schools to promote emotional health and wellbeing at a universal level and providing early and enhanced support for those children and young people who may be at risk or showing signs of needing further help.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether the UK government's recent announcement on pausing City Deal Funding will impact the redevelopment of Whitespots Country Park, Newtownards.
answer_text: In December 2021, the allocation of some £52 million of the £100 million Complementary Fund was agreed by the Executive for five projects. This included £7.4 million for the Whitespots Country Park. The Complementary Fund is an Executive initiative which sits alongside City and Growth Deals, rather than being part of it. Funding for these five projects has been agreed and therefore these projects should continue as planned.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the internal consultation process which was taking place in his Department on the findings of the Independent Review of Education.
answer_text: Departmental officials have completed this process. I have recently received advice from officials, and I will announce a response to the recommendations in the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when he will provide an update on the A24 Ballynahinch bypass.
answer_text: Major Road schemes are an important part of the work that is delivered by my Department as we seek to reduce journey times, increase reliability and improve road safety.The A24 Ballynahinch Bypass will deliver a 3.1km bypass to the east which will improve traffic conditions in the town centre. Details of the current status of the A24 Ballynahinch Bypass project will continue to be available on the project webpage which can be viewed at: A24 Ballynahinch Bypass - overview | Department for Infrastructure (infrastructure-ni.gov.uk)

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 11333/22-27, AQW 11334/22-27 and AQW 11336/22-27, whether he will publish his assessment of the Concluding Observations.
answer_text: The UN Committee’s Concluding Observations were published in June 2023 and circulated to all NICS departments.  There is no follow-up procedure on behalf of the Committee, which requires reporting on these in advance of the next State Party examination, likely to be 2030 at the earliest.  I have decided that, due to the significant resource pressures currently facing my department, DE will not be carrying out a coordinated assessment across departments at this stage.  I will keep this under review.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 12370/22-27, for an update on the review of suspensions and expulsions.
answer_text: I will be considering the draft report on the review of suspension and expulsion arrangements in the coming weeks. Once I have considered the draft report, I will determine the next steps to be taken.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 13936/22-27, when the review of the new funding scheme for youth work will be published.
answer_text: Officials are checking with the Education Authority on any recent actions which have been taken to ensure that the final summary report is as up-to-date as possible.   I expect to be able to publish the report in the coming days.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 13268/22-27, for an update on the creation of a more sustainable model of school transport.
answer_text: My Department continues to work with the Education Authority (EA) on developing the SMART transport project which aims to digitise certain transport management functions such as route planning and which will involve installing vehicle telematics and other digital solutions that will enable the EA to have visibility of all services travelling to a school. This should enable a more strategic approach to be taken with regards to route planning and the optimisation of the school transport network, thereby delivering efficiencies. The ability to monitor pupils boarding and the tracking of vehicles’ progress in real time will also offer benefits in terms of increased safeguarding of pupils on school transport and will enable schools and parents to be better informed about their children’s journey. The EA has also been piloting the use of a smaller seven-seater vehicle within its fleet since May 2024. Whilst it is too early to make a full assessment, the EA informs me that this pilot and the use of a smaller vehicle is proving to be promising, affording some flexibility within its fleet and has delivered financial savings. It has enabled the EA to deliver stable provision for several pupils with additional transport requirements for whom there had been difficulty in securing suitable taxi transport which had resulted in disruption to their transport to school. EA transport has advised that it has been utilising this vehicle since the start of term to provide regular transport for pupils which has directly replaced the need for two taxis and has freed up further capacity on other larger EA vehicles.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the Bangor Academy and Sixth Form College development proposal.
answer_text: On 8 February 2024, the Education Authority (EA) published Development Proposal 727 at the request of the Board of Governors (BoGs) of Bangor Academy and Sixth Form College proposing that:Bangor Academy and Sixth Form College will transform to Controlled Integrated Status with effect from 1 September 2025, or as soon as possible thereafter.The statutory two-month objection period in relation to this Development Proposal ended on 22 April 2024 and I am informed that a draft report is currently undergoing final review and will shortly be passed to me to inform my decision.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the development of Mid Down Integrated College.
answer_text: Land and Property Services have conducted a site search and have identified two sites. My department has been working with stakeholders to determine the scope of works for the appointment of an Integrated Consultant Team (ICT) for the project. On completion of this work, the procurement process will be initiated.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the development of Causeway Academy.
answer_text: As Causeway Academy is a controlled school, the Education Authority (EA) is progressing a project to locate the three former schools onto a single site by September 2026, subject to available funding. The EA is currently reviewing the feasibility of a number of sites for ‘Day One’ operation and will update the project board on options. However, the preferred option will ultimately be identified through the business case process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the implementation of the Integrated Education Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.
answer_text: As required by the legislation, the Department published the statutory report (the Section 10 Report) on Integrated Education on 26 April 2024. Officials are continuing to work closely with the Northern Ireland Council for Integrated Education (NICIE) and the Education Authority (EA) to implement relevant actions within the S10 report.In addition, my officials are currently drafting an updated Integrated Education Strategy and Action Plan as required under Section 10 (6) of the Act.  This revised Strategy and Action Plan will be published and open to public consultation as soon as practically possible following consultation and engagement with NICIE, Integrated Education Fund (IEF) and Controlled Schools Support Council (CSSC) as required under Section 3 of the Act.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he intends to publish the full business case for the Strule Shared Education Campus.
answer_text: The Full Business Case (FBC) for Strule and all accompanying Departmental advice are commercially sensitive documentation and there are no plans to publish the FBC at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the recommendation contained in the Review of Department of Education Targeted Early Years Interventions December 2023 report, that, given the significant health outcomes from SureStart and in line with the Children’s Service Co-operation Act (Northern Ireland) 2015, his Department should consider how they could make further contributions to SureStart.
answer_text: While I am unable to commit to making further contributions to SureStart at this time due to the scale of the financial challenge facing my department, we will continue to fund the programme’s management team and ensure that all Health and Social Care Trusts continue to prioritise active engagement in the 38 Sure Start programmes through the release of around 250 staff posts and participation in or leadership of individual projects.My Department also invests in a number of other important family support interventions such as the Family Support Hubs and the Early Intervention Services across Trusts.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the creation of an Inclusive Education policy.
answer_text: Officials are concluding some initial work with the European Agency for Special Educational Needs (SEN) and Inclusion which has considered current Northern Ireland education policy in the context of global developments in relation to inclusive education systems.  This work will subsequently be brought forward as proposals for my consideration.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on his Department's work to support students with special educational needs who are transitioning from school to further education.

To ask the Minister of Education for an update on any discussions with Education Training Inspectorate in relation to the implementation of the Addressing Bullying in Schools Act (Northern Ireland) 2016 forming part of school inspection.
answer_text: Officials work closely with colleagues in the Education and Training Inspectorate (ETI) to consider ways to support the implementation of the Addressing Bullying in Schools Act (Northern Ireland) 2016 (the Act). In particular, there is ongoing work to ensure that the new Inspection Framework, the ‘Empowering Improvement Framework’, supports the key requirements of the Act, including school level compliance.  As part of the new Inspection Framework, questionnaires are being piloted that include a focus on positive behaviour, child protection, safeguarding and bullying type behaviour. These will be provided to pupils, parents / carers and education setting staff.  Discussions will also take place with key staff, governors and pupils relating to the implementation of the school’s policy on addressing bullying type behaviour.  In addition, the Safeguarding proforma, available to all schools, includes prompts relevant to addressing bullying policy and procedures.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education when he will publish a Special Educational Needs Transformation Plan as part of the End to End Review of Special Educational Needs.
answer_text: My department is concluding the End to End (E2E) Review of Special Educational Needs (SEN) and intend to publish a summary report on its findings in autumn 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of the preventative value of providing counselling services in primary schools; and (ii) whether he is working with the Minister of Education to consider jointly funding these services.
answer_text: The case for investing in therapeutic interventions in schools is well made with recent studies on a UK basis showing the positive impact that they can have on both pupil wellbeing and prevention of long-term mental illness. I am unaware of any specific studies which have taken place relating to provision of these services in Northern Ireland.There is no specific work currently ongoing in relation to funding counselling services in primary schools. The challenging financial context across both Departments of Health and Education mean that budgets and investments are severely constrained. Schools do have the flexibility to use their individual budgets to meet pupil needs, which can include employing a counsellor or therapist. However, both Departments do collaborate to provide wider mental health support in primary schools through engagement and joint funding. In February 2021, the Department of Health and the Department of Education jointly launched the Children and Young People’s Emotional Health and Wellbeing in Education Framework.  The main emphasis of the Framework is to support educational settings to promote emotional health and wellbeing at a universal level, through a holistic, multi-disciplinary approach. This will enable our children and young people to build resilience in coping with challenges they may face in life and providing early and enhanced support for those who may be at risk or showing signs of needing further help. Services and projects that have been established under the Framework are available across primary and post-primary schools. The following initiatives have been piloted or rolled out to primary schools since the publication of the Framework.School Nursing Pilot (commenced 2021/22)Resilience in Education, Assisting Change to Happen (commenced 2021/22)Regional Integrated Support for Education - Expansion of the existing RISE service (expanded from up to primary 4 to up to primary 7) - (commenced 2021/22)Being Well Doing Well – (recently commenced in the 2023/24 academic year.)  The Attach Project (TAP) (commenced 2021/22)Further information on each of the above projects can be found at: Emotional Health and Wellbeing | Department of Education (education-ni.gov.uk)

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of children with special educational needs and disabilities who have a special transport need that are currently awaiting confirmation of their transport assistance.
answer_text: The transport requirements for a child with special educational needs (SEN) or disabilities are determined by the Education Authority (EA) during the statutory assessment and review process and a referral is only made to the EA transport service after a school place has been allocated. It takes time to put transport arrangements in place, particularly for those children who require more bespoke arrangements and where further input is needed from parents or health professionals. Where a new taxi run is required, EA must follow a procurement process and carry out driver and vehicle compliance checks. Access NI checks must be carried out on drivers and escorts and any specialist training delivered.  The EA informs me that as of 24 September 2024, 230 pupils of compulsory school age who have a special transport need identified on their statement of SEN were awaiting confirmation of their transport arrangements, 192 of whom were referred in September. It is important to note that this is a continuous process with 2,728 new SEN transport referrals having been received so far for the 2024/25 school year of which 373 were made in September. Work is in progress to secure appropriate transport for 200 of the 230 school aged children who have not yet received their confirmation and the EA is awaiting further information to establish the exact requirements of the remaining 30 open referrals.  In addition to those of compulsory school age, the EA transport service is currently progressing 79 referrals for transport assistance for children who have a nursery placement, many of whom have not yet started school, had their session times confirmed or whose parents have not yet decided if they wish to avail of school transport.I am advised that the EA transport service is working at pace to progress outstanding referrals and where it is unable to have a transport solution in place within 20 days, a parental payment will be offered.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) for his assessment of the effectiveness of the new Family Support Link Officer role in the Education Authority; and (ii) whether families who were assigned a Family Support Link Officer will be given the opportunity to provide feedback on this engagement.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) appointed a number of Family Support Link Officers (FSLOs) in June this year to support the families of children with a statement of special educational needs (SEN) who did not yet have a confirmed school place.  These officers provided a regular, family-led single point of contact with parents and carers as they awaited confirmation of their child’s placement.  The EA intends to complete an evaluation of the use of FSLOs by capturing the views of parents and carers in the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether an Equality Impact Assessment has taken place for his 2024-25 budget allocations.
answer_text: An Equality Impact Assessment has not yet been undertaken in respect of the 2024-25 budget allocation.  Similar to other departments, the need for an Equality Impact Assessment can only be determined after the final budget allocation has been confirmed.  As I have recently finalised decisions in relation to the 2024-25 Education Budget, my Department is now in the process of determining whether an Equality Impact Assessment is required.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether nursery schools and preschool settings have access to support from the Education Authority Children Looked After team.
answer_text: The Department of Education provides funding to the Education Authority (EA) Primary Children Looked After Advisory Service (PCLAAS), which provides a range of support to primary schools.   This service does not extend to nursery schools and preschool settings.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any additional financial support available to preschool and nursery settings which have children looked after on their enrolment.
answer_text: My department is not, at present, providing additional financial support to preschool and nursery settings which have children looked after on their enrolment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the work of the Transitions Inter-departmental Working Group.
answer_text: The Transitions Inter-departmental Working Group published its closing report in February 2006 and actions have been taken forward by respective owners.A new Transitions Working Group was established in 2022 as part of the End to End (E2E) Review of Special Educational Needs (SEN) and is co-chaired by the Departments of Health (DoH) and Education (DE).  The group has re-engaged with parents, carers and young people who have gone or are going through the transitions planning process.  This work is almost complete and proposals for moving forward are emerging and will be included in the summary report of the E2E Review of SEN.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the development of a successor programme to the Skills for Life and Work Programme.
answer_text: My officials are currently developing the successor programme to Skills for Life and Work to be in place from August 2025.The focus is currently on the development of the best programme for this cohort of young people, including those young people with disabilities or other barriers to participation and achievement, to ensure that the programme presents a person-centred approach to learning.A Public Consultation on proposals for disability support in the new provision was launched on 19 July 2024 and will run until 18 October 2024.  Responses will provide valuable insights into how we best support learners within the new programme.  A procurement competition for the new programme is scheduled to launch early in the New Year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the review of all further education student support measures.
answer_text: My Department is currently undertaking a review of Further Education student support. The review was commissioned from the Strategic Investment Board and a report with recommendations was received in January 2024. Officials are currently considering the feasibility of taking forward the recommendations contained within the report.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on when he will commence Section 4 of the Special Educational Needs and Disability (Northern Ireland) Act 2016 in order to undertake a review into how the Education Authority and the Health and Social Care authorities have co-operated in their duties in relation to the act.
answer_text: The Department of Education (DE) is working closely with the Education Authority (EA) and the Department of Health (DoH) on finalising the draft plan on co-operation.  Once developed, this will be discussed with the Education and Training Inspectorate (ETI) and the Regulation and Quality Improvement Authority (RQIA) as they are required to inspect the plan every three years after it is published. Section 4 of the Special Educational Needs and Disability (SEND) (Northern Ireland) Act 2016 is not reliant on supporting new SEN regulations being made through the Northern Ireland Assembly so can be commenced as soon as the EA and DoH are ready.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the creation of a costed implementation plan for the Adult Learning Disability Service Model.
answer_text: The Department established a Task & Finish Group to finalise a Learning Disability Service Model (LDSM) as part of a wider exercise to improve outcomes for children and adults. Throughout 2023/24, there has been a significant review of evidence, data, and stakeholder feedback to inform the development of a service model for adults with a diagnosed learning disability who have been assessed as needing additional health and social care support. A draft service model has been developed in collaboration with Trusts, independent sector providers and people that use HSC services. The draft model sets out a regionally consistent framework to improve outcomes across several HSC areas, including: (i) Transitions; (ii) Health & Wellbeing; (iii) Day Services and Meaningful Activity; (iv) Support for Families and Carers; (v) Home and Independent Living; and (vi) Mental Health and Behaviours of Concern.Work is currently underway to produce an implementation plan in collaboration with key partners across the sector. In August 2024, a regional workshop was held to finalise the delivery plan and take decisions on inpatient services ahead of the closure of Muckamore Abbey Hospital. In parallel to this work, officials are undertaking a financial review of learning disability services, which will form the basis for costings to implement the Learning Disability Service Model. In terms of next steps, my officials are preparing advice for my consideration.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the current estimated spend required to deliver the pre-school standardisation programme; and (ii) the budget that has been provided to the programme for the 2024-25 financial year.
answer_text: A Scoping Study was commissioned by my Department in late 2022 to assess options and costs for a pre-school standardisation programme.  The final report for the study is available on the DE website at Final Scoping Study Report | Department of Education (education-ni.gov.uk).In addition to the core objective of providing all children with 22.5 hours per week of funded pre-school education, the report identifies and costs a range of potential additional actions including changes to pre-school staffing ratios. The report estimates the additional cost of transitioning to a standardised session length to be within the range £242.8m to £337.8m, uplifted for wage and price inflation over a 10-year implementation period. Other options set out in the report such as increasing the staff-to-child ratio and improving access to SEN support would add a further £156m over the same 10-year implementation period and providing a funded school meal for all pupils would add a further £7.8m per annum.The scale of what I can do in implementing options contained in the report and the pace at which we can progress will be determined by the budget allocated by the Executive. £25m has been earmarked for an ELC Strategy in the current financial year, with £5m of that assigned for PSEP standardisation. This will allow me to begin the process of transitioning pre-school sessions to 22.5 hours and I hope to transition approximately 2,200 pre-school places for September 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will commit to retaining the expanded 153 Sure Start areas, in line with recommendations in the Review of Department of Education Targeted Early Years Interventions.
answer_text: Additional funding provided by the Executive for early learning and childcare has enabled me to approve continuation of Sure Start provision in the 153 legacy Super Output Areas (SOAs) as recommended in the Review of Targeted Early Interventions report.    I have also approved the permanent expansion of Sure Start to an additional 22 SOAs which had been included on a temporary basis since May 2021.  The Department of Health (DoH) is responsible for the administration of Sure Start on behalf of my Department.  My officials have been engaging with DoH colleagues to refine current processes and enable reprofiling of available budget across all Sure Start projects.The recommendation to provide flexible funding for Sure Start projects of five per cent on top of the existing budget to meet need outside of catchment areas will require consideration in the longer term and be subject to available budget.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will create a process that allows for re-profiling of available funding across all 38 Sure Start projects, as opposed to within each individual project, as recommended in the Review of Department of Education Targeted Early Years Interventions.
answer_text: Additional funding provided by the Executive for early learning and childcare has enabled me to approve continuation of Sure Start provision in the 153 legacy Super Output Areas (SOAs) as recommended in the Review of Targeted Early Interventions report.    I have also approved the permanent expansion of Sure Start to an additional 22 SOAs which had been included on a temporary basis since May 2021.  The Department of Health (DoH) is responsible for the administration of Sure Start on behalf of my Department.  My officials have been engaging with DoH colleagues to refine current processes and enable reprofiling of available budget across all Sure Start projects.The recommendation to provide flexible funding for Sure Start projects of five per cent on top of the existing budget to meet need outside of catchment areas will require consideration in the longer term and be subject to available budget.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will provide flexible funding for each Sure Start of 5 per cent on top of the existing budget on a case-by-case basis, as recommended in the Review of Department of Education Targeted Early Years Interventions.
answer_text: Additional funding provided by the Executive for early learning and childcare has enabled me to approve continuation of Sure Start provision in the 153 legacy Super Output Areas (SOAs) as recommended in the Review of Targeted Early Interventions report.    I have also approved the permanent expansion of Sure Start to an additional 22 SOAs which had been included on a temporary basis since May 2021.  The Department of Health (DoH) is responsible for the administration of Sure Start on behalf of my Department.  My officials have been engaging with DoH colleagues to refine current processes and enable reprofiling of available budget across all Sure Start projects.The recommendation to provide flexible funding for Sure Start projects of five per cent on top of the existing budget to meet need outside of catchment areas will require consideration in the longer term and be subject to available budget.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will implement the recommendation in the departmentally commissioned report from Ulster University, Free School Meal Entitlement as an Indicator of Socioeconomic Deprivation in NI: Advantages, Disadvantages and Alternatives, to use Free School Entitlement over a three to six year period as the measure of Socio-Economic Deprivation.
answer_text: The Ulster University research report recommended that further investigation should be carried out into replacing the current/past year FSME measure with an expanded free school meal entitlement (FSME) measure such as FSME ever6, which measures if a pupil has been entitled to free school meals at any time over the last six years. The Department collects information on FSME ever6 through the annual school census and is investigating how to collect FSME ever3. FSME ever6 will be available as a measure of socio-economic deprivation for policy officials who currently use FSME as an alternative indicator or to supplement other measures of deprivation to better target policies and programs.Preliminary analysis indicated that changing to the FSME ever6 measure would increase the number of pupils considered in the allocation of funding for tackling educational disadvantage. Without an increase in funding available, such a change would expand the number of eligible children, thereby reducing the amount allocated per pupil. Another limitation of FSME ever6 is that it is not available for most primary school children as they have not been in school for six years. In some cases, the use of ever6 could skew support towards older pupils and away from others such as newcomers. For these reasons, it has been decided that the current/past year FSME measure is the most appropriate for the purposes of those policies/programmes. The Department will continue the practice of supplementing this measure, where feasible, with additional indicators of socioeconomic disadvantage.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the publication of the final report from the Transitions Planning workstream as part of the End to End Review of Special Educational Needs.
answer_text: My department is concluding the End to End (E2E) Review of Special Educational Needs (SEN) and intend to publish a summary report on its findings in Autumn 2024, which will include the outworkings of the Transitions Workstream.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether the Education Authority maintains a record of how many statements are shared with future education providers when a child leaves school.
answer_text: This information is not held or recorded by my department or the Education Authority (EA).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the engagement his Department has undertaken with school leaders in relation to schools assuming responsibility for the preparation of a child’s first transition plan under the new Special Educational Needs Framework.
answer_text: The responsibility for schools to prepare a child’s first transition plan is contained within the new draft Special Education Needs (SEN) regulations and the associated SEN Code of Practice. The documents were publicly consulted on between 30 September 2020 until 2 March 2021 and a number of schools submitted responses to both.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education when he will confirm his response to the consultation on the Reduction and Management of Restrictive Practices in Educational Settings.
answer_text: My department’s response to the consultation will be published alongside revised guidance when it is finalised.  I have considered available analysis from the consultation responses and asked officials to work with stakeholders to revise and refine the draft guidance, reflecting on the issues raised.  The ongoing process is intended to ensure that forthcoming guidance will meet the needs of children and young people and be deliverable within our educational settings.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education when the review of the Common Funding Scheme will commence.
answer_text: I would refer the Member to my answers to his questions AQW 7706/22-27 and AQW 11870/22-27.  At this stage, there is no update from the previous answers provided.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) how his Department identified the need for a pilot project to support schools with restricting access to mobile phones; and (ii) whether a Business Case demonstrating value for money was submitted and approved for the pilot project.
answer_text: There are growing concerns about the impact of mobile phones on children and young people’s development and their unauthorised use in class or during the school day.  Many countries across the world are increasingly taking steps to restrict phone use at school.  There is evidence to indicate that by limiting the use of mobile phones during the school, there can be benefits for academic engagement and achievement.  Regarding the pilot project, the recently published guidance to schools outlines a range of different approaches that schools could potentially utilise to restrict mobile phone use.  These approaches will have different strengths and weaknesses and schools are encouraged to develop policies, through consultation with their own school community, that are deliverable and effective in their own circumstance. One possible approach that has been used internationally but not, thus far, in Northern Ireland is the use of mobile inhibiting devices.  To test and evaluate this specific approach further, I have asked departmental officials to develop a small pilot project.  It is anticipated that the delivery of the small-scale pilot will cost a maximum of £250,000.  Officials are preparing a business case for the pilot and an associated specification for the procurement process.  There is scope within this process to consider options to scale the pilot as needed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the support available for community-based childcare providers to help meet the needs of children with special educational needs and disabilities.
answer_text: The Department of Health, through the Childcare Partnership, facilitates an annual grant scheme to ensure children with additional needs are included in early years services, known as Fair Play. The funding provides support measures with a focus on the individual needs of children who are accessing quality play and learning experiences in registered community, voluntary and private provision throughout the academic year. These support measures can and do include additional staff capacity.The Childcare Partnership also provides free training for early years practitioners in a range of subjects to do with additional needs in tandem with this financial support. Throughout the 2023/24 period, 59 early years groups received financial support which included 248 children with additional needs including SEN and disability. In the 2024/25 financial year, this support to non-statutory early years providers has been £425k. Due to finite resources not all applicant groups can be supported.  This is an unintended consequence of the fund due to its current size and the level of support needed in the early years sector.   The forthcoming inter-departmental Early Learning and Childcare Strategy has added additional resources to extend the reach of the Fair Play Grant Scheme in 2024/25 bringing the fund to a total of £675k in this financial year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether the approved business case for Strule Shared Education Campus demonstrated value for money for the project.
answer_text: The procurement of the main work contract for the Strule Shared Education Campus was taken forward under a Ministerial Direction endorsed by the Northern Ireland Executive in 2021. The Direction gave authority to proceed with the programme to completion, with review at key points. Value for Money could not be determined definitively in regard to the main works procurement due to the lack of competitive tension in the Competitive Procedure by Negotiation process and the lack of a directly comparable benchmark project in the schools’ estateAs Strule proceeded to procurement under a 2021 Ministerial Direction which was endorsed by the then Executive, on 18 July 2024, the Northern Ireland Executive reviewed the completed Full Business Case and up-to-date programme position and costs and in light of the position re VFM as set out above, agreed it should immediately proceed to contract award.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any available data on the impact of Brexit on (i) student; and (ii) staff mobility in further education colleges, including any changes in participation in (a) exchange programs; (b) cross-border collaboration; and (c) international recruitment.
answer_text: My officials are advised by Further Education Colleges that while data is held in respect of student and staff mobility, analysis has not been conducted on the impact of EU Exit on such activity, advising that a range of factors will have an effect on student and staff mobility. Other than reporting that the Colleges have received less funding for the activities identified under the Turing Scheme post EU Exit in comparison to its predecessor Erasmus+ pre EU Exit, I am unable to provide further information.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the support provided to schools to resource the cost of school trips.
answer_text: All grant-aided schools are funded under the Common Funding Scheme (CFS).  The Common Funding Formula (CFF) is the Department’s mechanism for allocating budgets to schools funded through Local Management of Schools arrangements. A school’s CFF allocation is expected to meet a range of items of expenditure, as detailed in Annex A1 of the CFS, including education visits and journeys.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the development of a new programme to provide counselling services in primary schools.
answer_text: My department is not, at present, developing a programme for counselling services in primary schools.  The challenging financial context across the education system means that budgets and investments are severely constrained.  As with all services that are not statutory, investment is limited to budget availability.    Schools continue to have flexibility to use their individual budgets to best meet the needs of their pupils.  In some circumstances this can include employing the services of a counsellor or therapist from existing funds.  Any immediate and appropriately assessed need for counselling or therapy support for primary age children continues to be available through health services, including CAMHS and associated pathways and support services.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on plans to develop a park and ride facility in Comber.
answer_text: My Department has identified a list of priority Park and Ride sites, including a suitable site in Comber.  The preferred site is located along the A22 Belfast Road corridor immediately adjacent to the Comber Greenway with the potential to provide parking provision for 626 vehicles. Whilst I am supportive of expanding our Park and Ride schemes where needed, the member will be aware that my Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to under funding and austerity by the British Government.  As the capital budget for my Department in 2024/25 is less than required, available funding must be prioritised accordingly.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education when he will answer AQW 13613/22-27.
answer_text: AQW 13613/22-27 was answered on 8 July 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the impact of vagrancy laws on levels of chronic homelessness in Belfast.
answer_text: Vagrancy legislation is a matter for the Minister of Justice.The Complex Lives model takes a whole system approach to address chronic homelessness across Belfast. This includes supporting people who are caught in the cycle of rough sleeping.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, AQW 11791/22-27, whether he has updated the aggregated schools budget for 2024-25 to ensure schools are resourced to live within their means.
answer_text: As I advised in AQW 11791/22-27, the Executive agreed departments’ opening 2024-25 budgets at the end of April.  I am still in the process of finalising my Department’s 2024-25 budget strategy.  Schools will be notified of their 2024-25 final opening allocations in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the specific work undertaken to date by each of the seven project workstreams that have been established to take forward the recommendations contained within the Landscape Review of Education Authority.
answer_text: The Landscape Review of the Education Authority (EA) is a key priority for my Department and officials are working with the EA to address the recommendations and deliver the Action Plan. The specific work undertaken within the seven project workstreams is set out below: Clarity on Role and Function of EADE officials undertook a desktop review outlining relevant EA statutory functions and high-level policy requirements. This was completed in September 2023.Service Delivery Model for all EA functions and services (including alternative models)DE and EA are currently considering the current service delivery models for each of EA’s business areas. This will help inform which additional service areas should be prioritised for review (in addition to those already under review within Project 4). EA Performance, Data and GovernanceThe EA has reviewed its Data Strategy, which includes investigating data sharing/integration and departmental/sectoral needs. The EA has also streamlined performance metrics within its Performance Improvement Framework, with a view to improving measurement of outcomes for children and young people. In addition, the EA is progressing the EdIS data platform, which will support further development of the EA Insights Centre of Excellence, as well as a holistic governance approach to data management for schools.End-to-End ReviewsAs a result of two external reviews of EA Human Resources (HR) a People Focused HR Programme Board was established to deliver recommendations made in the two reviews. To date, this has delivered, stabilisation of pressures in order to manage peak recruitment demand for schools returning in the new academic year; a revised service delivery model for Resourcing and a new customer contact model for HR; the latter will launch in October 2024 and will include an ‘HR Helpdesk’. Furthermore, End-to-End Reviews have commenced on School Improvement and Special Educational Needs (SEN). These reviews are running concurrently and working closely with a dual focus of delivering high quality services for schools, pupils and parents, as well as ensuring value for money.  The school improvement policy, Every School a Good School’, was published in 2009. The End-to-End Review will develop a revised policy framework to take account of the changing education landscape and to focus on providing excellent education for every child. A key focus of the Review is to assess the effectiveness of the current school improvement services and develop options for an improved delivery model. To date, phase one (mapping of the delivery model) has been completed, phase two (shaping the future approach by examining school improvement levers) is progressing and phase three (to develop a draft policy and options for implementation) has just commenced.  The revised policy framework will be subject to public consultation, expected to commence in Spring 2025.  The SEN End-to-End Review brings together existing work being taken forward under the EA

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 12368/22-27, to detail the rationale for not fully implementing the action plan to address the Education Training Inspectorate evaluation of education otherwise than at school.
answer_text: Resource pressures within the Education Authority (EA) have meant that full implementation of the action plan has not been possible.Departmental and EA officials are currently considering the actions necessary to resolve outstanding issues identified within the evaluation by the Education and Training Inspectorate (ETI).   This work is informed by an ETI progress report, provided to the Department in September 2023, which identified a number of improvements in the quality of provision for children and young people being educated in EOTAS centres.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 9772/22-27 , for an update on the Public Health Agency's development of a regional protocol in relation to the notification and management of Sudden Unexpected Death in Childhood.
answer_text: Since the update in April 2024, the Public Health Agency (PHA), which leads on the development of the Regional Protocol on Sudden and Unexpected Death in Infancy and Childhood (SUDIC), has continued to work at pace. The robustness of the draft protocol was tested at a multi-agency workshop, organised by the PHA and in response to feedback further consultation is planned with key stakeholders, including Northern Ireland Ambulance Service, the Coroner Service, State Pathology and Adult Emergency Medicine. The PHA continues to optimise collaborative working by attending events such as a PSNI led Child Death and Injury CPD event, which also provided an opportunity to update on progress in Northern Ireland and learn from high profile cases in England. The current protocol has supported the development of a Trust-led SUDIC simulation day; which will form the basis of regional training for paediatricians.Once the protocol is approved in principle by all relevant key stakeholders, input will be sought from families with lived experiences of bereavement. Consideration will also be given on how to address service gaps that have been identified as a result of this work, including the need for bereavement support services for children up to 18 years of age.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 9567/22-27, for an update on the Miss School. Miss Out strategy review.
answer_text: Work is ongoing to review the strategy.  To support this further, my officials plan to engage with children and young people to take their views on priorities to support improved attendance.  This will inform a formal consultation, expected later in 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 8024/22-27, for an update on the review of Schemes of Management.
answer_text: The review of Schemes of Management is progressing. Officials are taking forward a review of the Model Scheme for Voluntary Grammar and Granted Maintained Integrated schools. Officials are also working with the Education Authority which is taking forward a review of the Scheme for Controlled schools.  CCMS is taking forward a review of the Scheme for Catholic Maintained schools. It is expected all elements of the review will progress throughout 2024 and into 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will ensure that schools are provided with the necessary resource to pay teachers based on the new teacher pay deal in the 2024-25 financial year without incurring a deficit.
answer_text: As I advised in AQW 11791/22-27, the Executive agreed departments’ opening 2024-25 budgets at the end of April.  I am still in the process of finalising my Department’s 2024-25 budget strategy.  Schools will be notified of their 2024-25 final opening allocations in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 11514/22-27, when the review of the new funding scheme for youth work will be published.
answer_text: My Department has been considering the findings of the ETI review with a view to publishing a summary report setting out both the findings and next steps to address the findings. Given the wide scope of the ETI policy advice, this process has taken longer than expected. However, the headline findings were verbally shared with the Regional Advisory Group at the most recent meeting on 12 June. My officials are working to complete this work with a view to publishing as soon as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of new Specialist Provision in Mainstream Schools places that (i) have been established for September 2024; and (ii) are still required for September 2024.
answer_text: New specialist provision places established for September 2024:Specialist Provision TypeNo of Classes establishedTotal Capacity createdLearning (SPL) – 10 Primary SPL classes with a capacity of 12; and 11 Post Primary SPL classes with a capacity of 14 21274Social Communication (SPSC) – capacity 854432Learning Special (SPEYF) – capacity 832256TOTAL107962Capacity noted above is an estimate based on all classes operating at full capacity.  In reality, class sizes are based on the specific assessed needs of the cohort of pupils and the size of the accommodation.As of 9 July 2024, 36 children were identified as requiring a place in a specialist provision in a mainstream class who do not yet have a confirmed placement for the new academic year.  The Education Authority (EA) is working to resolve this as soon as possible and is engaging with schools and settings to increase capacity and identify solutions across all areas.  This work will continue over the summer months and for as long as necessary.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 12272/22-27, how his Department is using this data to identify actions needed.
answer_text: The data provided to you in AQW 12272/22-27 is indicative of the number of additional interventions that have been put in place in educational settings where an incident of harmful sexual behaviour has occurred following the introduction of Circular 2022/02.  These figures are demonstrative of the number of children receiving additional supports, as a preventative measure, rather than of the number of incidents of harmful sexual behaviour.  I can confirm that my Department is working closely with the Departments of Health and Justice on the draft Domestic and Sexual Abuse Strategy and has included the number of Risk Assessment Management Plans as a performance indicator.  My officials are also contributing to ongoing work on the implementation of the Gillen Review and the development of the Ending Violence Against Women and Girls Strategy, where this data can be provided as the Departments work together to ensure that issues surrounding the rise of harmful sexual behaviour are addressed as widely as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) how much the proposed £2,500 lump sum payment to school support staff will cost the Executive; (ii) how he plans to fund this payment; and (iii) in which financial year he intends to make this payment.
answer_text: To allow staff to receive a gross non-consolidated payment (NCP) of £2,550 (pro-rata according to actual hours worked during the 2023-24 financial year), it is estimated that the cost will not exceed £80 million. I will be asking the Executive to set aside the necessary funding in the 2025-26 financial year.It is my intention that the NCP will be made to staff in April 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) for his assessment of the 2023 Education and Training Inspectorate Report on the Preventative Curriculum; and (ii) whether he intends to implement protocols to prevent further rises in Problematic Sexual Behaviour or Harmful Sexual Behaviour.
answer_text: The Department commissioned the Education and Training Inspectorate (ETI) to undertake a thematic evaluation of The Preventative Curriculum in Schools and EOTAS Centres. The 2023 ETI report contained a series of recommended next steps, and highlighted that the focus should be on the development of pupils’ knowledge, attitudes and behaviours set within a culture of respect and responsibility, with guidance required for school leaders and governors in relation to how this can be led and managed. Through a Task and Finish Group, my Department is currently considering its response to the ETI thematic evaluation,  including the actions which key delivery partners will be required to take to address the report’s findings. My officials will continue to engage with key stakeholders, schools and young people as part of this process. Guidance for schools will then be considered. It is anticipated that any additional or updated guidance will be published later in the year.From a safeguarding perspective, where there is concern in relation to harmful sexual behaviour in schools, the applicable protocols are outlined in the following  DE Circular: https://www.education-ni.gov.uk/publications/circular-202202-children-who-display-harmful-sexual-behaviour.   My officials continue to work with colleagues in the Department of Health and Department of Justice on relevant strategies, including the Domestic and Sexual Abuse Strategy and the Gillen Review, to take forward any appropriate actions for education settings and to assist in the overall approach to addressing the issue of harmful or problematic sexual behaviour.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of the findings from the Northern Ireland Affairs Committee Integrated Education inquiry.
answer_text: I acknowledge the findings of the Inquiry and recognise the potential of integrated education in promoting mutual respect, cultural diversity and reconciliation. My Department is committed to addressing the issues identified by the Committee and work is ongoing to ensure compliance with the Integrated Education Act (Northern Ireland) 2022. Officials are currently working with sectoral partners to update the current Integrated Education Strategy.My Department is addressing the complex issue of assessing and monitoring demand for integrated education by making use of available data and considering new ways to obtain more detailed information on localised demand.  We will report back once this evidence has been gathered and analysed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the proposed Park and Ride facility at Newtownards.
answer_text: Translink is taking the lead in delivering the Newtownards Park and Ride site that will create 430 parking spaces.  It will improve public transport links and become a central hub for connecting people and encourage modal shift from the private car to public transport that in turn will help reduce transport emissions and support the wider Climate Change Act (NI) 2022 commitments. I am pleased that my Department has already facilitated the land transfer from Ards and North Down Council for the site. Translink is currently taking forward the statutory processes including the planning application, the detailed design and the business case. The timescale for delivery will be dependent on conclusion to these pieces of work and future funding availability. Translink is working closely with the Council and my Department to take cognisance of proposals for active travel linkages.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education when Ballynahinch High School will be provided with the additional classroom space required to accommodate their additional social and communication provision in September 2024.
answer_text: The Education Authority has confirmed that the Interim Assistant Director of Special Educational Need (SEN) Statutory Services has been in contact with The High School Ballynahinch and provided assurance that the accommodation needs for the specialist provision classes at the school, including a review of resourcing requirements, is being prioritised.  They will maintain contact with the principal and the SEN Co-ordinator over the summer months.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, in relation to his Department’s work to address homelessness, for his assessment of the value of repealing current vagrancy laws.
answer_text: Vagrancy legislation is a matter for the Minister of Justice.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether a right of appeal is available to parents when a Board of Governors does not progress with a requested Temporary Variation to accommodate a place for their child.
answer_text: There is no legal requirement on a Board of Governors to seek a Temporary Variation and no right of appeal when one is not progressed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the spend on taxi provision for pupils with a statement of special educational need in the last three academic years; and (ii) his plans to deliver more cost-effective provision which meets the needs of children using the service.
answer_text: (i) My Department is responsible for the home to school transport policy and the Education Authority (EA) for its operation and delivery. The EA has provided the figures below in relation to the spend on taxi provision (including escort costs) for pupils with a statement of special educational needs (SEN) in the last three academic years.Sept 2020-June2021	Sept 2021-June 2022 	Sept 2022-June 2023£20,007,526			£21,889,804			£29,160,067(ii) Transport assistance is provided to over 90k pupils travelling to and from school each day, of which around 10k have an additional transport requirement detailed in their statement of SEN.  The number of children with a statement of SEN has increased significantly in recent years, as has the number of pupils requiring bespoke transport arrangements to enable them to attend school. The EA is currently considering the inclusion of smaller vehicles within its fleet to determine if this may afford some flexibility in the delivery of SEN transport and help to move away from the heavy dependence on taxis. It is also developing proposals to improve its transport management and route planning systems to optimise the school transport network which will deliver efficiencies. My Department is committed to working with the EA to develop a more sustainable model of transport which meets the needs of all pupils, including those with special educational needs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the replacement for the Professional Qualification for Headship.
answer_text: My Department has been working in collaboration with key stakeholders including practitioners and representatives of the various Arm’s-Length Bodies and Higher Educational Institutions to inform the development of a new leadership programme for aspiring Principals, which will replace the former “Professional Qualification for Headship” (PQH) programme. This has been informed by extensive engagement with other jurisdictions and their experience with similar programmes. Subject to securing the necessary resources in 2024-25 and beyond, this programme will be further developed, in collaboration with key stakeholders, in order to ensure aspiring Principals are equipped with the skills and knowledge necessary to handle the diverse and demanding responsibilities of school leadership.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of whether a reduction in industrial action in schools by teachers has influenced Targeting Social Need planner completion rates.
answer_text: There has been a small but encouraging increase in the completion of Targeting Social Need planners since industrial action by teachers ended on 9 April 2024.The agreement reached between the Department and the Northern Ireland Teaching Council was that no retrospective work would be asked of schools. This has had an impact on completion rates this year. A completion rate of 17.5% as at 12 June 2024, means we are able to report on how 100,000 pupils have been supported with more than £15 million of investment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on his Department’s work on a draft vision statement and guiding principles for Sixth Form provision.
answer_text: There are a range of individual policies and strategies impacting upon Sixth Form provision in schools, including the Entitlement Framework, qualifications, funding, transport, admissions and area planning.  An internal review of the 6th Form baseline position has been completed. This has involved a review of the evidence base, carrying out additional analysis and looking at strategic approaches in other jurisdictions.A document that will form the basis of further engagement with stakeholders is currently being drafted. It will articulate the issues that have been raised and propose a draft strategic vision statement and guiding principles for sixth form provision.During the consultation phase the department will ensure it engages fully with all key stakeholders.I recently met with the Minister for the Economy where we discussed our approach to the work and our shared priorities within the 14-19 Framework. We have prioritised careers and have tasked officials from our departments to produce a joint programme of work. On the wider framework and its actions, the Minister for the Economy and I will be considering how best to prioritise the other elements of the framework.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the rationale for his decision on Development Proposal 720, Queen Elizabeth II Primary School, Kilskeery, following the recommendation from his officials to approve closure; and (ii) where the rationale for this decision is available to the public.
answer_text: My rationale for my decision which is now published on the Department’s website is that: - “Having carefully considered all of the evidence and analysis with regard to this proposal against the sustainable schools’ policy and also carefully considered the comments received during the statutory objection period, I have decided not to approve the proposal for closure.  I would of course expect the board of governors to monitor the school closely against SSP criteria and ongoing support to be provided by the EA as the managing authority”.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he intends to progress Key Area 4 of A Fair Start Final Report and Action Plan.
answer_text: “A Fair Start” Key Area 4 action (i) is complete and Key Area 4 actions (iii) and (iv) are being progressed following my announcement of £20 million over the next two years, with potential for further funding to support the delivery of the RAISE programme.Key Area 4 actions (v), (vi) and (vii) are not being progressed in 2024/25 due to budget pressures. Key Area 4 action (ii) suggested that completion of Targeting Social Need Planner should be mandatory. Schools are being encouraged to complete the planner rather than mandating them.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on discussions between his Department, the Greater Shankill Project and Queen's University Belfast in relation to Key Area 5 of A Fair Start Final Report and Action Plan.
answer_text: Since the report “A Fair Start” report was published in June 2021, there has been on-going engagement with the Chief Executive of the Greater Shankill Project (GSP) on a ‘Greater Shankill Children & Young People Zone’ for the area and on "A Fair Start" more generally. My department has also been engaging with the QUB School of Social Sciences, Education and Social Work to support the development of the RAISE programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 10168/22-27, and given his assessment of the contribution Find Help NI makes towards the delivery of the Mental Health Strategy, whether he will consider (i) providing funding to deliver and expand their site; (ii) inviting them to participate in the relevant sub-group of the Mental Health Strategy Steering Group; and (iii) tasking the Public Health Agency to promote their site.
answer_text: Find Help NI received £259,022 of funding through the Department’s Mental Health Support Fund (MHSF). The MHSF was established using unspent Covid-19 monies and was a one-off, non-recurring fund for a period of three years, from April 2021 to March 2024. All monies allocated to the fund have now been exhausted and, unfortunately, due to the financial situation which the Department continues to face, there is no budget to extend such funding. Currently, there are no plans to invite Find Help NI to participate in the Mental Health Strategy Strategic Reform Board. However, in delivering on Action 17 of the Mental Health Strategy (MHS) in 2024/25, the Department intends to engage with many organisations, such as Find Help NI, to assess the capacity and scope within the Community & Voluntary sector in order to inform the required steps for integration of the sector within the delivery of mental health services, including the attendant structures of the Regional Mental Health Service. The Public Health Agency (PHA) has no ability to ensure that the services being offered by Find Help NI meet relevant quality standards, therefore the PHA are unable to promote the site.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the percentage of short notice bookings placed on the Northern Ireland Supply Teacher Register that are successful.
answer_text: The Department of Education is not the employer of school-based staff. The Northern Ireland Supply Teacher Register is administered by the Education Authority (EA).  EA has advised that from the period from August 2023 to 10 June 2024 93% of short notice bookings have been successfully filled through the NISTR Short Notice Supply function: •  Number of short notice supply bookings: 4013 • Number of short notice supply notices that have been a successful     booking: 3724.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the actions taken by his Department to reduce the number of recipients of Carer's Allowance referred to the debt repayment branch due to a breach in weekly earnings.
answer_text: I recognise the important role that unpaid carers play within Northern Ireland for our communities and for the people they care for.My Department continues to use a number of methods to assist with the early identification of recoverable Carers Allowance overpayments, including those caused by excess earnings. These include checks using His Majesty’s Revenue and Customs information on income on new claims and when any change of circumstances is reported to quickly identify a potential overpayment.My officials also conduct a monthly check where earnings regularly fluctuate, and an annual review on all self-employed cases.	In addition, officials are engaging with the Department for Work and Pensions to secure access to the Verification of Earnings and Pensions system which provides an alert when a person’s earnings breach the Carer’s Allowance earnings threshold.		Finally, officials are planning a programme of stakeholder engagement and a refresh of customer messaging to ensure that Carer’s Allowance customers understand the importance of reporting changes promptly, the type of changes that can impact a person’s entitlement to Carer’s Allowance; and are aware of the methods in place for quickly and efficiently reporting changes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 8584/22-27, whether he intends to publish the Education and Training Inspectorate’s commissioned work into Learning through Play.
answer_text: My officials requested internal policy advice from the Education and Training Inspectorate on the current levels of professional learning and support and future levels required for foundation stage teachers and classroom assistants in play-based pedagogy. This report was provided for internal use only.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will enhance the Getting Ready suite of programmes, as recommended in A Fair Start report, as part of his recent announcement on early years and childcare investment.
answer_text: £7.1m has been allocated by the Executive to stabilise and expand existing early years provision as part of the £25m package announced on 23 May 2024.  Work to confirm the components of this and the operational detail is ongoing and will be communicated in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether a targeted developmental programme for all two year olds at risk of poor educational outcomes, as recommended in A Fair Start report, will be included as part of his recent announcement on early years and childcare investment.
answer_text: As I said in my recent statement, limited budget and sector capacity mean that it is not possible to address everything in the first year.  A targeted developmental programme for all two-year-olds at risk of poor educational outcomes remains a priority but is not included within the package I recently announced.  However, the additional funding which will be made available for Sure Start in the current year will enable us to learn from its successful Developmental Programme for 2-3 Year Olds and inform future plans.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail how the RAISE Programme will contribute to improving attendance rates.
answer_text: The RAISE Programme will adopt a place-based approach to supporting children and young people at risk of exclusion, marginalisation and underachievement.  The unique range of needs in each locality and the selection of measures best suited to addressing those needs will be explored in tandem with local stakeholders as the programme is developed in each area.It is likely that this community-informed approach will recognise the value of regular school attendance given its importance in supporting emotional health and wellbeing and educational achievement.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on any work he has undertaken with the Minister of Health on developing a standard template to capture information from parents as children transition between early years services into preschool and primary school in order to ensure that practitioners can support children’s learning and development, as recommended in A Fair Start report.
answer_text: My department works collaboratively with the Department of Health to support the transition of children between Sure Start and pre-school and between pre-school and primary school.  Provision of support for children and their families in transitioning to pre-school, is a key area of focus for the Sure Start Developmental Programme.  Sure Start shares a transition report with pre-school providers which includes information on the child’s speech and language, physical, social, personal and emotional development.As part of the Getting Ready for Toddler programme, a joint Health/Education review is undertaken when children are aged 3 plus, aimed at ensuring the best possible outcomes as they transition from pre-school to primary school.  The Health Visitor in partnership with the child, parent/carer and pre-school practitioner completes the 3+ review in the pre-school setting. The 3+ review is based on the completion of the Ages and Stages, Social-Emotional Questionnaire (ASQ:SE) and a follow-up discussion between the Health Visitor and parent/carer.  The data collected is uploaded to the NI Child Health System. The review aligns with the pre-school curricular guidance and provides parents and practitioners with valuable information that can be used to support a child’s development, enable early identification of needs, and where necessary, appropriate referral to support / specialist services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether education otherwise than at school settings will be able to benefit from RAISE funding.
answer_text: There is scope for education otherwise than at school settings to be included in conversations in specific RAISE localities.  These settings are important stakeholders in the programme. Conversations with all stakeholders in each locality will take place in the coming months.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether RAISE funding will be used to contribute to reducing the negative impact of paramilitarism.
answer_text: A significant element of the RAISE Programme is the delivery of community informed interventions to raise aspirations and reduce educational disadvantage.  As part of this, there is scope for a wide range of issues to be considered in the identified localities. Reducing the impact of paramilitarism and organised crime could be amongst those issues.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) how much funding each RAISE Programme area will receive; and (ii) how funding will be distributed in the selected RAISE Programme areas.
answer_text: Budget details are being finalised.  The RAISE Programme will include both community informed interventions and the delivery of cross-cutting themes.  Funding for localities will be based on the number of eligible pupils in the RAISE area. Officials will be working with stakeholders on the details of the Programme and funding allocations over the coming months.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how he will ensure that the RAISE Programme will work in partnership with other funders to maximise outcomes.
answer_text: During the design process for RAISE, DE officials worked with a range of stakeholders to consider the potential for the programme to complement and support, where appropriate, other programmes to maximise impact and improve outcomes for children and young people. Those considerations have included opportunities for joint working across Departments on locality-based interventions. These opportunities will be further explored in coming months.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the criteria that has been used to select the areas the RAISE Programme will operate in.
answer_text: A wide range of data was considered from which seven child-centered indicators were chosen. These were:GCSEs – The percentage of leavers who did not achieve 5+ GCSEs (A*-C) including equivalents including English & maths.Pupil Absence from Years 1 to 12.Special Educational Needs - SEN Stages 2-3.Free School Meals Entitlement (FSME).Income Deprivation Affecting Children – a Northern Ireland Multiple Deprivation Measure.Health Deprivation & Disability – a Northern Ireland Multiple Deprivation Measure.Crime/Anti-Social Behaviour Incidents.The indicators were then used for two separate purposes:Shortlisting – the seven listed indicators were used to identify and shortlist Super Output Areas (SOAs); andRanking – GCSE achievement was used as a ranking methodology to prioritise areas of investment, i.e. lowest achievement ranks first.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance (i) for an update on her engagement with HM Treasury on the Non-Teaching Pay and Grading Review; and (ii) what impact the pre-election period of sensitivity will have on this engagement.
answer_text: I wrote to the Chief Secretary of the Treasury on 17 May 2024, seeking her agreement to re-profile £180m of future years’ funding into 2024-25 to enable implementation of the Non-Teaching Pay and Grading Review.
