I have not yet received a response.Since the Westminster General Election has now been called for 04 July 2024, it is now less likely that I will receive a response to this request before the Executive’s consideration of June Monitoring.Given the importance of resolving this issue, I wrote to the Education Minister on 30 May 2024 to advise that he may wish to consider submitting a bid to June Monitoring to enable the Executive to consider it in the round and in the context of the competing pressures across departments.I understand that the Education Minister is engaging with unions to ensure that the pay and grading review is implemented from 1 April this year, and I will continue to work with him to reach an early resolution on this matter, given the importance of these staff in delivering vital services to our children and young people.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 12455/22-27, to detail all inescapable pressure bids he made to the Minister of Finance ahead of the Budget 2024-25.
answer_text: My Department included the following inescapable pressures in its bids to the Minister of Finance ahead of Budget 2024-25 considerations.Inescapable Bids £mBlock Grant – SEN132.02ASB324.52Block Grant - Non SEN110.98EA Pay and Grading review171.00Maintenance (including Special Schools, RaAC Surveys & Wall Ties issues)31.13EA Youth2.54Departmental3.62Pre-School Education Programme – Non-statutory providers1.03Shared Education – Mainstreaming Shared Education Pupil Engagement0.40The Minister of Finance was subsequently advised that the estimate for the EA Pay and Grading had increased to c£180m.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) for his assessment of the current vagrancy laws; and (ii) whether he will repeal of any of these laws to deliver a holistic approach to addressing homelessness.
answer_text: My intention is to repeal the provisions of the Vagrancy Act 1824 and the Vagrancy (Ireland) Act 1847 as they apply to Northern Ireland in the current mandate. Homelessness, which is often linked to underlying issues such as drug addiction, alcohol addiction and poor mental health, should never be criminalised. It is a  wider societal, and cross cutting, issue which ultimately requires an Executive response.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any engagement he has had with the Minister of Justice in relation to the repealing of vagrancy laws.
answer_text: There has been no engagement with the Minister of Justice in relation to the repealing of vagrancy laws.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education why no legislative plans for his Department were included in the First Minister and Deputy First Minister statement on the Executive Legislation Programme 2024.
answer_text: I have a number of bills under development which are not at an advanced enough stage to introduce to the Assembly in the calendar year 2024.My officials are currently developing three bills planned for introduction in 2024/25, on School Uniforms, Nutritional Standards in Schools, and Clothing Allowance Awards, and further bills for introduction in 2025/26. I have instructed officials to progress the School Uniforms bill as a priority and if this or any other bill is ready for introduction sooner than anticipated I will endeavour to bring it to the Assembly as soon as I can.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) to detail the current average caseload for a Special Educational Needs Link Officer; and (ii) for his assessment of whether this represents a sustainable workload to support effective service delivery.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has confirmed that the current average caseload of a Special Educational Needs (SEN) Link Officer is 899.While all children with a statement will have a SEN link officer, the level of direct support provided will vary widely depending on the child’s needs.In recognition of the significant caseloads, the EA has recently approved the recruitment of additional SEN Link Officers to support effective delivery of this service.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of the Think 1265 Charter as an approach to addressing workload pressures facing teachers.
answer_text: Teachers’ terms and conditions of service are negotiated through the forum known as the Teachers’ Negotiating Committee (TNC) which comprises representatives of teachers’ employers, sectoral bodies, Department of Education and the five recognised teachers’ unions. The National Association of Schoolmasters Union of Women Teachers (NASUWT) is a member of TNC.Think 1265 is a website supported by the NASUWT but is not an NASUWT official website. I am aware that Think 1265 is recommending that teachers request their school to recognise the charter it is promoting.Through TNC an agreed workload agreement (TNC 2011/8) was produced which explains the requirements of the 1265 directed hours of a full-time teacher. To support principals in the design and implementation of directed time budgets explanatory documentation including frequently asked questions and training has been jointly agreed with trade unions. The workload agreement has recently been reviewed through a joint working group which included a senior representative from NASUWT and a revised workload agreement was agreed and issued TNC 2024/2.The workload pressures on teachers is being considered through the recognised and accepted negotiating machinery of TNC.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he included the Non-Teaching Pay and Grading Review as an inescapable pressure in his bids to the Minister of Finance ahead of the Budget 2024-25.
answer_text: I can confirm that my Department included the estimated cost of the Education Authority’s Non-teaching Pay and Grading Review as an inescapable pressure in my Department’s bids to the Minister of Finance ahead of the Budget 2024-25.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, further to the framework Developing a More Strategic Approach to 14-19 Education and Training, when will the agreed measures of success for the 14-19 landscape be published.
To ask the Minister of Education (i) for an update on the review of suspensions and expulsions; and (ii) whether he will introduce a single scheme for suspensions and expulsions across all sectors.
answer_text: I will be considering the draft report on the review of suspension and expulsion arrangements which includes recommendations for change in the coming weeks. A number of proposals for change have been identified in the report. Once I have considered the draft report, I will determine the next steps to be taken thereafter.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the action plan to address the Education Training Inspectorate evaluation of education other than at school settings.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has established a cross-directorate EOTAS (education otherwise than at school) Governance Group which has oversight of the implementation plan to address the recommendations of the Education and Training Inspectorate report on its review of EOTAS provision. Departmental officials attend this Governance Group as observers.The EA has taken action to address four of the five recommendations, alongside business-as-usual service developments and improvements.  Given that the review commenced five years ago, and in light of the work ongoing within the End-to-End Review of SEN and the End-to-End Review of School Improvement, Departmental and EA officials have agreed to review the action taken to date, its impact, and identify what work remains to be done in order to ensure that the service is able to best meet the needs of the children and young people in our EOTAS settings.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education when the provisions in the Special Educational Needs and Disability Act (Northern Ireland) 2016 and the supporting regulations and code of practice will be brought forward.
answer_text: I would refer the Member to the answer to questions AQW 8157/22-27, AQW 8698/22-27, AQW 8701/22-27 and AQW 8699/22-27.The answer for AQW 8157/22-27 is contained in the Written Answers Booklet for week ending 22 March 2024 and the others can be found in Written Answers Booklet week ending 29 March 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) for an update on the progress of the last tranche of nurture units; and (ii) when he will roll out a comprehensive whole school nurture approach, including for post-primary schools.
answer_text: All 16 of the Nurture Groups established in 2022 under the Fair Start Action Plan are delivering Nurture with the support of funding from the Department of Education. The building works have been completed for the Nurture rooms in ten of these schools.  In terms of the six remaining Nurture rooms: one has a number of minor building works to be resolved; one has planning permission granted with tender documents being finalised by the Education Authority (EA); and four are awaiting planning permission. The Department also funds the Nurture Advisory and Support Service (NASS) within the EA.  All 16 of the schools concerned have received advice, support and training from NASS.  As well as providing direct support to Nurture Groups, NASS also and delivers the Nurture in Education Programme (NEP). The NEP is an early intervention and preventative framework that embeds a whole school nurturing approach and ethos within schools.  Since 2018, 578 schools and EOTAS provisions engaged in the NEP, including 62 post primary schools.The Department is currently taking forward work to inform the development of options for its future long term approach to supporting Nurture provision. This work will include consideration of Nurture provision in post primary.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the systems and protocols in place to record incidences of harmful sexual behaviour, including problematic sexual behaviour, in schools and education otherwise than at school settings.
answer_text: Harmful sexual behaviour sits within the overall context of safeguarding and child protection.  Procedures for recording all safeguarding and child protection concerns are outlined in Circular 2020/07 - Child Protection: Record Keeping in Schools.  This Circular provides a framework for the management of the recording, storage, retention and destruction of both manual and electronic records where they relate to child welfare concerns. Safeguarding and Child Protection in Schools - A Guide for Schools | Department of Education (education-ni.gov.uk) also provides statutory guidance to educational settings on all aspects of child protection and safeguarding, including sexual abuse, harmful sexual behaviour and record keeping in relation to child protection concerns.The systems and protocols in place for educational settings impacted by an incident of harmful sexual abuse or an incident of problematic sexual behaviour are outlined in Circular 2022/02 - Children Who Display Harmful Sexual Behaviour.  The guidance relates to children alleged to display harmful sexual behaviour and any children it is directed towards. Where the guidance above has identified a need to develop a Risk Assessment Management Plan (RAMP) in relation to harmful sexual behaviour, the Education Authority have confirmed the following number of RAMPs have been developed in conjunction with EA’s Child Protection Support Service and other third parties as appropriate: Year2021/222022/232023/24*No. of RAMPS developed across Schools/EOTAS and ETA122156148*2023/24 academic year is incomplete

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of incidents of harmful sexual behaviour, including problematic sexual behaviour, recorded by schools and education otherwise than at school settings in (i) 2021-22; (ii) 2022-23; and (iii) 2023-24.
answer_text: Harmful sexual behaviour sits within the overall context of safeguarding and child protection.  Procedures for recording all safeguarding and child protection concerns are outlined in Circular 2020/07 - Child Protection: Record Keeping in Schools.  This Circular provides a framework for the management of the recording, storage, retention and destruction of both manual and electronic records where they relate to child welfare concerns. The guidance relates to children alleged to display harmful sexual behaviour and any children it is directed towards. Where the guidance above has identified a need to develop a Risk Assessment Management Plan (RAMP) in relation to harmful sexual behaviour, the Education Authority have confirmed the following number of RAMPs have been developed in conjunction with EA’s Child Protection Support Service and other third parties as appropriate: Year2021/222022/232023/24*No. of RAMPS developed across Schools/EOTAS and ETA122156148*2023/24 academic year is incomplete

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the systems and protocols in place for schools and education otherwise than at school settings where a child may have been impacted by an incident of harmful sexual abuse or an incident of problematic sexual behaviour, with reference to the (i) children alleged to display the behaviour; and (ii) children it is directed towards.
answer_text: Harmful sexual behaviour sits within the overall context of safeguarding and child protection.  Procedures for recording all safeguarding and child protection concerns are outlined in Circular 2020/07 - Child Protection: Record Keeping in Schools.  This Circular provides a framework for the management of the recording, storage, retention and destruction of both manual and electronic records where they relate to child welfare concerns. Safeguarding and Child Protection in Schools - A Guide for Schools | Department of Education (education-ni.gov.uk) also provides statutory guidance to educational settings on all aspects of child protection and safeguarding, including sexual abuse, harmful sexual behaviour and record keeping in relation to child protection concerns.The systems and protocols in place for educational settings impacted by an incident of harmful sexual abuse or an incident of problematic sexual behaviour are outlined in Circular 2022/02 - Children Who Display Harmful Sexual Behaviour.  The guidance relates to children alleged to display harmful sexual behaviour and any children it is directed towards. Where the guidance above has identified a need to develop a Risk Assessment Management Plan (RAMP) in relation to harmful sexual behaviour, the Education Authority have confirmed the following number of RAMPs have been developed in conjunction with EA’s Child Protection Support Service and other third parties as appropriate: Year2021/222022/232023/24*No. of RAMPS developed across Schools/EOTAS and ETA122156148*2023/24 academic year is incomplete

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the date on which he requested that the Minister of Finance contact HM Treasury in relation to the re-profiling of funds to support the Non-Teaching Staff Pay and Grading Review.
answer_text: I wrote to the Minister of Finance on 10 May 2024 asking that she urgently write to the Chief Secretary to the HM Treasury to ask for a reprofiling of funding to support the Non-Teaching Staff Pay and Grading Review.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the E grade given to the Ulster Hospital by the Sentinel Stroke National Audit Programme between July 2023 and September 2023 in relation to timely access to admission to stroke units.
answer_text: The Stroke Sentinel National Audit Programme (SSNAP) data provides the basis for ongoing performance management of stroke services regionally and within HSC Trusts. 1) SSNAP provides 10 acute key performance indicators to benchmark, monitor and manage performance all of which are evidence-based interventions.  Departmental Officials have reviewed the Ulster Hospital’s SSNAP team-centred data for October to December ’23 which have informed the overall C rating. A number of issues have been identified which impacted on performance during this period. These include the opening of the new Emergency Department (ED) which impacted time to initial scanning, wider system bed pressures delaying access to the stroke unit and teething difficulties importing performance data from Encompass to SSNAP.  Work is ongoing in the Ulster Hospital to improve patient flow through ED to the stroke unit and resolve importing issues to SSNAP.2) The thrombolysis score on SSNAP is based on four metrics. The Ulster hospital performed above the national median rate in three of the four in the period July – September ‘23. However, ongoing system wide bed pressures impacted on the fourth metric regarding direct admission to the stroke unit with 4 hours which affected the overall thrombolysis grade. Whilst the SSNAP score was a C, the South Eastern Trust has provided assurance that all patients who were eligible for thrombolysis did receive it. 3) System-wide bed pressures in the period July – September 2023 impacted on the number of patients admitted to a stroke unit within 4 hours.   However, the South Eastern Trust has provided reassurance that access to specialist stroke treatment was not impacted by time of admission to the stroke unit during this period.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the C grade given to the Ulster Hospital by the Sentinel Stroke National Audit Programme between July2023 and September 2023 in relation to thrombolysis.
answer_text: The Stroke Sentinel National Audit Programme (SSNAP) data provides the basis for ongoing performance management of stroke services regionally and within HSC Trusts. 1) SSNAP provides 10 acute key performance indicators to benchmark, monitor and manage performance all of which are evidence-based interventions.  Departmental Officials have reviewed the Ulster Hospital’s SSNAP team-centred data for October to December ’23 which have informed the overall C rating. A number of issues have been identified which impacted on performance during this period. These include the opening of the new Emergency Department (ED) which impacted time to initial scanning, wider system bed pressures delaying access to the stroke unit and teething difficulties importing performance data from Encompass to SSNAP.  Work is ongoing in the Ulster Hospital to improve patient flow through ED to the stroke unit and resolve importing issues to SSNAP.2) The thrombolysis score on SSNAP is based on four metrics. The Ulster hospital performed above the national median rate in three of the four in the period July – September ‘23. However, ongoing system wide bed pressures impacted on the fourth metric regarding direct admission to the stroke unit with 4 hours which affected the overall thrombolysis grade. Whilst the SSNAP score was a C, the South Eastern Trust has provided assurance that all patients who were eligible for thrombolysis did receive it. 3) System-wide bed pressures in the period July – September 2023 impacted on the number of patients admitted to a stroke unit within 4 hours.   However, the South Eastern Trust has provided reassurance that access to specialist stroke treatment was not impacted by time of admission to the stroke unit during this period.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, pursuant to AQW 6546/22-27, how they will ensure Child Rights Impact Assessments are carried out when developing any new policy likely to impact children and young people.
answer_text: The Department is committed to effective consultation and engagement when developing and implementing policies.  Policy makers are supported in a number of ways to best consider the equality implications of their policies.  There are a number of support teams that provide advice to policy makers in respect of equality assessments, including the appropriate statistical information to guide and inform policy making.  There is guidance from the Equality Commission and training for all staff in respect of equality assessments, including Child Rights Impact Assessments (CRIAs).  In addition, TEO chairs an interdepartmental group of Equality Officers for the purposes of sharing best practice in respect of screening and assessing equality impacts.  In April 2023, the Chief Executive of the Northern Ireland Commissioner for Children and Young people (NICCY) attended a meeting of this group to promote and further embed the importance of CRIAs in policy making across departments. NICCY has worked with the Department of Finance to develop two online training modules on the relevance and completion of CRIAs. In 2022, the Head of the Civil Service wrote to all Permanent Secretaries to make them aware of the new training resources and to invite them to help promote and embed the packages within departments. The Department will continue to consider the use of CRIAs where policies are likely to have a particular impact on children and young people, alongside our commitment to deliver and embed our section 75 duties.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of staffing levels of Special Educational Needs Link Officers in the Education Authority.
answer_text: The Education Authority has increased the number of Special Educational Needs Link Officers from 21 to 36 since April 2021.  In addition, they are currently in the process of recruiting an additional 14 Link Officers, bringing the total to 50 across the service. It is anticipated that this additional recruitment will be completed in the autumn term.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the date she contacted HM Treasury in relation to the re-profiling of funds to support the Non-Teaching Staff Pay and Grading Review.
answer_text: Following the Education Minister’s confirmation of the amount to be re-profiled, I and my officials have been engaging with Treasury over the past week, and I have written to the Chief Secretary on 17 May 2024 to formally request this.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will provide additional funding to the Education Authority to increase staffing levels in the Special Educational Needs Link Officer role.
answer_text: As stated in the response to AQW 11957/22-27, the Education Authority have already increased the number of Special Educational Needs Link Officers since 2021 and are currently in the process of recruiting additional Link Officers.The Executive agreed departments’ opening 2024-25 budgets at the end of April and I am currently finalising my Department’s 2024-25 budget strategy.  The Department’s Arms’ Length Bodies, including the EA, will be notified of their 2024-25 final opening allocations in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 10559/22-27, whether the 89 children receiving contingency educational support in September 2023 were recorded by the Department as having been allocated a school place.
answer_text: The department does not hold information on the allocation of school places as this is an operational function for the Education Authority. The department undertakes the annual school census which records pupils who are on the school register and attending each school on the Friday of the first full week in October.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the grades provided to the Ulster Hospital by the Sentinel Stroke National Audit Programme between October 2023 and December 2023.
answer_text: The Southern Health and Social Care Trust has advised it does not comment on individuals or their specific circumstances. However, the Trust has confirmed that none of the screeners involved are currently working within the Northern Ireland Cervical Screening Programme in any Trust.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 7703/22-27, to detail the assistance provided to (i) schools; and (ii) parents to support children not attending school due to emotionally-based school avoidance.
answer_text: My Department, the Education Authority and schools have a range of measures in place to support pupils. These include DE’s “Miss School. Miss Out Strategy”, “The Challenge of Improving Pupil Attendance at School - Case Studies paper” and the Absence Tiers Guidance. The Education and Training Inspectorate also published a series of case studies in 2016 which schools can continue to draw upon.I am conscious of the increasing challenges that are adversely affecting pupil attendance and my officials are currently reviewing and refreshing the existing attendance strategy in order to take account of these circumstances.My officials are also working with the Education Authority to ensure that the new school administration system, “Education Information Solutions” (EdiS), is able to facilitate the recording of absences at a more granular level. That work is continuing.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he intends to allocate capital funding to the Education Authority Library Service.
answer_text: No capital funding has been requested by the Education Authority Library Service as they have no planned capital expenditure in 2024/25.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 7475/22-27, to detail (i) the impact industrial action is having on the education welfare officer service; and (ii) what action he is taking to resolve industrial action.
answer_text: NIPSA members employed as Education Welfare Officers have been engaged in action short of strike measures since May 2021 and this action has escalated over time.  The Education Authority (EA) has advised that the major impact of this industrial action has been in regard to the waiting list for education welfare services, which has shown an incremental increase when combined with additional needs being observed in the period following the COVID pandemic.Resolution of the industrial action will require approval and funding to enable the implementation of the Education Welfare Service Transformation business case, as well as implementation of the Pay & Grading review for EA support staff.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how much of his 2024-25 budget allocation will be required to fulfil teachers’ pay awards.
answer_text: The anticipated recurrent cost in 2024-25 of the recent settlement for the three years (2021-2023) of outstanding non-contractual teaching pay awards uplift is estimated at c£166.2m and the September 2024 teachers’ incremental (i.e. contractual) uplift is estimated at c£10.0m.Any negotiated agreement for a September 2024 teachers’ pay award would also require funding, but the quantum is currently unknown as these negotiations will not commence prior to receipt of a pay claim from the Northern Ireland Teachers’ Council and publication of the School Teachers’ Review Body recommendations for 2024-25.  In addition to this the employers’ contribution rate for teachers’ pensions has increased in 2024-25 from 25.1% to 29.1%, requiring an estimated £42.4m.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 7706/22-27, for his assessment of whether the common funding scheme provides an equitable funding settlement to all schools.
answer_text: Education in Northern Ireland faces huge funding pressures.  The current education budget is not sufficient to provide a reasonable budget settlement for all schools.  In this difficult context, it is my intention to review the Common Funding Scheme, including the Common Funding Formula which calculates individual school budgets, to ensure it distributes the available funding as effectively as possible for the benefit of all pupils.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will provide an uplift in the aggregated schools budget for 2024-25 to ensure schools are resourced to live within their means.
answer_text: The Executive agreed departments’ opening 2024-25 budgets at the end of April.  I am currently finalising my Department’s 2024-25 budget strategy.  Schools will be notified of their 2024-25 final opening allocations in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 7703/22-27, how he will ensure that the numbers of children not attending school due to emotionally-based school avoidance are recorded and monitored.
answer_text: My officials are working with the Education Authority to ensure that the new school administration system,“Education Information Solutions” (EdiS), is able to facilitate the recording of absences at a more granular level. That work is continuing.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 7707/22-27, for his assessment of the statement contained in the final Fair Start Report, in relation to academic selection, that, as a panel, we feel that addressing this systemic inequality should be a priority going forward.
answer_text: As is apparent for the actual demand for places at schools which use academic selection, there are a range of views on academic selection in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 7476/22-27, what action his Department is taking to address school attendance rates.
answer_text: I would refer the member to the answer to question AQW 8908/22-27.This answer will be contained in the Written Answers Booklet for week ending 22 March 2024.Additionally my Department continues to publish A bulletin for information purposes each month, on the DE website highlighting pupil absence data by Free School Meal Bands.The Education Welfare Service (EWS) has been subject to industrial action since May 2021 and action short of strike has had an impact on the service’s waiting list and consequently upon attendance rates.I will be consulting on an "Attendance Matters" strategy in the coming months.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 6270/22-27, for an update on the creation of a roadmap for an inclusive education system.
answer_text: Pursuant to AQW 6270/22-27, officials continue to develop early policy considerations to define inclusive education within the context of the Northern Ireland education system.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 6695/22-27, to detail the workstreams that have been established to take forward the recommendations outlined in the Landscape Review of Education Authority.
answer_text: Seven project workstreams have been established to take forward the recommendations. They are: Clarity on Role and Function of EAService Delivery Model for all EA functions and services (including alternative models)EA Performance, Data and GovernanceEnd-to-End Reviews (including Special Educational Needs, School Improvement Services and Human Resources support services)EA PeopleEA Culture, Communication and EngagementEA Partnership Agreement

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education when he will confirm the Education Authority's budget allocation for 2024-25.
answer_text: The Executive agreed departments’ opening 2024-25 budgets at the end of April.The budget outcome for Education falls far short of what the sector requires.  Given the size of the funding shortfall faced, I am working with my Department’s officials to determine the options available and what difficult decisions are required to offer the education sector the best chance to operate within budget.  Once complete, my Department’s ALBs, including the Education Authority, will be notified of their 2024-25 final opening allocations shortly thereafter.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 6545/22-27, for an update on the review of the Healthy Child, Healthy Future Scheme.
answer_text: Further to AQW 6545/22-27, the review of the Healthy Child, Healthy Future policy remains ongoing, with the intention of completion before the summer recess.  To facilitate this, Writing Groups have been convened and have met to finalise qualitative input to the review. The outcomes of this work, a completed draft document, will be presented to the Programme Board before the summer recess, with the anticipation of a consultation exercise commencing soon thereafter.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on his engagement with the Minister for the Economy on the implementation of the 14-19 Education and Training Framework.
answer_text: I recently met with the Minister for the Economy to discuss the 14-19 Framework and other shared priorities. During this meeting we reaffirmed our commitment to our departments working collaboratively to support the educational opportunities for all our young people.Given the importance of this work, Minister Murphy and I jointly tasked officials with preparing proposals for our consideration to further enhance delivery of the Framework and joint working across the system.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 6946/22-27, (i) for his assessment of the value of Continuing Professional Development; and (ii) how he will ensure that it is resourced.
answer_text: Access to coherent, career-long Teacher Professional Learning (TPL) is essential for maintaining our high standards of teaching and learning in schools in Northern Ireland. My Department’s Strategy for TPL, Learning Leaders, provides the vision for doing so. Teachers are expected to undertake TPL throughout their careers to ensure their pedagogy, knowledge and skills remain up to date and their pupils’ learning outcomes are consistently maximised for the attainment of their full potential.In progressing the Strategy’s implementation, we have developed a draft TPL Framework upon which we plan to shortly consult.To facilitate TPL in schools, five ‘Baker Days’ are designated each academic year for staff professional learning and development and they can also utilise other directed time.  Additionally, in the current academic year, schools can avail of up to an additional five ‘School Development Days’ for this purpose.The Education Authority (EA) has a statutory duty to ensure the provision of professional learning opportunities for all teachers. In delivering this duty, TPL is made available to all schools through the EA’s School Development Service (SDS) and its Supporting Learning website. TPL is one of my Department’s key priorities and, I am seeking the expansion of TPL provision for all schools. However, this will be dependent on securing increased funding specifically for TPL, and without significant additional funding in-year, my Department will not even be able to deliver its basic statutory services without overspending.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on engagement she has had with HM Treasury in relation to the re-profiling of funds to support the Non-Teaching Staff Pay and Grading Review.
answer_text: My department has been awaiting confirmation of costs from the Department of Education to inform my engagement with the Treasury.  The Education Minister has now written to me on 10 May 2024, to confirm the funding requirement in 2024-25 to implement the pay and grading review.  Following the Education Minister’s request and confirmation of the amount to be re-profiled, I and my officials have engaged with Treasury including with the Chief Secretary on a call and formally in writing.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an updated assessment of the budgetary situation facing her Department in 2024-25.
To ask the Minister of Education whether he has been engaging with children and young people in relation to changes to school uniform grants.
answer_text: I plan to launch a public consultation exercise on the review of school uniform policy in June and this will include aspects of the uniform grant policy. This consultation will involve targeted engagement with children and young people and their parents in order to seek their input on this important area of policy work.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he intends to increase the School Uniform Grant for 2024-25.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) was able to uplift its school uniform grant rates by 20% in 2022/23 following an additional allocation of funding by the previous Minister of Education. Severe budgetary pressures mean that it has not been possible to allocate any additional funding to the EA to implement a further rise in the rates of uniform grant for the 2024/25 academic year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education when the review of the new funding scheme for youth work will be published.
answer_text: The Education and Training Inspectorate (ETI) were commissioned to provide advice to the Department on the impact of the Education Authority’s (EA) funding scheme on addressing children and young people’s assessed needs. As this advice was commissioned to inform policy development it was not designed to be released as a full report. That said it has been agreed that a summary of the findings and key messages will be provided to the youth sector. The final draft report has now been presented to the department and it is anticipated that the summary will issue in early June. My officials will work with the EA and voluntary youth sector to address the findings and recommendations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how much funding from the 2024-25 budget will be allocated specifically to youth work.
answer_text: My Department wrote to the Education Authority on 19 March to outline the Opening Interim Resource allocation for 2024/25, subject to the final agreed executive budget.The Basic EA Youth interim budget totalled £35,903k, the final budget will be issued to EA in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the steps he is taking to ensure that the Education Authority provide clarity in the decision-making process for funding arrangements and allocations for the community and voluntary youth work sector.
answer_text: The department’s youth work policy (Priorities for Youth) establishes the framework within which the EA delivers its operational remit, including funding decisions. Funding opportunities, open to voluntary youth centres, have been developed based on the Local and Regional Assessment of Need process and are advertised on a dedicated EA Youth Service (EAYS) Funding Website.  The policy established a Regional Advisory Group to provide advice to EA on the RYDP through stakeholder engagement.   A similar process operates at a local area level where Local Advisory Groups (LAGs) provide advice to support the development and implementation of local youth service provisions via Area Youth Development Plans. These forums provide an opportunity for the EA to provide clarity on their decision-making process.The Director of Qualifications, 14-19 Strategy and Youth Work Policy is a member of the EA Funding Project Board which has confirmed that a model of stakeholder engagement is in place to identify the issues, develop solutions and communicate the outcomes of engagement, which importantly includes an explanation of the rationale for decisions.Officials are working with the EA and the community and voluntary sector to ensure that the structures are working as intended and to enhance communications about funding arrangements and allocations to ensure transparency.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the scoping exercise which has commenced on issues for the controlled sector.
answer_text: The Department is currently considering the report produced by the Independent Review of Education Panel and carrying out internal consultation to inform a way forward. This process includes discussion with key education stakeholders including other relevant Departments and Arm’s-Length Bodies.    Following this consultation, officials are now producing a paper for my consideration setting out advice on the various recommendations in the report. I have also requested advice specifically on support for the controlled sector.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on his plans to create a specific support function for the controlled sector.
answer_text: The Department is currently considering the report produced by the Independent Review of Education Panel and carrying out internal consultation to inform a way forward. This process includes discussion with key education stakeholders including other relevant Departments and Arm’s-Length Bodies.    Following this consultation, officials are now producing a paper for my consideration setting out advice on the various recommendations in the report. I have also requested advice specifically on support for the controlled sector.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the amount of capital funding allocated to the nine capital bids his Department made as part of the Department of Finance information gathering exercise to inform the Executive’s consideration of Budget 2024-25.
answer_text: The recently agreed Executive Budget provides my Department with a Capital Budget of £254m in 2024-25. Whilst I very much welcome this opening allocation, which reflects an increase of over ££80m on the 2023/24 opening position, my Department faces significant capital pressures particularly in regard to SEN placements. My officials are currently finalising the capital budget strategy for 2024-25 and I hope to be in a position to make a detailed announcement in coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what consideration he has given to undertaking work to better understand the value of youth work, similar to the Economic Value of Youth Work report by UK Youth and Frontier Economics.
answer_text: The Department is aware of the UK Youth and Frontier Economics, Untapped report about the economic value of youth work in England. My officials attended an event run by the Ulster University, where UK Youth staff presented the report, and subsequently engaged with UK Youth staff to establish an estimate of the cost for a Northern Ireland specific version of the economic model. Given budget pressures and current youth work priorities the Department has been unable to secure the funding to procure a local assessment of the economic value of youth work. While a local assessment would undoubtably be of value, given the similarities of local youth work to that in England, there is nothing to suggest that a similar scale of return on investment would not be achieved.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the internal consultation process which was taking place in his Department on the findings of the Independent Review of Education.
answer_text: Departmental officials have now completed an internal consultation with DE officials, officials in other departments potentially impacted by the recommendations and with NDPB’s and other stakeholders.  A paper is now being produced for my consideration outlining the findings of the internal consultation process. I hope to receive and consider this paper shortly.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 8804/22-27, whether he intends to recommend that the Executive publishes a response to the Concluding Observations, as done by the Scottish government.
answer_text: My officials will be commissioning an assessment of the Concluding Observations in the coming weeks and aim to complete it over the summer.  The initial stage will involve inputs from departments.  Decisions regarding publication will be for the Executive to consider in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 8804/22-27, (i) when he intends to commission this cross-departmental assessment; and (ii) how long it will take to complete.
answer_text: My officials will be commissioning an assessment of the Concluding Observations in the coming weeks and aim to complete it over the summer.  The initial stage will involve inputs from departments.  Decisions regarding publication will be for the Executive to consider in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail how much resource funding was allocated to the nine resource bids his Department made as part of the Department of Finance information gathering exercise to inform the Executive’s consideration of Budget 2024-25.
answer_text: The recently agreed Executive Budget provides the Department with an additional resource allocation of £296.3m, over and above the anticipated, flat-cash budget.  This general resource allocation is not specifically linked to any of the nine resource bids, which my Department had previously submitted to the Department of Finance.  In the Minister of Finance’s Written Ministerial Statement of 25 April 2024, the EA Pay and Grading Review is specifically mentioned.  Specifically, the Minister of Finance advises that while no funding has been allocated for this pressure, the Executive has agreed to seek approval from HM Treasury to reprofile some of the repurposed funds provided in the Executive restoration financial package for this purpose.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 8804/22-27, (i) whether he intends to publish the completed cross-departmental assessment; and (ii) whether any other relevant stakeholders will inform this piece of work.
answer_text: My officials will be commissioning an assessment of the Concluding Observations in the coming weeks and aim to complete it over the summer.  The initial stage will involve inputs from departments.  Decisions regarding publication will be for the Executive to consider in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of the level of funding received by his Department in the Budget 2024-25.
answer_text: The recently agreed Executive Budget provides the Department with an additional resource allocation of £296.3m, over and above the anticipated, flat-cash budget. This is significantly less than what is required and leaves a considerable shortfall across the Education sector.  I therefore have serious concerns that without significant additional funding in-year, the Department will not be able to deliver basic statutory services without overspending.  Given that my Department is leading on the Childcare Strategy, I welcome the £25m that the Minister of Finance has set aside for this key area of work.  Whilst I very much welcome the Capital Budget of £254m, which reflects an increase of over £80m on the 2023-24 opening position, my Department faces significant capital pressures particularly in regard to placements for children with statements of special educational need. I will very shortly make final budget decisions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to provide an update on the actions he has taken since the Adjournment Debate on 12 March 2024 on the A20 Portaferry Road, Newtownards.
To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on their Department’s engagement with the THRiVE project in Newtownabbey.

To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of staff in his Department that have completed the two child rights based training packages which were made available via the NICS training portal in December 2022.
answer_text: The Department of Finance, Digital Learning Unit has advised that at 10th April 2024, the number of DE staff who had completed these training modules was as follows:Introduction to Children’s Rights (e-Learning) - 69Conducting Child Rights Impact Assessment (e-Learning) – 54

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 9871/22-27, how many of the 203 schools that have expressed an interest in facilitating specialist provision classes are (i) primary schools; and (ii) post-primary schools.
answer_text: Pursuant to AQW 9871/22-27, of the 203 schools that had expressed an interest in facilitating specialist provision classes, 15 were nursery, 140 primary and 48 post-primary schools.  Please note that this response refers to the data at that time.  The survey issued to schools on 23 April 2024 is not due to close until noon on Friday 3 May 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 9871/22-27, how many Specialist Provision in a Mainstream School are needed to deliver the required special educational needs places for September 2024 in (i) primary schools; and (ii) post-primary schools.
answer_text: As of 15 May 2024, the Education Authority (EA) has identified the need for 104 specialist provision in mainstream schools (SPiMS) classes, 66 of which are required for Primary and 38 for Post-Primary.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) for his assessment of the value of the work of Young Enterprise NI; and (ii) whether he will reinstate funding to this organisation.
answer_text: Young Enterprise Northern Ireland (YENI) provided a valuable contribution to schools in Northern Ireland, through the delivery of funded curriculum support programmes covering enterprise and entrepreneurial education. Due to significant budgetary pressures, my Department took the difficult decision to cease funding in May 2023.With regards to future funding, all Department’s opening budgets for 2024-25 were agreed last week by the Northern Ireland Executive.  The funding envelope that the Department has been allocated faces significant, unavoidable cost pressures and rising service demands associated with delivering statutory and policy obligations.  To address these, my Department will again need to take difficult decisions to enable it to operate to a balanced budget in 2024-25.  For this reason, I am unable to give you a response regarding future funding to YENI at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education when the Careers Advisory Forum will be reinstated.
answer_text: The Economy Minister and I met recently to discuss careers provision, including how we can enhance our strategic and operational partnerships. In taking forward this work, officials will maintain a focus on identifying and meeting the needs of a wide range of stakeholders and they are currently considering the potential role of the Careers Advisory Forum to enable system level engagement.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) for his assessment of the value of the work of Young Enterprise NI; and (ii) whether his Department will consider providing funding to this organisation.
answer_text: Developing and encouraging our future entrepreneurs is a vital aspect of a vibrant and modern economy. Organisations such as Young Enterprise NI (YENI), Further Education Colleges, Higher Education Institutions, Catalyst and others play an important role in equipping our young people with the skills, knowledge, and ambition to start their own businesses.    My Department, through Invest NI, has recently allocated £12,000 to YENI under an Environmental, Social, and Governance (ESG) Business Backer partnership to support the next generation of entrepreneurial talent in the North of Ireland. This will support 1,200 future entrepreneurs annually.I am supportive of the work that Young Enterprise NI (YENI) does with our young people, and I have instructed my officials to continue to engage with the YENI team to see how we can work together to deliver my policy objectives.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on the progress of the outline business case for redevelopment of Whitespots Country Park, Newtownards.
answer_text: My Department received the outline business case from DAERA on 11 December 2023 and responded with comments and queries to be addressed on 15 January 2024. My Department awaits a response from DAERA.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of the impact of the withdrawal of the Barnardo’s NI Time 4 Me Primary School Counselling service.
answer_text: I understand Barnardo’s decision to end their Time 4 Me primary counselling service was due to financial pressures within their organisation.  While schools have the autonomy to procure counselling or other therapeutic supports from within their existing budgets, there is no central funded service for primary school counselling services.  I am fully aware that the challenging financial context across the education system means that budgets and investments are severely constrained.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what consideration has been given to developing a workforce strategy for the education sector, with an emphasis on attracting specialist STEM teachers into the post-primary sector.
answer_text: The Department recognises the need to develop a sector-wide approach to strategic workforce planning and is working closely with the Education Authority to address this need. A survey for all schools in Northern Ireland is being planned, with the aim of gathering precise information on the challenges that schools are facing in recruiting and retaining teachers in specific subject areas.As part of the workforce planning strategy, consideration will be given to possible strategies to address specific teacher shortages, including STEM subjects. This will include consideration of alternative employment models such as shared posts across schools and the possibility of carefully managed bursary schemes for Initial Teacher Education programmes to encourage more individuals to pursue teaching careers in identified subject areas including STEM. However, such schemes would have to be tightly targeted and would remain subject to available funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail his engagement with the Minister of Education to co-design and co-fund primary school counselling and mental health support.
answer_text: The Department of Health and the Department of Education currently collaborate to provide mental health support in primary schools through engagement and joint funding.In February 2021, the Department of Health and the Department of Education jointly launched the Children and Young People’s Emotional Health and Wellbeing in Education Framework.  The main emphasis of the Framework is to support educational settings to promote emotional health and wellbeing at a universal level, through a holistic, multi-disciplinary approach. This will enable our children and young people to build resilience in coping with challenges they may face in life and providing early and enhanced support for those who may be at risk or showing signs of needing further help. Services and projects that have been established under the Framework are available across primary and post-primary schools. The following initiatives have been piloted or rollout out to primary schools since the publication of the Framework.School Nursing Pilot (commenced 2021/22)Resilience in Education, Assisting Change to Happen (commenced 2021/22)Regional Integrated Support for Education - Expansion of the existing RISE service (from up to primary 4 to up to primary 7) - (commenced 2021/22)	Being Well Doing Well – (recently commenced in the 2023/24 academic year.)  The Attach Project (TAP) (commenced 2021/22)Further information on each of the above projects can be found at: Emotional Health and Wellbeing | Department of Education (education-ni.gov.uk)

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what work has been undertaken alongside the Minister of Education to embed mental health support systems in educational institutions.
answer_text: The Department of Health and the Department of Education continue to collaborate in providing mental health support systems in educational institutions.The main driver for this is the Children and Young People’s Emotional Health and Wellbeing in Education Framework which was jointly launched by both Departments in 2021. A copy of the Framework is available at:  https://www.education-ni.gov.uk/publications/children-young-peoples-emotional-health-and-wellbeing-education-framework-final-version The main emphasis of the Framework is to support educational settings to promote emotional health and wellbeing at a universal level. A number of important projects and pilots have been taken forward through this Framework aimed at various elements of providing help and support to children and young people and their emotional and mental health. These include: the HSCT Text-a-Nurse service, School Nursing Pilot, REACH Regional Integrated Support for Education NI (RISE NI) and Being Well Doing Well (BWDW)One of the key projects from a health perspective is Emotional Wellbeing Teams in Schools (EWTS). The EWTS is a multi-disciplinary health led team which launched in academic year 2023/2024.  The aim is to provide intervention at the earliest opportunity by ensuring schools are aware of appropriate pathways for support in their local area and within CAMHS. This work involves working collaboratively with teaching staff and other providers to coordinate and deliver wellbeing programmes and interventions. These teams are currently in 48 schools across the region with a further roll-out planned this financial year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether figures in relation to Special Educational Needs children who have been allocated a school or nursery placement reflect children who are fully accessing their placement on a full time basis.
answer_text: Information sourced from the Education Authority states that for September 2023 89 pupils across 6 specialised settings in Northern Ireland were provided with contingency educational support in advance of starting in their new school place because of capital works not being completed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will provide support to Foras na Gaeilge as a matter of urgency in order to guarantee the continuation of the Scoil Spreagtha project for the 2024-25 academic year.
answer_text: It is for Foras na Gaeilge, and their Board, to make decisions around programme and funding priorities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) how many schools have confirmed that they will introduce a new Specialist Provision in Mainstream School for September 2024; and (ii) how he will ensure that these are ready by September 2024 in relation to (a) the funding required; and (b) the recruitment of staff.
answer_text: The process of securing additional specialist education provision for September 2024 is ongoing and the position changes on a frequent basis.   As of 28 April 2024, over 200 schools had expressed an interest in facilitating specialist provision classes.  The Education Authority (EA) have secured agreement or are in the process of discussions with schools for 48 additional Specialist Provision in Mainstream Schools (SPiMS) classes for September 2024.  Funding required varies from school to school depending on the level of adaptations required.  Schools are responsible for their own staff recruitment. On 23 April 2024, the Permanent Secretary issued a letter to all schools encouraging them to consider establishing specialist provision or expanding existing provision.  This letter was followed by a short survey to determine existing or new capacity which issued on 24 April 2024 and closes on 3 May 2024.  The results of the survey will be considered and followed up by the department and the EA and will inform planning and decisions about further specialist education provision to be created and funding required to facilitate these.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what steps he is taking to improve the accessibility of mental health services for young people.
answer_text: My Department recognises that the number of children and young people awaiting Child and Adolescent Mental Health Services (CAMHS) has increased since 30th June 2019. Official statistics published by my Department on the 14th February 2024 highlighted that as of 31st December 2023 the total number of young people awaiting assessment across Northern Ireland was 2,095.All Trusts are reporting recruitment and retention issues along with more complex presentations and both of these factors are having an impact on capacity. Action 32 of The Mental Health Strategy (MHS) recognises the staffing challenges across CAMHS and a comprehensive workforce review was completed in March 2022. This review considered; existing workforce, the need to adopt new models of working and the importance of increasing the focus on training, recruitment and retention issues. My Department is committed in creating a workforce that will meet the needs of the population and which is fit for the future.  The Mental Health Strategy acknowledged that CAMHS requires additional investment and Action 10 of MHS gives a commitment to increase funding to 10% of the Adult Mental Health Budget. My Department will continue to work closely and collaboratively with Health and Social Care Trusts and other key stakeholders to develop services that meet not only current but emerging needs of this population and work with executive colleagues to secure the necessary resources required to meet demands.My Department oversees a Service Delivery Plan process which monitors activity and performance across all Health & Social Care Trusts (HSCT). Officials have set targets for new and review contacts within CAMHS and these are reviewed on a monthly basis. If a HSCT is underperforming against its target, my officials require explanations from them on why this is the case. When necessary HSCT’s are required to submit a service, improvement plan that details what steps they will take to improve performance against set targets.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the success of the Scoil Spreagtha project.
answer_text: The Scoil Spreagtha scheme is an operational matter for Foras na Gaeilge.The Chief Executive has provided the following assessment of the success of Scoil Spreagtha:“While the scheme has not yet completed for this funding cycle and therefore no final post-project evaluation is available, we are confident that the scheme is successfully delivering upon its objectives. Research carried out by Ulster University suggests that teachers feel empowered by the various support structures and are confident with the materials provided.Teachers have reported that pupils have embraced the opportunity to learn the language and are engaging fully in the activities.  Parents have also relayed their satisfaction that the opportunity has been provided for their children to learn Irish.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Foras na Gaeilge directly at ceisteanna@forasnagaeilge.ie. Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of the success of the Scoil Spreagtha project in promoting primary level language learning.
answer_text: My Department did not have any involvement in the Scoil Spreagtha project nor did it provide any funding.  Therefore, I cannot offer any assessment on the success of the project.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) when she will recruit for the new role of Occupational Health Technician in the Occupational Health Service; (ii) the functions of this new role; and (iii) how many full time equivalent roles will be put out for recruitment.
answer_text: (i)	It is anticipated that an Occupational Health Technician competition for the Occupational Health Service will be advertised on 6 May 2024. (ii)	The function of the role is to promote and support the activities of the Occupational Health Service. Fulfilling the functions of the role introduces opportunities to utilise the medical and nursing staff resource more efficiently.(iii)	It is anticipated that 2 full-time Occupational Health Technicians roles will be put out for recruitment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) the number of full time equivalent nursing staff vacancies there are in the Occupational Health Service; (ii) when she will launch a recruitment exercise for Occupational Health Service nursing staff; and (iii) the number of full time equivalent roles that will be put out for recruitment.
answer_text: There are currently 2 full-time Senior Occupational Health Nurse vacancies in the Occupational Health Service. A Senior Occupational Health Nurse competition was advertised in May 2024, however, unfortunately the competition failed to appoint a suitable candidate.  The Occupational Health Service is intending to launch a new Senior Occupational Health Nurse recruitment exercise in Autumn 2024. The number of staff aiming to be recruited through this exercise will be dependent on the staff levels of Senior Occupational Health Nurses at that time.  There will be a minimum of 2 full-time vacancies.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of the ending of the Scoil Spreagtha project in June 2024 due to a lack of funding.
answer_text: My Department did not have any involvement in the Scoil Spreagtha project nor did it provide any funding.  Therefore, I cannot offer any assessment on the success of the project.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what steps he is taking to overcome the legislative and technical barriers preventing the delivery of auto-enrollment for Free School Meals.
answer_text: My officials are working with colleagues from the Department for Communities (DfC) and the Education Authority (EA) in taking forward initiatives to streamline and simplify the application process for free school meals (FSM) in order to minimise the application requirements for families. Agreement was reached between the EA and DfC that under a Data Sharing Agreement, FSM applications made on the basis of entitlement to Universal Credit that had been incorrectly resubmitted to EA by claimants could be sent to DfC for verification. This reduced the number of multiple resubmissions from UC claimants and has resulted in improved processing times for such UC claimants during the 2023 year.DE officials are exploring with DfC and the EA the potential for the Digital Economy Act 2017 to enable the necessary data sharing that may further simplify the application process.My officials are aware of a small number of local authorities in England who are currently piloting a wide variety of registration methods that seek to increase FSM uptake including an ‘opt out / right to object’ model. My officials continue to engage with the Department for Education in England, to better understand the barriers that prevent uptake of FSM, whilst also ensuring adherence to legal and data protection constraints, which still remain an important consideration. These include constraints around data-sharing, as well as the need for families to make a claim for free school meals to be eligible under the current process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) the number of full time equivalent medical officer vacancies there are in the Occupational Health Service; (ii) when she will launch a recruitment exercise for Occupational Health Service medical officers; and (iii) the number of full time equivalent roles that will be put out for recruitment.
answer_text: (i)	There are currently 2 full-time equivalent Medical Officer vacancies within the Occupational Health Service. (ii)	It is anticipated that a Medical Officer competition for the Occupational Health Service will be advertised on 6 May 2024.(iii)	It is anticipated that 2 full-time Medical Officer roles will be put out for recruitment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQO 247/22-27, to detail the average waiting time for an Occupational Health Service medical assessment for the last three years.
answer_text: Significant work has been ongoing within my Department’s OHS to reduce the waiting times for assessments. An Occupational Health Consultant has taken up the Lead Medical Officer position within the Occupational Health Service in March 2024 and the launch of a recruitment competition for additional medical officers will be launched at the start of May 2024.  As part of this recruitment competition, we are also seeking to appoint additional nursing staff, and are introducing a new role within the OHS of Occupational Health technicians. When additional medical officers, nursing staff and occupational health technicians are in post, we are confident that this will have a positive impact on waiting times to access occupational health services.The Occupational Health Service (OHS) broad range of activities often requires input from several internal medical staff, and from external sources such as GP’s and others who have provided specialist medical services to OHS service users.  It is often the case that obtaining all the information required from the various sources contributes to delays in the occupational health assessment process.  Given the complexity involved and the current resourcing pressures, this can result in some assessments with a waiting time of up to four months.  Assessment can be arranged more promptly depending on the range of factors involved and the complexity of the assessment.The current Occupational Health Service (OHS) case management system does not provide an average waiting time for medical assessments, or the ability to provide this over a period of time, for example over the last 3 years.  As part of the OHS transformation, a new cloud based IT system is being procured that will enable us to provide more detailed business intelligence and trend data, for analysis, comparison and improvement purposes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of children in 2023-24 that (i) were entitled to Free School Meals; and (ii) applied for Free School Meals.
answer_text: Figures below have been sourced from the Education Authority and relate to the 19 April 2024.Total number of children entitled to Free School Meals  - 97,374Total number of applications for Free School Meals – 109,406Education Authority Free School Meal entitlement and application figures include nursery, primary and post primary aged pupils, and FE students.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 5573/22-27, for an update on whether he intends to repeal Article 4 (1)(c) of the Education (Northern Ireland) Order 1998.
answer_text: I will shortly be considering the outcome of the recent public consultation on the draft statutory guidance on the use of restrictive practices in educational settings. As part of my consideration, officials will provide briefing in relation to Article 4 (1)(c) of the Education (Northern Ireland) Order 1998.  Once I have considered the outcome of the consultation, I will then determine the appropriate next steps in relation to Article 4 (1)(c).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 6547/22-27, how his Department routinely engages with children and young people.
answer_text: The Department uses a range of approaches to access the views of children and young people.   This includes in-house surveys, focus groups, workshops, school visits, Education Authority Youth Service facilitated events and engagement with representative bodies such as the Secondary Schools Student Union of Northern Ireland and the Youth Assembly Education Committee.  There is also regular contact with children and young people’s advocacy organisations including the Northern Ireland Commissioner for Children and Young People, the Childrens Law Centre, and the Northern Ireland Human Rights Commission. The Department will continue to adopt a range of approaches to engage with children and young people.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 9565/22-27, whether he will (i) formally request a report from Education Training Inspectorate detailing its assessment of the educational and reconciliation benefits of the Strule Shared Campus Project; (ii) publish this report; and (iii) use this report to form part of the business case evaluation process.
answer_text: The Education and Training Inspectorate (ETI) provides advice, on an ongoing basis, to officials developing the Strule Programme, including formal advice on the Full Business Case (FBC) which details the educational, reconciliation and societal benefits of the Programme and plans for a longitudinal evaluation of the campus post go-live.  The FBC for Strule and all accompanying Departmental advice are commercially sensitive documentation at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 6544/22-27, whether he plans to publish the 14 to 19 Strategy action plan.
answer_text: As advised in my response to AQW 6544/22-27, the 14-19 Education and Training Framework, including 34 strategic actions, was published in 2022 and the underpinning draft action plan was held as an internal document for officials to aid planning.  My Department has currently no plans to publish this action plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 6547/22-27, how his Department will engage with relevant stakeholders to ensure that the voices of children and young people are being heard in the development of policy.
answer_text: The Department uses a range of approaches to access the views of children and young people.   This includes in-house surveys, focus groups, workshops, school visits, Education Authority Youth Service facilitated events and engagement with representative bodies such as the Secondary Schools Student Union of Northern Ireland and the Youth Assembly Education Committee.  There is also regular contact with children and young people’s advocacy organisations including the Northern Ireland Commissioner for Children and Young People, the Childrens Law Centre, and the Northern Ireland Human Rights Commission. The Department will continue to adopt a range of approaches to engage with children and young people.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail how his Department currently promotes (i) the Find Help NI website; and (ii) Minding Your Head.
answer_text: The Department does not promote the Find Help NI and Minding Your Head websites.The Public Health Agency, however, undertakes a rolling programme of proactive messaging on mental health-related issues which features and signposts to the Minding Your Head website as a key source of information and advice. The most recent media advertising campaign promoting Minding Your Head ran from 2 December 2022 through to 15 January 2023 - News | HSC Public Health Agency (hscni.net) .

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how much funding has been provided to Minding Your Head since its launch.
answer_text: The Minding Your Head website was developed in-house and is promoted as part of the wider PHA programme of work on mental health. The PHA undertakes a rolling programme of proactive messaging on mental health-related issues which features and signposts to the Minding Your Head website as a key source of information and advice. General maintenance costs associated with the Minding your Head website are covered within the wider PHA Communications budget and it is not feasible to disaggregate these specifically for the Minding your Head website. Development work is currently underway to revise and update the format and content of the Minding your Head website in line with the recommendations of the Mental Health Early Intervention and Prevention Action plan. A budget of approximately £10k has been identified for this work.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for a comparative assessment of the services provided by (i) Find Help NI; and (ii) Minding Your Head.
answer_text: The Department has not undertaken a formal comparative assessment of the services provided by Find Help NI and Minding your Head, initiatives which provide a directory of service for mental health support. I understand that Find Help NI has conducted a comparative assessment of both services.Find Help NI is a community resource that places emphasis on personal stories, blogs and videos and provides a directory of support organisations that charge for services and those that provide free or subsidised services. Find Help NI was previously funded through the Mental Health Support Fund. Community Foundation Northern Ireland, as administrators of the Mental Health Support Fund provided the Department with regular updates of the services delivered, ensuring they adhered to the fund terms and conditions. Minding Your Head is supported by the Public Health Agency, through the Protect Life 2 Strategy. The Minding Your Head website provides information only on services that are free of charge. Separate booklets for each Trust area are also provided on Minding Your Head listing local services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the potential role of the Find Help NI website in delivering Action 4 of the Mental Health Strategy 2021-2031.
answer_text: Initiatives such as the Find Help NI organisation play an important role in the delivery of the Mental Health Strategy 2021-31.Find Help NI provides a range of services to the local community which will help meet one of the overarching themes of the Strategy which is to provide the right support, at the right time, to those who need it. It is likely to contribute to the delivery of several actions as outlined within the Strategy, including:Actions 1 & 2: Promotion & PreventionActions 3 & 4: Social Determinants and Mental HealthAction 30: Digital Mental Health

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how much funding his Department provided to establish the Find Help NI website.
answer_text: Find Help NI received a total of £264,962 through the Mental Health Support Fund. This was a one-off, non-recurring fund for a period of three years, from April 2021 to March 2024 that was established using Covid-19 monies. All monies allocated to the fund have now been exhausted.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what steps he is taking to address the 47 funded but unfilled Specialist, Associate Specialist, and Specialty doctor posts, as outlined in the Northern Ireland Emergency Medicine Workforce Census 2024.
answer_text: My Department has engaged with the employing HSC Trusts who are unable to verify the status of the 47 vacancies referred to in the College’s Northern Ireland Emergency Medicine Workforce Census 2024.The issue of ensuring a sufficient supply of medical professionals trained in the emergency medicine will be addressed in a forthcoming strategic workforce review for the Emergency Medicine specialty to be led by the Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, further to the publication of the Healthy Happy Minds evaluation, when he will deliver a new primary school counselling service.
answer_text: I will take the time to assess the findings of the evaluation and the recommendations contained therein.  It will also be important to consider other relevant research and feedback from stakeholders as my department develops a longer-term approach to supporting children and young people’s emotional health and wellbeing in education.  You will also be aware the pilot ceased as the education budget does not have this budget requirement included in its baseline.  Whatever is delivered must be within the budgetary resources available.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on General Dental Service contractual reform.
