answer_text: The need for dental reform has been acknowledged for a number of years, however the pandemic, followed by the absence of an Executive has hampered progress in this area.Most recently, efforts have been focused on addressing the immediate access issues patients are experiencing and establishing options to support the sustainability of dental practices while reform is progressed. This has resulted in the £9.2 million additional investment for 2024/25, which I announced on 27 March 2024.I am conscious of the developments in the rest of the UK in recent months, with Scotland, England and Wales adopting diverging models of remuneration. Officials are liaising with their counterparts in each region to monitor the impact of these models and assess the suitability of adopting a similar approach in Northern Ireland.In addition, officials have held a number of engagement events with practitioners from across NI, the results of which will help inform decision making on the future of General Dental Services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how he will address the levels of burnout and stress prevalent in the emergency medicine workforce.
answer_text: The health and wellbeing of all health and social care staff is recognised as a key theme in the Health and Social Care Workforce Strategy 2026: Delivering for our People.All HSC Trusts currently have in place a variety of measures to support staff health and wellbeing. These include psychological support helplines, reading resources, videos, and mindfulness relaxation exercises. In situations where specific individual help is needed, this is available through the Occupational Health arrangements in each Trust.To further support this provision, my Department recently commissioned work to develop a staff health and wellbeing framework.  This will focus on key elements of health and wellbeing and provide a series of steps to assist HSC employers in their ongoing planning and implementation of effective processes and resources for improving staff health, wellbeing and safety at work.  Development of this framework is due to conclude shortly and will be available for utilisation by HSC employers in the coming months.In addition, a review of HSC Occupational Health Services, commissioned by my Department and HSC Human Resource Directors, was completed in 2023. Work is currently ongoing on the implementation of recommendations arising from this review with the aim of further strengthening the provision of HSC Occupational Health Services.  Regarding the particular pressures faced by Emergency Departments, it is my priority to reduce pressures on these Departments and the staff working in them by ensuring that patients are diverted to the most appropriate care pathway first time.  Through the implementation of the Review of Urgent and Emergency Care, this includes the use of Phone First services, increasing the number of rapid access ambulatory clinics and increasing the focus on community provision, through services such as Hospital at Home.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when an in-reach service from Great Britain will be provided in Northern Ireland for facial palsy patients.
answer_text: The Facial Palsy Planning Group was set up to develop a facial palsy pathway for patients from presentation to treatment which include the ongoing management of the condition life long, if required.The Ulster Hospital, with agreement from the SPPG, arranged for a medical trainee’s fellowship in the Regional Specialist Facial Palsy Service in Newcastle, GB. On completion of this fellowship, the trainee then successfully secured a Plastic Surgery consultant’s post in Northern Ireland with an interest in facial palsy.  However, due to the small numbers of patients (less than 5 per year) requiring surgical intervention it has always been recognised that to ensure the service was safe and sustainable in Northern Ireland and to enable the maintenance and transference of skills a network arrangement would be required for facial palsy services with a GB Centre. This approach is used in a number of tertiary services in Northern Ireland. The Regional Plastics Surgery Service in South Eastern and Belfast Trusts have already established links into specialist services in GB which aids relationships when developing in-reach services and fellowship training. I understand that a draft pathway is currently being developed for local facial palsy services and this pathway is currently with members of the Facial Palsy Regional Planning Group for feedback.  It is the plan that the Facial Palsy Regional Multidisciplinary Team (MDT) will be further developed. However, it is important to note that this will be subject to securing the relevant funding within wider service pressures and priorities.  In the meantime my officials will continue to work with our Trust colleagues in ENT, ophthalmology, neurology and plastic surgery to move the pathway development forward without available funding for the service model.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) how many pupils have had a statement of special educational needs; and (ii) what percentage of the total pupil population this reflects, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The table below provides the number and percentage of pupils who had a statement of special educational needs in each of the last five years. Academic Year 2019/20  2020/212021/222022/232023/24(i) Number of pupils with a statement19,20820,50522,19824,29126,964(ii)  % Pupils with a statement5.5%5.8%6.3%6.8%7.6%Source: Annual School Census

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the prison population and the current prison capacity.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 9237/22-27, of the 69 reports relating to flags attached to street lighting in 2022 and 107 in 2023, how many of these were brought to the attention of the PSNI for an assessment of the impact of removal.
answer_text: I can confirm that PSNI were contacted for an assessment of the impact of the removal of flags  at all of the locations referred to in the complaints received.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, broken down by school type, (i) how many Specialist Provision in a Mainstream School are in place in schools; and (ii) how many schools have expressed an interest in facilitating a Specialist Provision in a Mainstream School from September 2024.
answer_text: There are currently 474 Specialist Provisions in Mainstream Schools (SPiMS): 1 nursery, 309 primary and 164 post-primary classes.As at 22 April 2024, 203 schools had expressed an interest in facilitating specialist provision classes.On 23 April 2024, the Permanent Secretary issued a letter to all schools encouraging them to consider establishing specialist provision or expanding existing provision.  This letter was followed by a short survey to determine existing or new capacity which issued on 24 April 2024 and closes on 3 May 2024.  The results of the survey will be considered and followed up by the department and the Education Authority (EA).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of the level of experience required to apply for a teaching post in a Specialist Provision in a Mainstream School.
answer_text: Specialist Provision in Mainstream Schools (SPiMS) aims to provide high quality educational experiences for the children attending them and ensuring they have confident and competent staff who are equipped to support children with special educational needs (SEN) is key to this.  Schools have responsibility for recruiting their own teachers.  As a minimum, an applicant for a teaching post in a SPIM must hold a teaching qualification which meets the requirements for recognition to teach in grant-aided schools in Northern Ireland.Each school then sets its own experience selection criteria using a template provided by Education Authority (EA), which includes ‘person specifications’ for teachers working with children and young people with SEN. Governors select from a list of experience, knowledge and skill requirements relevant to the post and may add any criteria that are specific to the needs of the pupils or school.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what criteria will be used to place children in Specialist Provision in a Mainstream school.
answer_text: Statutory responsibility for the special educational needs (SEN) assessment and statementing process and the placement of children in special education provision rests with the Education Authority (EA).The EA has the statutory duty to arrange the special educational provision indicated in a statement of SEN.  The recommended appropriate placement for a child with a statement of SEN is based on their individually assessed needs.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) whether he has been engaging with the Department for Work and Pensions in relation to their work on developing a new poverty metric; and (ii) whether consideration has been given to developing and implementing a new poverty measure in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: (i) The Department for Work and Pensions (DWP) is developing a new Below Average Resources (BAR) poverty metric in collaboration with stakeholders, including DfC. My officials are engaging with DWP on the potential new measure as part of an Experimental Statistics Senior Steering Group and an Expert Advisory Group.  			(ii) The UK Below Average Resources (BAR) publication will contain comparisons between England, Scotland, Wales, and Northern Ireland. As the project concludes, DfC Analytics Division will engage with key stakeholders to consider whether it is appropriate to produce a Northern Ireland version of the Below Average Resources report alongside the UK publication.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what cross-departmental working he has undertaken with the Department of Education in relation to a new child poverty strategy.
answer_text: The Executive previously approved the extension of the 2016/19 Child Poverty Strategy to May 2022. Subject to Executive agreement, future action to tackle child poverty will be included as part of the broader Anti-Poverty Strategy.To date, my officials have engaged with officials from all departments, including the Department of Education, on the development of a future Anti-poverty Strategy and they will continue to do so as this work moves forward. While my Department is leading on the Anti-Poverty Strategy, it remains an Executive Strategy.  I will engage with all my Executive colleagues, including the Minister of Education, to establish priorities and commitments their departments can make in respect of an Anti-Poverty Strategy. My focus will continue to be on working with my Executive colleagues to develop a sustainable, deliverable Anti-Poverty Strategy which prioritises the issues that make a tangible difference to people’s lives, including those of our children and young people. Final decisions on the content and implementation of the Anti-Poverty Strategy will be subject to Executive agreement.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 9453/22-27, for an update on the Public Health Agency's development of a regional protocol in relation to the notification and management of Sudden Unexpected Death in Childhood.
answer_text: The ongoing work into the development of the Regional Protocol on Sudden and Unexpected Death in Infancy and Childhood (SUDIC), led by the Public Health Agency (PHA), is being informed by extant guidance, policy and experience to date across the UK relating to the surveillance and investigation of child deaths.  Whilst the focus of the work primarily relates to the HSC, the PHA is working closely with key stakeholders from other agencies including the PSNI and Coroner’s Office to optimise collaborative working to better understand the circumstances surrounding the death of any child up to 18 years of age who dies suddenly and unexpectedly. The new SUDIC Protocol is currently in final draft and is being tested by stakeholder organisations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether Sudden Unexpected Death in Childhood is used as a cause of death by NISRA.
answer_text: NISRA collates data on death registrations as collected by the General Register Office.  Deaths are coded and reported according to the World Health Organisation (WHO) International Classification of Diseases (ICD tenth revision).  There is no one specific code that covers ‘Sudden and Unexpected Deaths in Childhood’. The ICD-10 codes NISRA routinely uses to identify Sudden and Unexpected Deaths in Childhood are;R95 (Sudden infant death syndrome); and R96 (Other sudden death, cause unknown, (under age 18)).  R99 (Other ill-defined and unspecified causes of mortality (under age 18))A text search of the cause of death as written on the death certificate by the certifying medical practitioner would also be carried out for mentions of either ‘sudden unexplained death’ or ‘sudden unexpected death’.The table overleaf details the number of childhood deaths described as sudden unexpected/unexplained death between 2020 and 2022, the latest year for which finalised figures are available.Number of Sudden Unexplained/Unexpected Deaths in Childhood1,2,3, Registered by Year (2020-2022)Registration YearNumber of Deaths2020320211202251 Deaths in childhood have been classed as those of persons aged under 18.2 Sudden unexplained/unexpected Deaths defined as ICD-10 codes R95, R96 & R99, where age at death was under 183 Cause of death text fields have been searched for mentions of either ‘sudden unexplained death’ or ‘sudden unexpected death’.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail his Department’s progress in implementing actions outlined in A Life Deserved - A Strategy for Looked After Children.
answer_text: My Department has made significant progress on actions within the A Life Deserved strategy to improve outcomes for Children Looked After (CLA). This includes:Facilitation of multi-agency working between schools and Health and Social Care Trusts through the Primary Children Looked After Advisory Service (PCLAAS) and CLA Champion.  Development of the Revised Personal Education Plan Guidance for Primary Age Children Looked After to provide tailored support to ensure positive learning experiences as well as improved social wellbeing.  Launch of the joint DE/DoH Emotional Health and Wellbeing in Education Framework.Delivery of the Attach Programme, by the PCLAAS, which builds capacity within the existing education workforce on trauma informed practice and the importance of relationships. Work to re-establish an Education and Economy forum to focus on pathways for CLA.  The funding of 62 Nurture Groups in primary schools and establishment of work to develop a long-term approach to Nurture.  Of course, the current financial climate presents challenges.  For instance, the implementation of the revised guidance for Personal Education Plans for primary-aged children has been delayed by ongoing industrial action within the education sector as well as wider workforce issues in relation to social work support.   My Department remains committed to the A Life Deserved Strategy and improving outcomes for these vulnerable children.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education when he will update teacher training in order to support delivery of the Emotional Health and Wellbeing Framework.
answer_text: It is important to clarify that I do not expect teachers to be health professionals or to deliver health services.  However, all teaching staff should be mindful of pupil wellbeing and that all pupils are supported in school, to ensure they are learning and to prepare them for future life experiences and challenges.There are a number of projects / services delivered under the joint Department of Education / Department of Health (DoH) Children and Young People’s Emotional Health and Wellbeing in Education Framework, including the DoH-led Emotional Wellbeing Teams in Schools project and the Education Authority-led Being Well Doing Well programme.  Both of these provide advice, information and resources to schools to help promote pupil emotional health and wellbeing, and the latter focuses on helping schools develop a whole school approach to emotional health and wellbeing.Additionally, Initial Teacher Education (ITE) providers in Northern Ireland review their ITE programmes each year, to take into account any significant changes to the educational landscape and ensure that student teachers continue to receive appropriate development in important areas, such as the health and wellbeing of pupils. Departmental officials have previously shared the Framework with ITE providers for dissemination to student teachers and met with each of them to discuss the incorporation of the Framework into their ITE programmes.The Council for the Curriculum, Examinations and Assessment’s Wellbeing Hub also provides a range of curriculum-linked resources for teachers and shows how the Northern Ireland Curriculum promotes learning and developing skills that support pupil wellbeing and mental health.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what measures he will take to address evidence that there is a link between poverty and cognitive outcomes in early years.
answer_text: Research indicates that there is a clear link between poverty and cognitive outcomes in children's early years.  In addition to the universal Pre-school Education Programme, my Department funds a range of targeted early years interventions for children aged 0-4 years.  These interventions include Sure Start, the Pathway Fund and the Toybox project which provide developmental support to children from socially disadvantaged backgrounds who are at risk of educational underachievement, including cognitive delay.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 6407/22-28, for an update on the development of the business case for a regional pathway.
answer_text: I understand that a draft pathway has been developed for local services and is currently with members of the Facial Palsy Regional Planning Group for feedback.  This has been a complex process with multiple services involved in the pathway development, and multiple access points into the service across all Trusts in Northern Ireland.  It is my hope that a facial palsy pathway for local, regional and tertiary services will be developed and agreed in the coming months.Once a model of care is agreed, a business case will be developed for the service, funding permitting. I understand SPPG is working background to collate data that will be required to demonstrate the need for further investment in this area. However, it is important to note that this will be subject to securing the relevant funding within wider service pressures and priorities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education when the report on the Education Training Inspectorate’s evaluation of the approaches and challenges in delivering effective preventative safeguarding practices in schools will be published.
answer_text: The Education and Training Inspectorate (ETI) was commissioned to undertake a thematic evaluation of the preventative curriculum in schools and EOTAS centres. During the period November 2021 to June 2022, the ETI engaged with primary, post-primary and special schools and EOTAS centres to collate their views and experiences of delivering the preventative curriculum. Over 14,000 pupils provided views about how well they are learning to make informed choices and keep themselves safe.The findings were published in April 2023 in the report entitled 'The Preventative Curriculum in Schools and Education Other Than at School (EOTAS) Centres’ please see link to the published report: https://www.etini.gov.uk/sites/etini.gov.uk/files/publications/the-preventative-curriculum-in-schools-and-education-other-than-at-school-eotas-centres_0.pdf. ETI hosted a series of dissemination events to share key findings from the evaluation and provide an opportunity for practitioners to discuss the delivery of the preventative curriculum within their school.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 6407/22-27, (i) whether feedback from group members on the draft local pathway has been received and collated; and (ii) when the local pathway will be finalised.
answer_text: I understand that a draft pathway has been developed for local services and is currently with members of the Facial Palsy Regional Planning Group for feedback.  This has been a complex process with multiple services involved in the pathway development, and multiple access points into the service across all Trusts in Northern Ireland.  The development of regional pathways and protocols for the management of patients with facial palsy will need to consider referrals of patients with multiple conditions and treatment pathways. Streamlining pathways into local, regional and tertiary level services is not without challenges. This is further complicated by the current funding constraints, as it is not always possible to reconfigure existing services without additional funding, particularly if this change is across multiple Trusts.It is my hope that a facial palsy pathway for local, regional and tertiary services will be developed and agreed in the coming months, funding permitting.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the estimated timeline for (i) completion of the construction of the Strule Shared Campus site; and (ii) the opening of all the schools on the site to receive pupils.
answer_text: My officials are finalising work on the Full Business Case for the Programme and a potential contractor is in place. On the current programme timeline, subject to all necessary approvals being in place, contract award is planned for Summer 2024.  Assuming successful appointment of a main works contractor and following the pre-construction design period, construction would then begin on site late 2024 with completion currently estimated as late 2027. Custom and practice is that schools do not normally move to a new site mid-academic year which may mean that full campus opening could be September 2028.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of the success of the (i) Monitoring and Reporting Pupil Attendance Using Absence Tiers guidance document; and (ii) Miss School Miss Out strategy in addressing the number of children entitled to free school meals missing out on school.
answer_text: There has been no formal assessment of the Absence Tiers guidance but feedback to date has been positive as the data enables schools to better focus interventions where they will be most effective. Schools are also able to compare their own Absence Tiers report with benchmarking data which is published monthly on the DE website. The “Miss School. Miss Out” strategy is currently being reviewed. The strategy provides an overview of the ways in which schools can support all children to attend school regularly. Particular attention should be paid by schools to pupils from disadvantaged backgrounds including those in receipt of free school meals.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether the Education Training Inspectorate has provided an evaluation of the (i) educational; and (ii) reconciliation benefits of the Strule Shared Campus project.
answer_text: The Education and Training Inspectorate provides advice on an ongoing basis to officials developing the Strule Programme; ETI will have a role in evaluating the educational and reconciliation benefits of the project as this work progresses.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will review the procurement system for schools to ensure they can access best value for money.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) is designated as Education’s Centre of Procurement Expertise (CoPE).  This means the EA is directly responsible for the procurement of goods and services on behalf of schools. Both the EA and schools are bound by the Public Contract Regulations (PCR) 2015 and Northern Ireland Public Procurement Policy (NIPPP). As the managing authority for controlled schools, the funding authority for all grant-aided schools and the education CoPE, the EA is responsible for ensuring that effective procurement practice is embedded across the education sector to provide best value for money across the entirety of the schools estate as a whole, rather than in isolation.Where an EA contract does not already exist, schools can conduct their own lower value transactional purchases up to the value of £5,000, as outlined in Procurement Policy Note (PPN) 04/21, which sets out the Procurement Control Limits (PCLs) for all public bodies, including schools.Notwithstanding, during my school visits, school leaders frequently highlight procurement as a cause for concern.  The EA recognises these concerns and intends to address this in its new maintenance and minor works contracts, which will be based on defined geographical areas and will allow schools the greater freedom to engage locally approved suppliers.  In the first instance, schools should engage with the EA Estates Team in this respect.Furthermore, I also understand that the Minister of Finance has commissioned a review of Public Procurement, including the Procurement Board, in line with recommendations made by the Northern Ireland Audit Office in the April 2023 Review of Public Procurement, in which my Department and the EA will participate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education why there is lower public spending per pupil on education in Northern Ireland than the rest of the UK and Ireland.
answer_text: There are inherent difficulties in trying to make comparisons on per pupil funding across jurisdictions, examples of which include, but are not limited to, regional differences in school starting ages, data sources, how different types of schools are funded and how funds, including support services to pupils, are accounted for (e.g. home to school transport, school meals, etc.). Numerous research studies have sought to provide relative budget/expenditure comparisons across jurisdictions, but for the most part these have necessarily been limited to high-level approximations, which contain numerous qualifications and/or annotations as to the comparability of data.That said, per pupil funding for Northern Ireland in 2022-23 was £7,200, as reported in the most recent report by the Institute for Fiscal Studies, How does school spending per pupil differ across the UK?.  Based on the Secretary of State’s opening budget for 2023-24, Northern Ireland’s 2023-24 per pupil funding started the year at £6,995. By comparison per-pupil funding in England and Wales in 2023-24 is estimated at £7,460 and £7,327 respectively. I have continually emphasised these issues to my Executive colleagues.  I will continue to take every opportunity to make the case for additional investment in our children and young people’s education.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department maintains a register of children who have died as a result of Sudden Unexpected Death in Childhood.
answer_text: The Department of Health does not maintain a register of sudden unexpected in infancy/childhood deaths (SUDIC). The Northern Ireland Statistics and Research Agency (NISRA) is responsible for holding and analysing statistics on causes of death within the jurisdiction. However, the Public Health Agency (PHA) is in the process of developing a regional protocol in relation to the notification and management of SUDIC. The PHA maintains a register of any child death notified to it; this includes those that are sudden and unexpected. In addition to the above, if a child dies due to SUDIC, as per standard procedure, his/her death will be reported to and investigated by the coroner. If the death occurs in hospital, it will be subject to a multi-disciplinary review to determine if there are opportunities for learning to be identified.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether Sudden Unexpected Death in Childhood is currently used as a cause of death in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The Department of Health does not hold the information requested.The Northern Ireland Statistics and Research Agency (NISRA) is the principal source of official statistics on the causes of death in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 8028/22-27, when he will undertake a public consultation on options for changing eligibility criteria for Free School Meals and School Uniform Grants.
answer_text: Further to AQW 8028/22-27, I anticipate that a public consultation on changes to the eligibility criteria for free school meals and uniform grant will be undertaken later in 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what support is provided to families bereaved by Sudden Unexpected Death in Childhood.
answer_text: The Public Health Agency (PHA) is currently leading work to develop a Regional HSC protocol for the response to and review of Sudden Unexpected Deaths in Infancy and Childhood (SUDIC). This work is being informed by extant guidance, policy and experience to date across the UK relating to the surveillance and investigation of child death.Whilst the focus of the work primarily relates to the Health and Social Care  the PHA is working closely with key stakeholders from other agencies including the PSNI and Coroner’s office to optimise collaborative working to better understand the circumstances surrounding the death of any child up to 18 years of age who dies suddenly and unexpectedly.  One of the aims of this new protocol is to improve the experience of and support for bereaved families, as well as professionals, after the death of an infant or child. With this in mind, the SUDIC development team are continuing to work closely with the NI Bereavement Network which includes important voluntary organisations, such as SANDS and CRUSE.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how he will ensure that Special Educational Needs places for September 2024 (i) meet the needs of each pupil; and (ii) will be accessible to the pupil from the beginning of the 2024-25 academic year.
answer_text: Children with a statement of Special Educational Needs (SEN) are individually placed, based on their needs as set out in their statement and through a process of consultation between the Education Authority (EA), schools and parents.  Their placement is reviewed annually to ensure it continues to meet their needs.The EA is working in collaboration with managing authorities and schools, to create additional capacity across the education system to ensure sufficient specialist education provision is available to meet the needs of children who require it for the 2024/25 academic year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what action his Department has taken to provide Special Educational Needs placements for the 1000 additional pupils places that have been identified.
answer_text: The significant increase in the number of children with special educational needs (SEN) has resulted in an increased demand for specialist education provision, both in special schools and in specialist provisions in mainstreams schools (SPiMS).The Education Authority (EA) has statutory responsibility for ensuring appropriate education provision for all children and it is working at pace, in collaboration with managing authorities and schools, to create additional capacity across the education system to ensure sufficient specialist education provision is available to meet the needs of children who require it for the 2024/25 academic year.  My department engages regularly with the EA through a strategic placements group which aims to put in place a more planned, data-informed approach that will facilitate the growing number of learners with statements of SEN and provide the appropriate number of specialist education places needed to create sufficient capacity in the system.  Given the immediate pressures for September 2024, weekly meetings between my department and the EA are now being held to seek solutions to address the shortfall in places.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) how many requests his Department received for the removal of unauthorised flags or emblems attached to street lighting columns in (a) 2022; and (b) 2023; and (ii) how many of these requests were fulfilled.
answer_text: The number of reports about flags attached to its street lighting columns received by my Department was 69 in 2022 and 107 in 2023.With the exception of a few banners which presented danger to road users, my Department did not remove any flags from its street lighting columns during this time.Finding a sustainable solution to the ongoing problem of illegally erected flags and emblems requires leadership and a united approach. I can assure you that I am committed to working with Executive colleagues and all stakeholders to deal with this issue comprehensively.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 7703/22-27, what is on the agreed list of codes that are used to record pupil absence.
answer_text: The absence codes available for schools to use can be found at: www.education-ni.gov.uk/publications/attendance-guidance-absence-recording-by-schools-circular-202311

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance when she will make a decision on the Non-teaching Staff Pay and Grading Review Business Case.
answer_text: A revised business case for this has been submitted by the Department for Education, and my Department is currently carrying out its scrutiny of this.Given the implications that the proposals will have for recurrent costs going forward, it is right and proper that DoF carries out the scrutiny required to allow it to make an informed decision.My officials are working at pace with their colleagues in the Department of Education to progress this scrutiny and reach a decision point.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what percentage of pupils are accessing the recommended two hours per week of statutory curriculum physical education, broken down by (i) primary level; and (ii) post-primary level.
answer_text: My Department monitors the percentage of time schools allocate to Physical Education (PE) via the School Omnibus Survey.  The last survey was conducted in 2022 and primary schools and post-primary schools were asked about the time spent per week on the delivery of curricular PE.  The primary results are set out in the table below.Time spent per weekFoundation StageKey Stage 1Key Stage 20-59 mins25.0%17.5%13.2%60-119 mins60.7%66.7%63.2%120 mins +14.3%15.8%23.7%No of responding school112114114Source: School Omnibus Survey 2022 | Department of Education (education-ni.gov.uk)The 2022 Omnibus Survey was affected by the industrial action short of strike taken by the Northern Ireland teachers' unions.  As part of this action, union members were advised not to comply with some data collections undertaken by my Department. As a result, the response rate for the 2022 Omnibus Survey (21.5%) was significantly below the rate in 2018 (39%).  The lower response rate should be borne in mind when making inferences about the primary results in the table above.  Unfortunately, the response rate for post-primaries to the question about the time spent on PE did not meet the response threshold and subsequently it was not possible to categorise the results for the relevant key stages.The 2023-24 School Omnibus Survey will be issued to schools in September 2024 and will again include questions on the provision of PE in schools.  This will help to provide an up-to-date picture of PE provision in our schools.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how many cases are waiting beyond the 26-week statutory timescale for the issue of a statement of special education needs.
answer_text: I would refer the Member to the answer to question AQW 7477/22-27. The answer is contained in the Written Answers Booklet for week ending 22 March 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 7704/22-27, to detail the average waiting time for the issue of a statement of special educational needs.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that the average time taken to issue a final statement between 1 March 2023 and 25 March 2024 was 28 weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 8271/22-27, how his Department monitors the extent to which schools adhere to the Department's recommended post-primary admissions criteria.
answer_text: The content of any school’s admissions criteria is a matter for that school’s Board of Governors by virtue of The Education (Northern Ireland) Order 1997, although it must ‘have regard to’ my Department’s guidance when setting criteria.My Department and the Education Authority (EA) will provide advice to individual schools as requested and the EA will proactively contact schools prior to the publication of admissions criteria if a school’s draft criteria appear to be unworkable.  Furthermore, my Department would intervene if a school proposed to use criteria for which it had no approval, for example if a co-educational school proposed to use its admissions criteria as a route to become a single-sex school.However, given it is for Boards of Governors to determine the admissions criteria they wish to use, my Department does not routinely monitor the extent to which every school adheres to the recommendations in respect of criteria.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education when he will deliver a programme to reduce educational disadvantage.
answer_text: Work is ongoing within the Department on the design of a programme to reduce educational disadvantage. My officials are finalising the details of the programme and I have asked for an update as soon as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education why funding for detached youth work has been removed for the new financial year.
answer_text: Funding for detached work has not been removed for the new financial year. Feedback from organisations within the voluntary youth sector indicated that the funding process was too prescriptive. As a result, funding opportunity titles no longer state ‘Detached Work’ or ‘Street Based Youth Work’ as a required methodology. Instead, the funding opportunity outlines the assessed need and invites applicants to set out how they will address an identified assessed need when making their application, which may or may not include detached youth work.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education when he will make provision through regulations for procedures to be followed when pooling resources, as part of The Children's Services Co Operation Act (Northern Ireland) 2015.
answer_text: Section 4 (2) of the Children’s Services Co-operation Act (Northern Ireland) 2015 (CSCA) provides for the sharing of resources and pooling of funds, while section 8 of the CSCA states that “The Department of Finance and Personnel may by regulations make provision for procedures to be followed by children's authorities when exercising the powers conferred by section 4(2)” The Department of Education, in consultation with the Department of Finance produced guidance on the operation of the CSCA in November 2018 (updated in March 2022).  The guidance includes advice on the sharing of resources and pooling of funds. It can be accessed at https://www.education-ni.gov.uk/publications/guidance-childrens-services-co-operation-act-northern-ireland-2015

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how his Department can best promote contextual safeguarding amongst those working with children and young people.
answer_text: My Department publishes statutory guidance, Safeguarding and Child Protection in Schools – A Guide for Schools, to promote the best practices in safeguarding and child protection for those who work with children and young people in educational settings. Contextual safeguarding is compatible with the guidance, which highlights the many potential extra-familial harms that some our children and young people may experience, including exploitation, grooming and online harms. The guidance requires that Boards of Governors develop their school’s safeguarding and child protection policies to meet the specific needs of their pupils.The safeguarding guidance is reviewed and updated annually working with key partners to reflect emerging issues and best practice in addressing these in the guidance.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) what actions his Department has contributed to the first four interdepartmental homelessness action plans; and (ii) what actions his Department plans to include in the fifth Interdepartmental Homelessness Action Plan.
answer_text: My Department has contributed to the Interdepartmental Homelessness Action Plans by providing support to schools, children and young people, through resources provided to the Council for Curriculum, Examinations and Assessment (CCEA). CCEA’s resources are designed to align to key areas of the curriculum to enable them to be easily covered in the classroom. These include Thinking Skills and Personal Capabilities, Wellbeing, Financial Education Guidance and Citizenship. CCEA also supports schools through resources that cover issues related to, or known to contribute to, homelessness.DE’s support for this work via CCEA will continue despite the absence of a budget for Departments in respect of the Interdepartmental Homelessness Action Plans.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister what consideration they have given to the UN Committee on the Rights of the Child’s Concluding observations on the combined sixth and seventh periodic reports of the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland, published in June 2023.
answer_text: The report makes a number of recommendations that fall within the remit of our Department and these are being considered.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will bring forward legislative proposals in this mandate to reform homelessness legislation to prioritise homelessness prevention.
answer_text: I am currently considering my legislative priorities for inclusion in a Legislative Programme to be agreed by the Executive and notified to the Assembly in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what actions his Department is taking to reduce the level of school absence amongst children who are entitled to free school meals.
answer_text: It is clear from recent statistics that pupil absence is a significant challenge for all pupils.  For pupils in receipt of free school meals, attending school regularly can be even more challenging and the Department, the Education Authority and schools have a range of measures in place to support pupils.In addition to DE’s “Miss School. Miss Out Strategy” published in 2016, the Department published “The Challenge of Improving Pupil Attendance at School - Case Studies paper” in November 2018 followed by Absence Tiers Guidance in September 2022 to encourage schools to monitor and report on pupil attendance using Absence Tiers.The latter helps schools to look at the level of absence within a school in a more nuanced way than simply reporting a percentage present figure or percentage absent figure. This enables schools to work with pupils by encouraging them to progress through the various tiers and setting realistic goals to cope with individual circumstances. Boards of Governors have also been encouraged to use this approach in addition to their current practice.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) how many teachers have completed the mandatory qualification in sensory impairment; (ii) how many of these have completed this in the last five years; and (iii) whether an estimate has been made of how many teachers will complete this qualification in the next five years.
answer_text: ANSWERMy Department has been provided with the following information by the Education Authority (EA).29 teachers have completed the mandatory qualification in sensory impairment, of whom three have completed it in the last five years.Eight teachers are currently completing the mandatory qualification in sensory impairment.The number of teachers that will complete this qualification in the next five years is dependent on vacancies which arise. The EA is committed to ensuring that all teachers in its Sensory Service (which provides support to children and young people with sensory impairment) complete the relevant mandatory qualification.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what role people with lived experience of homelessness will have in the development of the fifth Interdepartmental Homelessness Action Plan.
answer_text: The Homelessness Strategy Steering Group recently discussed 'lived experience' at their quarterly meeting.  The role of those with lived experience and their possible involvement in the Inter Departmental Homelessness Action Plan is currently under consideration.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what steps are being taken to ensure that (i) deaf children; and (ii) their families receive the support necessary from Teachers of the Deaf in (a) education settings; and (b) early years development.
answer_text: The Education Authority’s (EA) Sensory Service provides support to deaf children and their families from the point of identification, in the home in the early years and later in the classroom in both mainstream and specialist settings.  The Newborn Hearing Screening Programme (NHSP) sends referrals to the Sensory Service which aims to respond within two days of receipt as per Quality Standards for Sensory Services.The needs of young deaf children are assessed via the National Sensory Impairment Partnership Early Years Eligibility Framework in line with information from audiology and Ear Nose and Throat services and considering the views and needs of parents/carers.  The Service works in partnership with parents to help them support their child’s development of language and communication through play-based activities and advice and guidance is provided in both the home to families and early years settings.Qualified Teachers of Deaf (ToD) within the sensory service support children and families, focussing on the child’s development in communication, literacy and mathematics as well as social and emotional development and fostering independence.  ToDs also provide information and advice to education settings on the implications of deafness and strategies to promote access to the curriculum and inclusion, including bespoke training for staff.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to outline the average waiting time for an Occupational Health Service medical assessment.
answer_text: Significant work has been ongoing within my Department’s Occupational Health Service to reduce the waiting times for assessments. An Occupational Health Consultant has taken up the Lead Medical Officer position within the Occupational Health Service in March 2024 and the launch of a recruitment campaign for additional medical officers is imminent.  As part of this recruitment exercise, we are also seeking to appoint additional nursing staff, and are introducing a new role within the Occupational Health Service of Occupational Health technicians. Occupational Health Service assessments often requires input from external sources such as GP’s and others who provide specialist medical services to Occupational Health Service users.  It is often the case that obtaining all of the information required contributes to the time required to undertake the occupational health assessment process.  Unfortunately, given the complexity involved and the limited numbers of medical staff, this can result in some assessments with a waiting time of up to four months.  Assessment can be arranged more promptly depending on the range of factors involved and the complexity of the assessment.When additional medical officers, nursing staff and occupational health technicians are in post, this will have a positive impact on waiting times to access occupational health services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what work his Department is undertaking to ensure that children and young people are aware of the signs of (i) child sexual exploitation; and (ii) child criminal exploitation.
answer_text: ANSWERMy department has issued up to date guidance ‘Safeguarding and Child Protection in Schools – A Guide for Schools’ which covers both child sexual exploitation and exploitation of children and young people more widely. The Departments of Justice and Health are currently carrying out work on child criminal exploitation, once this is complete we will seek to incorporate specific guidance on the matter.In order to support implementation of the safeguarding guidance, Child Protection Support Service in the Education Authority (EA) provides training, information and resources for school Governors, Principals, Designated Teachers/Deputy Designated Teachers and for all EA staff including those with designated safeguarding responsibilities to assist them in safeguarding children and young people.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department will consider collecting Section 75 disaggregated data from affected children, young people and relevant service providers on (i) the number of children not attending school; and (ii) the reasons for non-attendance.
answer_text: Pupil attendance is reported each year through the annual school census and is published in a statistical bulletin.  This bulletin already provides disaggregated data with pupil attendance analysis by ethnicity, religion, gender, school phase and for pupils with Special Educational Needs.  The school census does not collect information on sexual orientation, marital status, political opinion or whether a pupil has dependants.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what consideration he has given to the UN Committee on the Rights of the Child’s concluding observations on the combined sixth and seventh periodic reports of the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland, published in June 2023.
answer_text: My department co-ordinates the United Nations Committee on the Rights of the Child (UNCRC) reporting process on behalf of the Executive. The UNCRC Concluding Observations were circulated to all departments for information following their publication in June 2023. My officials will be commissioning a cross-departmental assessment of the Concluding Observations in the coming weeks, and I intend to consider those that relate to DE as part of that process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the implementation of the Children and Young People Strategy.
answer_text: Implementation of the Executive Children and Young People’s Strategy (CYPS) is the responsibility of all departments.  My department coordinates monitoring and reporting on the strategy and has recently establishment a Monitoring and Reporting Board, comprising senior officials from across all departments.  The Board will promote greater collaboration and provide strategic direction on key policy issues affecting children and young people which require significant coordinated effort.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) whether local integrated teams are to be funded to meet the significant backlog of unmet need within the Special Educational Needs population; and (ii) whether they are to be cost neutral for 2024-25.
answer_text: ANSWERThe Education Authority (EA) has not at this point confirmed any additional funding requirement to implement the proposed new Local Integrated Teams (LITs) model.  If received, this will be considered by the Department of Education (DE)/EA Special Educational Needs (SEN) Programme Board and any additional funding required will be considered in the context of the 2024/25 budget position.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education when he will publish updated Revised Regulations and Codes of Practice in relation to the Special Educational Needs and Disability Act (Northern Ireland) 2016.
answer_text: ANSWERWork continues on finalising the new Special Educational Needs (SEN) Framework, including the new SEN Regulations and the associated statutory Code of Practice.  Once all the necessary approvals are in place they will be presented to the Education Committee for scrutiny and will then commence their legislative journey through the Northern Ireland Assembly.Once the SEN Regulations have been approved by the Northern Ireland Assembly, the Department will publish as soon as possible thereafter.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education when he will commence Section 4 of the Special Educational Needs and Disability (NI) Act 2016, which provides for co-operation between the Education Authority and Health and Social Care Trusts to identify, assess and provide services to children with special educational needs and to co-operate relation to transition planning.
answer_text: ANSWERThe Department of Education (DE) is working closely with the Education Authority (EA) and Department of Health (DoH) on preparing an outline of the draft plan on co-operation.  Once developed this will be discussed with the Education and Training Inspectorate (ETI) and the Regulation and Quality Improvement Authority (RQIA) as they are required to inspect the plan every three years after it is published. DE and DoH jointly chair the Transitions Working Group which specifically aims to (i) understand the experiences and perceptions of the transition planning process; (ii) focus on current processes and practices and on the information available to those going through the transition planning process, including parents and carers; and (iii) identify improvements and synergies to create better ways of working and sharing of information.  This will inform development of the joint plan.Section 4 of the Special Educational Needs and Disability (Northern Ireland) Act 2016 is not reliant on supporting new SEN Regulations being made through the Northern Ireland Assembly so can be commenced as soon as the EA and DoH are ready.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what action he is taking to ensure that all elements of the Special Educational Needs and Disability Act (Northern Ireland) 2016 are commenced as soon as possible.
answer_text: ANSWERWork continues on finalising the new Special Educational Needs (SEN) Framework, which includes the SEN and Disability (SEND) Act (Northern Ireland) 2016, new SEN Regulations and supporting Code of Practice.  The SEND Act cannot be fully implemented without the supporting SEN Regulations being passed by the Northern Ireland Assembly.  The Regulations are in the final stages of drafting and once all the necessary approvals are in place they will be presented to the Education Committee for scrutiny and then commence their legislative journey through the Northern Ireland Assembly.  In the interim, my officials are working closely with the Education Authority (EA) to agree a timetable for commencing each provision within the SEND Act.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 7926/22-27, whether he will commission an up to date audit of the cost of division in the education system.
answer_text: As I indicated in response to AQW 7926/22-27, since publication of the Ulster Economic Policy Centre audit in 2016, the Executive commissioned the Independent Review of Education.The Panel’s recommendations are extensive and embrace all aspects of the education system and as previously advised, it is my intention to consider them when my officials have completed their review.Consequently, I do not intend to commission such an audit at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what work he is undertaking alongside the Minister of Health to identify relevant interfaces between health, social care and education service provision, to ensure that children requiring early intervention can access effective multi-disciplinary teams which can cater for a range of complex needs.
answer_text: In line with the duty to co-operate set out in the  Children’s Service Co-operation (Northern Ireland) Act 2015, my officials have been working closely with their counterparts in the Department of Health (DoH), the Education Authority (EA), the Public Health Agency (PHA) and the local Health Trusts to create the conditions for enhanced integration across both systems to improve opportunities for early intervention and the development of integrated supports for children and young people with special educational needs/disability (SEN/D).The experience of joint working across both the health and education systems during Covid-19 highlighted the added value of working in a more collaborative way.  My officials having been engaging with colleagues from across health as part of the End-to-End (E2E) Review of SEN and the associated workstreams.It is my intention to write to the Minister of Health, outlining emergent opportunities, as they are identified via the workstreams, that will enable both departments to work together to progress transformation across two very distinct but interdependent systems to provide the efficient and effective outcomes to services for children and young people with SEN/D and their families.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on refresher training for teachers in the design principles behind the curriculum, as recommended in A Fair Start.
answer_text: The Department commissioned the Education and Training Inspectorate (ETI) to conduct a review and provide policy advice on the current state of play in Foundation Stage (FS) classes to determine the most appropriate support required for FS teachers and classroom assistants (CAs).The report has been received and is currently being considered by departmental officials.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, further to Bangor Central Integrated Primary School being granted planning permission for their new school site on 5 March 2024, how he will ensure the required capital investment is released to deliver the new school building.
answer_text: I have placed the 10 remaining former Fresh Start projects that are not yet in contract into my Department’s conventional major works programme to continue to advance in design and planning.  The projects will proceed to construction when the design and planning stage is complete and affordable within the wider education budget context.All of the projects have been submitted as capital bids for 2024-25 to the Department of Finance and I trust all Executive parties will support these bids for capital funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on his Department’s engagement with the Education Training Inspectorate to commission an evaluation of the Foundation Stage of the Curriculum, as recommended in A Fair Start.
answer_text: My Department commissioned the Education and Training Inspectorate to conduct a review and provide policy advice on the current situation regarding play in foundation stage (FS) classes. The policy advice was received recently and is currently being considered by departmental officials.  This advice will help to determine the most appropriate support required for FS teachers and classroom assistants whilst enabling a greater focus on play-based pedagogy.  It will also inform the work being taken forward associated with the refresh of the Northern Ireland Curriculum design principles.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the Education Training Inspectorate’s commissioned work in to Learning through Play to inform policy advice, as recommended in A Fair Start.
answer_text: ANSWERThe Education and Training Inspectorate (ETI) was commissioned to provide advice on the current and future levels of professional learning and support required for foundation stage teachers and classroom assistants in play-based pedagogy. ETI has concluded its review and the findings have been shared with the relevant policy officials.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the creation of a draft strategic vision statement and guiding principles for sixth form.
answer_text: There are a range of individual policies and strategies impacting upon Sixth Form provision in schools, including The Entitlement Framework, Qualifications, Funding, Transport, Admissions and Area Planning.  This was acknowledged in the 14-19 Framework, ‘Developing a more strategic approach to 14-19 education and training: A Framework to transform 14-19 education and training provision’ and in ‘A Fair Start’ which recommended a dedicated Sixth Form policy that sets out a clear, strategic vision for sixth form that has the potential to help move towards a more equitable and consistent approach to 14-19 education and training with young people at the centre. Officials are currently drafting a document for engagement with stakeholders, to propose a draft vision statement and guiding principles for Sixth Form provision and will bring forward a paper for my consideration.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission to provide an update on maternity provision for MLAs.
To ask the Minister of Education what measures his Department is taking to ensure that pupils, parents and governors have sufficient opportunities to contribute to the preventative curriculum.
answer_text: My Department requires all grant-aided schools to have a Relationships and Sexuality Education (RSE) policy and taught programme that has been developed based on the ethos of their school and in consultation with staff, governors, parents and pupils. This policy should be kept under review through consultation with parents and pupils whose views should be sought and reflected in it. The RSE policy and content to be taught should also be made available to parents and pupils. Schools are required to deliver the statutory minimum content of the Northern Ireland Curriculum; however, they have flexibility in how they develop and deliver the curriculum including their taught RSE programme. It is important that a school satisfies itself, through its self-evaluation and review process, that its RSE programme meets effectively the needs its pupils through consultation with them.  This includes the teaching of sensitive topics, such as consent, as well as building healthy and respectful relationships.In April 2023, the Education and Training Inspectorate (ETI) published an evaluation of the Preventative Curriculum in Schools and Education Other Than at School (EOTAS) Centres.  The report found that there has not been sufficient and consistent consideration across the education system to tackle in a meaningful and timely way more sensitive and, for some, controversial issues.  My Department has therefore established a task and finish group that is considering currently how best to respond to the ETI findings.  Further guidance on RSE in schools will be considered by my Department in due course.  Further to this, RSE is included in the statutory safeguarding guidance that applies in all schools.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education why health professionals are currently not included in the establishment of local integrated teams as part of the End to End Review of Special Educational Needs and Disability.
answer_text: The first phase of the Education Authority’s (EA) Special Educational Needs and/or Disability (SEND) Transformation Programme seeks to introduce a new delivery model for its Pupil Support Services (PSS) by September 2024, moving from a range of separate services to Local Integrated Teams (LITs) and removing the gatekeeping role of educational psychologists, with the aim of improving access to support services for children with SEN.The potential for integrating Allied Health Professional (AHP) support will be considered at a later phase of the EA’s SEN Transformation Programme, in collaboration with the Department of Health (DoH) and its delivery bodies.  The wider End to End (E2E) Review of SEN is looking more broadly at improving collaboration between Health and Education to support children with SEN.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will ensure that contracts for counselling services in post-primary schools are renewed before they expire later this year.
answer_text: The current contract ends on 31 August 2024 and the Education Authority has plans in place to advertise a tender opportunity for the new contract in the coming weeks.  It is intended that the new contract will be in place on 1 September 2024 to ensure continuity of service.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what measures his Department is taking to ensure that all educational settings include consent as a key aspect of the preventative curriculum.
answer_text: My Department requires all grant-aided schools to have a Relationships and Sexuality Education (RSE) policy and taught programme that has been developed based on the ethos of their school and in consultation with staff, governors, parents and pupils. This policy should be kept under review through consultation with parents and pupils whose views should be sought and reflected in it. The RSE policy and content to be taught should also be made available to parents and pupils. Schools are required to deliver the statutory minimum content of the Northern Ireland Curriculum; however, they have flexibility in how they develop and deliver the curriculum including their taught RSE programme. It is important that a school satisfies itself, through its self-evaluation and review process, that its RSE programme meets effectively the needs its pupils through consultation with them.  This includes the teaching of sensitive topics, such as consent, as well as building healthy and respectful relationships.In April 2023, the Education and Training Inspectorate (ETI) published an evaluation of the Preventative Curriculum in Schools and Education Other Than at School (EOTAS) Centres.  The report found that there has not been sufficient and consistent consideration across the education system to tackle in a meaningful and timely way more sensitive and, for some, controversial issues.  My Department has therefore established a task and finish group that is considering currently how best to respond to the ETI findings.  Further guidance on RSE in schools will be considered by my Department in due course.  Further to this, RSE is included in the statutory safeguarding guidance that applies in all schools.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the Education Training Inspectorate’s review of their scheme of inspection.
answer_text: The Education and Training Inspectorate (ETI) has engaged in an extensive process of consultation and co-design work with schools, organisations and a wide range of stakeholders, to inform the development of a new inspection process.  An analysis of the responses to the consultation on the development of inspection was published by ETI in August 2023, please see link : https://www.etini.gov.uk/news/eti-publishes-findings-consultation-development-inspectionThe analysis of feedback has informed the revised inspection process, including the function and purpose of inspection and the associated development of the new inspection framework, which is now at an advanced stage of readiness. To deliver on ETI’s vision of being ‘the voice for equity and excellence for all learners’, there is a renewed focus on the centrality of the learner in the inspection process, using five questions for consideration as the core of all inspections. The core questions include a renewed emphasis on the ethos and culture of the school/organisation, its context, vision and values, and how it creates a community of learning. School/organisation self-evaluation and development priorities are central to the new inspection process.A number of pilot inspections commenced in the autumn term of 2023 across the early years, work-based learning and youth phases. Due to ongoing industrial action, no pilot inspections have been undertaken in primary or post-primary schools. Examples of the reports from the organisations involved in the pilot inspections can be found on the ETI website: https://www.etini.gov.uk/publications/type/inspectionreports ETI is continuing to engage with schools on the new model of inspection and undertaking a series of ‘Empowering Improvement’ events with a range of school leaders.  The development of inspection has been broadly welcomed by stakeholders.  ETI will continue to review the feedback from the schools/organisations participating in the pilot inspections, in addition to its ongoing engagement with the wider education system to communicate the changes to the inspection process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of the June 2023 UN Convention on the Rights of the Child Committee recommendation that the NI Executive explicitly prohibit the use of restraint and seclusion in educational settings and adopt a child rights-based approach to addressing violence or other disturbances in schools.
answer_text: I have noted the concluding observations of the UN Convention on the Rights of the Child Committee on the combined sixth and seventh reports of the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland in relation to the issues of restraint and seclusion in educational settings. The UNCRC committee recommended the development of statutory guidance “on the use of restraint on children to ensure that it is used only as a measure of last resort and exclusively to prevent harm to the child or others and monitor its implementation.”My officials previously took forward a review of the use of restraint and seclusion in educational settings. Following publication of the report of this review in 2022, officials worked with a wide range of stakeholders to co-design draft “Statutory Guidance on the Reduction and Management of Restrictive Practices in Educational Settings in Northern Ireland”. The draft statutory guidance was developed using a child-rights based approach, and is underpinned by Article 3 (1) of the United Nations Convention on the Rights of the Child and Article 7 (2) of the United Nations Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities which state that in all actions concerning children, the best interests of the child shall be a primary consideration.The draft statutory guidance recognises there may be occasions when pupils display behaviour that due to its intensity and impact on themselves and/or others may require emergency intervention in the form of the use of restrictive practices (including restraint and seclusion) for their care and wellbeing and that of those whom they interact with.  However, the draft statutory guidance also makes clear that restrictive practices should never be used as a punitive measure and should only ever be used to prevent children, young people and others from harm.  The draft statutory guidance was consulted on at the end of last year, and the views of those who responded to the public consultation are the subject of a consultation report which I will consider in the coming weeks. Following this, I will determine the next steps to be taken in relation to the draft statutory guidance.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) for an update on the Education Authority Special Educational Needs Early Years Framework; and (ii) to detail the amount of hours per day special pre-schools will facilitate.
answer_text: The Education Authority’s (EA) Proposed Framework of Future Provision for Children in the Early Years with Special Educational Needs (SEN) was published for consultation in 2018.  It was not finalised and has subsequently been subsumed into the End to End (E2E) Review of SEN. Specialist education provision for pre-school children is provided in both special schools and in early years and foundation stage specialist provisions in mainstream schools (SPEYFs).  The hours per day in both types of provision ranges from 2.5 hours (part-time) to 4.5 hours (full-time).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on his Department’s plans to design, develop and implement new arrangements for Key Stage 1-3 assessment.
answer_text: The Department remains committed to commencing a review and making policy recommendations regarding the purpose, nature and design of Key Stage Assessment in Northern Ireland. Once the necessary resources have been secured, the review will consider international practice and the views of key stakeholders and practitioners to undertake a strategic assessment of the current arrangements and produce proposals and recommendations to support the development of ‘fit for purpose’ arrangements for Key Stage Assessment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of the appropriateness of his Department's recommended post-primary admissions criteria.
answer_text: My Department’s guidance on the post-primary admissions process, which includes recommendations in respect of admissions criteria, issued most recently in October 2023.  The guidance sets out criteria it is recommended post-primary schools should and should not use in their admissions criteria.  While it is the sole responsibility of Boards of Governors to draw up and apply admissions criteria, they do have a legal duty to ‘have regard to’ the guidance when setting criteria.The recommended criteria are intended to assist schools in drawing up criteria that are robust, are capable of selecting children down to the last available place and help schools consider the children they wish to prioritise for admission.The guidance also aims to ensure schools do not inadvertently discriminate against certain groups of children, for example rural dwellers or children who have elder siblings who do not attend a mainstream school.  It provides detailed explanatory notes on each of the recommended criteria, recommends how tie-breaker criteria can be structured and provides a worked example of how criteria are applied.The guidance is reviewed annually and updated as required to ensure it remains appropriate and reflects wider developments such as legislative change or case law.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether the Education Training Inspectorate report on Specialist Provision in Mainstream Schools has been completed.
answer_text: The Education and Training Inspectorate has undertaken an evaluation of Specialist Provision in Mainstream Schools; the report will be published by the end of June 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the implementation of the new Special Educational Needs Framework.
answer_text: Work continues on finalising the new Special Educational Needs (SEN) Framework which includes new SEN Regulations and supporting statutory Code of Practice.  The Regulations are in the final stages of drafting and once all the necessary approvals are in place, they will be presented to the Education Committee.  They will then commence their legislative journey through the Northern Ireland Assembly.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) all requests for temporary variation in enrolment by post-primary schools, in the last 5 years; and (ii) the number of additional places granted, by school, for the 2023-24 academic year.
answer_text: The information you have requested is extensive and therefore my officials have deposited a paper in the Assembly library on 21 March 2024 titled ‘Post-primary Places Requested from 2019/20 to 2023/24 and Approved in 2023/24’.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how they will provide inclusive policies and practices for children and young people with disabilities in mainstream educational settings, as recommended in the Children’s Law Centre Report to the UN Committee on the Rights of the Child.
answer_text: My department’s vision is that EVERY CHILD is happy, learning and succeeding and this is no different for those children and young people with Special Educational Needs (SEN).  In taking forward the End-to-End (E2E) Review of SEN, officials are considering the system from the legislative and policy intent, through to the statutory assessment process and beyond to workforce development and pupil support.  This is to inform early actions for reform that can deliver a more effective, efficient and sustainable model of SEN support services that will improve outcomes for children and young people. As advised in response to AQW 6270/22-27, the development of a roadmap for inclusion is now underway and a programme of engagement, with key educational stakeholders including political representatives, will take place over the coming months.With this at the forefront of my ambition for children and young people with SEN and their families, I am committed to securing support from my Executive colleagues for any additional investment that is required to ensure that children with SEN get the right support, delivered by the right people, at the right time and in the right place.I welcome the advocacy on behalf of children and young people with SEN provided by the Children’s Law Centre (CLC) on these issues. As key stakeholders, I am pleased that CLC representatives are actively engaged with my department’s E2E Review of SEN to inform and shape early opportunities for action that will support reform of the system.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what consideration his Department has given to the creation of a grant that can be provided to families with children requiring overnight hospital care to assist with costs.
answer_text: The Help with Health Costs (HwHC) scheme provides individuals, primarily on low incomes, with financial help towards the cost of dental treatment, eyesight tests, glasses or contact lens, and travel costs for HSC treatment on referral by a doctor or dentist.  The eligibility criteria are set out in legislation and certain benefit recipients automatically passport on to the scheme.  Preparatory work has recently commenced in advance of updating the Travelling Expenses and Remission of Charges Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2004. The focus of the update is to reinstate automatic passporting for those eligible to receive free sight tests and free Health Service dental treatment through the Help with Health Care (HwHC) scheme following the migration of their benefits to Universal Credit by the Department for Communities (DfC).The commencement of a wider review of the provisions within these Regulations and associated HwHC scheme will be considered once this urgent work is completed and in line with available resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how he will ensure additional support will be provided in schools for those for whom English is a second language, as recommended in the Children’s Law Centre Report to the UN Committee on the Rights of the Child.
answer_text: Children in schools who have English as an Additional Language (EAL) are supported via the Department’s Supporting Newcomer Pupils policy.   Through this, schools are provided with c£1,200 of additional funding per newcomer pupil under the Common Funding Formula.
