In 2023/24, a total of c£22m was provided to around 770 schools.  In addition to this, pupils from the Asylum Seeker and Refugee (ASR) community are supported via The Executive Office’s (TEO) ASR Dispersal Fund.   In 2023/24, £795k was provided to schools from this fund with a further £40k available from the UK Department for Levelling Up, Housing and Communities.Schools provide both English as a core curriculum subject as well as EAL support for newcomer pupils until their level of English reaches the level of their peers.  All schools signpost newcomer families to sources of additional English language learning in the community, including formal and informal English for speakers of other languages (ESOL) classes. The Education Authority provides support for staff working with newcomer children through the co-delivering of an intercultural education course in partnership with Queens University Belfast.  Over 350 school-based staff have completed this course since 2017 and the course will run again in September 2024.		The Department is currently reviewing its Supporting Newcomer Pupils policy with a view to presenting proposals for my consideration.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) how much of his Department’s budget is spent on Shared Education; and (ii) how much of the budget spent on Shared Education is funded through external sources.
answer_text: Funding allocated to Shared Education programmes in the 2023/24 financial year, including the source of the funding, is set out below.  Mainstreaming Shared Education - the Department of Education has provided £2m of resource funding from its core budget to support pupil engagement as part of the delivery of the Mainstreaming Shared Education Strategy.Peace IV - a total budget of  £7.262m was allocated to fund two Shared Education projects (now concluded). This comprised European Regional Development Fund (ERDF) funding of  £6.173m (85%) alongside match funding of  £1.089m (15%) from the Department of Education (Northern Ireland), funded through the Northern Ireland Executive. Peace Plus - a total budget of £196k was allocated for two education related programmes including Shared Learning Together and Youth Mental Health and Wellbeing. This comprised ERDF funding of £157k (80%) and match funding of £39k (20%) funded through the Northern Ireland Executive. As relevant projects are not yet operational, no spend has been incurred in 2023/24. Strule Shared Education Campus - £1.8m of capital funding, funded from Fresh Start Agreement capital funding provided by HM Treasury, and £75k of resource funding, funded from DE core budget, has been allocated to the Strule Shared Education Campus.  Shared Education Campus (SEC) Programme – As of 31 January 2024, the Department has spent £3.61m of capital funding on the SEC programme, funded from Fresh Start Agreement capital funding provided by HM Treasury.  The Department has also allocated £435k of resource funding to the SEC programme, provided by The Executive Office to ensure that SEC projects are fully funded and supported from the onset of planning.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he intends to undertake a review of Schemes of Management.
answer_text: I can confirm a review of Schemes of Management is ongoing.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will change the eligibility criteria for (i) Free School Meals; and (ii) the Uniform Grant to reflect child poverty measures.
answer_text: My Department is continuing to progress a review of the eligibility criteria for free school meals and uniform grants. The options being considered as part of this review include those that raise the income thresholds that applies to some of the benefits-based eligibility criteria. Any such a rise would enable more families on lower incomes to access free school meals and uniform grants. Ahead of any decisions being taken on changes to the eligibility criteria, a full public consultation on the options and associated impact assessments will need to be undertaken and I would plan to do this as soon as possible. However, a key issue in this review that we cannot simply ignore is affordability. The Department of Education is facing significant funding pressures and struggling to support existing key services. There are potentially significant budgetary implications of increasing the number of children eligible for free school meals and I will need to take these implications into account before taking any final decision.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education when the advertising campaign specifically aimed at promoting education for those most at risk of underachievement will be able to resume.
answer_text: The resumption of the advertising campaign “Try and Stop Me”, aimed at promoting education for those most at risk of underachievement is budget dependent and as such must be considered in the context of other departmental priorities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on his Department’s work on commissioning an independent research project regarding alternative deprivation measures to Free School Meal Entitlement, as per the recommendation contained within A Fair Start report.
answer_text: University of Ulster (UU) took forward the research project and the key findings were presented to the Fair Start Programme Board on 27th February 2024. The research will be published on the DE website on 21 March 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of the June 2023 UN Committee on the Rights of the Child recommendation that the NI Executive repeal provisions for compulsory attendance in collective worship and establish statutory guidance to ensure the right of all children to withdraw from religious classes without parental consent.
answer_text: I have no plans to repeal provisions for compulsory attendance in collective worship.   Parents continue to have the right to have their child withdrawn from religious education or collective worship.  Collective worship is governed by Article 21 of The Education and Libraries (NI) Order 1986. This article states:“21(1) Subject to the provisions of this Article, religious education shall be given in every grant-aided school other than a nursery school and the school day in every such school shall also include collective worship whether in one or more than one assembly on the part of the registered pupils at the school”. Since all pupils including those above compulsory school age are registered at school, the need for parental consent to be excused from religious education or collective worship would still be required as defined by article 21(5):“If the parent of any pupil requests that the pupil should be wholly or partly excused from attendance at religious education or collective worship or from both, then, until the request is withdrawn, the pupil shall be excused from such attendance in accordance with the request”.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what steps he is taking to ensure that the £150 million which has been removed from Fresh Start funding will be brought back into the Department of Education and invested in the ten building projects for shared and integrated schools which have had their funding withdrawn.
answer_text: I have written to the Secretary of State for Northern Ireland to ask the Government to honour its funding commitments to these ten projects.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of the June 2023 UN Committee on the Rights of the Child recommendation that the NI Executive end academic selection.
answer_text: I note the committee’s recommendation and believe decisions on this matter should be for people in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what work has been undertaken to address the cost of division in education, as identified by the Department of Finance commissioned Ulster Economic Policy Centre independent audit, to be up to £95 million per year.
answer_text: This audit was published in 2016 and used “surplus” places as a proxy for an estimate of the cost of division.  My Department does not recognise the term “surplus places” – the term used to consider the level of flexibility in the system is “available places”.The Department does not fund available places.  Schools are funded on the basis of their actual enrolment number, not their approved enrolment number.In the intervening period since the publication of the audit, the Executive commissioned the Independent Review of Education.  I will consider the Panel’s recommendations when my officials have completed their review.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of the 2023 Council of Europe Advisory Committee on the Framework Convention for the Protection of National Minorities recommendation that the NI Executive take measures to tackle racist bullying in schools, including through adding ethnic identifiers on recording of racist bullying incidents
answer_text: The Department has taken a number of measures in recent years to address bullying and the factors that contribute to it.The Addressing Bullying in Schools Act (Northern Ireland) 2016 (the Act), came into effect in 2021. The Act introduced statutory duties which require schools to record incidents or alleged incidents of bullying type behaviour, their motivation and their outcome.  Each school’s Board of Governors has responsibility to oversee the implementation of the Act in their school.  While the motivation recorded for incidents can include race, there is not currently any requirement record under legislation to record ethnic identifiers.  The legislation does not include the requirement for schools to share the data that they record with the Department.The Department established the Addressing Bullying in Schools Implementation Team (ABSIT) in the Education Authority (EA) in 2022. ABSIT provides support for schools, educational settings and young people to help them understand the requirements of the Act and embed cultures that help eradicate bullying.  The work of the team also includes providing programmes of direct support for those who experience all types of bullying.ABSIT assist with complex cases and, where appropriate, will engage with the EA’s Intercultural Education Service (IES).  IES helps schools and EA services to meet the additional educational needs of asylum-seeking, refugee, Traveller, Roma and other newcomer pupils, many of whom are from ethnic minority communities.  IES has also contributed to the to the intercultural content for Initial Teacher Education courses delivered by the Higher Education Institutions in Northern Ireland and co-delivers an intercultural education course in partnership with Queen’s University Belfast.The curriculum in both primary and post-primary school contributes to promoting good relations and is closely aligned with my Department’s Community Relations, Equality and Diversity in Education Policy. Schools and teachers can choose to deliver sensitive and important aspects of the curriculum at a time when they are the subject of national debate and when young people can make explicit connections between what they are learning in school and what is happening in the wider world. This includes issues around racism and other forms of discrimination.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the delivery of the Interdepartmental Homelessness Action Plan.
answer_text: Homelessness is more than a housing issue.  Issues such as addictions, poor mental health or discharge from an institution can underlie a lot of homelessness. The Interdepartmental Homelessness Action Plan focuses on addressing gaps in non-accommodation services which have the potential to improve the lives and life chances of people who are experiencing homelessness and those who are most at risk of homelessness. The Fourth Plan was published on 21 September 2023.A total of 13 actions have been identified for the Departments for Communities, Health, Justice (DoJ) and Education (DE) with my Department having lead responsibility for four actions. My Department and the Homelessness Strategy Steering Group monitor the implementation of the actions.My Department will again liaise with the Responsible Owners and produce and publish a Report in the Summer.  This Report will reflect whether each Action ultimately met its milestone or expected outcome. I would like to thank the other Departments for their commitment to this inter-departmental work.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) why the figures contained in the Non-Teaching Staff Pay and Grading review were inaccurate; and (ii) what action he will be taking to ensure that the Business Case is returned to the Department of Finance as soon as possible.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) have provided the following information:The Pay & Grading Review was negotiated in 2022 following which a business case was submitted in February 2023.  That business case contained estimates based on staffing figures from November 2021. These figures were taken at a point in time from the payroll system and therefore did not fully capture the recent significant growth in demand for SEN assistants that has resulted in increased staffing numbers. EA officials subsequently raised this issue with the Department in February 2024 and are currently updating the figures to reflect the actual pay outturn for 2022/23 and the associated full-time equivalent staffing. This work is being carried out as a priority to ensure that the staffing numbers will reflect the increases due to SEN and that the business case can be re-submitted to the Department as soon as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will introduce a program similar to the £320 million Physical Education and Sport Premium announced by the UK Education Minister, which guarantees children 60 minutes of sport and physical education each day.
answer_text: With regards to the future funding of any project, you will be aware that departments have not yet been advised of budget allocations for 2024-25.  While the Department of Education has been advised of an indicative baseline for planning purposes, detailed budgetary planning and prioritisation has yet to be undertaken.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how much of the School Ventilation Fund was used on remedial works to improve ventilation in schools.
answer_text: ANSWERThe Department of Education provided earmarked capital funding in the 2021/22 financial year for CO2 monitors and ventilation needs across the schools’ estate, in response to the Covid-19 pandemic. £1,008,386.82 was spent on remedial ventilation works in schools.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of the Education and Training Inspectorate's review of physical education in primary schools.
answer_text: The Department has established a Task and Finish group to address the recommendations from the Education and Training Inspectorate's (ETI) review of physical education (PE) in primary schools. The group is made up of representatives from the Department, the Education and Training Inspectorate, The Council for the Curriculum, Examinations & Assessment and the Education Authority.Work streams have been established, looking at Initial Teacher Education and professional development opportunities, the school estate, resources and providing support to schools to ensure that they can deliver the Department’s recommended two hours of PE per week.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education when he will publish the findings from the evaluation of the Healthy Happy Minds pilot.
answer_text: An appropriately redacted version of the post project evaluation for the Healthy Happy Minds pilot will be made available on my Department’s website in the next few weeks.  Redactions are required to ensure compliance with GDPR and the terms of the surveys undertaken as well as to remove commercially sensitive information.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he plans to progress legislation to address derelict and dilapidated buildings.
answer_text: I am considering introducing a Dilapidation bill to the Assembly, and will receive briefing from my officials in due course. Introduction would, of course, be subject to the agreement of my Executive colleagues. The proposed Bill would provide councils with a modern and fit-for-purpose regime, including clear powers to tackle dilapidated buildings and neglected sites.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the impact the Northern Ireland Troubles (Legacy and Reconciliation) Act 2023 will have on her Department.
To ask the Minister of Education how many schools are below the minimum thresholds set out in the Sustainable Schools Policy.
answer_text: The table below details the number of schools that are currently falling below the minimum threshold of sustainable enrolment numbers (105 for rural and 140 for urban primary schools, and 500 for years 8-12 and 100 for post-16 post-primary schools) using verified census data for the 2022/23 academic year.No of rural primary below 105No of urban primary below 140No of post- primary below 500 Y8 - 12No of post- primary below 100 post-16Total191414439

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what action his Department has taken to implement the Fair Start Expert Panel report recommendation that the systemic inequality of academic selection be addressed as a priority.
answer_text: The “A Fair Start” Expert panel did not make a formal recommendation regarding academic selection. Its Terms of Reference required the panel to focus on “the wide range of issues on which consensus can be found”.Delivery of the actions within the report are progressing well and updates on progress can be found at: Progress reports on A Fair Start Report and action plans | Department of Education (education-ni.gov.uk)

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what is the current longest waiting time for the issue of a statement of special educational needs.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that the current longest waiting time for the issue of a statement of special educational needs (SEN) is 72 weeks.While it is wholly unacceptable that any child is waiting this long for an assessment of their needs, my understanding is that the circumstances around the case are significantly complex and that the process itself can, in these type of cases, sometimes be the barrier.It is for this reason that the assessment and statementing process is a key workstream under the End to End (E2E) Review of SEN as this is a critical component to ensuring that children and young people receive the right support, from the right people, at the right time and in the right place.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department collects data on the number of children not attending school due to emotionally-based school avoidance.
answer_text: Pupil absence is recorded by schools through the selection of a code from an agreed list published by my Department.   There is not a specific code for emotionally based school avoidance and there are limitations to the existing codes available. It is therefore not possible to produce data on the number of children affected by this condition.The new school administration system EdiS, is under development. Consequently, we will be reviewing all existing attendance codes and this may result in new codes being created for use in our schools.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update of the review of the common funding scheme.
answer_text: The Review of the Common Funding Scheme (CFS) Project was closed by the then Education Minister in March 2021.  This was following advice from the Education Transformation Programme Board in light of funding uncertainty, the impact of Covid-19 and linkages to the Independent Review of Education.My department is currently considering the Independent Review of Education's recent report, Investing in a Better Future (the Report), as well as carrying out internal consultation to inform a way forward.  This process includes discussion with key education stakeholders including other relevant departments and Arms-Length Bodies.  The Report highlights the need to increase per pupil funding in Northern Ireland.  Closely related to the level of investment in our schools is the funding method (i.e. the CFS and the Common Funding Formula).  As such, re-starting the review of the CFS will be considered in conjunction with the various recommendations and the Report in my subsequent response.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) how many education welfare officer posts are currently vacant; (ii) where these vacant posts are located; and (iii) how long these posts have been vacant.
answer_text: There are currently nine Education Welfare Officer posts vacant in the Education Authority.  Further details on these posts are contained in the table below.(i) No of vacancies(ii) Locations(iii) Vacant fromComments1Newry01/02/2024Interview dates set for competition to refill post.1Ballymena01/09/20222Antrim Board Centre06/10/202317/11/20233Dundonald01/12/2023Interviews carried out and 3 successful candidates completing pre-employment checks. One post is being readvertised.15/09/202301/05/20232Downpatrick01/12/202331/08/2023

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the Education Welfare Service Transformation Project.
answer_text: Transformation of the Education Welfare Service has been impacted by industrial action however support to schools is continuing to be provided within that context. The associated Business Case is nearing completion and is subject to funding availability through the Department of Finance.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many full-time equivalent vacancies for speech and language therapists there are in each of Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Information on the full-time equivalent of vacancies is not collected centrally. However, information on the position count of HSC vacancies actively being recruited by staff group, is published by my Department on a quarterly basis, at the link below.https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/articles/staff-vacanciesInformation on the number of speech and language therapist positions actively being recruited to in each Health and Social Care (HSC) Trust as at 31 December 2023, most recent information available, is detailed in the following table.HSC Speech & Language Therapist Vacancies Actively Being Recruited, as at 31 December 2023HSC TrustHSC Speech & Language Therapist VacanciesBelfast19Northern3South Eastern 13Southern9Western12Source: NI HSC Active Recruitment Statistics, DoH

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how he will address shortages in the speech and language therapist workforce.
answer_text: I remain committed to implementing the recommendations of the Speech and Language Therapy Workforce Review 2019 – 2029. This will require increasing the number of undergraduate training places commissioned by my department each year by 12 to reach the recommendation of 40. Other recommendations include developing alternative routes to qualification; expanding the commissioning of postgraduate training; and developing a regional approach to AHP workforce issues such as recruitment, employment and skills.I will continue to make the case for the essential investment required for our future workforce. However, I expect that the 2024/25 budget will be extremely challenging.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how he will work with the Department of Health to address shortages in the speech and language therapist workforce which are impacting on speech and language provision in schools.
answer_text: Speech and language provision has been identified as a key priority area for the department as part of the emergent implementation plans flowing from the End to End (E2E) Review of Special Educational Needs (SEN). The department is working closely with the Department of Health (DoH) as part of the joint Health/Education oversight group to develop work in this area, part of which is focussed on workforce planning and provision.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) how many applications for a statement of special educational needs exceeded the 26 week statutory time limit; (ii) how long these assessments took; and (iii) the reasons for delay, for each year since 2020.
answer_text: In answer to part (i) and part (ii) of your question, the Education Authority (EA) has advised that as of 29 February 2024:Year Ending Number of Statements Over 26 Weeks for Completion Number of Statements Between 27-40 Weeks for Completion Number of Statements Over 40 weeks for Completion 31 March 2020 58329431431 March 2021 00031 March 2022 3939131 March 2023 13011422Year to Date 29 February 20241217743The EA has advised that any reasons for delay that would sit outside of noted ‘statutory exceptions’ are not available and would require manual checking of each file.Any delays within the statutory process are wholly unacceptable.  It is for this reason that there is a dedicated workstream on statementing under the End to End (E2E) Review of Special Educational Needs (SEN) to ensure that the department understands any reason for delay and to seek out early actions for improvement of this process for children, young people and their families.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on how he intends to prioritise investment in the integrated schools that have been advised that Fresh Start funding is no longer available to them.
answer_text: Once the projects reach pre-tender stage, a decision to release to tender will be taken on a case-by-case basis, dependent on the respective needs and the budget available at that time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of children currently falling below the 85 per cent school attendance threshold.
answer_text: The most recent management information, drawn from the unvalidated data recorded by schools, covers the September 2023 to January 2024 period of the 2023/24 academic year and shows 42,967 (13%) of pupils with less than 85 per cent school attendance.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) how many cases are currently waiting to be allocated to an education welfare officer; and (ii) how long each case has been waiting.
answer_text: The Education Authority have confirmed that as at 27 February 2024, there are 1,586 unallocated cases.Unallocated Open Cases – Waiting TimeNumber of CasesBand A: 0 – 2 weeks275Band B: 3 – 12 weeks458Band C: 13 – 36 weeks214Band D: 37 – 60 weeks458Band E: 60+ weeks181Total1,586The service has been subject to industrial action since May 2021 and this, together with increased levels of need, have impacted on the service’s waiting list.  Cases on the waiting list are subject to active ongoing triage and reprioritisation by Senior Education Welfare Officers.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the voluntary financial contribution recommended to parents by each post-primary school.
answer_text: Under current legislation, voluntary contributions are a matter for Boards of Governors.  For this reason, neither my department nor the Education Authority collect data on the requested voluntary contributions recommended to parents by schools.  While current legislation permits Boards of Governors to seek voluntary contributions from parents /guardians, any request must make it clear that:  there is no obligation to contribute; and registered pupils at the school will not be treated differently according to whether or not their parents/guardians have made a contribution. Further information on the fees and charges that can and cannot be levied by boards of governors in respect of registered pupils at grant-aided schools and requests for voluntary contributions, is detailed in Chapter 19 of my department’s Governor Guide.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will work with the Minister of Education to fund ongoing delivery of the Scoil Spreagtha project.
answer_text: Funding for Scoil Spreagtha is an operational matter for Foras na Gaeilge, who have legislative responsibility for the promotion of the Irish language in this jurisdiction. You should, therefore, direct your question to Foras na Gaeilge at ceisteanna@forasnagaeilge.ie .

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what consideration has been given to road safety measures during the completion of the widening scheme of the Comber Greenway, given that the closure of the greenway will result in additional cyclists using roads.
answer_text: The Department considered road closures, diversion routes and other road safety measures during the completion of the widening scheme on the Comber Greenway.  However, as the Comber Greenway is a traffic free active travel route with limited interaction with motorised vehicles, it was not possible to provide an alternative route of similar nature and no viable road safety measures could be introduced on the parallel main roads that would replicate the Comber Greenway.  In preparation for the construction phase of these works, and conscious of the impact on those who normally use the Greenway, consultation was undertaken with key stakeholders. Works were subsequently programmed during the winter months to minimise disruption to the public, however the department would recommend the use of public transport for essential journeys until the works are completed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will provide nursery principals with funding to cover principal release time, in line with principals in primary schools.
answer_text: While nursery schools do not currently receive a specific principal release time allocation, these schools do receive additional funding which can be used to facilitate release time.  Nursery schools currently receive a higher 'per pupil' funding rate as a result of the higher Age Weighted Pupil Unit (AWPU) weighting factor of 1.452 as compared to 1.08 for a primary school pupil.  The additional funding provides flexibility for nursery principals to use as required, including engaging substitute teaching cover to release time for the principal to carry out administrative duties.  The issue of nursery principal release time has been raised through the Review of Workload Impact on School Leaders and DE officials are engaging with the teachers’ unions regarding this matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will work with the Minister for Communities to fund ongoing delivery of the Scoil Spreagtha project.
answer_text: With regards to the future funding of any project, you will be aware that departments have not yet been advised of budget allocations for 2024-25.  While the Department of Education has been advised of an indicative baseline for planning purposes, detailed budgetary planning and prioritisation has yet to be undertaken.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what action he is taking to ensure a full return to pre-pandemic levels of day care and respite services for service users in the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust.
To ask the Minister of Education whether he will reinstate funding for the school Sports Programme.
answer_text: My Department funded the Curriculum Schools Sports Programme from 2007 to 2023, investing over £13m over that period.  As a result of the challenging budget position in 2023-24, funding for the Sports Programme ceased on 31 May 2023.With regards to future funding, you will be aware that departments have not yet been advised of budget allocations for 2024-25.  While the Department of Education has been advised of an indicative baseline for planning purposes, detailed budgetary planning and prioritisation has yet to be undertaken.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the development of a cancer research strategy.
answer_text: Work is progressing on the development of the cancer research strategy.  My officials are liaising with key stakeholders in its development, and I hope to publish by the Summer of 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, further to his Department's Corporate Plan 2023-28, for an update on the review of his Department's organisational structure.
answer_text: The internal review of my Department’s organisational structure is currently ongoing.  My officials are considering Departmental requirements in the context of key priorities and the Department of Education Every CHILD Corporate Plan and hope to conclude the review in the next number of months.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what action his Department is taking to improve access to NHS dental appointments for (i) adults; and (ii) children.
answer_text: A key part of the access issues facing patients – both adults and children – are the rates being paid to our dentists for treatment.  The absence of Executive Ministers has meant that it has not been possible to progress the recommended pay uplifts for staffing groups for 2023/24.  This is now my immediate priority. Officials have already been making arrangements in anticipation of funding being made available to ensure that uplifts can be implemented as quickly as possible.An additional £95m has been invested in General Dental Services via the Financial Support Scheme and Rebuilding Support Scheme since April 2020. This came to an end in July 2023 due to the severe financial pressures arising from the Department’s 2023/24 budget settlement.In addition, a pilot scheme is currently running to provide appointments to unregistered patients who require emergency dental treatment during the working week. If a patient is not registered with a dentist, requires emergency dental treatment, and has been unable to access an appointment, they can contact their local Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) office for advice.I am also conscious of the progress that has been made in the rest of the UK on dental reform and I will be discussing these issues with officials as soon as possible, to ensure that the sector here in NI is supported in delivering important care to patients. More immediately, officials have been developing investment proposals to further support General Dental Services and I will consider them in early course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how he is ensuring that policies to address (i) lockdown learning; and (ii) the emotional impact of the pandemic on children and young people are implemented.
answer_text: (i) In an effort to address the impact of the Covid-19 pandemic, the Engage Programme was launched in September 2020 using Covid-19 funding provided by the Department of Finance. The programme continued in 2021/22 and 2022/23 and over £57m in funding was allocated to educational settings across the three years of the programme. The Engage programme aimed to limit any long-term adverse impact of the COVID-19 pandemic and lockdown learning by supporting pupils’ learning and engagement through provision of high quality one-to-one, small group or team teaching support in every school/setting in Northern Ireland.  Due to budget pressures, the Engage programme came to an end on 31 March 2023.The Programme provided flexibility and autonomy to allow settings to assess the diverse needs of their individual pupils in light of the pandemic and provide bespoke interventions to best meet those needs in their unique setting environment. One of the key strengths of the programme was that it was shaped by those delivering it on the ground, which allowed for the delivery of a range of approaches, for example, to increase the confidence and motivation of pupils.(ii) There is no specific DE policy on the emotional impact of the pandemic on children and young people.  In relation to the wider policy to support the emotional health and wellbeing of children and young people in school, the Department of Education and Department of Health jointly published the Children and Young People’s Emotional Health and Wellbeing in Education Framework in February 2021.  The Framework recognised that the pandemic brought an added dimension to the challenges in supporting emotional health and wellbeing of children and young people. Previous DE and DoH Ministers agreed to annual investment of £5m and £1.5m respectively.  The main emphasis of the Framework is to support schools to promote emotional health and wellbeing at a universal level, through a holistic, multi-disciplinary approach, and providing early and enhanced support.  There are a number of projects currently in operation under the Framework, both education and health led, which aim to support children and young people’s emotional health and wellbeing including the impact of the pandemic.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how he will ensure that all (i) schools; and (ii) youth services deliver anti-discriminatory practices, such as opportunities to celebrate cultural and linguistic diversity, different genders, sexualities and abilities, as recommended in the Children’s Law Centre Report to the UN Committee on the Rights of the Child.
answer_text: In schools, the curriculum plays a role in shaping children’s and young people’s dispositions and provides the opportunity to explore diversity and inclusion.  The Addressing Bullying in Schools Northern Ireland Act 2016, which came into effect in 2021, requires schools to record incidents or alleged incidents of bullying type behaviour, their motivation and their outcome.  The Youth Service has a long history of actively promoting anti-discriminatory practices within youth centres and through a range of focused programmes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the creation of a region-wide children and families arm's-length body, as per recommendations within the Independent Review of Children’s Social Care Services.
answer_text: This is one of 53 recommendations made by the Independent Review of Children’s Social Care Services. Public consultation on 49 of 53 recommendations, including recommendations relating to a Children and Families Arms-length Body (ALB), closed on 1 December 2023. Work to analyse the responses to consultation is underway. It is hoped that work will be complete by mid-late March 2024.  The analysis will inform my assessment of this and other Review recommendations. Decision-making relating to the creation of a new ALB would be a matter for the Northern Ireland Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department will fund and proceed with the Portadown Integrated Primary School capital build project, pending a successful outcome of the planning application.
answer_text: The project for Portadown Integrated Primary School is in the latter stages of design with the planning application being submitted in late 2023.  The project will proceed to construction when affordable within the wider education budget context.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what action he is taking to address the workforce crisis in social care.
answer_text: The Department of Health has established the Adult Social Care Collaborative Forum and the Children’s Strategic Reform Board both of which seek to deliver reform and transformation in Health and Social Care.   Within both of these Forums, there are workstreams focused on supporting, developing and improving the social care workforce.These workstreams are implementing a range of measures that include:Publishing a Social Care Workforce Strategy.  This will set out a vision to develop a sustainable social care workforce over the next 10 years.  It will support the development of the social care workforce and build pride and professionalism across the social care system in all sectors.  The strategy will include actions to develop career pathways, support the training and education of the workforce, and to raise the profile and recognition of the social care workforce as a skilled and competent workforce.  It is anticipated that the Strategy will be published in late 2024. In partnership with the Northern Ireland Social Care Council, work is ongoing to develop the training, career and qualification pathways for the social care workforce.In partnership with all stakeholders’ social care campaigns are ongoing to raise the profile and recognise the significant contribution that the social care workforce provides.In addition to the above, the Social Care Fair Work Forum has also been established which will seek to embed Fair Work initiatives and improve the terms and conditions for everyone employed across all parts of the social care sector.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will consider introducing subject-specific Continuing Professional Development for primary school teachers.
answer_text: Professional development for the whole teaching workforce in Northern Ireland is progressed through my Department’s strategy for Teacher Professional Learning (TPL), “Learning Leaders”. Our TPL delivery partner, the Education Authority (EA), provides a range of Professional Learning Programmes (aligned to the Learning Leaders Strategy). The EA works with partnerships of primary schools to identify common areas of need and supports primary schools availing of professional learning opportunities within the existing resource constraints.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what consideration has been given to making compulsory modules on mental health and emotional support part of the Bachelor of Education programme and the Postgraduate Certificate in Education programme.
answer_text: Initial Teacher Education (ITE) providers in Northern Ireland already include mental health and emotional support in a number of their ITE modules. The ITE providers review their programmes every year to take into account any significant changes to the educational landscape, including the Children and Young People’s Emotional Health and Wellbeing in Education Framework. This ensures student teachers continue to receive appropriate development in this important area.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how he will ensure that primary school teachers have access to curriculum support in the teaching of science, as part of The World Around Us.
answer_text: The Education (Curriculum Minimum Content) Order (Northern Ireland) 2007 sets out the curriculum as high-level areas of learning.  In primary schools, at foundation stage and key stages 1 and 2, ‘science and technology’ is already a compulsory contributory element within the World Around Us area of learning. Pupils must be provided with opportunities to develop an understanding of interdependence, place, movement and energy, and change over time. The Council for the Curriculum, Examinations and Assessment (CCEA) provides curriculum support and teaching materials for use in schools on all areas of the statutory curriculum, including science and technology.  CCEA’s website hosts a range of useful and relevant resources around science and technology across all primary key stages. It is, however, a matter for individual schools to determine which resources they wish to use to support their delivery of the curriculum.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will consider reinstating science as a statutory core subject in the primary curriculum.
answer_text: The Education (Curriculum Minimum Content) Order (Northern Ireland) 2007 sets out the curriculum as high-level areas of learning.  In primary schools, at foundation stage and key stages 1 and 2, ‘science and technology’ is already a compulsory contributory element within the World Around Us area of learning. Pupils must be provided with opportunities to develop an understanding of interdependence, place, movement and energy, and change over time. The Council for the Curriculum, Examinations and Assessment (CCEA) provides curriculum support and teaching materials for use in schools on all areas of the statutory curriculum, including science and technology.  CCEA’s website hosts a range of useful and relevant resources around science and technology across all primary key stages. It is, however, a matter for individual schools to determine which resources they wish to use to support their delivery of the curriculum.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the schools that are in contract for capital works funded by Fresh Start; (ii) the schools that are in procurement for capital works funded by Fresh Start; and (iii) whether any of these projects will not proceed as a result of Fresh Start monies being reprofiled for the UK government's financial package.
answer_text: The Fresh Start projects in contract for capital works are:Ulidia Integrated College;New-Bridge Integrated College;Shimna Integrated College;Phoenix Integrated Primary School;Mill Strand Integrated Primary School;Parkhall Integrated College;Rowandale Integrated Primary School;Ballycastle Shared Education Campus; andStrangford Integrated College.Fresh Start funding remaining available from HM Treasury is now only sufficient for those schemes already in construction contract.Funding is in place for all Fresh Start projects in construction contract to be completed. I have submitted proposals to ask the Executive to recommit £150 million of funding to support the construction of the flagship Strule Shared Education Campus, which is currently in procurement.  Without this additional funding, the project cannot proceed. In light of my statutory duties to integrated and shared education, I have also placed the 10 remaining former Fresh Start projects that are not yet in contract into my Department’s conventional major works programme to continue to advance in planning. I have also written to the Secretary of State to set out the strategic importance of these projects and to seek the additional capital funding necessary to progress their construction given previous commitments made by the Government. I have particularly highlighted the position of Millennium Integrated Primary School, which is in procurement.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the publication of a 10-year Capital Investment Strategy.
answer_text: I have asked my officials to take forward work on a Capital Investment Strategy for education as a matter of priority.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on his Department’s plans to develop a participation network that will allow children and young people to engage with and co-design Department of Education policy.
answer_text: My officials are working in partnership with other departments and a range of key stakeholders, to develop proposals which can better support and facilitate the participation of children and young people in government decisions which affect them.  The work to date, which has involved input from children and young people has focused on developing participation principles to inform the engagement process.  The development of a cross-departmental participation network will be considered in the next phase.  DE teams already routinely seek input from children and young people on policy development.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the implementation of the Children and Young People’s Emotional Health and Wellbeing in Education Framework.
answer_text: Following guidance under the Gigabit Infrastructure Subsidy Scheme, my Department, in conjunction with Building Digital UK (BDUK), an executive agency of the Department for Science, Innovation and Technology, carried out an Open Market Review to establish existing and planned coverage of broadband services locally, seeking responses from existing and prospective broadband infrastructure providers. My Department and BDUK also carried out a Public Review to validate the results of the Open Market Review. An outcome report of the Public Review was published in February 2024. My Department has also been engaging with BDUK on the development of documentation for an anticipated procurement under Project Gigabit, and has begun Pre-Procurement Market Engagement. My officials continue to develop a business case and BDUK has confirmed that it will make available any necessary capital funding for Project Gigabit here, subject to the approval of a final business case, State aid / subsidy control assurance and value for money considerations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the use of restorative practice in schools, as set out in the A Fair Start report.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) uses Restorative Practice officers to support schools to access whole school training, enabling them to introduce restorative practice and create a cohesive and supportive school environment for all. More than 600 young people over the last two academic years have engaged with “restorative circles” to address wider classroom relationships. In the current academic year, more than 150 young people have completed a restorative practice peer mentoring program and the EA is currently seeking to have this program accredited with the Open College Network Northern Ireland. EA has been awarded Registered Training Provider status with the Restorative Justice Council (RJC).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the toolkit for educating and engaging boys.
answer_text: In delivering against “A Fair Start” Key Area 5(i), the framework for a “Maximising Potential Hub” has been developed. This will be an interactive framework that will be continuously populated. The Hub features regional and international research around identifying and removing those factors which impact upon pupil engagement and success in school; it holds a range of associated self-evaluative tools and professional learning.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what steps he is taking alongside other Departments to improve the provision of mental health services.
answer_text: A Cross-Departmental Working Group is in place to contribute to the implementation of the Mental Health Strategy 2021-31 to improve the mental health of people across Northern Ireland.  Its purpose is to identify opportunities for greater cross-government and cross-sectoral working to inform Mental Health Strategy implementation. Part of the remit of the Working Group is to identify existing areas of joined up working and encourage greater collaboration so that services are joined up at local level and to ensure that individuals and communities receive the seamless integrated support they need.The Working Group last met in November 2023 where a number of areas were examined relating to work ongoing in other Departments which could support delivery of the Mental Health Strategy, particularly around Early Intervention and Prevention.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how his Department will ensure that the relationship between the statutory funders of services and the voluntary and community sector becomes more of a partnership rather than a purchasing relationship, as per recommendations within the Independent Review of Children’s Social Care Services.
answer_text: While my Department is currently in the process of analysing all of the recommendations within the Independent Review, I recognise the vital contribution made by the voluntary and community sector (VCS) and the need for us to work in partnership together to address the health and social care needs of the population of Northern Ireland.My officials have held initial discussions with the Re-imagine Children’s Collective with a view to agreeing how the relationship between the HSC and the VCS can be improved. A further meeting is planned on 11 March, where the focus of the discussion will be on working in partnership with the sector as recommended by Professor Ray Jones.The Integrated Care System (ICS) Programme model has also been co-produced with a range of stakeholders including significant input from the Voluntary and Community sector. The programme continues to be supported by a Voluntary and Community Sector Workstream representing the views of the sector in the programme’s ongoing work. The sector forms an integral part of the ICS, being represented at both regional level and through local Area Integrated Partnership Boards.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the further review of the longer-term aspects of recommendations contained within the fundamental review of GCSEs and A levels, which was presented to the Minister of Education in June 2013.
answer_text: Further work on developing a longer-term vision for qualifications in Northern Ireland had reached an advanced stage with a further report by the expert group chaired by Sir Norman Apsley, and additional detailed work carried out by CCEA. However, this work had to be paused in early 2020, before being presented to the then Minister, due to the need to respond to the COVID pandemic.Officials, in conjunction with CCEA, are currently revisiting this work and updating it to take account of developments in the intervening years before making recommendations about how best to proceed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the work of the Religious Education Advisory Committee.
answer_text: The Religious Education Advisory Committee was established in 2006 to develop guidance and materials for schools to support the introduction of the Religious Education Core Syllabus.  By 2019 it was considered that the work of the Committee had been completed and it was stood down.In 2022, the Committee was reconvened.  Between October 2022 and March 2023, the reconvened Committee, chaired by the Council for the Curriculum, Examinations and Assessment (CCEA), met on four occasions to develop a draft Terms of Reference and discuss the membership, remit and scope of the group.  This includes discussion around to what extent CCEA has the authority to extend the membership or present proposals to do so. At present the Committee is not meeting pending the outcome of the Appeal in Judicial Review Case 87 (A minor) which was heard on 25 and 26 October 2023.I am, however, advised that individual members or groups within the Committee are taking forward some work around the role of Religious Education in the curriculum.  Specifically, they are considering an agreed definition of what Religious Education is and assessing its contribution to the curriculum including to cross-curricular skills and thinking skills and personal capabilities.The work of the Committee will recommence once the outcome of the Appeal is known.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the implementation of recommendations outlined in the Landscape Review of Education Authority.
answer_text: The Landscape Review highlighted the need for the EA to become more child and pupil centric in its approach and to review, test and refresh all its front-line services to ensure they are appropriately resourced and skilled and that the processes and systems they use allow them to interact with stakeholders in real time and provide an improved quality of service and information.My Department has been working with the Education Authority on the issues raised and have jointly developed an Action Plan to address the recommendations highlighted within the Report. Workstreams have been established to take forward the recommendations and I am keen to see progress at pace. Work includes end-to-end reviews of Special Education Needs (SEN), school improvement and HR support services; revision of service delivery models; improvements to customer engagement; progression of people and culture work; and improvements to governance, data and partnerships.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the review of the Healthy Child, Healthy Future Scheme.
answer_text: A review of the Healthy Child, Healthy Future Policy is ongoing. Completion of this review is expected before the summer.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how he will ensure that decisions which impact young people are communicated to young people in an appropriate manner.
answer_text: DE teams routinely engage with children and young people to assess the impact their policies may have and communicate decisions.  Work is ongoing to consider how the participation of children and young people in the decision making of all Departments can be improved.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education when he will publish the 14-19 Framework Action Plan.
answer_text: My Department is jointly responsible for delivery of the 14-19 Education and Training Framework along with the Department for the Economy. The Framework, including 34 strategic actions was published in 2022, however, the underpinning draft action plan was held as an internal document for officials to aid implementation. I will discuss next steps on implementation of the Framework with Conor Murphy, Minister for the Department of the Economy in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what action he is taking to ensure as many schools as possible are taking part in The Daily Mile initiative.
answer_text: Physical Education is an Area of Learning in the curriculum. It focuses on children’s physical development, health and wellbeing. The Department of Education recommends that schools should provide pupils with at least two hours of curricular Physical Education each week.In respect of the curriculum content that is taught in schools in Northern Ireland and the resources available to support teaching and learning, the Department of Education delegates as much funding and decision-making as possible to schools. Subsequently, it does not normally endorse or recommend any particular resource or programme for use by schools. It is entirely a matter for individual schools to determine which resources or programmes they wish to use to support their delivery of the curriculum.The Education Authority is a member of the Daily Mile Network Northern Ireland and has supported the development of the Daily Mile using a number of mechanisms.  This has included the facilitation of a webinar entitled the “Daily Mile Makes You Smile” which gave School Principals an opportunity to better understand the benefits of the Daily Mile based on the experience of participating schools. In addition, EA continues to support and share publicity in relation to local Daily Mile Network activities, for example the Santa Run.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister what measures the Executive Office has put in place to ensure that young people are actively consulted on the creation of policy which will impact them.
answer_text: Our officials follow the relevant advice from the Equality Commission and that contained within the Department’s Equality Scheme when preparing to undertake any policy consultation. Where policies are likely to have a particular impact on children and young people, policy makers are also asked to consider whether a Child Rights Impact Assessment (CRIA) is appropriate.  The CRIA aims to embed children’s rights considerations at the outset of developing legislation, strategies and policies. TEO conducted a CRIA on the proposals for the allocation of its 2023/24 budget and included this in the public consultation.A recent example of the Department’s direct engagement with children and young people would be the Race Relations Order Review consultation which was launched on 27 March 2023. A number of events were held and social media was used to raise awareness. The consultation ran for 12 weeks and formally ended on 18 June 2023. To obtain views from children and young people the Department engaged the Education Authority to conduct eight separate consultation events on our behalf.  These events were divided into two groups and included four schools and four youth groups from a disparate geography. Officials are in receipt of the report covering the events, comments from which will be incorporated into the final consultation report. The formal public consultation also included an easy read version and a specific children and young people’s questionnaire for responses. Similarly, TEO officials are seeking the participation of children and young people in the development of the Ending Violence Against Women and Girls draft Strategic Framework. A series of youth panels have been established and includes a mix of genders, ages and Section 75 representation. Developed in partnership with the Education Authority, the youth panels input has informed the development of the draft Framework. The youth panels also contributed to an easy read version of our public consultation and advised on terminology used to increase accessibility.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on his Department’s plans to progress programmes in partnership with the voluntary, community and youth sector in support of the Tackling Paramilitarism and Organised Crime Programme.
answer_text: The Executive Programme on Paramilitarism and Organised Crime (EPOCC) delivers over one hundred projects that help keep people safe, reduce the risk of exploitation and over time build community resilience to criminality and paramilitarism. The programme includes investment in youth interventions, led by the Department of Education (DE) and Education Authority (EA), that support vulnerable young people who are at risk of paramilitary influence, coercion and violence in targeted geographical locations across Northern Ireland. The interventions are delivered in collaboration with other government departments, statutory agencies and voluntary and community sector partners.The WRAP Programme is a wrap-around educational support programme for children and young people facing significant challenges. WRAP is a programme of flexible interventions to deliver against both DE and EPOCC objectives and was designed and implemented in partnership with delivery agents in four areas. These are Greater Shantallow Area Partnership, Derry / Londonderry; Eastside Learning, East Belfast; Forward South Partnership, South Belfast; and THRiVE, Rathcoole/Newtownabbey. The EPOCC is evidence-based, data-driven and outcomes focused that draws on best international and local practice. It is also a long-term endeavour that requires action across the Executive.A proposal to continue youth interventions in 2024/25, including continued delivery in collaboration with community and voluntary youth sector providers, has been submitted and is pending confirmation of budgets. A Phase 3 rollout of the EPOCC from 2025/26 is not yet confirmed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the creation of a care pathway for those living with long Covid.
answer_text: The following services to support and assist people who are living with this condition have been established in all 5 HSC Trusts.Strand 1 - Post-COVID-19 syndrome (PCS) patients referred by primary or secondary care to a one-stop-shop MDT assessment servicePatients can be referred to the MDT assessment clinic from primary and secondary care.  Patients receive an holistic assessment to include:identifying patients with breathlessness who may benefit from pulmonary rehabilitation; and, those who may need onward referral to secondary or community services and/or signposting to self-management resources.Strand 2: - Bespoke pulmonary rehabilitation / dysfunctional breathing service for patients with significant respiratory symptoms post-COVID-19The purpose of this strand is to put in place timely, effective arrangements for outpatient rehabilitation following COVID-19 resulting in significant respiratory symptoms.  Strand 3 - Patients discharged from critical care (both COVID-19 and non-COVID-19)The purpose of this strand is to put in place timely, effective  arrangements for the follow-up and assessment of people discharged from critical care (both COVID-19 and non-COVID-19), reflecting the known risk of long-term sequelae following a long stay in critical care.Strand 4 - Strengthening psychology support to all TrustsThe psychological impact of COVID-19 (depression, anxiety disorders, PTSD, traumatic bereavement, psychosis, risk to self and/or others) is recognised in extant national guidance.  Each Trust has been supported to recruit an additional senior psychologist, with their main role supporting strand 3, but also providing advice to staff working in strands 1 and 2.Strand 5 - Regional MDT clinical physiology, SLT and dietetic support Selected patients may need diagnostic tests by respiratory clinical physiologists, onward referral for dietetic and speech and language therapy (SLT) from the one-stop-shop MDT assessment service, the critical care follow-up and pulmonary rehabilitation strands.  Strand 6 - Signposting and access to self-management resourcesPatients in NI can access self-management resources which include the NHS App Your Covid Recovery (Managing The Effects | Your COVID Recovery)

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on funding plans for Nurture Groups in schools.
answer_text: The Department’s budget allocation for 2024/25 is still to be confirmed and I am therefore not in a position to provide an update on the Department’s funding allocation for Nurture provision as a whole, or for Nurture Groups specifically, in the upcoming financial year.Officials have commenced work to develop options for the Department’s future, longer-term approach to supporting Nurture provision, with a view to establishing a sustainable model that will ensure we are effectively targeting and supporting those children who are most in need of this type of intervention.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the roll-out of the Tackling Educational Disadvantage programme.
answer_text: Work is ongoing within the Department on the design of a new programme focussed on a whole community approach to education.   This has been supported by input from a Stakeholder Reference Group consisting of representatives from schools and the voluntary and community sector.The programme will deliver a key recommendation of the A Fair Start Report with place-based partnerships focusing on communities and families in the most disadvantaged areas.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the creation of a care pathway for those living with facial palsy.
answer_text: A Facial Palsy Regional Planning Group was established in September 2023.  The group has agreed an action plan which will lead to putting in place local and regional pathways. A draft pathway has been developed for local services and is currently with group members for feedback.  A business case will then be developed to secure funds to commission a regional pathway for those requiring highly specialised services.  This will be subject to securing the relevant funding within wider service pressures and priorities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the creation of a revised newcomer policy.
answer_text: The Department commenced a review of its Supporting Newcomer Pupils policy in October 2017 in response to the growing number and increasing diversity of newcomer pupils arriving in Northern Ireland.  In 2019, the Department held a public consultation on its review of the policy however, work was temporarily suspended in March 2020 due to the outbreak of the COVID-19 pandemic.  In 2022, work recommenced with policy proposals prepared for consideration of an incoming Minister.		Officials will now build on the previous consultation and revisit policy proposals to account for the current context, with a view to presenting proposals for my consideration.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what measures he is taking to promote CCEA’s Relationships and Sexuality Education Hubs to schools.
answer_text: The Council for Curriculum, Examinations and Assessment (CCEA) published a Relationships and Sexuality Framework for years 1 to 14 in April 2023; the Framework signposts to resources in the CCEA RSE Hub.  The CCEA Hub will be relaunched in early 2024 and teachers will be able to download guidance documents and resources that CCEA has developed. The new resources will provide minimum content guidance and the additional RSE resources will support teachers and offer schools flexibility in their approach to the wider delivery of RSE.  This approach is intended to enable all schools to tailor and deliver effective programmes in line with the ethos and RSE policy of the school and to ensure that the wide-ranging needs of our young people are met.The Department has advised schools of the RSE Hub recently through guidance that was issued on 1 January 2024.  The associated Education Authority’s Governor Training Module on  amendments to RSE in minimum content for Learning for Life and Work at key stages 3 and 4 also references to the Hub as a source of further information.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in relation to the Speech and Language Therapy Workforce Review 2019 – 2029, whether he will increase the number of commissioned undergraduate places for Speech and Language Therapy for 2024 to forty.
answer_text: The commissioning of pre-registration education and training by my Department is reviewed annually, taking into account a range of factors including staffing and vacancy levels, recommendations from workforce reviews, priorities, and affordability.  I remain committed to implementing the recommendations of the Speech and Language Therapy Workforce Review 2019 – 2029, which will require the increasing the number of undergraduate training places commissioned each year by 12 to reach the recommendation of 40.A budget for Northern Ireland Departments has not yet been set for 2024/25 and accordingly I am unable to make any commitment to expand commissioned places at this time.  I will continue to make the case for this essential investment in our future workforce. However, I expect that the 2024/25 budget will be extremely challenging.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the creation of a roadmap for an inclusive education system.
answer_text: Meeting the changing needs of our children and young people is a key priority for my department and one that requires us to refine a definition of inclusive education within the context of our education system.  The development of a roadmap for inclusion is now underway and a programme of engagement, with key educational stakeholders including political representatives, will take place over the coming months. This will inform the development of an Inclusive Education policy and an implementation plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on his Department’s plan to introduce a more agile and responsive Area Planning process.
answer_text: Area Planning is not a static process.  My Department continually works to make Area Planning agile and responsive to the needs of children and young people in education for example ‘Resetting’ is a process to allow those schools with consistent admission and enrolment numbers below their approved numbers to be reset to a more realistic level.  During exercises in 2020 and 2021, 5242 unneeded available places were removed from the system. My Department will consider running this exercise again in 2026/27 in advance of the next Strategic Area Plan. ‘Normalisation’ is a process for primary and post primary schools who have consistently exceeded their approved admission and enrolment numbers to have their approved numbers normalised without the need for a Development Proposal (DP). My Department is operating a pilot exercise to test this process and is currently finalising this to determine how it can be taken forward in area planning. The collective impact of these initiatives will give an effective assessment of the supply of and demand for school places in an area.Given the urgent need to address the increasing demand for Special Educational Needs provision, my Department has worked with the Education Authority, to develop a new process which facilitates the establishment, closure or change of Specialist Provision at (sustainable) mainstream schools in an agile, efficient and expedient manner without the requirement for a DP. Work is also ongoing to consider if this arrangement can be extended to special school provision.The ‘Additional Places’ exercise has aided the Schools Admission Process for the post primary transfer for Year 8s where there is not always sufficient number of places in some areas. An exercise for 2023-25 has provided 267 additional Year 8 places allocated to a number of schools to ensure the admissions processes could be concluded on time and the number of children still to be placed at the conclusion of the admission processes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he intends to commission a review of the Healthy Happy Minds project.
answer_text: The Healthy Happy Minds Pilot concluded on 31 March 2023.  An evaluation of the pilot has been carried out and I will assess its findings as I seek to better support the emotional health and wellbeing of learners.  The Independent Counselling Service for Schools (ICSS) remains available to post-primary age pupils.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on his Department’s plans to legislate to create a professional body to support the teaching profession.
answer_text: Since then Education Minister, Michelle McIlveen MLA, announced in the Assembly on 13 December 2021 her decision to dissolve and replace the General Teaching Council for Northern Ireland (GTCNI), the Department has undertaken a public consultation on the form a replacement should take and the functions it should exercise.Subsequently, further targeted engagement with teachers was undertaken, alongside discussions with the other United Kingdom and Republic of Ireland teaching regulators and other key stakeholders.  These all helped to refine the Department’s thinking on an optimal GTCNI replacement; and on 5 February 2024, having previously shared its thinking with our teaching unions, the Department has engaged with all Northern Ireland’s teachers, on its thinking of the key elements to be addressed within a GTCNI replacement Bill.The Department has requested that teachers provide us with feedback on our proposals and, subject to the views received, the Department will continue to develop a GTCNI replacement Bill, and to seek its Assembly introduction at the earliest possible opportunity.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what work his Department has undertaken alongside the Department of Health to ensure that teachers are trained to support young people with early intervention mental health measures.
answer_text: As part of the joint Department of Education (DE) and Department of Health (DoH) Children and Young People’s Emotional Health and Wellbeing in Education Framework, work is underway to enhance awareness and knowledge of emotional health and wellbeing through access to training and provision of other resources.  The Emotional Wellbeing Teams in Schools Project (DoH-led) helps post primary schools link to local support services and better understand the Child and Adolescent Mental Health Services (CAMHS) referral process.   Furthermore, the Being Well Doing Well Programme led by the Education Authority (EA) will deliver a whole-school approach to emotional health and wellbeing across all schools.In addition, the Council for the Curriculum, Examinations and Assessment provides curricular guidance and teaching support materials for use in schools on its Wellbeing Hub.  This contains a range of age-appropriate, up-to-date and relevant resources to support the emotional health and wellbeing of pupils.Finally, there are a significant number of compulsory elements on mental health and wellbeing issues as part of Initial Teacher Education.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will consider increasing the number of counselling sessions available in schools.
answer_text: The Independent Counselling Service for Schools (ICSS) provides counselling services for all post primary aged pupils in mainstream schools, special schools and EOTAS provisions. The current ICSS contract ends on 31 August 2024.  The Education Authority (EA) is progressing the governance and procurement requirements for a new contract to commence on 1 September 2024.  This process will take account of the needs of children and young people.  Of course, any enhanced or additional provision is likely to require additional investment and will be subject to budget.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether the Bookstart Baby fund will be reinstated.
answer_text: Due to significant budgetary pressures in 2023/24, the Department took the difficult decision not to continue funding the Bookstart Baby Programme beyond March 2023.  Any decision to reinstate funding in 2024/25 will be made in the context of my Department’s 2024/25 allocation and wider financial pressures. I recognise the importance of encouraging parents to read with their children from birth.  I also acknowledge the positive impact the programme had in promoting an early love of reading, developing early communication skills and supporting attachment between children and their parents.   The importance of reading to young children continues to be promoted by my Department through the ‘Big Bedtime Read’ within the Getting Ready to Learn Programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he intends to publish a review and evaluation of the Engage Programme.
answer_text: The Education and Training Inspectorate (ETI) has evaluated both the Engage programme and the Engage II programme.  The two reports are published on the ETI website as outlined below.The Engage programme: An evaluation of the Department of Education’s Engage Programme in primary and post-primary schools during 2020-2021 (etini.gov.uk) Published December 2021.The Engage II programme: ETI publishes an evaluation of the Department of Education’s Engage II Programme in schools, pre-school settings and EOTAS centres during 2021-2022 | Education Training Inspectorate (etini.gov.uk) Published May 2023. I have no immediate plans for further evaluations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what support can be provided to Irish Medium teachers to ensure that Special Educational Needs assessment of Irish Medium Education pupils can take place.
answer_text: I have assumed that you are referring to statutory assessment of educational needs. Statutory assessment of all children and young people is conducted by the Educational Psychology service in the Education Authority (EA).  For children in Irish-medium education (IME), where possible the EA attempts to provide educational psychologists with experience in Irish language or bilingualism.The department is engaging with Comhairle na Gaelscolaíochta (CNaG) and the IME sector to ensure the specific needs of the sector are considered in the End to End (E2E) Review of Special Educational Needs (SEN).
