question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will develop a recruitment program to attract new Irish Medium teachers.
answer_text: I am aware of concerns about teacher supply in the Irish-medium sector.  In relation to Initial Teacher Education, my Department has protected the number of places available for student teachers training to teach in Irish-medium schools.  Through A Fair Start funding, work is ongoing with Comhairle na Gaelscolaíochta (CnaG) in the first instance - as the Departmental ALB funded to encourage and promote Irish-medium education – to consider what practical steps may support the recruitment and retention of teachers for Irish-medium schools.  My Department has also made funding available through A Fair Start to support CnaG in taking forward work to promote careers in Irish-medium education to post-primary pupils.Post-primary schools in all sectors are facing challenges for subject specialist teachers in particular areas of learning.  My Department is working alongside CnaG and the Irish-medium post-primary schools to support a pilot mentorship programme for non-subject specialists.  There is no single solution to teacher supply in any sector, but my Department will continue to work with the Irish-medium sector in identifying and considering solutions to the concerns raised.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will develop a career progression plan for Irish Medium teachers.
answer_text: Professional development for the whole teaching workforce in Northern Ireland is progressed through my Department’s strategy for Teacher Professional Learning (TPL), “Learning Leaders”. This strategy is for all teachers in all schools, including those in the Irish-Medium (IM) sector, with the vision that every teacher is a learning leader, accomplished in working collaboratively with all partners in the interests of children and young people. My Department will soon be consulting on proposals which have emerged from the implementation of the Strategy, specifically in relation to a TPL framework (providing a structured and informed approach to career-long TPL), a learning leadership lens and a new set of teacher competences.Progression for both student teachers and newly qualified teachers in Northern Ireland is detailed in the Department’s Teacher Education Partnership Handbook. Our TPL delivery partner, the Education Authority (EA), provides a range of Professional Learning Programmes (aligned to the Learning Leaders Strategy) that can be accessed by all educators, including those from the IM sector. The EA facilitates a bespoke IM Professional Learning Programme and is currently considering how this can be further enhanced to meet the needs of the sector, in conjunction with IM school leaders and educational partners. Leadership programmes, including Steps into Leadership and Pathways into Senior Leadership, are being reviewed and will include updates to support the requirements of leaders from the IM sector. A number of research-based initiatives are also being progressed under “A Fair Start” to support the linguistic development of IM practitioners. In addition, a mentoring scheme is being piloted for non-subject specialist IM teachers.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will develop a professional development program specifically for Irish Medium teachers.
answer_text: Professional development for the whole teaching workforce in Northern Ireland is progressed through my Department’s strategy for Teacher Professional Learning (TPL), “Learning Leaders”. This strategy is for all teachers in all schools, including those in the Irish-Medium (IM) sector, with the vision that every teacher is a learning leader, accomplished in working collaboratively with all partners in the interests of children and young people. My Department will soon be consulting on proposals which have emerged from the implementation of the Strategy, specifically in relation to a TPL framework (providing a structured and informed approach to career-long TPL), a learning leadership lens and a new set of teacher competences.Progression for both student teachers and newly qualified teachers in Northern Ireland is detailed in the Department’s Teacher Education Partnership Handbook. Our TPL delivery partner, the Education Authority (EA), provides a range of Professional Learning Programmes (aligned to the Learning Leaders Strategy) that can be accessed by all educators, including those from the IM sector. The EA facilitates a bespoke IM Professional Learning Programme and is currently considering how this can be further enhanced to meet the needs of the sector, in conjunction with IM school leaders and educational partners. Leadership programmes, including Steps into Leadership and Pathways into Senior Leadership, are being reviewed and will include updates to support the requirements of leaders from the IM sector. A number of research-based initiatives are also being progressed under “A Fair Start” to support the linguistic development of IM practitioners. In addition, a mentoring scheme is being piloted for non-subject specialist IM teachers.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the Together: Building a United Community strategy.
answer_text: There has been significant progress in delivering the Together: Building a United Community Strategy since its implementation, with a number of the headline actions fully achieving the targets set in the Strategy.Over 30,000 young people have taken part in 870 T:BUC Camps and five Urban Villages areas have been established. The target of building 10 shared neighbourhoods has been met. Furthermore, this programme has acted as the catalyst for DfC’s Housing for All programme and to date 76 shared housing schemes totalling over 2,500 shared homes have been delivered or are in development.Over 27,000 participants took part in the Uniting Communities programme of sporting events and young leader training. Over 6,000 young people participated in the United Youth programme. The first Shared Education Campus at Limavady is now operational, with work expected to commence soon on the Ballycastle Shared Education Campus.Approximately one third of interface barriers have been removed, with a further third reduced in stature. Work continues across other interface sites to secure the conditions needed for reduction or removal.Officials are currently conducting a review of the T:BUC Strategy and we recognise the ongoing importance of Good Relations in creating a place where everyone can prosper.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how he will ensure that Irish Medium Education settings have appropriate teaching and lesson resources.
answer_text: Through A Fair Start, my Department has funded an officer in Comhairle na Gaelscolaíochta (CnaG) to lead on work to provide focused support for the Irish-medium sector on a range of matters, including teaching and learning resources.  My Department also funds CCEA to provide teaching and learning resources across all sectors and, for Irish-medium education, it manages this through its Irish-medium Programme Board.  The Education Authority also publishes links to Irish-medium resources as they are developed via a dedicated hub.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the Integrated Education Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 Action Plan.
answer_text: In line with its duty under the Integrated Education Act 2022, the Department published an Integrated Education Action Plan on 26 April 2023 as a constituent part of the first strategy for Integrated Education. That action plan committed DE to fifteen specific actions in delivering implementation of the Act and forms the basis of the Department’s implementation plans.Since publishing the Action Plan, the Department has continued to engage with education partners alongside regular meetings with key stakeholders, to take forward the actions as set out.  Currently seven actions have been completed and eight are actively progressing through consultation with key stakeholders.A public consultation was held on the Action Plan, which commenced on 31 August 2023 and closed on 30 November 2023. A total of 176 responses were received to the online consultation. In addition, the Department hosted two online events to provide further opportunities to gather views from a range of interested parties.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what action he is taking to address the lack of Irish Medium teachers currently teaching in Irish Medium schools.
answer_text: I am aware of concerns about teacher supply in the Irish-medium sector.  In relation to Initial Teacher Education, my Department has protected the number of places available for student teachers training to teach in Irish-medium schools.  Through A Fair Start funding, work is ongoing with Comhairle na Gaelscolaíochta (CnaG) in the first instance - as the Departmental ALB funded to encourage and promote Irish-medium education – to consider what practical steps may support the recruitment and retention of teachers for Irish-medium schools.  My Department has also made funding available through A Fair Start to support CnaG in taking forward work to promote careers in Irish-medium education to post-primary pupils.Post-primary schools in all sectors are facing challenges for subject specialist teachers in particular areas of learning.  My Department is working alongside CnaG and the Irish-medium post-primary schools to support a pilot mentorship programme for non-subject specialists.  There is no single solution to teacher supply in any sector, but my Department will continue to work with the Irish-medium sector in identifying and considering solutions to the concerns raised.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the pilot programme of revised classroom assistant deployment.
answer_text: The Department of Education (DE) and the Education Authority (EA) embarked on an End to End Review (E2E) of Special Educational Needs (SEN) in early 2023 to examine the entire SEN system from a policy perspective to ensure delivery and outcomes are in line with policy intent. While there is currently no ‘pilot programme’ in place, how professional support is deployed within the classroom is currently being taken forward as part of the E2E.  This work will explore alternative models of providing professional support in the classroom with a view to developing a range of options to allow for more flexible, evidence-based, and well-planned supports for children with a statement of SEN.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the redevelopment of Newtownards library.
answer_text: Libraries NI continues to work in partnership with Ards and North Down Borough Council to progress plans for a replacement library facility in Newtownards.   A business case supporting the proposed redevelopment of the library on its present site is currently being considered by my Department.  I am informed that, in parallel, Ards and North Down Borough Council will develop their own proposals for the development of the remainder of the shared site. Any decision to proceed with a new library facility will be dependent on prioritising of capital investment in a very tight budgetary situation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on his Department’s review of Free School Meals and Uniform Grant.
answer_text: My Department is continuing to progress a review of the eligibility criteria for free school meals and uniform grants.  I will be considering the next steps for this review in the time ahead.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the rollout of integrated teams to provide support to SEN learners in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: As part of its Special Educational Needs and Disability (SEND) Transformation Programme, the Education Authority (EA) is proposing to adopt a new delivery model for its Special Educational Needs (SEN) Pupil Support Services involving the creation of five new Local Integrated Teams (LITs), with the aim of delivering better outcomes for children and young people with SEN.In accordance with the EA’s current plans, the new LITs are proposed to be in place by September 2024.  The EA is currently undertaking a programme of stakeholder engagement including with schools and parents.  The outcomes of this engagement will inform further development of the proposed delivery model.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department will provide a response to the recommendations outlined in the Independent Review of Education.
answer_text: The Department is currently considering the Report and carrying out internal consultation to inform a way forward. This process includes discussion with key education stakeholders including other relevant Departments and Arm’s-Length Bodies.  Following this consultation, officials will produce a paper for my consideration setting out advice on the various recommendations in the report.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education when his Department will publish the final Statutory Guidance on the Reduction and Management of Restrictive Practices in Educational Settings in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: My Department  engaged with a wide range of stakeholders to co-design draft “Statutory Guidance on the Reduction and Management of Restrictive Practices in Educational Settings in Northern Ireland”.  A public consultation on the draft guidance ran from 5th September to 10th November 2023. The responses to the consultation are currently being analysed. Subject to my approval, a consultation report will be published on the Department’s website and I will consider the next steps to be taken thereafter.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he intends to repeal Article 4 (1)(c) of the Education (NI) Order 1998.
answer_text: I plan to consider this matter further with my officials before determining the appropriate next steps.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how they will address the ongoing (i) teachers'; and, (ii) head teachers' pay dispute.
answer_text: Teachers’ pay is not unilaterally decided by the Department of Education (DE), as DE is not the employer of teachers; it is negotiated through the forum of the Teachers’ Negotiating Committee (TNC) which comprises the employers, sectoral bodies and DE. Engagement has been ongoing between Management Side and Teachers’ unions to address the pay concerns for all teachers and when the necessary funding is made available formal negotiations will commence. I hope that the conditions will be in place for this to happen soon.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how they will ensure that Specialist Provisions in Mainstream Schools receive sufficient capital spend to meet the needs of learners with special educational needs.
answer_text: As I outlined in my Statement to the Assembly on Monday 12 February, I intend to bring to the Executive a bid of circa £528 million of capital funding for education in 2024/25 and subsequent years.  This bid includes the additional accommodation requirements of Special Needs placements, including Specialist Provisions in Mainstream Schools.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on the non-teaching staff pay and grading review business case.
answer_text: ANSWER The business case is progressing through scrutiny processes. Given the implications that the proposals will have for recurrent costs going forward, it is right and proper that DoF carries out the scrutiny required to allow it to make an informed decision regarding whether to give approval. My officials are working at pace with their colleagues in the Department of Education to progress this scrutiny and decision-making.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how much capital funding is required to address essential maintenance backlogs across the school estate.
answer_text: The capital funding required to address the maintenance backlog across the School Estate is estimated at £450m.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what steps his Department is taking to ensure that all children with special educational needs requiring placements in (i) pre-school, (ii) Primary 1; and (iii) Year 8 for the academic year 24/25, will receive a placement in a setting appropriate to their needs before the end of the current academic year.
answer_text: Children on the Special Educational Needs (SEN) register but without a statement of SEN (63% of all children with SEN) are admitted to schools in line with the general schools admissions process and, therefore, should receive notification of their school placement at key transition points at the same time as their peers without SEN.  These timescales are published on my Department’s website (DE Circular 2023/12).  Children with a statement of SEN (37% of all children with SEN) follow a different process for admission to schools, which seeks to ensure that their school placement is appropriate to meet the needs set out in their statement and subject to any change at the annual review of their statement.  There is a continual flow of children being referred for SEN assessment, which creates challenges in aligning placement of all children with the general schools admissions process.  There has been a significant increase in recent years in the number of children with a statement of SEN and in those requiring specialist education provision – either in special schools or specialist education provision classes within our mainstream schools, which provide smaller classes with targeted support to ensure that children’s needs can be met.  Meeting this increased demand has been and remains challenging as our special schools are operating at full, and in many cases over, capacity and there is a rising need for more specialist provision in our mainstream schools. My Department is collaborating with the Education Authority (EA) to work towards ensuring that the majority of children with SEN receive notification of an appropriate school place at the same time as their peers.  The EA is working at pace to create additional capacity across the education system and to ensure that new specialist education provision is resourced with well qualified, capable and competent staff.  There is a significant programme of work ongoing to establish more places in special schools and to create more specialist provision in mainstream schools for the 2024/25 academic year.In order to create this much needed additional capacity within the system, the Department, working closely with the EA, has tested and agreed a new process to provide a more flexible and agile system to effect changes in the creation of specialist provision in mainstream school settings without the requirement for a Development Proposal (DP).  Work is also ongoing to consider if this arrangement can be extended to streamline the process for changes in relation to special school provision.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether High street dental practices will automatically avail of the Energy Bill Relief Scheme for businesses in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: This question was asked in the previous mandate and to the previous Minister for the Economy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what action he is taking to ensure that Long Covid patients in Northern Ireland can get tested for blood microclots, as is the case for patients in England.
answer_text: There is, as yet, no published peer-reviewed evidence that removal of microclots is an effective treatment for patients who have Long Covid.    Testing and treatment for microclots remains experimental.   If the evidence base were to change, and these are recommended by NICE as being effective, consideration would be given as to their availability in NI.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how he is ensuring that Long Covid patients in Northern Ireland have access to the same drugs and treatments as those in the rest of the UK.
answer_text: There are as yet no evidence-based drug regimes specifically to treat Long Covid.   Knowledge of Long Covid is still evolving.  The condition is complex with more than one body system affected.  Individuals can have unique combinations of fluctuating symptoms.   There is no specific diagnostic test for PCS and the published evidence base for interventions specifically aimed at a curative treatment is sparse at present.  High quality research is needed to inform future treatments and models of care.   Until then, the current service is focussed on holistic assessment, exclusion of other significant conditions, identifying patients with breathlessness who may benefit from pulmonary rehabilitation and those who need onward referral to secondary or community services.  This is complemented by signposting to self-management resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail how (i) Clopidogrel; and (ii) Rivaroxaban are currently being used to treat Long Covid patients in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: There are as yet no evidence-based drug regimes specifically to treat Long Covid.   Knowledge of Long Covid is still evolving.  The condition is complex with more than one body system affected.  Individuals can have unique combinations of fluctuating symptoms which provides challenges when diagnosing and developing treatments for this condition.Scientists still know very little about the underlying mechanisms of long COVID despite widespread reports about it since near the start of the pandemic. COVID-19 affects the lining of the blood vessels, and I understand scientists are currently investigating whether Long Covid symptoms could have links to this and if antiplatelet therapy and anticoagulants would be viable options in the treating of individuals who may have Long Covid.However, the published evidence base for interventions specifically aimed at a curative treatment are sparse at present and further high quality research is required in order to inform future treatments and models of care for those who may have Long Covid.  Until then, the current service is focussed on holistic assessment, exclusion of other significant conditions, identifying patients with breathlessness who may benefit from pulmonary rehabilitation and those who need onward referral to secondary or community services.  This is complemented by signposting to self-management resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline the average (i) percentage increase; and (ii) total increase in rents for social homes, owned by each of the twenty housing associations in Northern Ireland, for each year between 2012 and 2022.
answer_text: I would refer the Member to AQW 5498/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) for an update on the Development Proposal for Mid Down Integrated College; (ii) when her Department will make a decision on the Development Proposal; and (iii) why the Development Proposal is overdue.
answer_text: Development Proposals (DP) 668 - Blackwater Integrated College (IC) will discontinue with effect from 31 August 2023 – and 667 - Establish a new non-selective, 11-18, co-educational grant maintained integrated post primary school in the Mid Down area with effect from 1 September 2023, or as soon as possible thereafter.  The school will have an enrolment number of 600 pupils and an admission number of 100 pupils – linked proposals published on 20 May 2021.  The statutory objection period for both proposals ended on 16 September 2021.The proposals have attracted significant representation and correspondence during the objection period, which my officials are currently assessing alongside all other pertinent information and evidence to provide me with a submission to assist me in taking my decision. The assessment of any DP is a complex process and requires careful consideration of all evidence to ensure decisions are taken in the best interests of all learners.  Given the extent of representation in respect of these proposals and the need to ensure that all views that have been expressed are considered and assessed, I do not consider that a decision is overdue.  I do, however, appreciate the need for certainty in the interests of children, parents and staff

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health where can members of the public who notice any potential signs or symptoms of oral cancers go to be checked out if they are not registered with a health service dentist, and cannot get registered with a health service dentist due to the current access problems.
answer_text: Oral cancer can affect anyone and members of the public are encouraged to check for unusual changes in their mouths. Any person who is concerned about changes in their mouth is advised to contact a dental practitioner and explain their concern, along with any known risk factors including smoking or drinking alcohol. I am aware that lower levels of dental activity combined with a large backlog of patients are resulting in a continuing reduced capacity in general dental practices. To ensure that this more limited capacity is targeted appropriately, dentists are asked to prioritise patients requiring emergency or urgent care. This includes any patient concerned about oral cancer signs and symptoms.  My Department has increased the availability of appointments for non-registered patients who have urgent and emergency treatments needs with a pathfinder scheme underway for unregistered patients, including asylum seekers, who have an urgent need.In addition, Emergency Dental Clinics have capacity to provide emergency dental care at weekends and public holidays.  Members of the public concerned about any suspect changes in their mouth can contact a central triage telephone number where their issue will be triaged remotely, and appropriate advice given.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice what assessment has been made of the impact a vacancy in the ministerial office post 28 October 2022 will have on departmental (i) decision making; and (ii) spending.
answer_text: The absence of a Minister will significantly hamper decision making to advance a wide range of priorities within the Department of Justice.  That includes both decisions that are specified in statute, such as exercising the Royal Prerogative of Mercy, as well as decisions that would ordinarily be taken by a Minister, such as public appointments.   I previously approved a draft primary legislative programme for this Assembly mandate which aims to improve the experiences of children, young people and victims drawn into the justice system; reform sentencing arrangements following a review of sentencing policy; tackle hate crime; and modernise the justice system.  While it would not be possible to progress that programme in the absence of an Assembly and Executive, the absence of a Minister will inhibit the pace of the necessary preparatory work including policy development.  Another key challenge facing the Department has been the absence of a confirmed budget.  The Department has been operating within a Contingency Planning Envelope (CPE) which is broadly equivalent to the proposed allocation in the draft Budget 2022-23.  The allocation within the CPE was not just difficult, but was damaging for the justice system with the Department facing significant pressures this financial year.  The absence of a Minister will significantly hamper decision-making on funding priorities for the delivery of public services with constrained resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what action he is taking to ensure that dental practices can continue to sustainably provide health service treatments, in light of 12 per cent dental inflation rates.
answer_text: I have considered and accepted the recommendations made by the Review Body on Doctors' and Dentists' Remuneration (DDRB) in their 50th report that would provide a 4.5% pay uplift to General Dental Practitioners for 2022/23.  However, unlike my colleagues in other parts of the United Kingdom, I am unable to announce the immediate implementation of these pay awards locally, as Northern Ireland still does not have an agreed Executive Budget for 2022/23.Whilst elements of the pay uplift are outwith the control of the Department of Health, particularly this year, I will do everything I can to implement as soon as possible.Additional support is also being provided to the sector via the General Dental Services Rebuilding Support Scheme, which has paid an enhancement on all Items of Service undertaken in the first two quarters of 2022/23, and will continue with a 10% enhancement throughout Q3 and Q4.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 3196/22-27, (i) whether her officials are considering legislating for a price cap on school uniforms; and (ii) to outline the rationale for this position.
answer_text: In August I instructed officials to identify an approach that will seek to ensure, on a statutory basis, that schools are required to make their uniforms more affordable for families.  This process includes consideration of a maximum price on the cost of uniforms alongside a range of approaches that could be used to make uniforms more affordable.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 3594/22-27, what protections are in place for staff who are attending consideration of dismissal or inefficiency meetings and are on Occupational Health Service waiting lists for medical assessment.
answer_text: For staff referred to OHS for medical assessment, as part of the decision making process for ‘consideration of dismissal or inefficiency meetings’, decisions are not taken by NICSHR until the OHS assessment has been completed, except where consent from an individual has not been provided to allow an OHS assessment to be undertaken.  For those staff subject to inefficiency sickness absence procedures support is available from their line manager, Welfare Support Services and the Employee Assistance Programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health why more notice was not given to dental practices, informing them that the 25 per cent uplift on fees paid for health service treatments will be reduced to 10 per cent next month.
answer_text: The Rebuilding Support Scheme (RSS) was established specifically in response to the COVID-19 pandemic. With the impact of the pandemic significantly reducing over the past number of months and most restrictions now removed, the justification for ongoing COVID-specific support has also reduced. However, it is acknowledged that the impacts have not entirely receded with inefficiencies created by increased “Did Not Attends” and associated staff absences. It is for this reason that I committed to continuing support to the end of the financial year.Confirmation of the further extension of RSS to the end of 2022/23 was provided to General Dental Practitioners at the earliest opportunity, following a review of the ongoing impact of the pandemic and confirmation of the availability of the additional funding required.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 3402/22-27, (i) what proportion of the homelessness budget is required to deliver long term prevention measures; and (ii) what aspects of the Ending Homelessness Together strategy are not being delivered as a result of the absence of an agreed budget.
answer_text: I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive of the Northern Ireland Housing Executive, who has advised me as follows:“(i) The Housing Executive’s initial budget bid of £48.8m for 2024/25 included £4.5m for strategic prevention initiatives, or 9.2% of the total. As noted in the Department’s EQIA Consultation, there is currently a shortfall of circa £14m in the homelessness budget and in such circumstances, programmes included in the strategic prevention initiatives are either funded at risk (e.g. funding of outside agencies) or not currently subject to funding (e.g. Homelessness Prevention Fund). (ii) The Homelessness Strategy 2022-27 is guided by annual action plans. The Year 2 Action Plan included 42 Actions, 5 of which were not completed. Each of these actions had been identified as subject to funding. The Year 2 Annual Progress Report will be published in the near future.The Year 3 Action Plan covering 2024/25 recognises the funding challenges associated with delivery of a number of actions. It includes 46 actions, with 7 of these actions subject to securing funding.”

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health why the 25 per cent uplift to the Rebuilding Support Scheme for dental practices is to be reduced to 10 per cent.
answer_text: The Rebuilding Support Scheme (RSS) was established specifically in response to the COVID-19 pandemic. With the impact of the pandemic significantly reducing over the past number of months and most restrictions now removed, the justification for ongoing COVID-specific support has also reduced. However, it is acknowledged that the impacts have not entirely receded with inefficiencies created by increased “Did Not Attends” and associated staff absences. It is for this reason that I committed to continuing support to the end of the financial year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the development of a new Oral Health Strategy for Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Prior to the Covid 19 pandemic my Department decided that the two sections of the Northern Ireland population that would benefit most from updated oral disease prevention programs where children and the older population. Unfortunately, the pandemic delayed the establishment of the two planned working groups, but significant progress has been made by two oral health groups - one dedicated to improving the oral health of children and the other specifically looking at improving the oral health of older people.The challenges facing the HSC and DoH today mean that, unfortunately, the significant resources required to develop a new wide-ranging OHS are not available at this time. However, the importance of analysing the oral health status of the population and recommending evidence-based approaches to health improvement is recognised. In addition, my Department has funded a dental health survey of five year old children this financial year, and a survey of twelve year old children is planned for 2022/23.  These surveys will provide key information for future planning of oral health improvement in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what action he is taking to ensure that everyone is able to access Health Service dental treatments.
answer_text: Access to routine dental care continues to be significantly affected by the COVID-19 pandemic, which has caused reduced activity and therefore reduced capacity in dental practices. In order to increase activity in General Dental Services, I introduced the Rebuilding Support Scheme (RSS), which rewards dentists based on treatments being provided.  The RSS has seen an increase in activity levels of around 16.5% over the first 5 months of 2022/23, and support will continue until the end of the financial year.Any General Dental Practitioner signed up to the RSS is required to treat patients based on clinical need, regardless of their registration status. This is to ensure that those patients most in need are able to obtain a dental appointment.In addition, any patient who has an urgent or emergency dental need, is able to make use of an Emergency Dental Clinic during weekends or Public Holidays. There the patient will be triaged remotely, and appropriate advice given or treatment arranged.To further help those in most need, on 3 October 2022, my Department commenced a scheme throughout Northern Ireland to provide access to unregistered patients who are experiencing an emergency or urgent dental condition.Support being provided to dental practices will remain under ongoing review.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether (i) an analysis has been carried out in relation to the impact of reduced access to health service dental treatment in the community on the number of emergency dental procedures being carried out; and (ii) the cost impact of any increase in emergency dental procedures been assessed.
answer_text: Whilst dental activity has been closely monitored on a weekly basis since the beginning of the pandemic, there has been no specific analysis undertaken regarding the impact on the number of emergency dental procedures being carried out.Any patient who has an urgent or emergency dental need is able to make use of an Emergency Dental Clinic during weekends or Public Holidays. There the patient will be triaged remotely, and appropriate advice given or treatment arranged.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how his Department promotes the Spring Social Prescribing Project to GP’s and other health professionals to encourage referrals.
answer_text: The Department and previously the Health & Social Care Board provided opportunities for the SPRING project co-ordinators to deliver presentations about their work to Integrated Care Partnerships (ICPs) across Northern Ireland in order to raise awareness and to encourage referrals.  My Department will continue to work with stakeholders including other Departments and local councils through the Regional Social Prescribing Development Board to support the development of social prescribing services in NI.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the funding for the SPRING Social Prescribing project for 2023 onwards.
answer_text: Through my Departments Tackling Rural Poverty and Social Isolation (TRPSI) Programme funding is in place to continue the Social Prescribing Rural Enhanced project until 30 June 2023. This commitment will bring my Department’s total contribution, since commencement of the rural Social Prescribing project in October 2019, to £888k.A Regional Social Prescribing Development Board, which includes DAERA representatives, has been established to take a co-design and co-production approach. This includes a wide range of stakeholders and government Departments and provides regional leadership and co-ordination of effort in relation to Social Prescribing. This work will continue in the months ahead and I welcome the further discussions planned with the intended outcome of identifying a sustainable solution for Social Prescribing going forward.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the creation of a new Disability Employment Strategy.
answer_text: The Department is within the final development stages of the next Disability and Work Strategy, which will set a long term commitment to increasing the rate of employment for people with disabilities.In developing the Strategy, the Department has embarked upon an extensive process of co-design of interventions with the disability sector, central and local government, employers and those with disabilities, supported by newly commissioned labour market research.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance how he will ensure that the Disability Employment sector is recognised as a priority for Shared Prosperity Funds, in the absence of European Social Funds.
answer_text: The SPF (Shared Prosperity Fund) is the British Government’s replacement for the European Social Fund (ESF). The SPF is being administered by Department of Levelling Up, Housing and Communities (DLUHC) and the British Government have, to date, not provided a suitable role for Executive Departments which respects our Devolved Status.I have raised issues and discussed this fund at length with British Ministers, most recently with the SoS Levelling Up Housing and Communities. I have urged the British Government to reconsider both the allocations to this fund and how it will operate here as it will deliver too little money, risks duplication of provision and it lacks alignment to our local priorities. I will continue to press this issue with the British Government and I remain committed to ensuring that we receive as much funding as possible and will not stand in the way of any funding flowing to the North. However, in the absence of a decision making role in this fund I have no way to ensure that the British Government will treat the Disability Employment Sector as a priority for their fund.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what support is her Department providing to the homelessness sector in Northern Ireland with regards to the rising cost of living.
answer_text: The cost-of-living crisis is having major impact, particularly on those who are already struggling financially. I am deeply frustrated that the continued absence of a functioning Executive and agreed Executive budget prevents additional measures being introduced.	My Department will do all it can to support those experiencing hardship, and all options are being explored to provide both immediate and medium to longer term interventions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on his Department’s efforts to secure funding for a pilot for dedicated social work services in a psychiatric hospital.
answer_text: The current Inter-Departmental Homelessness Action Plan includes an action for the Department of Health to seek funding for an evaluated pilot of a dedicated social work service in a psychiatric hospital to prevent homelessness on discharge.  The proposal was for a pilot of a service to take place in Holywell Hospital within the Northern Health and Social Care Trust that would provide relationship-based support to bridge the difficult transition from hospital to stable accommodation.  Unfortunately, due to budgeting constraints, funding has not been secured for this pilot.  However, this remains a project that I would very much like to see implemented and one we will seek to retain on the Inter-Departmental Homelessness Action Plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what engagement she has had with the Minister of Health with regards to appropriate mental health support for staff working within the homelessness sector.
answer_text: My Department has engaged with the Department of Health on this issue.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on his Department’s work to implement a flexible drugs and alcohol service as part of the Year 3 Action Plan, within the Interdepartmental Homelessness Action Plan.
answer_text: Protecting the health and wellbeing of homeless people in our society is a cross-government issue. The Complex Lives initiative, established by the Public Health Agency, Belfast City Council, and the NI Housing Executive is now up and running and providing support to those who are homeless and are at risk of related mental health and substance use issues in the Belfast area. With a Multi-Disciplinary Team in place, it is providing a whole system, accountable care approach to supporting people experiencing multiple disadvantages with the aim of helping people engage, stabilise, and find a new way forward with their lives.   The initiative involves all key stakeholders – from health services to the Housing Executive, justice agencies including the PSNI and the community and voluntary sector. While the pilot has so far been working with 15-20 of the most complex cases, I hope that additional investment will help ensure that more at-risk individuals can be supported by the scheme in the coming period. This is in addition to existing low threshold outreach services which are in place in all Health and Social Care Trust areas, and the related needle exchange and take-home naloxone programmes. The Strategic Planning Group, set up as part of the implementation of the new substance use strategy, is also currently co-designing a new whole-system substance use services delivery framework to address trends in increasing demand and complexity, current and future resource, gaps provision, service pressures, workforce issues and equity of access.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether she will undertake a benchmarking exercise for pay and working conditions across the homelessness sector, compared against those in other regions.
answer_text: Pay and conditions for employees working in the homelessness sector are the responsibility of individual employers.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to provide an update on the implementation of Preventing Harm, Empowering Recovery: A Strategic Framework to Tackle the Harm from Substance Use 2021-31.
answer_text: I launched Northern Ireland's 10-year Substance Use Strategy, “Preventing Harm, Empowering Recovery”, last year. The Strategy’s Vision is that people in Northern Ireland are supported in the prevention and reduction of harm and stigma related to the use of alcohol and other drugs, have access to high quality treatment and support services, and will be empowered to maintain recovery.Robust governance structures have now been put in place to oversee the delivery and implementation of the strategy. Chaired by Professor Sir Michael McBride, a cross-sectoral Substance Use Strategy Programme Board has been established to provide strategic oversight of progress against the outcomes and actions in the strategy. The Programme Board is underpinned by the HSC Substance Use Strategic Planning Group, which is co-chaired by the Public Health Agency and the Strategic Planning & Performance Group and is leading on the co-delivery of the strategy. The first task of this Strategic Planning Group is the development of a new whole-system delivery framework to address trends in increasing demand and complexity, current and future resource, gaps provision, service pressures, workforce issues and equity of access.At the same time, we have been taking forward a number of early actions to deliver of the key priorities of the strategy. A key initial focus has been on improving access to services. Work on the Rapid Review of Opioid Substitution Therapy in Prisons has been undertaken. A specification has also been developed for an Independent Review Tier 4 Inpatient Services, and this review is about to begin. In addition, a Population-Based Needs Assessment for Substance Use Services in the Western HSC Trust Area is also about to commence. A further early action in the new strategy was to hold a public consultation on the introduction of Minimum Unit Pricing for Alcohol in Northern Ireland. This consultation ran from February to May 2022 – 521 responses were received and are being analysed, and research has been commissioned to update the modelling of the impact of Minimum Unit Pricing in Northern Ireland.Finally, I recently allocated £500,000 to support the implementation of the strategy, however full delivery will be dependant on further resources becoming available.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what work her Department is undertaking to address staff shortages in the homelessness sector.
answer_text: The Housing Executive has advised that it funded the recently published Homeless Connect research on Staffing Challenges within the Homelessness Sector and welcome the findings.The Housing Executive will now examine the key aspects of this research and work out next steps to ensure the sector is supported, in terms of training and development, staff retention and other matters raised during the research process.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to (i) provide an update on the evaluation of the Condition Management Programme; and (ii) detail what consideration has been giving to rolling this programme out to Housing Associations.
answer_text: An evaluation of the Condition Management Programme is due to be undertaken, by my Department, in April 2023.		Officials from my Department have had ongoing engagement with officials in the Housing Executive in relation to how their tenants and tenants from their partner organisitions may avail of the Condition Management Programme. The outcome from that engagement being that Housing Association Case Workers can signpost tenants to the programme, as appropriate.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to provide an update on the implementation of the five further homelessness initiatives across Northern Ireland, as outlined in the Interdepartmental Homelessness Action Plan - Year 3 Action Plan.
answer_text: The Year 3 Interdepartmental Homelessness Action Plan was published on 6 May 2022.  Officials are now preparing to report against the actions in the Plan and this Year 3 Report will be published shortly with the agreement of other Departments.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what measures she has taken to ensure that a Housing Strategy, which includes homelessness, is a priority for a future Programme for Government.
answer_text: I am on record as stating that it is essential to have a standalone Housing Outcome in our next Programme for Government (PfG), in order to fully recognise the central role that good housing plays. I will continue to press for this commitment to be fulfilled.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail what representations she will be making to the UK Government, along with her Scottish and Welsh counterparts, in relation to the loss of European Social Fund funding from April 2023 and of the need for the Shared Prosperity Fund to be increased and delivered locally.
answer_text: My Executive colleagues and I, led by the Minister of Finance, engage regularly with the UK Government and the other devolved administrations regarding delivery of funding streams under the Levelling Up agenda, including the Shared Prosperity Fund.We will continue to press the UK Government on plans for post-2025 funding and seek to maximise opportunities and secure the best possible outcome for Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how her Department will ensure that any voluntary sector partners that are recruited as part of the implementation of Fair Start recommendations will be resourced adequately.
answer_text: Key Area 4 of the "A Fair Start" report relates to "Promoting a whole community approach to education".  Action (iii) within Key Area 4 states "DE should co-design a specification for a whole community, partnership approach entitled the “Reducing Educational Disadvantage” (RED) programme. This should be strategic in scale and collaborative in nature, mandating co-design and the building of authentic partnerships between schools and communities using a place-based approach".Work on the RED Programme is at an early stage of development and involves numerous stakeholders and partners from the public and voluntary and community sectors.  Currently emphasis is on developing those partnerships and relationships to enable place based approaches to be developed depending upon the needs of the children and young people, families and communities in the area. We are also working with other Departments to explore the possibility of greater collaboration in areas of specific need. The resources available for this action and the "A Fair Start" report as a whole is dependent upon budget settlements going forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how her Department will measure the success of financial assistance that is given to developing new professional qualifications for school leadership as part of Fair Start.
answer_text: This work is at an early stage, as money was first allocated in the 2022/23 financial year.  A working group has been convened to establish, in the first instance, a baseline of need and to review existing provision.   Following this, a suite of quality-assured programmes and, where appropriate, qualifications will be developed.  The success of the finance invested will be determined by the provision of this suite of programmes and by subsequent evaluations as cohorts of school leaders undertake them.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education why the delivery of a System Evaluation Framework has been delayed.
answer_text: Key Area 3 of the "A Fair Start" report relates to "Ensuring relevance and appropriateness of Curriculum and Assessment".  Action (i) within Key Area 3 states "DE should progress its work to put in place a System Evaluation Framework by September 2022.Due to an unforeseen staffing issue there was a short delay to work on this action getting underway. Delivery of the prototype is now scheduled for end December 2022 with the final report due by end of March 2023.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, in relation to the additional action identified in the June 2022 Fair Start Report, what proposals are being considered for children with additional needs outside the Belfast area.
answer_text: The additional action reported in the June 2022 “A Fair Start” Progress Report relates to a Belfast-wide pilot supporting children with additional needs using a two-year pre-school model.  It is being tested across the whole of the city and part of its evaluation will consider future wider feasibility, subject to the availability of budget.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how the locations of the 16 new Nurture Groups that have been taken forward as part of Fair Start were decided.
answer_text: The locations of the 16 new Nurture Groups were selected using the following criteria: The school has:a high number of pupils with social services involvement (Children Looked After (CLA), child on the Child Protection Register (CPR) or child known to social services); anda high number of pupils living in areas of deprivation (as measured by the Extended Schools eligibility criteria); There are no school sustainability issues;The latest P1 and P2 enrolment number is at least 20 for each year group;The school’s Board of Governors and Senior Management Team are supportive of nurturing principles and whole school nurture practice.For those schools which were not included in the recent announcement who remain committed to nurturing principles they can engage in the Nurture in Education Programme, which is delivered by the Education Authority.  This programme is available to all primary, special, post-primary schools and Education Otherwise Than At School (EOTAS) settings to allow staff to avail of training and support in nurture approaches.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what long-term measures her Department is undertaking to increase the supply of special educational needs trained teaching staff.
answer_text: I am aware of the enormous challenges that are currently being felt within all schools particularly in relation to the availability of teaching and non-teaching staff with the appropriate skills to meet the growing needs of children and young people with special educational needs (SEN). While the Department is not responsible for the supply of special educational needs (SEN) trained teachers, it is responsible for the overall approval of Initial Teacher Education intakes and programmes. The Higher Education Institutions (HEIs), which deliver these programmes, are responsible for ensuring the courses they provide are aligned with the Northern Ireland Curriculum and are ‘fit-for-purpose’ to  prepare future teachers for the classroom.The Department has met with representatives from the HEIs to discuss their initial teacher education and SEN offer.  This work will be developed further following publication of the Independent Review of SEN, which is due shortly.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the actions within the A Fair Start Progress Report from June 2022 which currently have a red classification.
answer_text: There are 47 actions being taken forward this year, none of which are classified with a status of Red. 31 actions are Green (started / underway / achieved); 10 are Amber (work imminent; likely to be delivered with some delay) and 6 Blue (preliminary work underway to inform future or timed for Medium Term (Yr3+) to Long Term (Yr5+).There are some actions which are not being progressed this year due to either prioritisation of other actions which means they can’t be undertaken this year or financial constraints. These actions are: Key Area 4 (vi) Exemplars of effective practice should be identified by EA youth Sector and voluntary sector teams and Key Area 4 (vii) EA should establish a formal mechanism through which Area Learning Communities (ALCs) and Youth Service Local Area Groups (LAGs) and Youth Service Regional Area Groups (RAGs) can form strategic links and share effective practice.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what action her Department is taking to encourage more people into Early Years and childcare professions.
answer_text: Issues faced by the early years workforce are being considered as part of the development of an Executive Early Learning and Childcare Strategy.  An Independent Review of Childcare Services commissioned by my Department is currently ongoing and will assist in determining the extent of recruitment and retention challenges within the sector and help inform potential actions.  Workforce issues will also be considered by the Early Learning and Childcare Stakeholder Engagement Forum, which I have established to inform the work on the Strategy. The Department for the Economy (DfE) is the sponsor department for the six Further Education (FE) colleges that deliver a range of relevant subjects including Child Development and Wellbeing.  DfE has advised that there is no evidence of a decline in the numbers of students studying these subjects in recent years.  However, not all students which complete such courses choose to enter, or remain within the early years workforce.  The Review of Childcare Services will explore these issues.  The Department of Health has statutory responsibility for the regulation and inspection of childcare provision and I have asked the Health Minister to consider what can be done to streamline some of the processes around staff recruitment/transfer.  In recognition of the staffing challenges facing the childcare sector, a number of easements have already been made to the Minimum Standards for Childminding and Day Care. The Department of Health has also given a commitment to carry out a comprehensive review of the current Minimum Standards for Childminding and Day Care and to review recruitment requirements, with a view to streamlining the process where possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what work her Department is undertaking to address staff shortages in the Early Years sector.
answer_text: Issues faced by the early years workforce are being considered as part of the development of an Executive Early Learning and Childcare Strategy.  An Independent Review of Childcare Services commissioned by my Department is currently ongoing and will assist in determining the extent of recruitment and retention challenges within the sector and help inform potential actions.  Workforce issues will also be considered by the Early Learning and Childcare Stakeholder Engagement Forum, which I have established to inform the work on the Strategy. The Department for the Economy (DfE) is the sponsor department for the six Further Education (FE) colleges that deliver a range of relevant subjects including Child Development and Wellbeing.  DfE has advised that there is no evidence of a decline in the numbers of students studying these subjects in recent years.  However, not all students which complete such courses choose to enter, or remain within the early years workforce.  The Review of Childcare Services will explore these issues.  The Department of Health has statutory responsibility for the regulation and inspection of childcare provision and I have asked the Health Minister to consider what can be done to streamline some of the processes around staff recruitment/transfer.  In recognition of the staffing challenges facing the childcare sector, a number of easements have already been made to the Minimum Standards for Childminding and Day Care. The Department of Health has also given a commitment to carry out a comprehensive review of the current Minimum Standards for Childminding and Day Care and to review recruitment requirements, with a view to streamlining the process where possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the review of Healthy Child, Healthy Future.
answer_text: My Department agreed a review and update of the Healthy Child, Healthy Future (HCHF) programme in October 2018 to take account of the evidence provided in the Fifth edition of ‘Health for All Children’ and further evidence which has emerged since its publication in 2019.  While this key strategic review was delayed due to the pandemic response, work commenced in February 2022 and has progressed at pace.	The structure of the review has been formalised with the Programme Board and Programme Team having met several times in the last 6 months. Working Groups have been established to cover three key areas: 	Working Group 1– Pre-conception and Pregnancy  	Working Group 2 – Pre-school (0 to age at primary school entry)	Working Group 3 – School Aged (age at primary school entry to 19 years)	The Groups have the capacity to work to scrutinise the recommendations in line with current procedure and practice since their memberships are drawn from representatives of those professions that deliver the HCHF programme.  This includes nursing and midwifery representatives from each Trust, with additional input from a range of other health professional groups and Education professionals. 	Engagement workshops have taken place within Trusts, for Health Visitors and School Nurses, with regional workshops also held for Midwives, Health Visitor managers and School nursing managers.  A Regional Survey has been disseminated to all relevant Practitioners with the feedback being analysed currently.   Further Engagement workshops have taken place with the voluntary/community sector and SureStart staff supported by the Public Health Agency (PHA). Service users have been represented from the onset and views are being captured through Care Opinion.It is anticipated that the Review and update of the HCHF programme will be completed in January 2023

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether local decision makers in Northern Ireland will be required to implement the benefit sanctions as announced by the Chancellor in relation to Universal Credit claimants working up to 15 hours at minimum wage.
answer_text: No decision has yet been made as to whether changes announced to the Administrative Earnings Threshold (AET) level for Universal Credit will be implemented here. DfC officials continue to engage with the Department for Work and Pensions to establish if there are any costs and/or parity issues of having a different AET here.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance what the target time is for NICS staff to be seen by Occupational Health Service following a referral.
answer_text: The Occupational Health Service (OHS) 12 day target for completion of sick absence referrals was suspended during the Covid-19 pandemic.  OHS is currently working through the backlog of cases created during the pandemic, and as a result of ongoing challenges faced due to increasing demands on services and resourcing pressures in Medical Officer and Nursing positions.  The operating model for the OHS is currently under review and recruitment competitions for key medical positions are underway.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for an update on the work to evaluate Long Covid services; and (ii) when details of any future service model will be released.
answer_text: (i) My Department of Health is currently leading on a review of post-Covid services, to ensure that we continue to provide appropriate post-Covid services. This will be based on an assessment of the ongoing demand for services; a review of current service provision; and taking cognisance of service developments elsewhere in the UK. Subject to the outcome of that evaluation, a new business case will be developed for funding of post-Covid services in the long term. (ii) It is anticipated that the evaluation will be completed, and a new business case developed for services going forward, by the end of the financial year, with a view to new services being in place as appropriate from 1 April 2023 onwards.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether she intends to establish a further Emergency Fuel Payment Scheme.
answer_text: The cost of living crisis is having a major impact, particularly on those who are already struggling financially.I am deeply frustrated that the continued absence of a functioning Executive and agreed Executive budget prevents additional measures being introduced.I am committed that my Department will do all that it can to support those experiencing hardship, and all options are being explored to provide both immediate and medium to longer term interventions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) to provide an update on the review of homelessness legislation; and (ii) what provisions are being considered around creating statutory duties in relation to homelessness prevention.
answer_text: The Homelessness Duties are set out in the Housing Order. My officials recently carried out a desk top review of homelessness legislation. Options to improve homelessness legislation are under consideration.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance (i) how many NICS staff are waiting for an Occupational Health Service assessment; and (ii) how long these staff have been waiting for an assessment.
answer_text: As at 11 October there is a caseload of 195 NICS staff waiting for an Occupational Health Service assessment (which we have taken to mean assessments in relation to employees who are absent from work due to sickness absence).  Waiting times vary depending on the type of referral and the nature of medical assessment required, but the average referral waiting time for sickness absence Occupational Health Service assessments is 18 weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the delivery of the Complex Lives model for addressing homelessness across Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The Housing Executive has statutory responsibility for responding to homelessness and has conducted an evaluation of the prototype phase of the Complex Lives initiative.  The evaluation found that the Multidisciplinary Team (MDT) has demonstrated success in facilitating positive outcomes in regards to accommodation, support and sustained meaningful engagement.  All Housing Executive partners have agreed that working relationships across agencies have been significantly enhanced and agencies, who are present in the city centre, have reported a reduction in disturbance.   Following the evaluation of the prototype phase and based on a range of strategic proposals made by the Steering Group, a Business Case was completed to progress the partnership approach in 2022/23.  Approval has been given to continue to operate the internal Housing Executive staff team dedicated to the Complex Lives approach within Belfast and to contribute to the acquisition for external resources in partnership with other Strategic Leadership Group members until 31 March 2023.  Subject to available funding and evaluation of the impact of the acquisition of external resources, it is likely this initiative will be further expanded within the Belfast Region and consideration given to expanding to other areas.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on her Department’s review of the Free School Meals and Uniform Grant eligibility criteria.
answer_text: My Department is currently finalising a costed options paper on the review of the Free School Meals and Uniform Grant eligibility criteria that will form part of a public consultation later this year.The review has considered a wide range of options that include the provision of a universal free school meals to some/all year groups as well as raising the income thresholds that apply to the eligibility criteria for applicants in receipt of Universal Credit or Working/Child Tax Credit. As I announced in August, it is my intention that guidance on the costs of school uniforms will be placed on a statutory footing. My officials are continuing to engage with the Departmental Solicitor’s Office on how this can best be achieved, recognising my wish that this guidance is in place as soon as possible.  As is normal practice, there are no plans to publish the legal advice the Department receives on this issue. The development process will continue to involve a range of stakeholders whose input will be crucial in ensuring that the guidance is able to meet its aim of making uniforms more affordable for families. It will also take into account the statutory guidance on this issue that now applies in England and Wales.  Following the completion of initial engagement with key stakeholders and the development of draft guidance on the costs of uniforms, a public consultation will be carried out.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether her Department officials have engaged with their counterparts throughout the UK regarding school uniform guidance.
answer_text: My Department is currently finalising a costed options paper on the review of the Free School Meals and Uniform Grant eligibility criteria that will form part of a public consultation later this year.The review has considered a wide range of options that include the provision of a universal free school meals to some/all year groups as well as raising the income thresholds that apply to the eligibility criteria for applicants in receipt of Universal Credit or Working/Child Tax Credit. As is normal practice, there are no plans to publish the legal advice the Department receives on this issue. The development process will continue to involve a range of stakeholders whose input will be crucial in ensuring that the guidance is able to meet its aim of making uniforms more affordable for families. It will also take into account the statutory guidance on this issue that now applies in England and Wales.  Following the completion of initial engagement with key stakeholders and the development of draft guidance on the costs of uniforms, a public consultation will be carried out.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail what measures are in place to prioritise stroke prevention in the community.
answer_text: I recognise that stroke is preventable and I am committed to making improvements towards stroke prevention. To that end, My Department’s Reshaping Stroke Care Action Plan, published on 20th June outlines steps taken to date to improve stroke prevention including the endorsement of a range of guidance from the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence.  Alongside this, the Action Plan sets out specific priority actions to establish seven day access to Transient Ischaemic Attack (TIA) assessment within 24 hours of stroke symptoms. As the Action Plan is implemented, my Department will continue to work across the health and social care sector, and with other government departments and agencies, to ensure we create the environment that better supports and empowers people to manage and improve their health and wellbeing and will look for further opportunities to improve stroke prevention.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) to detail the current budget allocated for homelessness prevention; and (ii) what proportion of the overall homelessness budget does this represent.
answer_text: Due to the continued absence of a functioning Executive and agreed Executive budget the Department is working with a Contingency Planning Envelope.The current forecast spend for homelessness prevention is £5.5m, approximately 20% of the forecast homelessness services expenditure for 2022/23.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline the different options under consideration by her Department regarding changing the guidance to schools on school uniform policy.
answer_text: My Department is currently finalising a costed options paper on the review of the Free School Meals and Uniform Grant eligibility criteria that will form part of a public consultation later this year.The review has considered a wide range of options that include the provision of a universal free school meals to some/all year groups as well as raising the income thresholds that apply to the eligibility criteria for applicants in receipt of Universal Credit or Working/Child Tax Credit. question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether she will implement a temporary freeze on benefit deductions to assist claimants with the rising cost of living.
answer_text: Any pause in debt recovery would require a break in parity from the Department for Work and Pensions. The financial cost of any divergence would require Executive agreement.The Department for Communities recognises the financial hardship that many people may be experiencing because of the cost of living and is fully committed to supporting and helping individuals who may be finding difficulty with the repayment of social security benefit debt. Individuals should contact the Department’s Debt Management team, who will work with them to agree an affordable repayment plan. Contact details for Debt Management can be found on https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/contacts/debt-management-northern-ireland

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) when she expects to receive a legal opinion on a school uniform price cap; and (ii) whether she will make this information available to the public.
answer_text: My Department is currently finalising a costed options paper on the review of the Free School Meals and Uniform Grant eligibility criteria that will form part of a public consultation later this year.The review has considered a wide range of options that include the provision of a universal free school meals to some/all year groups as well as raising the income thresholds that apply to the eligibility criteria for applicants in receipt of Universal Credit or Working/Child Tax Credit. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when the workforce survey for Community Stroke Services will be completed.
answer_text: I recognise the vital role of the stroke workforce in delivering high quality stroke services. That is why a Stroke Workforce Review was identified as a key priority in the recently published Reshaping Stroke Care Action Plan. The Workforce Review will encompass both the hospital-based and community-based stroke workforce.My Officials are currently undertaking a stakeholder mapping exercise both to inform membership of a Stroke Workforce Review Group and an approach to wider engagement as the work progresses. Once complete, it is intended that work will begin at pace to deliver a Stroke Workforce Review report by Summer 2023 as set out in the Action Plan

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance what barriers exist to permit data sharing between Industrial Injuries Benefit and Civil Service Pensions for the purpose of assessing Permanent Injury Awards.
answer_text: The Department for Communities (DfC) has provided the following response: From an Industrial Injuries Disablement Benefit (IIDB) perspective, DfC are unaware of any barriers. The IIDB branch receive requests from Civil Service pensions to confirm rates of IIDB in payment.  These are received with signed authority from the claimant, giving permission to share the information. Industrial Injuries branch supply the requested rates in writing. For the purposes of assessing Permanent Injury Awards, Civil Service Pensions, Department of Finance (DoF), obtains explicit consent, signed authority, from the claimant of a Permanent Injury Award to contact the relevant benefit Branch(es), including IIDB branch, for confirmation of any entitlement to specified benefits. This signed consent permits data sharing between IIDB branch and Civil Service Pensions. There are no statutory powers in place to permit DfC or DoF to take forward secondary legislation to permit the sharing of information without explicit consent. Therefore, it would have to be pursued using primary legislation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education when her Department will issue statutory guidelines to require schools to make uniforms more affordable.
answer_text: My Department is currently finalising a costed options paper on the review of the Free School Meals and Uniform Grant eligibility criteria that will form part of a public consultation later this year.The review has considered a wide range of options that include the provision of a universal free school meals to some/all year groups as well as raising the income thresholds that apply to the eligibility criteria for applicants in receipt of Universal Credit or Working/Child Tax Credit. As I announced in August, it is my intention that guidance on the costs of school uniforms will be placed on a statutory footing.
